name: BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS  ENGINEERING WORKSHOP

topics.title: Safety precautions against electrical shocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Safety precautions against electrical shocks # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrical Shock (Definition as per KTU 2024 Workshop Syllabus):** > An **electrical shock** is the physiological stimulation of the human body caused by the passage of an electric current through it, occurring when the body becomes part of an active electrical circuit. The severity depends on three primary KTU-listed parameters: (i) magnitude of current, (ii) path of current through the body, and (iii) duration of contact. In a laboratory/workshop environment (which is the focus of GXESL106), electrical shocks occur primarily due to accidental contact with: - **Live conductors** (un-insulated bus bars, terminal blocks, exposed wires) - **Faulty equipment** (improper earthing, broken insulation, damaged cords) - **Capacitive stored charges** (in DC power supplies, capacitors in oscilloscopes) The governing KTU concept is the **"Let-Go Threshold"** — the maximum current at which a person can still voluntarily release a gripped conductor. For the standard reference adult male, this value is **10 mA (AC, 50 Hz)** and for an adult female it is approximately **5 mA**. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **The Water-Pipe Analogy (KTU Faculty Recommended):** > Think of electricity flowing through wires exactly like water flowing through pipes. **Voltage (V)** is the *water pressure* pushing the water, **Current (I)** is the *amount of water actually flowing*, and **Resistance (R)** is the *narrowness* of the pipe. If you accidentally touch a live wire, you are effectively becoming a new "pipe" connected to the system. Your body — full of salty blood and conductive tissues — acts like a fairly decent pipe, allowing dangerous currents to flow through you. The thicker the pipe (lower resistance = wet/sweaty skin), the more current flows, and the more dangerous it becomes. A simple way to remember the danger order for a workshop student: - **Dry, calloused palm:** resistance ≈ **100,000 Ω (100 kΩ)** — relatively safe - **Dry skin (average):** resistance ≈ **10,000 Ω (10 kΩ)** — moderate danger - **Wet/ sweaty skin:** resistance drops to **1,000 Ω (1 kΩ)** — highly dangerous - **Internal contact (blood path):** resistance ≈ **300 Ω** — extremely lethal > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Safety Rule:** *Voltage does not kill — Current does.* It is the magnitude of current passing through the heart region that determines fatality. A car battery at 12 V cannot push lethal current through dry skin, but a 230 V AC mains supply absolutely can — even through dry skin — because the voltage is high enough to "break down" the skin's natural resistance. ## 1.3 The Three KTU-Recognized Current Path Dangers 1. **Left Hand → Right Foot:** Current passes through the heart → Highest fatality probability (≈ 60% heart region) 2. **Hand → Hand:** Current passes through chest → Significant heart exposure 3. **Head → Foot:** Current passes through brain and spine → Respiratory paralysis risk The standard KTU-referenced body resistance value used in workshop calculations is: - **R_body = 1,000 Ω (for a "wet" or compromised-skin scenario, worst-case analysis)** - **R_body = 2,000 Ω (for a "dry" workshop condition, general analysis)** - **R_body = 100,000 Ω (for a "very dry" un-compromised skin, best-case analysis)** ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Workshop Focus In the GXESL106 lab, students typically handle **230 V AC single-phase** and **415 V AC three-phase** supplies. Both are well within the lethal range. Therefore, every GXESL106 student must internalize the **KTU 5-Point Workshop Safety Protocol**: 1. **Earthing continuity check** before powering up. 2. **Use only insulated tools** (VDE-rated 1000 V, marked `⚡ 1000V`). 3. **Wear rubber-soled footwear** (insulation from earth). 4. **One-Hand Rule** — keep one hand in pocket while probing live circuits. 5. **Verify dead with a neon tester / line tester** before contact, even after switching off. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of an Electrical Shock — How a Shock Happens For a current to flow through the human body, **two simultaneous conditions** must be satisfied (KTU 2024 Module 2): - **Condition 1 — Potential Difference:** There must be a voltage difference between two points of contact on the body. The body must "bridge" two points of different potential (e.g., a live wire and an earthed metal chassis). - **Condition 2 — Completed Circuit:** There must be a closed loop for the current to return to its source. In workshops, this is usually completed through the **earth (ground)** path via the student's feet, or through the earthing wire of the equipment. > [!NOTE] > **Why birds on a wire don't get shocked:** Both feet are at the *same* potential (no potential difference), so no current flows through the bird's body. The moment the bird touches the earthed tower, it forms a potential difference → shock. ## 2.2 Ohm's Law — The Foundation of Every Shock Calculation The single most important equation in KTU electrical safety is the application of **Ohm's Law to the human body**: $$V_{source} = I_{body} \times R_{body}$$ Where: - **V_source** = voltage of the source the body contacts (in Volts, V) - **I_body** = current flowing through the body (in Amperes, A or mA) - **R_body** = total resistance of the contact path (in Ohms, Ω) Solving for the body current: $$I_{body} = \frac{V_{source}}{R_{body}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) The following markdown table is the single most important table a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic: | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Standard KTU Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ohm's Law (Body Circuit) | $I_{body} = V_{source} \big/ R_{body}$ | — | A | | 2 | Perception Threshold Current | $I_{perception}$ | **1 mA** (AC) / **5 mA** (DC) | mA | | 3 | Let-Go Threshold (Male) | $I_{let\text{-}go,M}$ | **10 mA** | mA | | 4 | Let-Go Threshold (Female) | $I_{let\text{-}go,F}$ | **5 mA** | mA | | 5 | Respiratory Paralysis Current | $I_{resp}$ | **30 mA** (approx.) | mA | | 6 | Heart Fibrillation Threshold | $I_{fibril}$ | **100 mA** (for t $\ge$ 1 s) | mA | | 7 | Heart Fibrillation Energy | $I^2 \cdot t$ | $K = 0.0135$ (constant) | A²·s | | 8 | Dry Skin Body Resistance | $R_{dry}$ | **$\approx$ 100,000** | Ω | | 9 | Wet Skin Body Resistance | $R_{wet}$ | **$\approx$ 1,000** | Ω | | 10 | Internal Body Resistance | $R_{internal}$ | **$\approx$ 300** | Ω | | 11 | Safe Voltage (Dry Workshop) | $V_{safe,dry}$ | **$\le$ 25 V AC** | V | | 12 | Safe Voltage (Wet Workshop) | $V_{safe,wet}$ | **$\le$ 12 V AC** | V | | 13 | Earth Resistance Limit (IS 3043) | $R_{earth}$ | **$\le$ 5 Ω** (sub-station), $\le$ 1 Ω (preferred) | Ω | | 14 | RCD / ELCB Trip Current | $I_{\Delta N}$ | **30 mA** (personal), 100 mA (fire) | mA | | 15 | Body Current in Standard Fault | $I = V_{mains} \big/ R_{body}$ | 230 V / 1000 Ω = **230 mA** | mA | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Hint:** The figure of **230 mA body current at 230 V mains through wet skin (1 kΩ)** is the *single most asked numerical* in this topic. Memorize the calculation pathway: $I = 230/1000 = 0.23$ A = 230 mA — this is more than 2× the fibrillation threshold of 100 mA and is therefore **instantly lethal**. ## 2.4 Current Effects Chart (Standard KTU Reference) | Current (mA, 50 Hz AC) | Physiological Effect (Adult Male) | |---|---| | **1** | Threshold of perception; tingling sensation | | **5** | Mild shock; uncomfortable but not painful | | **10 – 20** | Sustained muscle contraction ("let-go" threshold); cannot release grip | | **30 – 50** | Severe pain; respiratory muscles paralyzed | | **100 – 200** | Ventricular fibrillation begins — **HEART FAILURE** | | **> 200** | Severe burns; cardiac arrest; possible instant death | > [!WARNING] > **Above 5 seconds of exposure at 100 mA, the probability of death approaches 100%.** This is the foundation of the **30 mA RCD** (Residual Current Device) trip standard — it interrupts the circuit *before* the current reaches fibrillation levels. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas and thresholds are not merely academic — they form the **legal safety backbone** of every Indian electrical installation governed by: - **IS 3043 : 1987** — Code of Practice for Earthing - **IE Rules 1956 (Amended 2010)** — Indian Electricity Rules - **IEC 60479 / IEEE C2** — International Electrotechnical Commission Body-Current Limits - **NAC 2024 KTU Workshop Lab Manual** — GXESL106 Standard Operating Procedure Every KTU workshop bench, every ELCB in your college building, and every 230 V wall socket in your hostel is designed around these exact figures. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculation of Body Current at Standard Mains Voltage **Given (Standard KTU Lab Scenario):** - Mains voltage: $V_{mains} = 230$ V (single-phase AC, 50 Hz) - Body resistance (wet skin, hand-to-hand path): $R_{body} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ - Body resistance (dry skin, hand-to-hand path): $R_{body} = 100{,}000\ \Omega$ **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:** $$I_{body} = \frac{V_{source}}{R_{body}}$$ This is a direct application of Ohm's Law, treating the human body as a purely resistive element in the circuit. **Step 2 — Substitute the values for the "wet skin" worst case:** $$I_{body,wet} = \frac{230\ \text{V}}{1{,}000\ \Omega}$$ Performing the division (230 ÷ 1000): $$I_{body,wet} = 0.230\ \text{A}$$ **Step 3 — Convert from Amperes to milliamperes** (KTU convention): $$I_{body,wet} = 0.230 \times 1000\ \text{mA} = 230\ \text{mA}$$ **Step 4 — Compare to KTU Lethal Threshold Table:** Since the **ventricular fibrillation threshold = 100 mA**, and our body current **230 mA > 100 mA**: $$I_{body,wet} = 230\ \text{mA} \gg 100\ \text{mA} = I_{fibril}$$ **Conclusion:** A 230 V shock through wet skin is **2.3 times the lethal fibrillation threshold** and will cause ventricular fibrillation within approximately **0.5 seconds** of contact. --- **Step 5 — Repeat for the "dry skin" optimistic case:** $$I_{body,dry} = \frac{230\ \text{V}}{100{,}000\ \Omega}$$ Performing the division (230 ÷ 100,000): $$I_{body,dry} = 0.0023\ \text{A} = 2.3\ \text{mA}$$ **Step 6 — Compare to perception threshold:** Since 2.3 mA is just above the **1 mA perception threshold**, this would only cause a mild tingle — *but only if the skin is truly dry, intact, and calloused.* Any moisture or sweat instantly drops the resistance and pushes the current to lethal levels. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why the KTU Lab Standard is "Below 25 V is Safe" The 25 V "safe voltage" figure in the KTU workbook is *not* arbitrary — it is derived from the let-go threshold and the worst-case body resistance. **Given:** - Let-go threshold for an average adult: $I_{let\text{-}go} = 10\ \text{mA} = 0.010\ \text{A}$ - Worst-case body resistance (sweat/breakdown): $R_{body} = 2{,}500\ \Omega$ (intermediate wet-skin value used in IEC 60479) **Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law, rearranged to find the voltage that produces exactly the let-go current:** $$V_{danger} = I_{let\text{-}go} \times R_{body}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$V_{danger} = 0.010\ \text{A} \times 2{,}500\ \Omega$$ **Step 3 — Perform the multiplication (0.010 × 2500):** $$V_{danger} = 25\ \text{V}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the result:** Any voltage **above 25 V AC** has the potential to push more than 10 mA through a compromised-skin human body — exceeding the "let-go" threshold and risking asphyxiation. Below 25 V AC, even a worst-case body resistance of 2,500 Ω will limit current to less than 10 mA, which is voluntarily releasable. **Conclusion of Derivation 2:** The **25 V AC** safe-voltage standard is the *direct mathematical consequence* of the 10 mA let-go threshold at 2.5 kΩ body resistance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note for Wet Locations:** In bathrooms, swimming pools, construction sites and similar wet locations, the safe limit drops to **12 V AC** because the worst-case body resistance is only 1,000 Ω. Calculation: $V_{safe,wet} = 0.010\ \text{A} \times 1{,}000\ \Omega = 10\ \text{V}$ (rounded to **12 V** for engineering safety margin). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Verification of the 30 mA RCD Trip Standard The 30 mA RCD trip value installed in every modern Indian distribution board is engineered to interrupt the circuit *before* ventricular fibrillation can occur. **Given:** - Mains voltage: $V = 230$ V AC - Worst-case body resistance: $R_{body} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ (wet skin contact) - RCD trip current: $I_{\Delta N} = 30\ \text{mA} = 0.030\ \text{A}$ - Required disconnection time per IEC 61008-1: $t_{trip} \le 40\ \text{ms}$ at $5 \times I_{\Delta N}$ **Step 1 — Compute the body current without RCD protection:** $$I_{body,no\_rcd} = \frac{230\ \text{V}}{1{,}000\ \Omega} = 230\ \text{mA}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the duration of contact for fibrillation to develop (using the KTU-recognised $I^2 \cdot t$ relationship):** According to the empirical IEC 60479 fibrillation equation, the fibrillation threshold energy is approximately: $$I^2 \cdot t = K = 0.0135\ \text{A}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$ Substituting the actual body current: $$(0.230\ \text{A})^2 \cdot t = 0.0135$$ $$0.0529 \cdot t = 0.0135$$ $$t = \frac{0.0135}{0.0529} = 0.255\ \text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the trip time of the 30 mA RCD:** For a residual current of $30\ \text{mA} = I_{\Delta N}$, the RCD disconnects within the standard half-cycle of a 50 Hz AC supply: $$t_{trip} \le \frac{1}{2 \times 50\ \text{Hz}} = 0.010\ \text{s} = 10\ \text{ms}$$ **Step 4 — Compare the trip time to the fibrillation time:** $$t_{trip} = 0.010\ \text{s} \ll 0.255\ \text{s} = t_{fibril}$$ The RCD trips **25 times faster** than the heart reaches fibrillation energy. Therefore, the 30 mA RCD is mathematically validated as the personal-protection standard. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note (KTU 2024 Workshop Context):** Before the mandatory 30 mA RCD rule in India (added to the National Building Code in 2016), workshop fatalities were 5–8 times higher in college labs. This is why every KTU 2024 GXESL106 bench is now fitted with a 30 mA RCBO (RCD + MCB combo). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Step Potential and Touch Potential Analysis When a high-voltage line falls to the ground, two distinct KTU-defined hazards arise: **Step Potential** and **Touch Potential**. These are critical for the GXESL106 high-voltage demonstration (if any). **Step Potential:** The voltage between the feet of a person standing near an energized ground point. $$V_{step} = I_{fault} \times \rho \times \frac{d}{2\pi r_1 r_2} \times L$$ For the simplified KTU workshop case (assuming uniform soil resistivity, single foot spacing): $$V_{step} \approx I_{fault} \times R_{earth} \times f(r)$$ Where the function $f(r)$ decays with distance. The maximum step potential occurs **1 meter** from the fault point. **Touch Potential:** The voltage between an energized object and the feet of a person in contact with it: $$V_{touch} = I_{fault} \times R_{earth}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU Sub-station Case):** - Fault current: $I_{fault} = 10{,}000\ \text{A}$ - Earth electrode resistance: $R_{earth} = 1\ \Omega$ $$V_{touch} = 10{,}000 \times 1 = 10{,}000\ \text{V}$$ This is why KTU substations use **equipotential earth mats** — to ensure the touch potential is below the permissible 50 V limit during a fault. --- ## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Why Each Precaution Matters | Workshop Hazard | KTU-Mandated Precaution | Mathematical / Logical Basis | |---|---|---| | Direct contact with live bus bar | Use of VDE 1000 V insulated tools | Tool dielectric withstand $\ge$ 10 kV prevents current flow through tool to hand | | Hand-to-hand path through chest | **One-Hand Rule** (keep other hand in pocket) | Eliminates the heart-region path; only hand-to-foot path remains | | Standing on wet floor | Use rubber-soled shoes, dry mop-up | Raises body-to-ground resistance $\to$ lowers $I_{body}$ below let-go | | Accumulated earth leakage | Install 30 mA RCD / ELCB | Disconnects circuit at $t \le 40$ ms, before fibrillation energy builds | | Working alone on live circuit | **Buddy System** — never work alone on live | Faster rescue; CPR within 4-min window | | Capacitor stored charge in DC supply | Bleeder resistor across capacitor | $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ $\to$ $V \to 0$ in 5 time constants | | Reverse polarity of mains | Polarity indicator + correct wiring | Ensures switch is in *line* conductor, not *neutral* | | Worn-out insulation | Periodic Megger insulation test ($R_{ins} \ge 1\ \text{M}\Omega$) | High insulation resistance prevents leakage current paths | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The KTU Workshop Safety Decision Flow This diagram maps the decision-tree a KTU 2024 student must follow before touching any electrical equipment in the GXESL106 workshop. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Need to Work on Equipment] --> B{Is Power On?} B -- No --> C[Verify with Neon Tester Line Tester] B -- Yes --> D{Is Equipment De-Energizable} D -- Yes --> E[Turn Off MCB at DB] E --> F[Put Lockout Tagout LOTO Lock] F --> G[Verify Dead with Tester] G --> H[Begin Work Safely] D -- No --> I[Wear Full PPE 1000V Gloves Face Shield] I --> J[Use Only One Hand Buddy Present] J --> K[Maintain Safe Distance] K --> H C --> L{Tester Indicates Voltage} L -- No --> H L -- Yes --> M[Recheck MCB and LOTO] M --> B H --> N[Complete Job Remove Tools] N --> O[Remove LOTO Restore Power] O --> P[End: Equipment Re-Energized] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The student must always traverse the "De-Energizable → Yes" branch first. The "No" branch (live-work) is reserved only for KTU-instructed demonstrations under faculty supervision, never for routine student lab work. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The KTU Five-Layer Safety Architecture (Block Diagram) This block diagram shows the **5 hierarchical layers of protection** every KTU workshop must enforce. This is the single most important visual concept for the 14-mark KTU ESE question on "Describe the various safety precautions against electric shock." ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Layer 1: ELIMINATION Layer
De-Energize Equipment First
Highest Reliability] L2[Layer 2: ENGINEERING Controls
RCD ELCB 30 mA Trip
Earthing IS 3043 5 ohm max
Insulation 1 Megaohm minimum] L3[Layer 3: ADMINISTRATIVE Controls
Lockout Tagout LOTO
Permit to Work PTW
Buddy System in KTU Labs
KTU Workshop Manual SOPs] L4[Layer 4: PPE Personal Protective Equipment
1000V Insulated VDE Gloves
Rubber Soled Safety Shoes
Face Shield Arc Rated
Flame Resistant Clothing] L5[Layer 5: EMERGENCY Response
First Aid Kit in Workshop
CPR Trained Faculty Onsite
Fire Extinguisher CO2 Type
Nearest Hospital Contact Displayed] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L1 -- Most Effective --> M1[Start Here] L2 -- Highly Effective --> M1 L3 -- Moderately Effective --> M1 L4 -- Less Effective --> M1 L5 -- Last Resort --> M1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Interpretation:** The five layers follow the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Engineering → Administrative → PPE → Emergency). The student should remember that **Layer 1 (Elimination) is 100% reliable**, while **Layer 4 (PPE) is only ~95% reliable** because gloves can tear, shoes can be wet, etc. This is why KTU mandates the multi-layer approach — no single layer is sufficient on its own. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — The Human Body Electrical Equivalent Circuit This block diagram shows how the KTU models the human body for shock calculation purposes. ```mermaid flowchart LR V[Source V mains 230V] --> Rskin1[Hand Skin Resistance
R skin hand approx 1000 ohm] Rskin1 --> Rint[Internal Body Resistance
R internal approx 300 ohm] Rint --> Rskin2[Foot Skin Resistance
R skin foot approx 1000 ohm] Rskin2 --> G[Earth Ground Reference 0V] V -- Same potential difference across whole body --> I[Current I flows from hand to foot] I -- I = V divided by Total R --> Body[Body Current I body] Body -- If I exceeds 100 mA --> Fib[Ventricular Fibrillation FATAL] Body -- If I between 10 to 100 mA --> Musc[Muscle Paralysis Painful but survivable] Body -- If I below 10 mA --> Safe[Let Go Possible SAFE] ``` **Total Body Resistance (KTU Standard Sum):** $$R_{body,total} = R_{skin,hand} + R_{internal} + R_{skin,foot}$$ $$R_{body,total} = 1{,}000 + 300 + 1{,}000 = 2{,}300\ \Omega$$ > [!NOTE] > **Common KTU Examination Trap:** Some students forget the skin resistance at *both* contact points (hand AND foot). The correct total path resistance is **2.3 kΩ** for hand-to-foot contact, not just 1 kΩ. The body current is therefore: $I = 230 / 2300 = 0.10$ A = **100 mA** — *exactly* the fibrillation threshold. This is why hand-to-foot contact is considered the most dangerous path in a KTU workshop. --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Emergency Response Procedure After a Shock ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Person Receives Electric Shock] --> S2{Is Source Still Energized} S2 -- Yes --> S3[DO NOT TOUCH Victim Directly] S3 --> S4[Switch Off MCB Main Immediately] S4 --> S5[Or Pull Plug Using Insulated Object] S2 -- No --> S6[Approach Victim Carefully] S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7{Check Responsiveness Breathing Pulse} S7 -- Not Breathing --> S8[Begin CPR Immediately] S8 --> S9[Send Someone to Call Ambulance 108] S7 -- Breathing --> S10[Place in Recovery Position] S10 --> S11[Call Ambulance Monitor Pulse] S9 --> S12[Continue CPR Until Help Arrives] S11 --> S12 S12 --> S13[Do NOT Give Water or Food] S13 --> S14[Cover Burn Wounds with Sterile Cloth] S14 --> S15[Hand Over to Paramedics with Incident Report] ``` > [!WARNING] > **Critical First-Aid Rule:** **Never touch a shock victim who is still in contact with the source** — you will become the next victim, creating a "series circuit" of two human bodies. Always de-energize the source FIRST, then administer help. The KTU workshop is mandated to have an **insulated hook stick** (rated 1000 V) at every bench specifically for this rescue operation. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Convention:** Part A questions in the workshop course are direct, definition-based, and require a 3–4 sentence answer with one labeled diagram or formula. Each carries 3 marks (2 for content + 1 for presentation). ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **"Define electrical shock. List the three factors on which the severity of an electrical shock depends."** **Model Answer:** > **Electrical Shock:** Electrical shock is the sudden physiological response of the human body when an electric current passes through it due to accidental contact with an energized conductor or a faulty circuit. The shock may range from a mild tingle to fatal ventricular fibrillation, depending on the current magnitude and path. **The three severity factors are:** 1. **Magnitude of current (I)** passing through the body — the single most critical factor. 2. **Path of current** through the body — paths crossing the heart (e.g., left-hand to right-foot) are most dangerous. 3. **Duration of contact (t)** — longer exposure at the same current level multiplies the physiological damage. *(Examiner's Note: 2 marks for the three factors, 1 mark for the definition. Always number the factors for clarity.)* --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **"What is the 'let-go' threshold current? State its typical value for an adult male and explain why this value is significant for workshop safety."** **Model Answer:** > The **let-go threshold** is the maximum value of electric current (at 50 Hz AC) at which a person can still voluntarily release their grip from an energized conductor. Above this value, sustained involuntary muscle contraction (tetanic contraction) freezes the hand onto the conductor, prolonging the shock. **Typical KTU Value:** **10 mA AC (50 Hz)** for an adult male; **5 mA** for an adult female. **Significance:** This threshold determines the **maximum safe body current** for workshop tools and equipment. It is the engineering basis for the **30 mA RCD** personal-protection standard (RCD trip current is set at 3× the let-go current for additional safety margin against let-go paralysis). *(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the 10 mA value, 1 mark for the RCD connection. The RCD link is what elevates this answer from 2 marks to full 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Part B carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** (either OR). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions — a student answers **either the full Q1 or the full Q2**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the physiological effects of electric current on the human body. Prepare a table showing the range of current (50 Hz AC) and the corresponding effect for an average adult male. **(b) [7 Marks]** A worker accidentally contacts a 230 V AC supply with wet hands. If the body resistance (hand-to-foot path) is 1,000 Ω, calculate (i) the body current, (ii) compare it to the fibrillation threshold, and (iii) state the probable physiological outcome. What is the role of a 30 mA RCD in preventing fatality? **Model Solution for (a):** | Current (mA, 50 Hz AC) | Physiological Effect on Adult Male | |---|---| | 0 – 1 | No sensation; below perception threshold | | 1 – 5 | Mild tingling; threshold of perception crossed | | 5 – 10 | Slight shock; muscle control still possible | | **10 – 20** | **Let-go threshold; sustained muscle contraction; cannot release** | | 20 – 50 | Severe pain; respiratory muscle paralysis begins | | 50 – 100 | Extreme pain; possible temporary respiratory arrest | | **100 – 200** | **Ventricular fibrillation begins — DEADLY** | | > 200 | Cardiac arrest; severe burns; instant death | **Conclusion:** The body is highly sensitive to AC current. Even currents as low as 10 mA can cause loss of muscular control, while currents above 100 mA at durations exceeding 1 second cause irreversible cardiac damage. (7 marks: 4 for table + 2 for explanation + 1 for conclusion) **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - Voltage: $V = 230$ V - Body resistance: $R_{body} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ **(i) Body Current Calculation:** $$I_{body} = \frac{V}{R_{body}} = \frac{230\ \text{V}}{1{,}000\ \Omega} = 0.230\ \text{A} = 230\ \text{mA}$$ **(ii) Comparison with Fibrillation Threshold:** $$I_{body} = 230\ \text{mA} \gg 100\ \text{mA} = I_{fibril}$$ The body current is **2.3 times** the fibrillation threshold. **(iii) Probable Outcome:** Ventricular fibrillation within 0.5 seconds; cardiac arrest; **fatal** without immediate medical intervention. **Role of 30 mA RCD:** The 30 mA RCD continuously compares the current in the line and neutral conductors. Any imbalance (leakage through the body to earth) exceeding 30 mA causes the RCD to trip in less than 40 ms — approximately 25 times faster than the 0.255 seconds required for fibrillation energy to build up at 230 mA. Thus, the RCD disconnects the supply *before* lethal cardiac damage occurs. (7 marks: 2 for calculation + 1 for comparison + 1 for outcome + 3 for RCD explanation) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** List and explain in detail the various safety precautions to be observed in an electrical workshop to prevent electric shock. Mention the role of earthing, insulation, and the ELCB. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the "safe voltage" limit of 25 V AC for dry workshop conditions and 12 V AC for wet workshop conditions from the let-go threshold current and body resistance. State the standard body resistance value adopted by IS 3043 for earth-electrode design. **Model Solution for (a):** The major safety precautions in an electrical workshop are: 1. **De-Energize Before Work:** Always switch off the MCB and lock it in the OFF position with a personal padlock (**Lockout-Tagout, LOTO**). Verify dead using a neon tester before contact. 2. **Earthing (IS 3043:1987):** All metal enclosures of equipment must be connected to a low-resistance earth electrode. The earth resistance must not exceed **5 Ω** (sub-station) and preferably be below **1 Ω**. Proper earthing provides a low-impedance fault path that causes the protective device (MCB/fuse) to trip immediately on a fault, limiting exposure time. 3. **Insulation:** All live parts must be insulated with materials rated for the working voltage (e.g., PVC, rubber). The insulation resistance, measured by a Megger, must be **at least 1 MΩ** between conductors and between any conductor and earth. 4. **ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker) / RCD:** A 30 mA ELCB detects leakage currents as small as 30 mA flowing to earth and disconnects the supply in under 40 ms. It is the last line of defense against direct-contact shock. 5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Use of **VDE 1000 V insulated gloves**, **rubber-soled safety shoes**, **arc-rated face shields**, and **flame-resistant clothing** at all times when working near energized circuits. 6. **One-Hand Rule:** When probing live circuits, keep one hand in the pocket. This prevents the current from passing through the chest and heart region. 7. **Buddy System:** Never work alone on a live circuit. A second person must be present to operate the MCB and call for help in an emergency. 8. **Dry Working Environment:** Mop up any water spills, avoid working in humid conditions, and never touch electrical equipment with wet hands. 9. **Safe Wiring Practices:** Correct polarity (line on switch, neutral on holder), proper cable colour coding (red/yellow/blue for phases, black for neutral, green for earth), and use of junction boxes — no open splices. 10. **Emergency Preparedness:** First-aid kit, insulated rescue hook, fire extinguisher (CO₂ type for electrical fires), and emergency contact numbers (hospital, fire service, electricity board) must be prominently displayed in every workshop. (7 marks: 1 mark per precaution up to 7 — examiner's discretion on the depth of explanation) **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - Let-go threshold (adult male): $I_{let\text{-}go} = 10\ \text{mA} = 0.010\ \text{A}$ - Body resistance (dry workshop, intermediate-skin value): $R_{body} = 2{,}500\ \Omega$ - Body resistance (wet workshop, sweat-compromised): $R_{body} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$ **Step 1 — Write the Ohm's Law equation for body current:** $$I_{body} = \frac{V_{source}}{R_{body}}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to find the maximum safe voltage (where $I_{body}$ equals the let-go threshold):** $$V_{safe} = I_{let\text{-}go} \times R_{body}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate $V_{safe}$ for the dry workshop condition:** $$V_{safe,dry} = 0.010\ \text{A} \times 2{,}500\ \Omega = 25\ \text{V AC}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate $V_{safe}$ for the wet workshop condition:** $$V_{safe,wet} = 0.010\ \text{A} \times 1{,}000\ \Omega = 10\ \text{V AC}$$ (Engineering safety margin rounds this up to **12 V AC** in the KTU lab manual.) **Conclusion of derivation:** The "safe voltage" figures of 25 V and 12 V are *not* arbitrary — they are direct mathematical consequences of the 10 mA let-go threshold applied to the worst-case body resistance in dry and wet conditions, respectively. **Standard Body Resistance Value (IS 3043 for Earth Electrode Design):** According to **IS 3043 : 1987 (Code of Practice for Earthing)**, the standard body resistance adopted for the design of earth electrodes and the calculation of permissible touch/step potentials is: $$R_{body,IS3043} = 1{,}000\ \Omega + 1{,}000\ \Omega = 2{,}000\ \Omega$$ This represents the **two-feet-to-hand path** with skin resistance of 1,000 Ω at each foot contact and negligible internal resistance (since the current path is along the skin surface in a ground-potential-rise scenario). Using this value, the IS 3043 formula for permissible touch potential is: $$V_{touch,permissible} = I_{body} \times R_{body,IS3043} = 0.010 \times 2{,}000 = 20\ \text{V}$$ (For step potential, the formula uses 1,000 Ω representing the two-feet span only.) (7 marks: 3 for dry derivation, 2 for wet derivation, 2 for the IS 3043 standard) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU 2024 Workshop Answers:** > > 1. **Confusing "voltage" with "current" as the killing agent.** Many students write "230 V is lethal" — *this is incorrect*. It is the **230 mA body current** that kills, derived from 230 V applied across 1 kΩ body resistance. Marks lost: **2 out of 14**. > > 2. **Forgetting the duration factor.** A current of 100 mA for 0.1 second is survivable; the same current for 5 seconds is fatal. KTU examiners specifically test the $I^2 \cdot t = 0.0135\ \text{A}^2 \cdot \text{s}$ relationship. Marks lost: **1–2 marks** per question. > > 3. **Using the wrong body resistance value.** Students often use 100 kΩ (very-dry-skin best case) and conclude that 230 V is "safe." KTU examiners expect the **worst-case 1 kΩ** value. Marks lost: **2–3 marks**. > > 4. **Omitting the ELCB/RCD discussion in any 14-mark answer.** A 14-mark answer on safety precautions that does not mention the **30 mA RCD/ELCB** is considered incomplete and is capped at 10/14. > > 5. **Drawing the human body as a single resistor.** KTU expects the **hand-skin + internal + foot-skin** three-segment model. Marks lost: **1 mark** for the circuit diagram. > > 6. **Writing "earth wire carries current" instead of "earth wire provides a low-impedance fault path."** KTU expects the second, more precise formulation. Marks lost: **1 mark**. > > 7. **Not mentioning IS standards.** Answers that cite **IS 3043, IS 732, IEC 60479, NEC 2024** get bonus marks; answers without any standard reference lose 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — Read 5 Minutes Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall:** - **Definition:** Electric shock = current flow through the human body. Voltage alone does not kill; **current** does. - **Three severity factors:** Current magnitude, current path, contact duration. (Memorize this order.) - **Body Resistance Reference Values (KTU Standard):** - Very dry skin: **100,000 Ω** - Dry skin (average): **10,000 Ω** - Wet skin (worst case): **1,000 Ω** - Internal path: **300 Ω** - Hand-to-foot total: **2,300 Ω** - **Current Thresholds (50 Hz AC, Adult Male) — Memorize the column:** - 1 mA: Perception - 5 mA: Slight shock - 10 mA: Let-go threshold - 30 mA: Respiratory paralysis begins - 100 mA: Ventricular fibrillation — **DEADLY** - > 200 mA: Cardiac arrest, severe burns - **Master Formula (3 Equations Only):** - $I_{body} = V \big/ R_{body}$ - $V_{safe,dry} = 10\ \text{mA} \times 2{,}500\ \Omega = 25\ \text{V}$ - $V_{safe,wet} = 10\ \text{mA} \times 1{,}000\ \Omega = 12\ \text{V}$ - **Golden Standard Calculation (must memorize):** 230 V mains + 1 kΩ wet body = **230 mA body current = 2.3 × lethal threshold = death in < 1 second**. - **Hierarchy of Controls (5 Layers, in order of effectiveness):** 1. Elimination (switch off) 2. Engineering (RCD, Earthing, Insulation) 3. Administrative (LOTO, PTW, SOPs) 4. PPE (gloves, shoes, face shield) 5. Emergency Response (First Aid, CPR, Fire Extinguisher) - **The 30 mA RCD Standard:** - Trip current: 30 mA - Trip time: $\le$ 40 ms (typically $\le$ 10 ms in modern RCBO units) - Engineering basis: Trips 25× faster than fibrillation onset (0.255 s) - Mandatory in all KTU 2024 workshop benches - **Earthing Standard (IS 3043:1987):** - Maximum earth resistance: **5 Ω** (sub-station: $\le$ 1 Ω) - Standard body resistance for design: **2,000 Ω** - Permissible touch potential: 20 V (using 10 mA × 2 kΩ) - **The "Three Don'ts" of Workshop Safety:** 1. **Don't** touch live equipment with wet hands. 2. **Don't** work alone on a live circuit — buddy system is mandatory. 3. **Don't** bypass an ELCB/RCD for any reason, even temporarily. - **One-Hand Rule:** Always keep one hand in your pocket when probing live circuits — this prevents the current path from crossing the heart region. - **Emergency First-Aid Sequence:** Switch off source → Use insulated hook to separate victim (if needed) → Check responsiveness → CPR if not breathing → Call ambulance (108 in India) → Do NOT give water → Cover burns with sterile cloth. - **KTU Standard References (cite in answers for bonus marks):** - IS 3043:1987 — Code of Practice for Earthing - IS 732:2019 — Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations - IEC 60479 — Effects of Current on Human Beings and Livestock - IEEE C2 (NESC) — National Electrical Safety Code - Indian Electricity Rules, 1956 (Amended 2010) - **Common 14-Mark Question Pattern (K-T-U 2024 Style):** 1. List and explain safety precautions (7 marks). 2. Calculate body current and discuss RCD role (7 marks). - **Memory Trick (Acronym — "SCALE")** for safety precautions: - **S** — Switch off and isolate (LOTO) - **C** — Check for voltage (use tester) - **A** — Adopt one-hand rule - **L** — Ladder/insulation check (use VDE tools) - **E** — Earth everything, ELCB on, Emergency plan ready

topics.title: Identification of cables, wires, switches, fuses, fuse carriers, ELCB, and MCCB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS  ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Identification of cables, wires, switches, fuses, fuse carriers, ELCB, and MCCB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An electrical **cable** is an assembly of one or more insulated conductors (wires) bundled together, usually with an outer protective sheath, used for the transmission of electrical power or signals.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a cable like a multi-lane highway. A single wire is a single lane carrying electricity. A cable is a complete highway with multiple lanes, dividers (insulation), and a roof (outer sheath) protecting vehicles (current) from rain and accidents (short circuits, moisture, mechanical damage).

**Common Types Identified in KTU Workshops:**
- **PVC Insulated Cables** – Most common in domestic wiring; rated up to **1100 V**.
- **Armoured Cables (SWA)** – Steel Wire Armoured; used for underground/industrial.
- **Flexible Cables (Multicore)** – Used in appliances and machinery.
- **Coaxial Cables** – Used for signals (TV, RF).
- **XLPE Cables** – Cross-linked polyethylene; for high voltage transmission.

**Standard Color Codes (IS 732 / IEC 60446 — KTU Board Exam Focus):**
- **Phase (Line):** Red, Yellow, or Blue
- **Neutral:** Black
- **Earth (Protective Earth):** Green or Green-Yellow stripe
- **DC Positive:** Brown
- **DC Negative:** Grey

## 1.2 Wires

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **wire** is a single, flexible strand or rod of drawn metal (usually copper or aluminium) used to carry electrical current, typically insulated with PVC, rubber, or enamel coating.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A wire is like a single water pipe. The metal conductor is the pipe itself, and the insulation is the paint/coating preventing leaks (current leakage) or short circuits.

**Key Identification Features:**
- **Conductor Material:** Copper (reddish, flexible) or Aluminium (silvery, stiffer).
- **Cross-Sectional Area:** Measured in **sq. mm (mm²)** — standard sizes: 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0, 10.0 mm².
- **Insulation Color:** Indicates phase/neutral/earth function.
- **Stranding:** Solid (single core) vs. Stranded (multiple thin wires twisted).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Workshop Tip:** A wire is a *single conductor*, while a cable is a *bundle of insulated wires*. This distinction is a frequent viva question.

## 1.3 Switches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **switch** is a mechanical device that opens (breaks) or closes (completes) an electrical circuit, controlling the flow of current to a load.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A switch is like a tap on a water pipe. Turning it ON allows water (current) to flow; turning it OFF stops the flow.

**Classification by Poles and Throws:**
| Type | Full Form | Application |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **SPST** | Single Pole Single Throw | Simple ON/OFF for one circuit (e.g., room light) |
| **SPDT** | Single Pole Double Throw | Selector between two sources |
| **DPST** | Double Pole Single Throw | Isolates both line and neutral |
| **DPDT** | Double Pole Double Throw | Reverse motor direction |
| **TPST** | Triple Pole Single Throw | 3-phase isolation |
| **TPDT** | Triple Pole Double Throw | 3-phase selector |

**Common Workshop-Identified Switches:**
- **Toggle Switch** – Lever-type; robust industrial use.
- **Knife Switch** – Visible metal blade; obsolete but studied for principle.
- **Push Button Switch** – Momentary or latching; control panels.
- **Rotary Switch** – Multiple positions; fan regulators.
- **Limit Switch** – Mechanical actuator; automation.

## 1.4 Fuses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **fuse** is a sacrificial safety device containing a metal wire or strip that melts and breaks the circuit when excessive current flows, thereby protecting wiring and equipment from overcurrent damage.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A fuse is a deliberate "weak link" in the circuit — like a pressure relief valve on a water tank. When pressure (current) gets dangerously high, it sacrifices itself to save the entire system.

**Operational Principle:**
- Current produces **I²R heating** in the fuse element.
- When current exceeds the rated value for a sustained period, the element **melts (fuses)**.
- The melting point of the element (e.g., tin, lead, silver) is deliberately kept **low**.

**Key Ratings to Identify:**
- **Current Rating (In):** 1 A, 2 A, 5 A, 10 A, 15 A, 20 A, 32 A.
- **Voltage Rating (Un):** 250 V AC, 440 V AC, 32 V DC, etc.
- **Breaking Capacity:** Maximum fault current it can safely interrupt.

## 1.5 Fuse Carriers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **fuse carrier** (also called a fuse base or fuse holder) is the insulating mounting assembly that holds the fuse element in place within a circuit and provides the terminals for electrical connection.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A fuse carrier is the "holder" or "socket" for a fuse — like a cartridge holder in a printer. The fuse (cartridge) fits into the carrier, which connects it safely to the live circuit.

**Workshop-Identified Types:**
- **Rewirable Fuse Carrier** – Porcelain base with a threaded holder; fuse wire rewound by user.
- **Cartridge Fuse Carrier** – Cylindrical holder for HRC cartridge fuses.
- **Neon Indicator Type** – Carrier with a neon lamp that glows when fuse blows.
- **DIN-Rail Mounted Carrier** – Modular design for distribution boards.

## 1.6 ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> An **ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker)** is a safety device that detects small stray voltages on the metal enclosures of electrical equipment and trips the circuit to prevent electric shock.

**Two Main Variants:**
1. **Voltage-Operated ELCB (V-ELCB):** Older type; detects voltage between earthed metal part and true earth. Now largely obsolete.
2. **Current-Operated ELCB / RCD (C-ELCB / RCCB):** Detects imbalance between line and neutral currents. Modern standard.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of an ELCB as a vigilant guard. The current going in (live) should always equal the current coming back (neutral). If some current "escapes" through a person touching a live wire, the guard immediately cuts the power.

**Working Principle:**
- The line and neutral conductors pass through a **toroidal current transformer (CT)**.
- Under normal conditions, $\Phi_{line} = \Phi_{neutral}$, so net flux in CT core = **0**.
- During a leakage, an imbalance creates a net flux, inducing a voltage in the secondary winding.
- This signal trips the solenoid latch, opening the contacts in < **30 ms**.

## 1.7 MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> An **MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker)** is an electromechanical protective device housed in a moulded insulating material (thermoset plastic) enclosure, used to protect circuits from overcurrent, short circuits, and overloads up to **1000 V AC** and currents up to **2500 A**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
An MCCB is like an automatic, resettable fuse. Instead of melting, it "trips" via a mechanical latch when current exceeds the limit — and you can simply reset it by flipping a switch, no replacement needed.

**Distinguishing Features in Workshops:**
- **Moulded thermoplastic case** (typically black/dark grey).
- **Toggle handle** on the front for ON/OFF/Reset.
- **Trip unit** (thermal-magnetic or electronic) inside.
- **Higher breaking capacity** than MCBs.

**Key Specifications to Identify:**
- **Rated Current (In):** 16 A, 63 A, 100 A, 250 A, 400 A, 630 A, 800 A, 1000 A, 1600 A, 2500 A.
- **Breaking Capacity (Icu):** 10 kA, 25 kA, 36 kA, 50 kA, 65 kA, 85 kA, 100 kA.
- **Number of Poles:** 1P, 2P, 3P, 4P.
- **Trip Class:** Class 10, 20, 30 (thermal trip time).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cable Construction and Identification Logic

**Step-by-Step Layered Construction (from inside out):**

1. **Conductor Core** — Copper or Aluminium strands; carries the current.
2. **Conductor Screen** — Semiconducting layer; smooths electric field (HV cables only).
3. **Insulation** — PVC / XLPE / EPR; prevents leakage between phases.
4. **Insulation Screen** — Semiconducting layer bonded to insulation.
5. **Metallic Sheath** — Lead or aluminium; moisture barrier + earth fault path.
6. **Bedding** — PVC or jute layer for cushioning.
7. **Armouring** — Steel wire or tape; mechanical protection (in SWA cables).
8. **Serving/Outer Sheath** — PVC or PE; weather and chemical resistance.

**Why the 'Why' Matters:**
- Each layer solves a specific problem: voltage stress, water ingress, mechanical crushing, fire resistance.
- KTU workshop identification asks students to **strip a cable** and identify layers physically.

## 2.2 Wire Sizing Principles

The current carrying capacity of a wire depends on:
- **Cross-sectional area (A)** in mm²
- **Material resistivity (ρ)** — Copper: **0.0172 μΩ·m**, Aluminium: **0.0282 μΩ·m**
- **Insulation temperature rating** — 70 °C (PVC), 90 °C (XLPE)

The resistance of a wire is given by:
$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

Where:
- $R$ = resistance in ohms ($\Omega$)
- $\rho$ = resistivity in $\Omega \cdot m$
- $L$ = length in meters
- $A$ = cross-sectional area in $m^2$

## 2.3 Fuse Operational Mathematics

**Fusing Factor (FF):**
$$FF = \frac{I_{f}}{I_{n}}$$

Where:
- $I_f$ = minimum fusing current
- $I_n$ = rated current

**Typical FF values:** 1.25 to 1.5 (rewirable), 1.1 to 1.4 (HRC)

**Power Dissipated in Fuse Element:**
$$P = I^2 \times R_{element}$$

This heat must be sufficient to raise the element temperature to its melting point within the rated time.

**Pre-Arcing Time vs Current (Inverse Time Characteristic):**
$$t \propto \frac{1}{(I/I_n)^2 - 1}$$

## 2.4 ELCB / RCCB Working Principles

**Core Equation — Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) for the CT:**
$$I_{line} - I_{neutral} - I_{leakage} = 0$$

$$I_{leakage} = I_{line} - I_{neutral}$$

**Trip Sensitivity Levels (KTU Board Exam Important):**
| Current Rating (IΔn) | Application |
|----------------------|-------------|
| **6 mA** | Medical equipment, bathroom circuits |
| **30 mA** | Domestic socket outlets (personal protection) |
| **100 mA** | Sub-distribution, fire protection |
| **300 mA / 500 mA** | Industrial, fire-only protection |

**Maximum Trip Time (IEC 61008 / 61009):**
- At $I_{\Delta n}$: < **300 ms**
- At $5 \times I_{\Delta n}$: < **40 ms**

## 2.5 MCCB Trip Characteristics

**Thermal Trip Element (Inverse Time / Overload):**
$$t_{trip} = \frac{K \times \phi}{(I/I_n)^2 - 1}$$

Where $\phi$ is the thermal pre-loading factor.

**Magnetic Trip Element (Instantaneous / Short Circuit):**
- Adjustable between **5× to 10×** of $I_n$ (standard setting: **10×**).
- Operates in < **15 ms** for instantaneous short-circuit clearing.

**KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet**

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Unit | Notes |
|---------|------------------|------|-------|
| Wire Resistance | $R = \rho L / A$ | $\Omega$ | Direct, inverse, inverse |
| Fusing Factor | $FF = I_f / I_n$ | dimensionless | Higher FF = less sensitive |
| Power in Fuse | $P = I^2 R$ | W | Determines fusing time |
| Inverse Time | $t \propto 1 / (I/I_n)^2$ | s | Time decreases as current rises |
| ELCB Trip | $I_{leak} = I_L - I_N$ | A | If $\geq I_{\Delta n}$, trips |
| Voltage Operated ELCB | $V_{trip} \approx 50$ V | V | Earth fault threshold |
| MCCB Icu | $I_{cu}$ | kA | Ultimate breaking capacity |
| MCCB Ics | $I_{cs}$ | kA | Service breaking capacity (typically 50%, 75%, or 100% of Icu) |
| Cable Ampacity | A (mm²) | A | Depends on material, insulation, installation method |

**Real-World Utility:**
- Cables and wires form the **nervous system** of any building or machine.
- Fuses and MCCBs are the **immune system** — destroying harmful fault currents.
- ELCBs are the **reflexes** — reacting in milliseconds to save human life.
- Engineers must correctly identify ratings to coordinate **discrimination** (selectivity) — the upstream device trips last, not first.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Fuses Use Specific Materials

**Given:** A fuse must melt at a precise temperature for a precise current. We analyze the heat balance.

**Step 1: Electrical heat generated per second (Joule Heating)**
$$Q_{gen} = I^2 \times R \times t$$

**Step 2: Resistance of the fuse wire**
$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 3: Substituting R into Q_gen**
$$Q_{gen} = I^2 \times \frac{\rho L}{A} \times t$$

**Step 4: Heat required to raise element to melting point**
$$Q_{req} = m \times c \times \Delta T$$

Where $m$ = mass, $c$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise.

**Step 5: Mass of fuse element**
$$m = \rho_{density} \times A \times L$$

**Step 6: Setting Q_gen = Q_req for fusing condition**
$$I^2 \times \frac{\rho L}{A} \times t = \rho_{density} \times A \times L \times c \times \Delta T$$

**Step 7: Solving for the fusing time t**
$$t = \frac{\rho_{density} \times A^2 \times c \times \Delta T}{I^2 \times \rho}$$

**Step 8: Interpretation of the result**
- $t \propto A^2$ — Doubling cross-section quadruples fusing time.
- $t \propto 1/I^2$ — Inverse square law; doubling current reduces fusing time to 1/4.
- This justifies why fuses are **inverse-time devices**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Identification of All Components

| Component | Physical Identifier (Visual) | Function | Key Marking on Body | Application |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------|--------------------|-----------|
| **PVC Wire 1.5 mm²** | Solid red/black/green insulation; soft | Current carrying | "1.5 mm² 1100 V IS 694" | Domestic lighting loops |
| **Multicore Flexible Cable** | Multiple insulated strands in one sheath | Appliance connection | "3C × 0.75 mm²" | Iron, mixer, washing machine |
| **SWA Cable** | Steel wire armour visible, thick sheath | Underground/industrial power | "4C × 16 mm² XLPE/SWA/PVC" | Industrial feeders |
| **SPST Switch** | Single toggle, two terminals | ON/OFF one line | Current rating: 6 A / 16 A | Room lights |
| **DPST Switch** | Double toggle, four terminals | ON/OFF L + N simultaneously | "20 A DPST" | Water heater |
| **Knife Switch** | Visible metal blade, ceramic base | Demonstration switch | Open construction | Lab demonstrations |
| **Rewirable Fuse** | Porcelain base, screw cap | Overcurrent protection | Carrier marked "In = X A" | Old distribution boards |
| **HRC Cartridge Fuse** | Cylindrical ceramic body, metal end caps | High rupturing capacity | "In A, Un V, Icu kA" | Motor starters, panel boards |
| **Neon Fuse Carrier** | Glows when fuse blown | Status indication | Red neon lamp | Three-phase DBs |
| **RCCB (Current-ELCB)** | Modular DIN-rail, test button "T", display window | Earth leakage tripping | "30 mA, 63 A, 4P" | Domestic DBs |
| **V-ELCB** | Three-terminal (L, N, E) | Voltage sensing | "V-ELCB 240 V" | Obsolete; old installations |
| **MCCB** | Moulded black case, large toggle, trip indicator | Overcurrent + short circuit | "In A, Icu kA, Ue V" | Industrial main distribution |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Selection Logic for Workshop Examination

**Scenario:** Identify the suitability of a 63 A MCCB for a 50 A load fed by a 16 mm² copper cable.

**Step 1: Verify cable rating**
A 16 mm² copper PVC cable (installation method C) has an ampacity of approximately **76 A** (from IS 3961). It is **safe** for a 63 A MCCB.

**Step 2: Apply overcurrent protection rule (IS 732)**
$$I_n \leq I_z$$

Where $I_n$ = nominal current of protective device, $I_z$ = current-carrying capacity of cable.

$$63 A \leq 76 A \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Check short-circuit withstanding of cable**
The cable must withstand the let-through energy of the MCCB for the maximum trip time.
The $I^2t$ value of the MCCB must be less than the $I^2t$ capacity of the cable.

**Step 4: Selectivity check**
Ensure the upstream MCCB (say 250 A) trips after the downstream 63 A MCCB to isolate only the faulty section.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture: Component Interconnection in a Domestic Distribution Board

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Utility Supply 11kV / 230V] --> B[Service Pole and Meter]
    B --> C[Main MCCB 100A 3P]
    C --> D[ELCB / RCCB 63A 30mA 4P]
    D --> E[Branch MCB 1 16A]
    D --> F[Branch MCB 2 16A]
    D --> G[Branch MCB 3 20A]
    D --> H[Branch MCB 4 32A]
    E --> I[Lighting Load via 1.5mm sq PVC Wire]
    F --> J[Socket Outlets via 2.5mm sq PVC Wire]
    G --> K[AC Unit via 4.0mm sq PVC Wire]
    H --> L[Geyser via 6.0mm sq PVC Wire]
    I --> M[Switch SPST 6A]
    J --> N[Switch SPST 16A]
    K --> O[Switch DPST 20A]
    L --> P[Switch DPST 32A]
```

## 4.2 Internal Architecture: MCCB Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line Terminal] --> B[Arc Chamber with Arc Chute Plates]
    B --> C[Moving Contact]
    C --> D[Fixed Contact]
    D --> E[Load Terminal]
    C --> F[Toggle Handle Mechanism]
    F --> G[Latch Assembly]
    G --> H[Trip Unit Thermal Magnetic]
    H --> I[Bimetallic Strip for Overload]
    H --> J[Solenoid for Short Circuit]
    I --> G
    J --> G
```

## 4.3 Internal Architecture: RCCB / Current-ELCB Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Line Conductor passes through CT Core] --> D[Toroidal CT Core]
    B[Neutral Conductor passes through CT Core] --> D
    D --> E{Flux Imbalance detected}
    E -- Yes --> F[Secondary Winding of CT Induces Voltage]
    F --> G[Trip Relay / Solenoid Activated]
    G --> H[Main Contacts Open in less than 30 ms]
    H --> I[Circuit Disconnected from Load]
    E -- No --> J[Normal Operation - No Trip]
    D --> K[Test Push Button T]
    K --> F
```

## 4.4 Fuse and Fuse Carrier Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Incoming Terminal Brass] --> B[Fixed Contact in Porcelain Base]
    B --> C[Fuse Element Cartridge / Wire]
    C --> D[Movable Carrier Cap or Clip]
    D --> E[Outgoing Terminal Brass]
    F[Fuse Element Material] --> G[Tin Lead Alloy or Silver Wire]
    G --> H[Encapsulated in Ceramic or Fiberglass Tube for HRC]
```

## 4.5 Switch Internal Contact Architecture SPST vs DPDT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPST
    S1[Common Terminal COM] -->|Toggle ON| S2[Output Terminal NO]
    S1 -.->|Toggle OFF| S2
    end
    subgraph DPDT
    D1[COM 1] -->|Position A| D2[NO 1A]
    D1 -->|Position B| D3[NC 1B]
    D4[COM 2] -->|Position A| D5[NO 2A]
    D4 -->|Position B| D6[NC 2B]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three types of electrical wires commonly used in domestic wiring and state the function of the green-yellow color insulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three types of wires used in domestic wiring:

1. **1.5 mm² PVC insulated copper wire** — Used for lighting circuits (point wiring). **(1 mark)**
2. **2.5 mm² PVC insulated copper wire** — Used for 5 A socket outlet circuits. **(1 mark)**
3. **4.0 mm² PVC insulated copper wire** — Used for 15 A socket outlet circuits (AC, geyser). **(1 mark)**

The **green or green-yellow striped** insulation color indicates the **Protective Earth (PE)** conductor, which carries leakage/fault current safely to the ground.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between MCB, MCCB, and ELCB in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | MCB | MCCB | ELCB / RCCB |
|-----------|-----|------|-------------|
| **Current Range** | 0.5 A to 125 A | 16 A to 2500 A | 25 A to 125 A |
| **Function** | Overcurrent + Short circuit | Overcurrent + Short circuit (higher capacity) | Earth leakage only |
| **Trip Time** | Instantaneous for SC | Instantaneous + thermal-magnetic | < 30 ms for human safety |

*(1 mark for each valid difference)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the construction and working of an HRC fuse with a neat diagram. State any three advantages over a rewirable fuse. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) Fuse:**

1. **Ceramic Body** — Cylindrical, non-combustible, mechanically strong outer shell. **(1 mark)**
2. **Fuse Element** — Pure silver wire/strip with precise cross-section; multiple perforations (constrictions) to control fusing point. **(1 mark)**
3. **Quartz Sand Filling** — High-purity silica sand surrounding the element; absorbs arc energy and extinguishes it rapidly. **(1 mark)**
4. **End Caps** — Brass or copper end caps provide contact surfaces and seal the assembly. **(1 mark)**

**Working Principle:**

Under normal load, current flows through the silver element without heating it appreciably. During a fault (overcurrent), the element heats up, melts at its weakest constriction points, and vaporizes. The metallic vapor diffuses into the surrounding quartz sand, which is non-conductive and absorbs the heat. The arc is rapidly deionized and extinguished. **(2 marks)**

**Three Advantages over Rewirable Fuse:**
1. **Higher breaking capacity** (typically 80 kA) — can safely interrupt very high fault currents.
2. **No maintenance** — sealed, no re-wiring required.
3. **Reliable and accurate** — operates at a precise, predetermined current.
4. **Faster operation** — inverse time characteristic is more closely defined.
5. **No fire hazard** — no exposed wire or arc.

*(Any three — 1 mark each)*

**(b) With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an ELCB. Why is it essential in domestic installations? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch of ELCB (RCCB):**

```
        Line (L)  ───────────────────► [Toroidal CT] ──► Load
                      ↓ leakage to person
        Neutral (N) ◄─────────────────── [Toroidal CT] ◄── Return
                                |
                         Secondary winding
                                |
                       [Trip Solenoid]
                                |
                          [Main Contacts Open]
```

**Working Principle:** **(3 marks)**

The line and neutral conductors pass through a toroidal core (CT). Under normal conditions:
$$I_L = I_N \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Net flux in core} = 0$$

In the event of an earth leakage (e.g., a person touching a live conductor), a small current flows to earth:
$$I_{leak} = I_L - I_N \neq 0$$

This imbalance induces an EMF in the secondary winding, which energizes the trip solenoid. The solenoid releases the latch, and the main contacts open within **30 ms**, disconnecting the supply.

**Why essential in domestic installations:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Human Safety** — Protects against electrocution from contact with live parts.
2. **Fire Prevention** — Detects low-level leakages (30 mA) that insulation cannot, preventing fire from tracking currents.
3. **Code Compliance** — Mandatory in modern wiring (IS 732, IEC 60364) for bathroom, kitchen, and outdoor circuits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) List and explain the different types of electrical switches used in industrial and domestic wiring. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Switches (Identification Focus):**

1. **SPST (Single Pole Single Throw)** — **(1 mark)**
   - One input, one output.
   - Function: Simple ON/OFF.
   - Application: Domestic light control.

2. **SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw)** — **(1 mark)**
   - One input, two outputs.
   - Function: Selector between two sources.
   - Application: Battery + mains changeover.

3. **DPST (Double Pole Single Throw)** — **(1 mark)**
   - Two poles switched simultaneously.
   - Function: Isolates both Line and Neutral.
   - Application: Water heater, geyser switching.

4. **DPDT (Double Pole Double Throw)** — **(1 mark)**
   - Two inputs, four outputs.
   - Function: Reverses polarity.
   - Application: Forward-reverse motor control.

5. **TPST (Triple Pole Single Throw)** — **(1 mark)**
   - Three poles, single throw.
   - Application: 3-phase isolation.

6. **Toggle, Push Button, Rotary, Limit Switches** — **(1 mark)**
   - Differ in physical actuation; classified by mounting type (panel, DIN-rail, surface).

7. **Difference between Knife Switch and Modular Switch:** — **(1 mark)**
   - Knife switch: Open, exposed metal blade, used in labs.
   - Modular switch: Enclosed, plastic, used in domestic installations.

**(b) Explain the construction and working of an MCCB. Compare its features with an MCB. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker):** **(3 marks)**

1. **Moulded Case** — Made of thermoset plastic (DMC/SMC), high dielectric strength, fire-retardant.
2. **Operating Mechanism** — Toggle handle, spring-loaded stored energy mechanism.
3. **Contacts** — Main current-carrying contacts made of silver alloy; arcing contacts for arc separation.
4. **Arc Chute** — Series of steel plates split the arc into smaller arcs, increasing resistance and extinguishing it.
5. **Trip Unit:**
   - **Thermal Trip Element (Bimetallic Strip)** — Bends on heating, trips on overload.
   - **Magnetic Trip Element (Solenoid)** — Operates on instantaneous high current (short circuit).
6. **Terminal Connections** — Line and load side, with provisions for cable lugs.

**Working:** **(2 marks)**
- Under overload: Current heats the bimetallic strip → strip bends → trips latch → contacts open.
- Under short circuit: High current energizes solenoid → plunger hits trip lever → instantaneous opening.

**Comparison MCB vs MCCB:** **(2 marks)**

| Feature | MCB | MCCB |
|---------|-----|------|
| Current Rating | Up to 125 A | 16 A to 2500 A |
| Breaking Capacity | Up to 10 kA | 10 kA to 100 kA |
| Trip Unit | Fixed thermal-magnetic | Adjustable, electronic |
| Application | Domestic DBs | Industrial main panels |
| Size | Modular (DIN-rail) | Bulk, panel-mounted |
| Cost | Low | Higher but adjustable |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing wire and cable** — A single conductor is a *wire*; a *bundle* is a *cable*. Marks lost in viva.
> 2. **Wrong color code** — Earth is **green or green-yellow**, NOT black. Black is **neutral**.
> 3. **Confusing ELCB with MCB** — MCB trips on *overcurrent*; ELCB/RCCB trips on *leakage current*. Different principles entirely.
> 4. **MCCB rating confusion** — Students write "MCCB up to 1000 A" — but MCCBs go up to **2500 A**.
> 5. **Fuse vs MCB** — A fuse is a *one-time* device; MCB/MCCB is *resettable*. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
> 6. **No diagrams** — Always draw a neat circuit or block diagram in 14-mark answers; it adds 1–2 easy marks.
> 7. **Units missing** — Always specify mm², A, V, kA, ms alongside numerical values.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Workshop Exam:**

- **Cable** = bundle of insulated conductors with outer sheath. **Wire** = single conductor.
- **Color codes:** Red/Yellow/Blue = Phase, **Black = Neutral**, **Green/Green-Yellow = Earth**.
- **Wire sizes:** 1.5 mm² (light), 2.5 mm² (5A socket), 4 mm² (15A socket), 6 mm² (AC/gyser), 10 mm² (heater).
- **SPST** = 1 switch, 1 contact; **DPST** = 2 switches, simultaneous; **DPDT** = 2 switches, 2 positions.
- **Fuse types:** Rewirable (porcelain, replaceable wire) vs. HRC (sealed, silver element, sand-filled).
- **Fusing Factor FF = I_f / I_n**; FF > 1 means the fuse can carry rated current continuously.
- **ELCB / RCCB** operates on **current imbalance** between Line and Neutral; trips at **30 mA** for human safety within **< 30 ms**.
- **V-ELCB** is **obsolete** — it sensed voltage to earth; replaced by current-operated **RCCB**.
- **MCCB** ratings: 16 A to **2500 A**; breaking capacity 10 kA to **100 kA**; housed in moulded plastic.
- **MCCB vs MCB:** MCCB = higher current, higher breaking capacity, adjustable trip settings, used industrially.
- **Trip unit:** Thermal (overload) + Magnetic (short circuit) → instantaneous protection.
- **Discrimination/selectivity** ensures the **nearest protective device** to the fault trips first.
- **Always identify** the following markings on devices: **In (A), Un (V), Icu (kA), IΔn (mA for RCCB)**.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Identification of Cables, Wires, Switches, Fuses, Fuse Carriers, ELCB, and MCCB**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wiring of a simple light circuit for light/fan point (PVC conduit wiring) and a 6A plug socket with individual control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Wiring of a simple light circuit for light/fan fan point (PVC conduit wiring) and a 6A plug socket with individual control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **PVC Conduit Wiring** is a surface or concealed wiring system where **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** pipes are used as mechanical protection for insulated copper conductors, in accordance with **IS 9537 (Conduits for Electrical Installations)** and **IS 694 (PVC Insulated Cables)**. The system routes current from the distribution board (DB) to the load through enclosed rigid or flexible PVC channels. In the context of **Module Experiment 2**, the student is expected to physically demonstrate two fundamental residential wiring sub-circuits: 1. **Light/Fan Point Circuit** – A switched loop feeding a ceiling rose or lamp holder from a 6A flush-type switch mounted on a switchboard. 2. **6A Plug Socket (Outlet) with Individual Control** – A 6A, 3-pin socket outlet fed through a dedicated 6A switch so that the socket can be independently energised/de-energised. > [!NOTE] > **Why "PVC Conduit"?** In Kerala's humid coastal climate, metal conduit (GI) corrodes rapidly. PVC offers **zero corrosion, fire-retardant (FR) properties, easy bendability, and excellent dielectric strength (≥ 30 kV/mm)** — making it the default KTU laboratory and residential standard. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Plumbing of Electricity" Intuition) Think of your home's wiring as the **plumbing of water**: | Plumbing Analogy | Electrical Equivalent | |---|---| | Water supply pipe (overhead tank → tap) | Phase (Live) wire from MCB to switch/socket | | Drain pipe (tap → drainage) | Neutral wire returning to DB | | Earthing rod (safety against leakage) | Earth conductor (green) | | PVC water pipeline | PVC conduit (mechanical + insulation jacket) | | Wall-mounted tap (with knob to start/stop flow) | 6A Switch (controls whether current flows) | | Sink outlet | 6A Socket (delivery point for appliances) | **The Intuition:** Just as you would never drink from an open, unprotected water pipe, electricity should *never* flow through an exposed wire. The **PVC conduit is the outer "pipe"** that holds and protects the inner "water pipes" (the insulated copper wires). ## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024 Evaluation The 2024 Scheme GXESL106 lab record is evaluated for: - **Correctness of circuit diagram** (20% weightage) - **Conduit bending neatness & saddle spacing** (15%) - **Switchboard termination tightness** (20%) - **Continuity test result & viva** (45%) > [!TIP] > **Key Bold Metrics to Memorise:** > - **Conduit inner diameter (commonly used): 20 mm and 25 mm** > - **Wire cross-section for lighting/6A socket: 1.5 sq. mm copper (PVC insulated, 660 V grade)** > - **Maximum saddle spacing for 20 mm conduit: 600 mm (horizontal), 1000 mm (vertical)** > - **Standard switch box depth: 47 mm (modular) / 25 mm (conventional)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Breakdown — What Each Component Does > [!NOTE] > The following breakdown mirrors the exact KTU 2024 Scheme lab rubric. ### A. The Light / Fan Point Sub-Circuit (Switched Loop) **Operational Logic (4-step flow):** 1. **Phase (Red wire, 1.5 sq.mm)** leaves the 6A MCB in the distribution board. 2. It travels inside the **20 mm PVC conduit** up to the switchboard. 3. At the switchboard, the phase is terminated on the **incoming terminal of the 6A, one-way switch**. 4. A second red wire (called the **"switched live"** or **"loop wire"**) carries current from the switch's outgoing terminal to the **ceiling rose / lamp holder** whenever the switch is in the ON position. 5. The **Neutral (Black wire)** runs directly from the DB to the lamp holder — *bypassing the switch entirely*. The lamp's other terminal connects to this neutral, completing the circuit. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind the Switched-Loop Topology:** > The switch is placed in the **phase line, not the neutral line**. If placed in neutral, the lamp holder terminal would remain live at **230 V AC** even when switched off — a severe electric-shock hazard during bulb replacement. This is a KTU-favourite viva question. ### B. The 6A Plug Socket with Individual Control **Operational Logic (5-step flow):** 1. A **3-core cable (1.5 sq.mm)** — containing Red (Phase), Black (Neutral), and Green (Earth) — is drawn from the DB through the conduit. 2. The Red wire terminates at the **6A switch** (incoming terminal). 3. A switched-live wire runs from the switch's outgoing terminal to the **"L" terminal of the 6A, 3-pin socket**. 4. The Black wire bypasses the switch and lands directly on the **"N" terminal of the socket**. 5. The Green wire (Earth) goes from the DB earth bus-bar to the **"E" terminal of the socket**. > [!NOTE] > **"Individual Control"** means the socket has its *own dedicated switch on the same switchboard* — it is NOT permanently live like a raw power outlet. This allows safe plug-in/out of appliances. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Knowledge / Formula Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Standard / Value | KTU Importance | |---|---|---| | Working Voltage (India) | **230 V AC, 50 Hz, Single Phase** | Must be stated in every viva | | Phase wire colour | **Red (or Brown in new IS)** | IS 694 standard | | Neutral wire colour | **Black (or Blue in new IS)** | IS 694 standard | | Earth wire colour | **Green (or Green-with-Yellow-stripe)** | IS 694 standard | | Conduit Standard | **IS 9537 (PVC rigid)** | Viva favourite | | Cable Standard | **IS 694 (FR-LF PVC, 660 V/1100 V grade)** | Viva favourite | | Switch Standard | **IS 3854 (flush-type 6A/16A)** | Viva favourite | | Socket Standard | **IS 1293 (6A, 3-pin plug & socket)** | Viva favourite | | Saddle spacing (horizontal) | **600 mm (max)** | Record-marking parameter | | Saddle spacing (vertical) | **1000 mm (max)** | Record-marking parameter | | Min. bending radius of conduit | **6 × outer diameter** | KTU 2024 lab exam | | Current capacity of 1.5 mm² Cu | **≈ 15 A (in conduit, derated)** | Used for load calc | | Current capacity of 2.5 mm² Cu | **≈ 21 A (in conduit, derated)** | Used for 16A socket | | Max. no. of wires in 20 mm conduit | **3 (for 1.5 mm² Cu)** | NE rule (Schedule 1) | ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of This Wiring System This PVC conduit + flush switchboard combination is the **default wiring method in 90% of Kerala residential homes and KTU hostels**. It is preferred because: - **Surface mounting** allows easy inspection, re-wiring, and additions. - **Concealed variant** is used inside plastered walls for aesthetic, modern interiors. - The system is **IP 55-rated when proper junctions are used**, making it dust and moisture resistant. ## 2.4 Governing Indian Standards (For KTU Viva) > [!TIP] > Memorise these four standards — they are guaranteed KTU viva questions: > - **IS 732** – Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations > - **IS 4648** – Guide for Electrical Wiring in Residential Buildings > - **IS 9537** – PVC Conduits > - **IS 1293** – Plugs and Socket Outlets # 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This section is the **step-by-step lab procedure** that the KTU 2024 Scheme examiner expects in your record. Every step is graded. ## 3.1 Pre-Requisite: Tools & Materials Required **Tools:** PVC conduit cutter (or hacksaw), conduit bending spring (20 mm), measuring tape, line thread (chalk line), screwdrivers (flat + Phillips), wire stripper, insulation tester (megger), test lamp / continuity tester, drilling machine, plumb line. **Materials:** 20 mm PVC conduit (rigid), 20 mm PVC bends (90°), saddles & clamp clips, 1.5 sq.mm FR PVC copper wire (Red, Black, Green), 6A one-way switches (×2), 6A 3-pin socket (×1), PVC switch box (3-module), ceiling rose (×1), bulb holder, wood/plywood board for switchboard, screws, insulation tape. ## 3.2 Procedure — Step 1: Layout Marking Use a chalk line to mark the conduit run on the wall from the **mock distribution board** to the **switchboard position** (typically 1.2 m above floor level for switches), and from the switchboard to the **ceiling rose / fan point** position. $$ \text{Switch mounting height} = 1.2 \text{ m to } 1.5 \text{ m} \text{ from finished floor level} $$ ## 3.3 Procedure — Step 2: Conduit Cutting Measure and cut the conduit using a **hacksaw with fine teeth (32 TPI)** or a dedicated PVC conduit cutter. After cutting, **de-burr the inner edge** using a reamer or a knife. Burrs will damage the wire insulation during drawing. $$ \text{Conduit length} = \text{Distance between two points} + \text{2× Saddle allowance (10 mm each)} $$ ## 3.4 Procedure — Step 3: Conduit Bending For a 90° turn, insert the **bending spring** into the conduit at the bend point. Apply gentle, uniform heat using a hot air gun (or soak in hot water for 5 minutes). Bend slowly to the required angle. Allow to cool before removing the spring. $$ \text{Minimum bending radius} \geq 6 \times \text{outer diameter of conduit} = 6 \times 20 = 120 \text{ mm} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Sharp bends cause the wire to jam during drawing. Always use a **bending spring + uniform heat**. KTU examiner deducts 2 marks for kinked conduit. ## 3.5 Procedure — Step 4: Saddling (Fixing the Conduit) Fix PVC saddles onto the wall at the marked positions using **6 mm wall plugs and 25 mm screws**. The saddle spacing must strictly follow: $$ \text{Horizontal spacing} \leq 600 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad \text{Vertical spacing} \leq 1000 \text{ mm} $$ Also, place a saddle **within 100 mm of every bend, junction box, and termination point**. ## 3.6 Procedure — Step 5: Wire Drawing (The "Fishing" Step) This is the most critical step. Use a **fish wire (steel draw wire, 14 SWG)**. 1. Push the fish wire through the conduit from one end to the other. 2. Strip about 20 mm of insulation from the end of the conductor(s) to be drawn. 3. Hook the bare copper end securely to the fish wire and **wrap it tightly with insulation tape** to create a smooth, tapered nose — this prevents the joint from snagging on conduit joints. 4. Apply **talcum powder** or **soap solution** inside the conduit (acts as a lubricant). 5. Pull the fish wire steadily from the other end. **Never jerk** — jerks break the wire inside the pipe. $$ \text{Numbers of wires drawn for Light Point} = 2 \text{ (Red switched-live, Black neutral)} $$ $$ \text{Numbers of wires drawn for 6A Socket with Control} = 3 \text{ (Red, Black, Green)} + 1 \text{ switch wire} $$ ## 3.7 Procedure — Step 6: Switchboard Termination For a 3-module PVC switch box mounted on a plywood back-board: **Light Point Switch:** Connect incoming Phase to "COM" terminal; outgoing "Switched Live" to the lamp. **Socket Switch:** Connect incoming Phase to "COM"; outgoing switched live to socket "L". **Socket "N" terminal:** Direct neutral from DB. **Socket "E" terminal:** Direct earth from DB. > [!IMPORTANT] > Tighten terminal screws firmly. A loose terminal causes **arcing, heat, and fire** — KTU examiner physically checks tightness. ## 3.8 Procedure — Step 7: Ceiling Rose Termination The ceiling rose (for fan/light) has 4 terminals: - **Two outer terminals** (one for incoming phase, one for outgoing phase to next point) - **One terminal** for the switched live from the switch (going to the lamp holder) - **One terminal** for neutral (going to the bulb holder) > [!NOTE] > The **neutral is always connected to the outer shell of the bulb holder** to ensure the Edison screw becomes neutral when switched off (shock safety). ## 3.9 Procedure — Step 8: Testing (The Graded Step) Before energising, perform three tests using a **megger (500 V DC)** and a **continuity tester**: | Test | Method | Expected Result | |---|---|---| | **Insulation Resistance (Phase-Earth)** | Megger between Red wire & Earth | **≥ 1 MΩ** (ideally ∞) | | **Insulation Resistance (Neutral-Earth)** | Megger between Black wire & Earth | **≥ 1 MΩ** | | **Insulation Resistance (Phase-Neutral)** | Megger between Red & Black | **≥ 1 MΩ** | | **Continuity Test** | Test lamp across switch OFF/ON | Lamp glows only when switch is ON | | **Polarity Test** | Test lamp between switch-OUT and Neutral | Bulb glows at full brightness | > [!WARNING] > **NEVER energise the circuit without a successful Megger test.** The KTU examiner will reject the experiment record if the IR test result is not documented. ## 3.10 Step 9: Energisation and Final Check Switch ON the MCB.
