Test: 1. **Light point:** Switch ON → bulb glows; Switch OFF → bulb extinguishes. 2. **Socket with control:** Plug in a table lamp → Switch ON → lamp glows; Switch OFF → lamp extinguishes. 3. **Earth continuity:** Using a test lamp between socket's "L" and "E" — should NOT glow under normal operation (should glow only under fault). ## 3.11 Analytical Matrix — Load Calculation Example > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a KTU-favourite **"Part A" calculation question** that may accompany this experiment in the viva. **Problem:** A 6A socket feeds a table fan of rating **60 W** and a laptop charger of **65 W**. Find the total current drawn and check whether the 1.5 sq.mm wire is safe. $$ P_{total} = 60 + 65 = 125 \text{ W} $$ $$ I = \frac{P}{V \times \cos\phi} $$ For purely resistive loads (laptop SMPS, fan motor) we take $\cos\phi \approx 0.8$ average: $$ I = \frac{125}{230 \times 0.8} = \frac{125}{184} \approx 0.68 \text{ A} $$ Since $0.68 \text{ A} \ll 6 \text{ A}$ (socket rating) $\ll 15 \text{ A}$ (1.5 mm² Cu safe current), the wiring is **safe and well within limits**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Circuit Diagram — Complete Wiring ```mermaid graph TD DB[("Distribution Board
230V AC 50Hz")] MCB["6A MCB"] SW1["6A Switch
Light/Fan"] SW2["6A Switch
Socket Control"] LAMP["Lamp Holder
100W Bulb"] SOCK["6A, 3-Pin Socket
IS 1293"] CR["Ceiling Rose
4 Terminal"] EARTH[("Earth Bus
DB")] DB -->|Red Phase| MCB MCB -->|Red Wire| SW1 MCB -->|Red Wire| SW2 SW1 -->|Switched Live
Red| CR CR -->|Switched Live| LAMP SW2 -->|Switched Live
Red| SOCK DB -->|Black Neutral| CR CR -->|Neutral| LAMP DB -->|Black Neutral| SOCK EARTH -->|Green Wire| SOCK EARTH -->|Green Wire| CR ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Supply 230V AC] --> B[Main Switch / MCB] B --> C[Distribution Board Bus-bar] C --> D[Sub-Circuit 1: Lighting] C --> E[Sub-Circuit 2: 6A Socket] D --> F[6A Switch] F --> G[Ceiling Rose / Lamp] E --> H[6A Control Switch] H --> I[6A 3-Pin Socket] C --> J[Earth Bus-bar] J --> I J --> G ``` ## 4.3 Standard Switchboard Layout (KTU Reference Diagram) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Board["PVC Switchboard (3-Module)"] S1["6A Switch 1
LIGHT"] S2["6A Switch 2
SOCKET"] SK["6A 3-Pin
Socket"] end Board --> Mount["Mounted on 12mm Plywood
1.2m above floor"] ``` ## 4.4 Conduit Routing Pattern ```mermaid graph LR P1["DB Point
(Wall A)"] -->|Vertical
Conduit| P2["Junction
1.5m Height"] P2 -->|Horizontal
Conduit| P3["Switchboard
(Wall A)"] P2 -->|Vertical
Conduit| P4["Ceiling Rose
(Ceiling)"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **"Why is the switch always connected in the Phase line and not in the Neutral line in a domestic wiring circuit?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The switch is connected in the **Phase (Live) line** to ensure that when the switch is in the OFF position, the **entire downstream circuit becomes de-energised at near-zero potential** (1 mark). If the switch were placed in the neutral line, the load terminals (e.g., bulb holder socket) would still remain at **230 V AC relative to earth** even when switched off (1 mark). This poses a **severe electric-shock hazard during maintenance, such as bulb replacement**, as the human body could provide a path to earth. Hence, IS 732 mandates switch-in-phase wiring (1 mark). ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]** **"State the Indian Standard (IS) code numbers for (a) PVC conduits, (b) 6A plugs and sockets, and (c) PVC insulated wires used in domestic wiring."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(a) PVC Conduits:** **IS 9537** (1 mark) - **(b) 6A Plugs and Sockets:** **IS 1293** (1 mark) - **(c) PVC Insulated Wires:** **IS 694** (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Do NOT skip the wire colour-coding step** in your circuit diagram — it costs 2 marks. > - **Conduit saddle spacing** must be explicitly dimensioned (600 mm horizontal / 1000 mm vertical) — missing dimensions = 1 mark deduction. > - **Megger test values must be in MΩ**, not "OK" or "Pass" — examiners expect the numerical reading. ### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Draw the **circuit diagram of a PVC conduit wiring system** consisting of one light/fan point controlled by a 6A switch and one 6A, 3-pin socket outlet controlled by an individual 6A switch, both fed from a single-phase 230 V distribution board. Show proper wire colour coding and earth connections. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List the **materials, tools, and Indian Standard specifications** required for the above wiring. Describe the **step-by-step procedure for drawing wires through the PVC conduit** and the **insulation resistance test** performed before energising the circuit. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Circuit Diagram (7 marks):** The student must draw a clear line diagram showing: - 230 V, 50 Hz single-phase supply → **Main Switch → 6A MCB** in DB (1 mark) - From MCB: **Red (Phase)** wire routed to switchboard through 20 mm PVC conduit (1 mark) - **6A Switch 1** receives Phase; its outgoing terminal connects via **switched-live red wire** to the **ceiling rose → lamp holder** (1 mark) - **Black (Neutral)** wire runs directly from DB to lamp holder (1 mark) - **6A Switch 2** receives Phase; its outgoing terminal connects to the **"L" pin of the 6A socket** (1 mark) - **Black (Neutral)** from DB to socket's **"N" pin**; **Green (Earth)** from DB earth bus to socket's **"E" pin** (1 mark) - Conduit routing shown as straight lines with **90° bends** using **bending spring**; saddles marked (1 mark) **(b) Materials, Tools, Procedure & Testing (7 marks):** **Materials (2 marks):** 20 mm PVC conduit (IS 9537), 1.5 sq.mm FR-PVC copper wire in Red/Black/Green (IS 694), 6A one-way switches (IS 3854), 6A 3-pin socket (IS 1293), PVC switch box, ceiling rose, bulb holder, saddles, insulation tape. **Tools (1 mark):** PVC cutter/hacksaw, bending spring, screwdrivers, pliers, megger (500 V DC), test lamp, measuring tape, drilling machine. **Wire Drawing Procedure (2 marks):** Insert **fish wire (14 SWG)** through the conduit (0.5 mark). Strip 20 mm of insulation from the conductor end and hook it to the fish wire (0.5 mark). Wrap with insulation tape to form a smooth, tapered nose (0.5 mark). Apply **talcum powder** as lubricant and pull steadily — never jerk (0.5 mark). **Insulation Resistance Test (2 marks):** Using a **500 V DC megger**, measure IR between **(i) Phase-Earth, (ii) Neutral-Earth, (iii) Phase-Neutral**. Each reading must be **≥ 1 MΩ** (1 mark). Test is conducted **before connecting the load** to avoid damage to the megger (1 mark). ### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, 2024 Scheme – Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **constructional details of a PVC conduit wiring system**. State **four advantages** of PVC conduit wiring over metal conduit wiring. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A residential room has **two lamp points** and **one 6A socket with individual control**, all wired using 1.5 sq.mm copper conductors in PVC conduit from a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. Calculate the **total current drawn** when all loads are ON, given: Lamp 1 = 60 W, Lamp 2 = 100 W, Socket load = 150 W. Assume $\cos\phi = 0.85$. Verify whether the 1.5 sq.mm wire (current rating ≈ 15 A) is safe. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Construction & Advantages (7 marks):** **Construction (4 marks):** The PVC conduit system consists of **rigid 20 mm / 25 mm PVC pipes** (1 mark) fixed to walls using **PVC saddles** spaced at ≤ 600 mm horizontally and ≤ 1000 mm vertically (1 mark). Bends are made using a **bending spring with gentle heating**; junctions use **PVC junction boxes** (1 mark). **1.5 sq.mm FR-PVC insulated copper wires** (IS 694) in **Red (Phase), Black (Neutral), and Green (Earth)** are drawn through the conduit using a **fish wire** (1 mark). **Four Advantages of PVC over Metal Conduit (3 marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **Corrosion-resistant:** Ideal for Kerala's humid, coastal climate; metal conduit rusts. 2. **Lightweight:** Easier to transport, cut, and install. 3. **Excellent insulation:** PVC is a dielectric; no need for separate insulation on the conduit body. 4. **Fire-retardant:** FR-grade PVC self-extinguishes, enhancing safety. 5. *Bonus point:* Lower cost and easier bendability. **(b) Load Calculation (7 marks):** Total connected load: $$ P_{total} = P_{L1} + P_{L2} + P_{socket} = 60 + 100 + 150 = 310 \text{ W} $$ Total current drawn (single-phase AC): $$ I = \frac{P_{total}}{V \times \cos\phi} $$ $$ I = \frac{310}{230 \times 0.85} = \frac{310}{195.5} $$ $$ I \approx 1.585 \text{ A} $$ Comparison with wire rating: $$ I_{drawn} = 1.585 \text{ A} \;\; \ll \;\; I_{rated} = 15 \text{ A} $$ **Safety margin:** $$ \text{Margin \%} = \frac{15 - 1.585}{15} \times 100 \approx 89.4\% $$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** Since the drawn current (1.585 A) is only ~10.5% of the safe current capacity of the 1.5 sq.mm Cu wire, the wiring is **highly safe** and well within the derated limits specified in IS 3961. **Grading split:** Formula (2 marks), substitution (2 marks), calculation (2 marks), conclusion with safety check (1 mark). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Lab Exam!** - [ ] **Always switch the Phase, never the Neutral** (IS 732 mandate; shock safety). - [ ] **Wire colours:** Red = Phase, Black = Neutral, Green = Earth (IS 694). - [ ] **Conduit size:** 20 mm standard for 1.5 sq.mm wires; **max 3 wires** per 20 mm conduit. - [ ] **Saddle spacing:** ≤ **600 mm horizontal**, ≤ **1000 mm vertical**, ≤ **100 mm from every bend/box**. - [ ] **Min. bend radius:** **6 × outer diameter** (i.e., 120 mm for 20 mm conduit). - [ ] **Wire drawing:** Use **fish wire (14 SWG) + talcum powder**; never jerk. - [ ] **Megger test:** Use **500 V DC**; readings must be **≥ 1 MΩ** between every pair of conductors. - [ ] **Socket earth:** The **"E" pin** is the longest pin in IS 1293 — it engages first during plug insertion (safety). - [ ] **Bulb holder wiring:** Neutral goes to the **outer Edison screw shell**; switched live to the **bottom contact** (shock safety during unscrewing). - [ ] **IS Codes to memorise:** IS 732, IS 9537, IS 694, IS 1293, IS 3854, IS 4648. - [ ] **Continuity test:** Switch ON → test lamp glows; Switch OFF → test lamp does NOT glow. - [ ] **Polarity test:** Phase must be at the **"L" terminal of the socket**, neutral at **"N"**, earth at **"E"**. - [ ] **Cross-section rule of thumb:** **1.5 mm² for lighting/6A**, **2.5 mm² for 16A sockets**, **4.0 mm² for AC/geyser**. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes.** > *Practise the wire-drawing step at least 3 times in lab — it is the step where the majority of KTU 2024 Scheme students lose marks due to wire insulation damage during pulling.*

topics.title: Wiring of a light/fan circuit using two-way switches (Staircase wiring)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Wiring of a light/fan circuit using two-way switches (Staircase wiring)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Staircase Wiring** is a specialized electrical wiring arrangement that allows a single lamp (or fan) to be independently controlled from **two different locations** using **Two-Way Switches** (also known as **Single-Pole Double-Throw — SPDT switches**). This configuration is universally employed in long corridors, staircases of multi-storey buildings, and large halls where a person entering from one end must be able to switch ON the light and a person leaving from the opposite end must be able to switch it OFF without traversing the entire path.

In KTU workshop terminology, the experiment aims to provide hands-on competency in identifying the **Common (C)**, **L1**, and **L2** terminals of a two-way switch, routing phase and neutral conductors, and verifying the switching logic through continuity testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Wiring of a light/fan circuit using two-way switches (Staircase wiring)"* is a **mandatory practical experiment** carrying assessment weightage in the **Lab Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and **University Lab Exam (ULE)** components. Students are evaluated on circuit drawing, connection neatness, continuity test, and viva-voce.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are climbing a staircase at night. You enter from the **ground floor** and switch ON the light. As you climb, you need the light to remain ON. Once you reach the **first floor**, you should be able to switch OFF the same light without going back down. This is precisely what staircase wiring does.

Think of a **railway signal lever**:
- The signal can be set to **ON (Red)** or **OFF (Green)** from **either end** of the platform.
- Two-way switches behave like that lever — the same bulb can be flipped ON or OFF from **any of the two switch positions**.

Alternatively, visualize a **Y-shaped water pipe with a valve at each branch**. No matter which valve you turn, the same water flow (current) to the tap (lamp) can be started or stopped.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Used:**
> - **Standard Supply Voltage (India):** $V = 230\text{ V}$, $50\text{ Hz}$ AC (single-phase)
> - **ISI Standard Wire Colours:** Phase = **Red/Brown**, Neutral = **Black/Blue**, Earth = **Green/Yellow**
> - **Two-Way Switch Rating:** Typically **6 A / 250 V AC** for domestic use
> - **Conductor Size:** $1.5\text{ mm}^2$ copper for light/fan circuits (as per IS 732)

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **SPDT Switch** | Single-Pole Double-Throw switch with 3 terminals (C, L1, L2) |
| **Common Terminal (C)** | The terminal connected to either the phase supply or to the lamp |
| **Traveler Terminals (L1, L2)** | Two interchangeable throw terminals linking the two switches |
| **Phase (Line)** | The live conductor carrying $230\text{ V}$ AC from the supply |
| **Neutral** | The return conductor completing the circuit back to the source |
| **Continuity Test** | Verification method using a tester/ohmmeter to confirm correct wiring |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Two-Way Switch

A two-way switch is essentially a **changeover switch** with one common terminal that can be electrically connected to **one of two** alternate terminals. The internal mechanism uses a moving contact that toggles between two fixed contacts.

### Switching Logic Truth Table

Consider two switches **S1** (at bottom) and **S2** (at top). Let **Position 1** mean the switch toggles towards L1, and **Position 2** mean it toggles towards L2.

| S1 Position | S2 Position | L1-L1 Linked? | L2-L2 Linked? | Lamp Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | **ON** |
| 1 | 2 | ❌ No | ❌ No | **OFF** |
| 2 | 1 | ❌ No | ❌ No | **OFF** |
| 2 | 2 | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | **ON** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The lamp is **ON** when **both switches are in the same position** (both at L1 or both at L2) and **OFF** when the switches are in **opposite positions**. This is why toggling *either* switch reverses the state of the lamp.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Circuit Logic

The wiring follows this exact sequence:

1. **Phase (L)** from the main supply enters the **Common (C) terminal of Switch S1** (bottom of staircase).
2. The **L1 terminal of S1** is connected to the **L1 terminal of S2** using one insulated wire (called the **traveler wire**).
3. The **L2 terminal of S1** is connected to the **L2 terminal of S2** using a second insulated wire (the **second traveler wire**).
4. The **Common (C) terminal of Switch S2** is connected to **one terminal of the lamp holder**.
5. The **other terminal of the lamp holder** is connected to the **Neutral (N) wire** from the supply.
6. The **Earth (E) wire** is connected to the metal body of the lamp holder (for safety).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Since staircase wiring is a practical experiment, the supporting electrical formulas are essential for the **viva-voce** and **theory paper**.

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electric Power** | $P = V \times I$ | Watts (W) | Lamp/fan load sizing |
| **Current Drawn** | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | Ampere (A) | Switch rating selection |
| **Energy Consumed** | $E = P \times t$ | kWh | Electricity bill calculation |
| **Resistance of Wire** | $R = \dfrac{\rho \times L}{A}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | Voltage drop calculation |
| **Number of Switches** | $N = n + 1$ for $n$ control points | — | Determining intermediate switches |
| **Total Conductor Length** | $L_{total} = 2L_{travel} + L_{phase} + L_{neutral}$ | metres | Wire estimation |

> Where:
> - $V$ = Supply Voltage ($230\text{ V}$)
> - $I$ = Current in Amperes
> - $\cos\phi$ = Power factor ($1.0$ for lamp, $\approx 0.85$ for fan)
> - $\rho$ = Resistivity of copper ($1.72 \times 10^{-8}\text{ }\Omega\cdot\text{m}$)
> - $L$ = Length of conductor, $A$ = Cross-sectional area

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Residential Buildings:** Staircases of apartments, basement-to-terrace access.
- **Hotels & Hospitals:** Long corridors, patient wards with multiple entry points.
- **Industrial Applications:** Assembly lines, warehouses with multiple access doors.
- **Smart Home Extension:** The classical two-way switch concept is the **electromechanical foundation** of modern **3-way smart switches** (using wireless communication modules like Zigbee/Wi-Fi) and **multi-way switching systems** with intermediate (cross/4-way) switches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beyond Two Points — Intermediate Switch:**
> For controlling a lamp from **three or more locations**, an **Intermediate (Cross) Switch** is inserted between the two-way switches. For $n$ control points, you need **2 two-way switches + $(n-2)$ intermediate switches**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Practical Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Tools, Components & Materials Required

| S.No. | Item | Specification | Quantity |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Two-Way Switch (SPDT) | 6 A, 250 V AC | 2 |
| 2 | Lamp Holder | Bakelite, BC type | 1 |
| 3 | Incandescent Lamp / LED Bulb | 230 V, 60 W / 9 W | 1 |
| 4 | PVC Insulated Copper Wire | $1.5\text{ mm}^2$, Red/Black | As required |
| 5 | Wooden/PVC Wiring Board | $30 \times 20\text{ cm}$ | 1 |
| 6 | Screwdriver, Wire Stripper, Pliers | Insulated handles | 1 set |
| 7 | Tester (Neon or Digital) | 230 V AC range | 1 |
| 8 | Multimeter (Continuity Mode) | — | 1 |
| 9 | Insulation Tape | PVC, Black | 1 roll |

## 3.2 Safety Precautions (Mandatory)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Safety First!**
> Examiners *specifically deduct marks* if safety precautions are not stated in the record. Always mention:
> 1. **Switch OFF the main supply** before making any connection.
> 2. Use **insulated tools** and wear **rubber-soled shoes**.
> 3. **Never work on live circuits** without faculty supervision.
> 4. After completion, get the circuit **verified by the instructor** before switching ON.
> 5. **Avoid loose connections** — they cause sparking, fire hazards, and incorrect switching.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Wiring Procedure

### **Step 1: Circuit Diagram & Layout Planning**
Draw the staircase wiring circuit diagram on the wiring board using a pencil/marker. Mark the positions of S1, S2, lamp holder, and the routing paths of phase, neutral, and traveler wires.

### **Step 2: Component Mounting**
Fix the two two-way switches and the lamp holder onto the wiring board at the designated positions using screws. Ensure the switches are mounted with the **ON/OFF markings** clearly visible.

### **Step 3: Phase Wire Connection**
Connect the **Phase (Red wire)** from the main supply input terminal to the **Common (C) terminal of Switch S1**. Tighten the terminal screw firmly.

### **Step 4: Traveler Wire Connections (Critical Step)**
- Take one insulated wire (say **yellow**) and connect the **L1 terminal of S1** to the **L1 terminal of S2**.
- Take a second insulated wire (say **green**) and connect the **L2 terminal of S1** to the **L2 terminal of S2**.

$$
\text{These two wires are called "traveler wires" or "strappers."}
$$

### **Step 5: Switch-to-Lamp Connection**
Connect the **Common (C) terminal of Switch S2** to **one terminal of the lamp holder** using a red wire.

### **Step 6: Neutral Connection**
Connect the **Neutral (Black wire)** from the supply directly to the **other terminal of the lamp holder**.

### **Step 7: Earth Connection (Safety)**
Connect the **Earth wire (Green/Yellow)** to the **metal casing/body of the lamp holder** for protective earthing.

### **Step 8: Continuity Test (Pre-Power Verification)**
Using a multimeter in **continuity (buzzer) mode**:

$$
\text{Test Sequence: Set switch positions and verify the following:}
$$

| S1 Position | S2 Position | Expected Multimeter Reading (Phase to Lamp Terminal) |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| L1 | L1 | **Buzzer sounds** (continuity) → Circuit complete |
| L1 | L2 | **No buzzer** (open circuit) |
| L2 | L1 | **No buzzer** (open circuit) |
| L2 | L2 | **Buzzer sounds** (continuity) → Circuit complete |

If the readings match the table, the wiring is **correct**. If not, recheck the traveler wire connections.

### **Step 9: Power-On Test (After Faculty Verification)**
- Switch ON the main supply.
- Toggle **S1** — the lamp should turn ON or OFF depending on the position of S2.
- Toggle **S2** — the lamp state should reverse.
- Verify all **four switching combinations** and record the observations in the manual.

### **Step 10: Observation Table (To Be Recorded in Lab Record)**

| Trial No. | S1 Position | S2 Position | Lamp Condition | Remarks |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 1 | UP | UP | ON | Both at L1 |
| 2 | UP | DOWN | OFF | Opposite |
| 3 | DOWN | UP | OFF | Opposite |
| 4 | DOWN | DOWN | ON | Both at L2 |

## 3.4 Analytical Verification Using Ohm's Law

For a **60 W incandescent lamp** operating at $230\text{ V}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:}\quad & P = 60 \text{ W}, \quad V = 230 \text{ V} \\
\text{Current drawn:}\quad & I = \frac{P}{V} \\
& I = \frac{60}{230} \\
& I \approx 0.261 \text{ A} \quad (\text{or } 261 \text{ mA})
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **ceiling fan** rated $75\text{ W}$ with power factor $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& I = \frac{P}{V \times \cos\phi} \\
& I = \frac{75}{230 \times 0.85} \\
& I = \frac{75}{195.5} \\
& I \approx 0.384 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Switch rating (6 A)} \gg \text{Load current (0.38 A)} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Switch is safely rated.}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Staircase Wiring Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mainsAC["AC MAINS SUPPLY 230V 50Hz"]
    phaseLine["PHASE L - Red Wire"]
    neutralLine["NEUTRAL N - Black Wire"]
    earthLine["EARTH E - Green Wire"]
    sw1["TWO WAY SWITCH S1 Common C Terminal"]
    sw1L1["S1 L1 Terminal"]
    sw1L2["S1 L2 Terminal"]
    sw2["TWO WAY SWITCH S2 Common C Terminal"]
    sw2L1["S2 L1 Terminal"]
    sw2L2["S2 L2 Terminal"]
    traveler1["TRAVELER WIRE 1 Yellow"]
    traveler2["TRAVELER WIRE 2 Green"]
    lamp["LAMP HOLDER with Bulb"]
    lampOn["LAMP GLOWS ON"]
    lampOff["LAMP REMAINS OFF"]

    mainsAC --> phaseLine
    mainsAC --> neutralLine
    mainsAC --> earthLine

    phaseLine --> sw1
    sw1 --> sw1L1
    sw1 --> sw1L2
    sw1L1 --> traveler1
    traveler1 --> sw2L1
    sw1L2 --> traveler2
    traveler2 --> sw2L2
    sw2L1 --> sw2
    sw2L2 --> sw2
    sw2 --> lamp
    neutralLine --> lamp
    earthLine --> lamp
    lamp --> lampOn
    lamp --> lampOff
```

## 4.2 Switching State Diagram — Logic Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LampOFF
    LampOFF --> LampON : Both switches at L1
    LampOFF --> LampON : Both switches at L2
    LampON --> LampOFF : Switch S1 toggled
    LampON --> LampOFF : Switch S2 toggled
    LampOFF --> [*]
    LampON --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Terminal Identification Diagram (Two-Way Switch)

```mermaid
graph TD
    swBox["TWO-WAY SWITCH FRONT VIEW"]
    termC["COMMON C - Center Terminal"]
    termL1["L1 - Top Throw Terminal"]
    termL2["L2 - Bottom Throw Terminal"]
    toggle["METAL TOGGLE LEVER"]
    onMark["ON Position Marking"]
    offMark["OFF Position Marking"]

    swBox --> termC
    swBox --> termL1
    swBox --> termL2
    swBox --> toggle
    swBox --> onMark
    swBox --> offMark
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Memory Aid for Exams:**
> In the **OFF position**, the common terminal connects to **L1**. In the **ON position**, the common terminal connects to **L2** (this convention varies by manufacturer, so always test with a multimeter). The key rule is: **The common terminal is always the "pivot" point** — it is either the input (phase) or the output (to lamp).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define staircase wiring. State any two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Staircase wiring** is a method of electrical wiring in which a single lamp or fan can be controlled from **two different locations** using two **two-way switches (SPDT switches)**. The switching logic is designed such that the load is **ON** when both switches are in the **same position** (both at L1 or both at L2) and **OFF** when the switches are in **opposite positions**.

**Applications:**
1. **Staircases** of multi-storey buildings — switch ON at ground floor, switch OFF at first floor.
2. **Long corridors** in hospitals, hostels, and hotels — control from either end.
3. **Bedroom wiring** — control the same light from the bedside and the door.
4. **Large halls** with multiple entry/exit doors.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a one-way switch and a two-way switch.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | One-Way Switch (SPST) | Two-Way Switch (SPDT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Single-Pole Single-Throw | Single-Pole Double-Throw |
| **Terminals** | 2 (Common + L) | 3 (Common + L1 + L2) |
| **Positions** | 2 (ON/OFF) | 2 (toggle between L1 and L2) |
| **Control Points** | One location only | Two locations |
| **Symbol** | Single line break with toggle | Single pole with two throws |
| **Use Case** | Simple room lighting | Staircase / corridor wiring |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**a)** Draw the neat circuit diagram of staircase wiring used to control a lamp from two different places. Label all components and wires. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(Student must draw the circuit diagram — the Mermaid block in Section 4.1 serves as the reference. Key elements to include:)*

- **Main supply** ($230\text{ V}$, $50\text{ Hz}$, single-phase AC) shown with Phase (L), Neutral (N), and Earth (E).
- **Switch S1** with terminals labelled **C, L1, L2**.
- **Switch S2** with terminals labelled **C, L1, L2**.
- **Two traveler wires** connecting L1↔L1 and L2↔L2.
- **Lamp holder** with the bulb.
- Phase connected to **C of S1**; **C of S2** connected to the lamp.
- **Neutral** connected directly to the lamp's other terminal.
- **Earth** connected to the metallic body of the lamp holder.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU Key):**
- Correct supply representation: 1 mark
- Two-way switch symbols with labels: 2 marks
- Traveler wire connections: 2 marks
- Lamp and neutral connection: 1 mark
- Neatness and labelling: 1 mark

---

**b)** Explain the working of staircase wiring with a suitable truth table. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

In staircase wiring, two **SPDT (Single-Pole Double-Throw) switches** are connected such that their **common terminals** act as the **input (Phase)** and **output (to Lamp)**. The **L1 and L2 terminals** of both switches are interconnected by **two traveler wires** (also called strappers).

**Switching Logic:**
- When the **common of S1** is connected to **L1** of S1, and the **common of S2** is also connected to **L1** of S2, a **closed circuit** is formed: Phase → S1(C) → L1 → traveler wire → S2(L1) → S2(C) → Lamp → Neutral. The lamp **glows**.
- If **either switch** is toggled, the connection shifts to the L2 path, and since the other switch is still at L1, the circuit becomes **open** — the lamp **stops glowing**.
- Toggling **the same switch again** (or the other switch) re-establishes the circuit through the L2 path.

**Truth Table:**

| Switch S1 | Switch S2 | Lamp Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| L1 | L1 | **ON** |
| L1 | L2 | **OFF** |
| L2 | L1 | **OFF** |
| L2 | L2 | **ON** |

**Conclusion:** The lamp is **ON** in **2 out of 4** possible switch combinations, and toggling **either switch** reverses the lamp's state — fulfilling the requirement of independent control from two locations.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU Key):**
- Explanation of SPDT mechanism: 2 marks
- Traveler wire concept: 2 marks
- Correct truth table with all 4 cases: 2 marks
- Conclusion and real-world relevance: 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**a)** List the tools and components required for staircase wiring. State the safety precautions to be observed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Tools Required:**
1. Insulated screwdrivers (flat-head and Phillips)
2. Wire stripper / insulation remover
3. Insulated pliers and cutting pliers
4. Neon tester / digital multimeter
5. Insulation tape (PVC, black)
6. Drilling machine (for mounting on board)

**Components Required:**
1. Two-way switches (SPDT) — 6 A, 250 V rating — **Quantity: 2**
2. Lamp holder (BC type, Bakelite) — **Quantity: 1**
3. Incandescent lamp or LED bulb (60 W / 9 W) — **Quantity: 1**
4. PVC insulated copper wire ($1.5\text{ mm}^2$, Red/Black/Green/Yellow) — as required
5. Wooden or PVC wiring board ($30 \times 20\text{ cm}$) — **Quantity: 1**
6. Wooden screws for mounting

**Safety Precautions:**
1. **Switch OFF the main supply** at the MCB/DB before beginning the wiring.
2. Use **properly insulated tools** and stand on a **dry, rubber-soled surface**.
3. Ensure **all terminal connections are tight** — loose connections cause arcing and fire.
4. **Do not energize the circuit** until it has been **verified by the faculty/instructor**.
5. Use the **correct wire colour code**: Red for Phase, Black for Neutral, Green/Yellow for Earth.
6. After completion, perform a **continuity test** using a multimeter before switching ON.
7. In case of any **spark, shock, or burning smell**, immediately switch OFF the main supply.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU Key):**
- Tools listed correctly: 2 marks
- Components with specifications: 2 marks
- Safety precautions (minimum 4): 2 marks
- Neatness and presentation: 1 mark

---

**b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to wire a staircase circuit. How will you verify the correctness of wiring? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Mounting:** Fix the two two-way switches and the lamp holder on the wiring board at suitable positions.
2. **Phase Connection:** Connect the **Phase (Red)** wire from the mains to the **Common (C) terminal of Switch S1**.
3. **Traveler Wire 1:** Connect the **L1 terminal of S1** to the **L1 terminal of S2** using a **yellow insulated wire**.
4. **Traveler Wire 2:** Connect the **L2 terminal of S1** to the **L2 terminal of S2** using a **green insulated wire**.
5. **Switch-to-Lamp:** Connect the **Common (C) terminal of S2** to **one terminal of the lamp holder** using a red wire.
6. **Neutral:** Connect the **Neutral (Black)** wire from mains to the **other terminal of the lamp holder**.
7. **Earth:** Connect the **Earth (Green/Yellow)** wire to the **metal body of the lamp holder**.

**Verification Method — Continuity Test:**

Using a **multimeter in continuity (buzzer) mode**, with the **mains supply OFF**:

- Place one probe of the multimeter on the **Phase input terminal** and the other probe on the **lamp terminal connected to S2's common**.
- Set S1 to **L1** and S2 to **L1** → **Buzzer sounds** (continuity exists).
- Set S1 to **L1** and S2 to **L2** → **No buzzer** (open circuit).
- Set S1 to **L2** and S2 to **L1** → **No buzzer** (open circuit).
- Set S1 to **L2** and S2 to **L2** → **Buzzer sounds** (continuity exists).

If the results match the expected pattern, the wiring is **verified correct**. Then, after faculty approval, the main supply is switched ON and the **functional test** is performed by physically toggling the switches.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU Key):**
- Correct procedure sequence: 3 marks
- Verification method with all 4 cases: 3 marks
- Clarity and logical flow: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mark Loss — Wrong Terminal Identification:** Students often connect Phase to **L1** instead of **Common (C)**. The common terminal is always the **center/pivot** terminal — identify it by inspection or continuity test with the toggle.
> 2. **Mark Loss — Traveler Wires Swapped:** The wires connecting L1↔L1 and L2↔L2 must be **separate**; do not use a single wire with a junction.
> 3. **Mark Loss — Missing Earth Wire:** Many students forget to draw/connect the earth wire. This costs **1–2 marks** in the university exam.
> 4. **Mark Loss — No Safety Precautions:** The **3-mark safety precaution** question is frequently omitted. Always include a separate section on safety.
> 5. **Mark Loss — Incomplete Truth Table:** You must show **all 4 switching combinations**, not just 2.
> 6. **Mark Loss — Unlabeled Diagram:** Every component, terminal, and wire colour must be labelled. An unlabelled circuit diagram fetches **zero marks** in the KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Staircase wiring** controls a single load from **two locations** using **two SPDT switches**.
- ⭐ Each two-way switch has **3 terminals**: **Common (C)**, **L1**, and **L2**.
- ⭐ The **Common terminals** are the **pivot points** — one receives Phase, the other feeds the Lamp.
- ⭐ **L1 of S1** connects to **L1 of S2**; **L2 of S1** connects to **L2 of S2** (traveler wires).
- ⭐ **Neutral** is connected **directly to the lamp** — it does NOT pass through the switches.
- ⭐ **Earth** is connected to the **metal body of the lamp holder** for safety.
- ⭐ **Lamp is ON** when both switches are in the **same position** (both L1 or both L2).
- ⭐ **Lamp is OFF** when the switches are in **opposite positions** (cross-state).
- ⭐ Toggling **either switch** **reverses** the lamp's state.
- ⭐ **Wire Colour Code:** Red = Phase, Black = Neutral, Green/Yellow = Earth.
- ⭐ **Switch Rating:** $6\text{ A}$, $250\text{ V}$ AC for domestic applications.
- ⭐ **Wire Size:** $1.5\text{ mm}^2$ copper for light/fan circuits (per IS 732).
- ⭐ For **3+ control points**, use **intermediate (cross) switches** between the two two-way switches.
- ⭐ **Always perform a continuity test** with a multimeter **before** switching ON the mains.
- ⭐ **KTU Viva-Favourite Questions:**
  - *"What happens if you interchange L1 and L2?"* → The circuit still works (since the switches are symmetric), but the ON/OFF logic relative to the toggle position reverses.
  - *"Can staircase wiring be done with regular one-way switches?"* → **No.** One-way switches have only 2 terminals and cannot create the cross-connection logic required.
  - *"What is the role of the neutral wire in staircase wiring?"* → It provides the **return path** for current back to the source, completing the circuit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wiring of a fluorescent lamp and a power plug (16A) socket with a control switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Wiring of a fluorescent lamp and a power plug (16A) socket with a control switch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluorescent Lamp Wiring**: The electrical interconnection of a fluorescent tube, its associated ballast (choke), starter, and holder assembly with the AC mains supply such that the lamp operates safely at its rated voltage and frequency. As per **IS 1913 (1982)** and **IE Rules 1956**, a fluorescent lamp installation must include proper switching arrangements and a separate 16A socket outlet controlled by an independent switch.

A **fluorescent lamp** is a low-pressure mercury-vapour gas-discharge lamp that uses fluorescence to produce visible light. It operates on the principle of gas discharge wherein mercury vapour, when excited by an electric arc, emits ultraviolet (UV) radiation which strikes the phosphor coating on the inner wall of the glass tube and converts it to **visible light**.

### 1.2 Components of the Fluorescent Lamp Assembly

| Sl. No | Component | Function | Standard Rating |
|:------:|:----------|:---------|:---------------:|
| 1 | **Fluorescent Tube** | Produces visible light via UV–phosphor conversion | 40 W, 230 V, 50 Hz |
| 2 | **Ballast (Choke)** | Limits current through the tube; provides high starting voltage | 40 W, 230 V |
| 3 | **Starter (Glow-type)** | Pre-heats electrodes and momentarily breaks circuit to induce kick voltage | 40 W class |
| 4 | **Lampholders (Bi-pin)** | Mechanical & electrical support to tube terminals | Porcelain/PC |
| 5 | **Switch (SPST/Switch-Socket combo)** | Manual ON/OFF control of the lamp | 6 A, 250 V |
| 6 | **16A Power Plug Socket** | Outlet for connecting heavy loads (heaters, ACs, etc.) | 16 A, 250 V |
| 7 | **PVC Insulated Copper Wire** | Current carrying conductor | 1.5 mm² / 2.5 mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 16 A socket?** A 16 A socket is mandatory for appliances drawing higher current such as geysers, air-conditioners, mixers, and ovens. Standard 6 A sockets are used for low-power loads like mobile chargers and table lamps.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Tap System** 💧
> - **Ballast (Choke)** = A narrow constriction in a water pipe. It limits the flow of water (current) to a safe level, preventing the pipe (lamp) from bursting.
> - **Starter** = The "ignition key" that gives a small initial push to start the flow; once the flow is established, the starter becomes redundant.
> - **Switch** = The main tap on your wall. It opens or closes the water (current) path.
> - **16 A Socket** = A large-diameter outlet for high-flow appliances like washing machines, analogous to a big-bore connection.
> - **Fluorescent Tube** = The showerhead. Water (current) flows through it to give you the "shower" (light).

### 1.4 Governing Standard Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Rated Voltage (V):** **230 V AC ± 10%**, **50 Hz** (as per Indian standard)
> - **Power Factor of Fluorescent Lamp:** **0.5 lagging** (approx.)
> - **Colour Temperature of Cool Daylight Tube:** **6500 K**
> - **Lamp Efficacy:** **60–80 lumens/watt** (vs. 12–15 lm/W for incandescent)
> - **IS Standard:** **IS 2418 (Part 1) – 1977** for tubular fluorescent lamps
> - **Plug-Socket Standard:** **IS 1293 – 2005**
> - **Wiring Standard:** **IS 732 – 1989** (Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of the Fluorescent Lamp

A fluorescent lamp does **not** light up instantly like an incandescent bulb. It requires a specific sequence of electrical events:

1. **Initial State**: The tube is filled with low-pressure mercury vapour and inert argon gas at ~3 torr. The internal resistance of the tube is initially very high (almost open-circuit).
2. **Starter Activation**: When the switch is closed, full mains voltage appears across the starter. The neon gas inside the glow-type starter ionises, causing a bimetallic strip to bend and close the contact.
3. **Pre-heating Phase**: Current flows through: **Phase → Switch → Ballast → Tube Filament 1 → Starter (closed) → Tube Filament 2 → Neutral**. This heats the tungsten-coated electrodes (filaments) to ~1000 °C, causing thermionic emission of electrons.
4. **Kick Voltage**: After ~1–2 seconds, the bimetallic strip cools and snaps open, abruptly interrupting the current through the ballast. The ballast (an inductor) opposes this sudden change, generating a high **back-EMF kick of ~600–1000 V**.
5. **Arc Striking**: This high-voltage pulse ionises the mercury vapour inside the tube, establishing an arc between the two filaments. The lamp now conducts.
6. **Starter Disengages**: Once the tube conducts, voltage across the starter drops to ~110 V (below its glow-discharge sustain voltage), so the starter remains open and is effectively disconnected.
7. **Steady-State Operation**: The ballast now acts as a current limiter, maintaining stable arc current. The arc produces UV radiation → phosphor coating emits visible light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why can't the lamp run directly on 230 V?**
> A fluorescent tube has a **negative resistance characteristic** — once the arc strikes, its resistance drops sharply. Without the ballast, the current would rise uncontrollably and destroy the tube. The ballast provides the essential **impedance to stabilise** the arc current.

### 2.2 Role of the Power Factor Capacitor

A fluorescent lamp circuit without a power-factor correction capacitor has a lagging power factor of approximately **0.4 to 0.5** due to the highly inductive ballast. This causes:
- Higher line current for the same useful power
- Increased I²R losses in cables
- Penalty from electricity board

A **shunt capacitor** is connected across the supply (in parallel) to improve the PF to ~0.85–0.95.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|:---------|:--------|:------------|:----:|
| **Power Consumed** | $P = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | Real power drawn by lamp | Watt (W) |
| **Apparent Power** | $S = V \times I$ | Total VA drawn | VA |
| **Reactive Power** | $Q = V \times I \times \sin\phi$ | Reactive component | VAR |
| **Lamp Current** | $I_{L} = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi}$ | Current through tube | Ampere (A) |
| **PF Correction Capacitor** | $C = \dfrac{P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)}{2\pi f V^2}$ | Capacitance for PF improvement | Farad (F) |
| **Energy Consumed (per day)** | $E = P \times t \times n$ | $t$ = hours/day, $n$ = lamps | Wh |
| **Cost of Energy** | $\text{Cost} = E \times \text{Rate}$ | Daily/monthly cost in ₹ | ₹ |
| **Total Load Current** | $I_{total} = I_{lamp} + I_{socket}$ | Sum of branch currents | A |
| **Voltage Drop in Cable** | $V_{drop} = \dfrac{2 \rho L I}{A}$ | For single-phase, both conductors | V |

> [!NOTE]
> $\rho$ = Resistivity of copper = **$1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$**, $L$ = length, $A$ = cross-section area.

### 2.4 Wire and Switch Rating Selection

| Load Type | Current | Switch Rating | Socket Rating | Cable Size |
|:---------:|:-------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|:----------:|
| Lamp (40 W) | ~0.35 A | 6 A | — | 1.5 mm² |
| 16 A Socket | Up to 16 A | 16 A | 16 A (3-pin) | 2.5 mm² |
| Combined | ~16.5 A | 20 A (MCB) | — | 4 mm² |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Use**: Factory bays, warehouses, parking lots (high-bay fluorescent fixtures).
- **Commercial Use**: Offices, classrooms, hospitals (energy efficient + cool operation).
- **Domestic Use**: Kitchen, study room, balcony.
- **Why this experiment matters**: Teaches proper **load segregation** (lighting circuit separate from power circuit), **switch-socket coordination**, and adherence to **IE Rules 1956** for safe domestic wiring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Wiring Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Tools, Components & Materials Required

| Sl. No | Item | Specification | Quantity |
|:------:|:-----|:--------------|:--------:|
| 1 | Fluorescent tube with holder | 40 W, 4 ft | 1 |
| 2 | Choke (ballast) | 40 W, 230 V | 1 |
| 3 | Starter + Starter holder | 40 W glow type | 1 each |
| 4 | SPST switch (6 A) | 250 V | 1 |
| 5 | 5-pin 16 A socket | 250 V, ISI marked | 1 |
| 6 | 6 A 3-pin plug | Top | 1 |
| 7 | PVC copper wire | 1.5 mm² (lamp), 2.5 mm² (socket) | As required |
| 8 | Wooden/ply board | Mounting | 1 |
| 9 | Insulation tape, screws, cable clips | — | As required |
| 10 | Tester (line tester) | Neon-type | 1 |

### 3.2 Pre-Wiring Calculation: Lamp Current

**Given:** Lamp rating = 40 W, Supply = 230 V, 50 Hz, $\cos\phi = 0.5$ (inductive, without capacitor)

Step 1: Identify the power factor.

$$
\cos\phi_1 = 0.5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60^\circ
$$

Step 2: Compute the lamp current.

$$
I_{L} = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi_1}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
I_{L} = \dfrac{40}{230 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{40}{115} = 0.3478\ \text{A}
$$

Step 3: Convert to milliamps for clarity.

$$
I_{L} \approx 348\ \text{mA}
$$

### 3.3 Pre-Wiring Calculation: Power-Factor Correction Capacitor

**Objective:** Improve PF from $\cos\phi_1 = 0.5$ to $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$.

Step 1: Find the angle of the target PF.

$$
\cos\phi_2 = 0.95 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) \approx 18.19^\circ
$$

Step 2: Compute the tangent values.

$$
\tan\phi_1 = \tan(60^\circ) = 1.732
$$

$$
\tan\phi_2 = \tan(18.19^\circ) \approx 0.3287
$$

Step 3: Apply the capacitor formula.

$$
C = \dfrac{P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)}{2\pi f V^2}
$$

Step 4: Substitute the values.

$$
C = \dfrac{40 \times (1.732 - 0.3287)}{2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 230^2}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{40 \times 1.4033}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 52900}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{56.133}{16{,}607{,}000}
$$

Step 5: Final capacitance in microfarads.

$$
C \approx 3.38 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F} = 3.38\ \mu F
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a **3.5 µF / 440 V AC** capacitor is selected (standard commercial rating).

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Wiring Procedure

**Step 1: Layout the components on the wooden board**

Mark positions of the lampholders (at tube length apart), choke, starter holder, switch, and 16 A socket. Maintain proper clearance and avoid crossing wires.

**Step 2: Mount the components**

- Fix the **choke** with screws.
- Insert the **tube holders** in the marked positions.
- Clip the **starter holder** in series path.
- Fix the **switch** and **16 A socket** at convenient heights.
- Mount the wooden board on a wall/demonstration setup.

**Step 3: Connect the Lamp Circuit (Lamp Loop)**

- Connect the **Phase (Line)** wire from the mains to **one terminal of the switch**.
- Connect the **other terminal of the switch** to **one terminal of the choke**.
- Connect the **other terminal of the choke** to **one pin of lampholder 1** (right-side holder).
- From **lampholder 1's other pin**, run a wire to **one terminal of the starter holder**.
- From the **other terminal of the starter holder**, run a wire to **one pin of lampholder 2** (left-side holder).
- Connect the **other pin of lampholder 2** to the **Neutral** wire of the mains.

**Step 4: Connect the 16 A Socket (Power Loop)**

- Connect the **Phase** to the **L (Line) terminal of the 16 A socket**.
- Connect the **Neutral** to the **N terminal of the 16 A socket**.
- Connect the **Earth wire (green/yellow)** to the **E (Earth) terminal of the 16 A socket**.

**Step 5: Connect the PFC Capacitor (Optional but recommended)**

- Connect the **3.5 µF capacitor** between the **Phase and Neutral** at the choke end, in parallel with the supply.

**Step 6: Insert the Starter and Tube**

- Insert the **starter** into its holder.
- Insert the **fluorescent tube** by rotating it 90° in the lampholders.

**Step 7: Earth Connection**

- Connect the **metallic body of the choke** and **switch/socket metal box** to the **earth wire** for safety.

**Step 8: Inspection and Testing**

- Use a **continuity tester** to verify the circuit.
- Switch ON the mains; the lamp should blink twice and then glow steadily.
- Plug a test load (e.g., 1000 W heater) into the 16 A socket to verify socket operation.

### 3.5 Circuit Schematic Summary Table

| Node | Connected From | Connected To | Wire Colour (Standard) |
|:----:|:---------------|:-------------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | Mains Phase | Switch (L) | Red |
| 2 | Switch (Lo) | Choke (In) | Red |
| 3 | Choke (Out) | Lampholder 1 (Pin A) | Red |
| 4 | Lampholder 1 (Pin B) | Starter Holder (T1) | Black |
| 5 | Starter Holder (T2) | Lampholder 2 (Pin A) | Black |
| 6 | Lampholder 2 (Pin B) | Mains Neutral | Black |
| 7 | Mains Phase | 16 A Socket (L) | Red |
| 8 | Mains Neutral | 16 A Socket (N) | Black |
| 9 | Mains Earth | 16 A Socket (E) | Green/Yellow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Wiring Colour Code (as per IS 732):**
> - **Phase / Line:** **Red** (or any colour except black, light blue, green/yellow)
> - **Neutral:** **Black** (or light blue in some modern systems)
> - **Earth:** **Green with Yellow stripe**

### 3.6 Troubleshooting Matrix

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Remedy |
|:--------|:---------------|:-------|
| Lamp does not glow | Starter faulty, no mains | Replace starter; check MCB |
| Lamp flickers continuously | Choke failing, starter short | Replace choke, starter |
| Lamp glows at ends only | Starter not breaking contact | Tap the starter; replace |
| Tube blackens at ends | Lamp nearing end-of-life | Replace tube |
| Socket dead | Loose connection at L or N | Tighten terminals |
| MCB trips on switch-on | Short in choke or socket | Check with multimeter; isolate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Fluorescent Lamp Circuit

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional interconnections** between the mains supply, switch, choke, starter, tube, and the parallel 16A socket branch.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[230V AC 50Hz Mains Supply]:::mains -->|Phase - Red Wire| B[SPST 6A Switch]
    A -->|Neutral - Black Wire| F[16A Socket - N Terminal]
    A -->|Earth - Green/Yellow| G[16A Socket - E Terminal]
    A -->|Phase - Red Wire| E[16A Socket - L Terminal]

    B --> C[Choke / Ballast 40W]
    C --> D[Tube Holder 1 - Right Filament]
    D --> H[Starter Holder with Glow Starter]
    H --> I[Tube Holder 2 - Left Filament]
    I --> J[Neutral Bus - Return to Mains]

    D <-->|Arc Discharge Path| I

    K[3.5uF PFC Capacitor 440V]:::cap -.->|Parallel across mains| A

    classDef mains fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Sequence-of-Operation Flowchart (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Mains Switched OFF]) --> S1[Switch Closed - Phase Energised]
    S1 --> S2[Starter Glows - Bimetallic Strip Bends]
    S2 --> S3[Filaments Pre-Heat ~1-2 sec]
    S3 --> S4[Starter Contacts Open - Current Breaks]
    S4 --> S5[Choke Generates Kick Voltage ~600-1000V]
    S5 --> S6{Arc Strikes in Tube?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Tube Conducts - Starter Drops Out]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Check Starter/Choke - Replace]
    S7 --> S9([Steady Glow - 40W Output])
    S8 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Load Distribution Block Diagram (Workshop Board Layout)

```mermaid
graph LR
    DB[Distribution Board - MCB 20A]:::mains --> L1[Lamp Branch - 6A MCB]
    DB --> L2[Socket Branch - 16A MCB]

    L1 --> SW1[6A Switch] --> CH[Choke 40W] --> ST[Starter+Tube]:::lamp
    L2 --> SK[16A 3-Pin Socket]

    classDef mains fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lamp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Board Layout (Top View Reference)

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [SWITCH]              [STARTER HOLDER]                  |
|    (6A)                       |                         |
|     |                    [TUBE HOLDER 1]---[TUBE 40W]---|
|     |                         |            [TUBE]        |
|  [CHOKE]                       |             |          |
|     |                    [TUBE HOLDER 2]---[TUBE 40W]---|
|     |                         |                         |
|     +--------[16A SOCKET]-----+                         |
|              (L N E)                                     |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
              WOODEN MOUNTING BOARD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** List the main components of a fluorescent lamp circuit and state the function of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A fluorescent lamp circuit consists of the following components:
1. **Fluorescent Tube (1 mark)** – A low-pressure mercury-vapour discharge tube with phosphor coating that converts UV radiation into visible light.
2. **Ballast/Choke (1 mark)** – A series inductor that limits the arc current to a safe value and provides a high voltage kick during starting.
3. **Starter (0.5 mark)** – A glow-type switch that pre-heats the tube filaments and then abruptly breaks the circuit to induce a high-voltage kick from the ballast.
4. **Lampholders and Connecting Wires (0.5 mark)** – Provide mechanical support and electrical interconnections.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Why is a ballast (choke) necessary in a fluorescent lamp circuit? Can a capacitor replace it? Justify.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- A ballast is **necessary** because fluorescent tubes have a **negative resistance characteristic** (1 mark). Once the arc is struck, the tube current would otherwise rise indefinitely, damaging the tube.
- The ballast provides the required **impedance (inductive reactance, $X_L = 2\pi f L$)** to limit the current to a safe value (1 mark).
- A **pure capacitor cannot replace the ballast** because it does not limit current in the same sustained manner and would not generate the high-voltage kick required for starting (0.5 mark). However, a **PFC capacitor** is connected in *parallel* to improve the power factor.
- A **resistance** (lamp-style ballast) could limit current but would dissipate too much heat and reduce efficiency (0.5 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Internal Choice Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a fluorescent lamp fitted with a choke and starter. State the role of the starter in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 40 W fluorescent lamp operates at 230 V, 50 Hz with a power factor of 0.5 lagging. Calculate (i) the current drawn by the lamp, (ii) the value of the capacitor required to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging, and (iii) the line current after correction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

1. **Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**
   - Mains → Switch → Choke → Lampholder 1 (filament) → Starter → Lampholder 2 (filament) → Neutral.
   - PFC capacitor connected across the supply.

2. **Working Sequence (3 marks):**
   - On switching ON, mains voltage appears across the starter. Neon gas inside ionises, closing the bimetallic contact.
   - Current flows through the choke and both tube filaments, heating them to ~1000 °C (thermionic emission).
   - After ~1 s, the bimetallic strip cools and opens, interrupting the current.
   - The choke's collapsing magnetic field generates a high back-EMF (~600–1000 V), which ionises the mercury vapour and strikes the arc.
   - The arc emits UV radiation; phosphor coating converts it to visible light.

3. **Role of the Starter (2 marks):**
   - **Pre-heats** the tube filaments to enable thermionic emission.
   - **Momentarily breaks** the circuit to cause a voltage kick from the ballast.
   - **Disengages** automatically once the tube conducts (voltage across starter drops below sustain voltage).

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Lamp current:**

$$
I_{L} = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi_1} = \dfrac{40}{230 \times 0.5} = 0.3478\ \text{A} \approx 348\ \text{mA}
$$

**(1 mark for formula, 0.5 mark for substitution, 0.5 mark for answer)**

**(ii) Capacitor value for PF correction:**

$$
\cos\phi_1 = 0.5 \Rightarrow \phi_1 = 60^\circ,\ \tan\phi_1 = 1.732
$$

$$
\cos\phi_2 = 0.95 \Rightarrow \phi_2 = 18.19^\circ,\ \tan\phi_2 = 0.3287
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)}{2\pi f V^2}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{40 \times (1.732 - 0.3287)}{2\pi \times 50 \times 230^2} = \dfrac{40 \times 1.4033}{16{,}607{,}000} \approx 3.38\ \mu F
$$

**Selected standard value: 3.5 µF, 440 V AC**

**(1.5 marks for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 0.5 mark for answer)**

**(iii) Line current after correction:**

$$
I_{new} = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi_2} = \dfrac{40}{230 \times 0.95} = 0.1830\ \text{A} \approx 183\ \text{mA}
$$

**(1 mark for formula and final answer)**

> **Total for Q.A: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks = 14 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain, with a wiring diagram, how you would wire a fluorescent lamp along with a 16 A power socket controlled by separate switches on a wooden mounting board. Mention the colour code used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between an incandescent lamp and a fluorescent lamp on the basis of (i) luminous efficacy, (ii) working principle, (iii) power factor, and (iv) life span. Also list any three safety precautions to be observed while wiring. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

1. **Components Layout (2 marks):** Wooden board, switch (6A), choke, starter with holder, two tube holders, fluorescent tube, 16A 5-pin socket, PVC copper wires, insulation tape.

2. **Wiring Procedure (3 marks):**
   - **Phase loop:** Mains Phase → Switch → Choke → Lampholder 1 → Starter → Lampholder 2 → Neutral.
   - **Socket loop:** Mains Phase → L of 16A socket; Mains Neutral → N of socket; Earth → E of socket.
   - **Earth loop:** Earth wire connected to metal body of choke, switch box, and socket earth.

3. **Colour Code (1 mark):**
   - Phase = **Red**, Neutral = **Black**, Earth = **Green/Yellow**.

4. **Key Points (1 mark):** The lamp and socket are on **separate branches** with independent control. The choke's metal body must be earthed.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

| Parameter | Incandescent Lamp | Fluorescent Lamp |
|:----------|:------------------|:-----------------|
| **Luminous efficacy** | 12–15 lm/W | 60–80 lm/W |
| **Working principle** | Heating of tungsten filament to incandescence | UV–phosphor fluorescence via gas discharge |
| **Power factor** | Unity (1.0) | 0.4–0.6 lagging (with ballast) |
| **Life span** | ~1000 hours | ~8000–10,000 hours |

**(4 marks for the comparison table — 1 mark per parameter)**

**Three Safety Precautions (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. Switch off the MCB/main switch before any wiring or maintenance.
2. Use ISI-marked wires, switches, sockets, and ensure proper insulation.
3. Earth all metallic bodies (choke, switch box, socket) and use proper cable size (2.5 mm² for 16A socket).

> **Total for Q.B: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks = 14 marks**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Confusing Choke with Capacitor (–1 mark):** A choke is an **inductor** connected in **series** with the tube. A capacitor (if used) is connected in **parallel** for power-factor correction. Examiners deduct marks for swapped connections.
> 2. **Wrong Colour Code (–0.5 mark):** Students often write "Phase = Red, Neutral = Black, Earth = Green". The correct earth colour is **Green with Yellow stripe** (not plain green).
> 3. **Skipping the role of starter (–2 marks):** Many students state "starter starts the lamp" without explaining the **pre-heat + kick** mechanism. Always describe both phases.
> 4. **MCB/Isolator confusion:** A switch and an MCB are not the same. Switch = manual control; MCB = automatic protection.
> 5. **Units missing (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always write units (V, A, W, VA, µF, lm/W) in numerical answers.
> 6. **Socket rating confusion (–1 mark):** A 16A socket uses a **larger pin configuration** (IS 1293). It must NOT be interchanged with a 6A socket. For 16A, use **2.5 mm² wire minimum**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- [x] **Fluorescent lamp** operates on the principle of **gas discharge + fluorescence** (mercury vapour + phosphor coating).
- [x] **Ballast (Choke)** is an **inductor** connected in **series** with the tube — limits current, provides starting kick.
- [x] **Starter** is a **glow-type** device that pre-heats filaments and breaks the circuit to induce kick voltage.
- [x] **Starter becomes inactive** once the tube conducts (voltage across it drops below sustain voltage).
- [x] **PFC Capacitor** (3.5 µF, 440 V AC) is connected in **parallel** to improve PF from ~0.5 to ~0.95.
- [x] **16 A Socket** uses a **3-pin (L, N, E) configuration** with larger pin diameter; requires **2.5 mm² wire**.
- [x] **Wire colour code (IS 732):** Phase = **Red**, Neutral = **Black**, Earth = **Green/Yellow**.
- [x] **Power formula:** $P = VI\cos\phi$; **Capacitor formula:** $C = \dfrac{P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)}{2\pi f V^2}$.
- [x] **Indian Standards:** IS 1913, IS 2418 (lamps); IS 1293 (plugs); IS 732 (wiring).
- [x] **Mains supply:** **230 V, 50 Hz, AC, single-phase**.
- [x] **Earth wire** must be connected to **metallic body of choke** and **metal switch/socket boxes**.
- [x] **Flickering of tube** = starter faulty or choke failing. **Tube blackens at ends** = end of life.
- [x] **Lamp efficacy** (60–80 lm/W) is **4–5×** that of incandescent (12–15 lm/W).
- [x] **Lamp life:** ~8000–10,000 hrs vs. ~1000 hrs for incandescent.
- [x] **Switch rating for lamp = 6 A; Socket = 16 A; MCB = 20 A** (combined protection).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wiring of a power distribution arrangement using a single phase MCB distribution board with ELCB, main switch, and energy meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS  ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Wiring of a power distribution arrangement using a single phase MCB distribution board with ELCB, main switch, and energy meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **single-phase power distribution arrangement using a MCB distribution board with ELCB, main switch, and energy meter** is a standardized residential/light-commercial wiring topology mandated by the **IE Rules 1956 (India)** and **IS 732:2019** (Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations), in which the incoming 230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase AC supply from the utility (KSEB / state electricity board) is sequentially routed through an **Energy Meter** (kWh meter) → **Main Switch (Isolator)** → **ELCB/RCCB** (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker) → **MCB Distribution Board** (consisting of outgoing SP MCBs) → final sub-circuits feeding sockets, lighting, and appliances.

The topology is governed in KTU by the course outcome **CO2: Demonstrate the wiring of a domestic/industrial power distribution arrangement with protective devices**, mapped to **Bloom's Level: Apply (L3)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Vocabulary Lockdown**
> - **MCB** = Miniature Circuit Breaker (overload + short-circuit protection)
> - **ELCB** = Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker (now superseded by **RCCB/RCBO** in modern practice, but the term is still retained in KTU 2024 syllabus)
> - **RCCB** = Residual Current Circuit Breaker (modern equivalent of ELCB)
> - **SPN** = Single Pole with Neutral (used in single-phase DBs)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine the electrical wiring of your house as the **water supply system of a building**:

| Water Plumbing | Electrical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Municipal water meter (measures litres) | **Energy Meter** (measures kWh) |
| Main stop-cock at the entrance | **Main Switch (Isolator)** |
| Water purifier + leak detector | **ELCB** (detects leakage to earth) |
| Multiple smaller valves for each floor/room | **MCB Distribution Board** with individual MCBs |
| Taps and showers in rooms | **Sockets, switches, and loads** |

Just as the municipal water authority first installs a meter, then a stopcock, then a leak detector, then a manifold of valves, our single-phase supply follows the **same sequential hierarchy**. The MCB is the smallest "valve" that protects a single room/load, while the ELCB is the *body-guard* that trips the *entire* house if someone accidentally touches a live wire (saving life).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard Indian domestic voltage: 230 V ± 10 %, 50 Hz, single-phase, AC**
- **Standard frequency: 50 Hz** (with **3-phase 4-wire** at **415 V** line-to-line)
- **ELCB/RCCB trip sensitivity for human protection: 30 mA** (must trip within **40 ms** at 5× IΔn per **IS 12640**)
- **ELCB for fire protection: 100 mA / 300 mA**
- **Maximum permissible earth resistance: < 1 Ω** for substation, **< 5 Ω** for domestic (per **IS 3043**)
- **Permissible voltage drop in sub-circuit: ≤ 3 %** of declared voltage (per **IS 732**)
- **Body color coding:** **Phase (RED/BROWN)**, **Neutral (BLACK/BLUE)**, **Earth (GREEN / GREEN-YELLOW striped)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this experiment is in KTU 2024 Scheme:** As a workshop course, this experiment develops the psychomotor skill of physically wiring a distribution board, the cognitive skill of selecting protective device ratings, and the affective skill of respecting electrical safety — a tripartite learning outcome aligned with **NEP 2020's emphasis on experiential learning**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-wise Deep Analysis

### A. Energy Meter (Single-Phase, Electromagnetic Induction Type)

**Why it is used:** To measure active energy (kWh) consumed by the premises for billing.
**Working Principle:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. Two electromagnets — a **current coil** (in series with the line) and a **voltage/potential coil** (in parallel across L-N) — produce two alternating fluxes. These fluxes induce **eddy currents** in a thin aluminum disc; the interaction produces a torque proportional to **V × I × cos(φ)** = power. A permanent **brake magnet** provides a retarding torque proportional to speed. At equilibrium, disc speed is **directly proportional to active power**, and the gear-train register displays cumulative **kWh**.

### B. Main Switch (Isolator / TP-IC Switch)

**Why it is used:** To provide a *single point of isolation* for the entire installation during maintenance. It is a **load-break switch** with **No Overload Protection (NOP)** — meaning it can be opened only when the load current is *near zero* or at a safe level.
**Construction:** Double-pole (DP) for single-phase, with interlocked dolly handle. Rated in **Amperes** (commonly 32 A, 63 A, 100 A).
**Why before ELCB and MCB:** Acts as the master "OFF" — first to be switched on last to be switched off, ensuring the protective devices are *energized* in a controlled manner.

### C. ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker) / Modern RCCB

**Why it is used:** To detect *residual current* (difference between line current and neutral current) flowing through unintended paths (like the human body). If I_L − I_N ≠ 0, the current is leaking somewhere — possibly through a person. ELCB disconnects the supply.
**Operating Principle:** A **current transformer (CT)** with a toroidal core encircles both Line and Neutral conductors. Under normal conditions, the *vector sum* of currents is zero → no flux in the core → no induced EMF. If a leakage of **≥ 30 mA** flows, the imbalance induces a voltage in the sensing coil, triggering a solenoid that opens the contacts within **< 40 ms**.

### D. MCB Distribution Board (SPN DB)

**Why it is used:** To distribute the protected single-phase supply to multiple sub-circuits (lighting, sockets, AC, geyser, etc.) with *individual* overload and short-circuit protection for each circuit.
**Construction:** A sheet-metal enclosure housing a **bus-bar** (for phase distribution), a **neutral link** (removable for isolation), and **DIN-rail mounted SP MCBs** (e.g., 6 A for lighting, 16 A for sockets, 20 A/25 A for AC, 32 A for geyser).
**MCB Trip Curves:** **B-curve** (3-5× In, for resistive loads), **C-curve** (5-10× In, for inductive/motor loads), **D-curve** (10-20× In, for highly inductive loads like welding transformers).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are directly testable in KTU ESE questions.** Note the avoidance of the vertical bar `|` — we use `\vert` for absolute value wherever required.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\varphi$ | Single-phase AC real power | **W (Watts)** |
| 2 | $P = V \cdot I$ | For purely resistive loads ($\cos\varphi = 1$) | **W** |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \cdot \cos\varphi}$ | Line current from power | **A** |
| 4 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy consumed | **Wh** or **kWh** (if P in kW, t in h) |
| 5 | $I_{trip}^{MCB} \approx 1.13 \times I_n$ | MCB *holds* current indefinitely | **IS 60898** |
| 6 | $I_{trip}^{MCB} \approx 1.45 \times I_n$ | MCB *trips* within 1 hour | **IS 60898** |
| 7 | $I_{\Delta n}$ (ELCB) | Earth-fault trip sensitivity: **30 mA** (life), **300 mA** (fire) | **IS 12640** |
| 8 | $R_{earth} \leq \dfrac{50}{I_{\Delta n}}$ | Earth electrode resistance | **Ω** |
| 9 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R \cdot L$ | Voltage drop across cable (R per m) | **V** |
| 10 | $R_{cable} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Cable DC resistance | **Ω**, with $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!m$ |
| 11 | $\%\ V_{drop} = \dfrac{V_{drop}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Voltage drop percentage | **%** (must be $\leq 3\%$) |
| 12 | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\varphi$ | Reactive power (3-phase) — for reference | **VAR** |
| 13 | Energy meter constant $K = \dfrac{N \times 3600}{P \times t}$ rev/kWh | Disc revolutions per kWh | **rev/kWh** |
| 14 | Disc torque $T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\varphi$ | Deflecting torque in induction meter | **N·m** |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This arrangement is the **backbone of every domestic and small commercial installation** in India. It is mandated by:
- **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations, 2010** — Safety provisions for electrical installations
- **National Building Code of India, 2016 (Part 8 Section 2)**
- **IS 732:2019** — Code of practice for electrical wiring
- **IEC 60364** — International low-voltage electrical installations standard

A professional electrician who masters this arrangement is qualified to wire houses, offices, shops, and small industrial panels. In **Kerala**, given the heavy monsoon humidity and frequent thunderstorms, the **ELCB and proper earthing** are non-negotiable safety features — this experiment thus has direct life-saving significance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: MCB Rating Selection for a Domestic Load

**Problem Statement:** A single-phase domestic load consists of the following appliances connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz supply:
- 5 LED tube lights × 20 W = 100 W
- 4 ceiling fans × 75 W = 300 W
- 3 plug-in sockets (general purpose) × 100 W = 300 W
- 1 geyser = 2000 W
- 1 air conditioner (1 ton split) = 1200 W

**Total load:** $P_{total} = 100 + 300 + 300 + 2000 + 1200 = 3900\ W$

**Step 1: Compute total current (assuming power factor 0.9 for mixed load)**

$$
I_{total} = \frac{P_{total}}{V \cdot \cos\varphi} = \frac{3900}{230 \times 0.9} \approx 18.84\ A
$$

**Step 2: Apply diversity factor ($D = 0.6$ for residential, since not all loads run simultaneously)**

$$
I_{diversity} = I_{total} \times D = 18.84 \times 0.6 \approx 11.30\ A
$$

**Step 3: Select standard MCB size (next higher standard rating from 11.30 A)**

Standard MCB ratings: 6 A, 10 A, **16 A**, 20 A, 25 A, 32 A. Hence select **20 A SP MCB** (with 20% design margin for future load addition).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always apply diversity factor. KSEB typically sanctions **2 kW to 5 kW** for domestic single-phase connections, and the MCB DB is sized accordingly.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Cable Sizing with Voltage Drop Constraint

**Problem Statement:** A sub-circuit runs **15 m** from the DB to a socket. The load is **3 kW**, supply is **230 V single-phase**, **cos φ = 1** (resistive), and the permissible voltage drop is **3%**.

**Step 1: Compute the load current**

$$
I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{3000}{230} \approx 13.04\ A
$$

**Step 2: Compute the maximum permissible voltage drop**

$$
V_{drop}^{max} = 0.03 \times 230 = 6.9\ V
$$

**Step 3: Compute the maximum loop resistance (go + return path = 2 × 15 m = 30 m)**

$$
R_{max} = \frac{V_{drop}^{max}}{I} = \frac{6.9}{13.04} \approx 0.529\ \Omega
$$

**Step 4: Compute the resistance per meter allowed**

$$
R_{per\ m}^{max} = \frac{0.529}{30} \approx 0.0176\ \Omega / m
$$

**Step 5: Solve for minimum conductor cross-sectional area**

Using $\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!m$:

$$
A_{min} = \frac{\rho_{Cu} \times 1}{R_{per\ m}^{max}} = \frac{1.72 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0176} \approx 9.77 \times 10^{-7}\ m^{2} = 0.977\ mm^{2}
$$

**Step 6: Select standard cable size (next higher) and verify current capacity**

Standard copper cable sizes: **1.5 / 2.5 / 4.0 / 6.0 mm²**. The current of 13.04 A is within the **1.5 mm² (16 A)** capacity. However, for a 3 kW socket (likely a heavy-duty geyser or AC point), the next higher standard **2.5 mm²** is recommended (current capacity 24 A). Final selection: **2.5 mm² copper, PVC insulated**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often forget the *return path* (neutral) when calculating voltage drop. The total length is always **2L**, not **L**, because current flows from source to load through phase and back through neutral.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: ELCB Trip Time and Earth Resistance

**Given:** ELCB rating $I_{\Delta n} = 30\ mA$, body contact resistance $R_{body} = 1000\ \Omega$ (wet skin), supply $V = 230\ V$.

**Step 1: Compute body current on direct contact**

$$
I_{body} = \frac{V}{R_{body}} = \frac{230}{1000} = 0.230\ A = 230\ mA
$$

**Step 2: Compare with let-go threshold**

Human let-go threshold is approximately **10–20 mA** (50 Hz AC). At 230 mA, **ventricular fibrillation** will occur within **< 0.2 s**. The ELCB *must* trip faster.

**Step 3: Verify ELCB trip characteristic**

Per **IS 12640**, at 5× IΔn (= 150 mA), the RCCB must trip within **40 ms**. Since 230 mA > 150 mA, trip occurs in **< 40 ms** — well within the safety window. **LIFE SAVED.** ✅

**Step 4: Compute required earth resistance (Rule of 50 V safe touch voltage)**

$$
R_{earth} \leq \frac{V_{safe}}{I_{\Delta n}} = \frac{50}{0.030} \approx 1666\ \Omega
$$

In practice, **R_earth ≤ 5 Ω** is enforced by IS 3043, which is far safer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Component Comparison for KTU Board Exam

| Parameter | MCB | ELCB / RCCB | Main Switch (Isolator) | Energy Meter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary function** | Overload + short-circuit protection | Earth leakage (residual current) protection | Master ON/OFF isolation | Energy (kWh) measurement |
| **Trips on** | $\geq 1.45 \times I_n$ or short circuit | $I_{\Delta n}$ imbalance ($\geq 30$ mA) | Manual operation only | Never trips (continuous measurement) |
| **Sensing element** | Bimetallic strip + solenoid | Toroidal CT | Mechanical dolly | Eddy current disc |
| **Standard** | IS 60898 | IS 12640 / IEC 61008 | IS 13947 | IS 722 |
| **Typical rating (domestic)** | 6 A / 16 A / 20 A / 32 A | 63 A, 30 mA | 32 A / 63 A DP | 5–20 A, class 1.0 |
| **Reset after trip** | Manual (or auto on MCB) | Manual | Manual (lever) | N/A (continuously rotating) |
| **Position in sequence** | Downstream of ELCB | Downstream of Main Switch | After Energy Meter | First (utility-side) |
| **Replaced modernly by** | — | RCCB / RCBO | — | Smart / prepaid meter |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Wiring Procedure (Workshop Sequence)

1. **Step 1 — Mount the components** on the wooden/PVC board in the order: Energy Meter → Main Switch → ELCB → MCB DB (left to right, top to bottom).
2. **Step 2 — Identify the incoming supply** (Phase-Red, Neutral-Black from KSEB).
3. **Step 3 — Wire the Energy Meter input** (P-in, N-in) from the supply.
4. **Step 4 — Take output from Energy Meter** (P-out, N-out) to the Main Switch input terminals.
5. **Step 5 — From Main Switch output** connect Phase to the ELCB Line-in and Neutral to the ELCB Neutral-in.
6. **Step 6 — From ELCB output** (L-out, N-out), Phase feeds the **bus-bar of the MCB DB**, Neutral feeds the **Neutral Link**.
7. **Step 7 — Connect individual MCBs** to the bus-bar (input side) and to the sub-circuits (output side).
8. **Step 8 — Run separate sub-circuits** to lighting (6 A MCB), sockets (16 A MCB), AC (20–25 A MCB), geyser (32 A MCB).
9. **Step 9 — Earth wire (green)** is taken from the ELCB earth terminal to the earth bus and to every sub-circuit's earth pin.
10. **Step 10 — Test the wiring** with a continuity tester and **megger** (insulation resistance ≥ 1 MΩ) before energizing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Block Diagram — Sequential Hierarchy

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **power flow from KSEB utility to final loads** through the protective cascade. The node IDs are alphanumeric (per V10 safeguard) and labels contain only plain uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[KTU1_KSEB_INCOMING_230V_50HZ] --> A2[KTU2_ENERGY_METER_KWH_REG]
    A2 --> A3[KTU3_MAIN_SWITCH_DP_63A]
    A3 --> A4[KTU4_ELCB_RCCB_30MA]
    A4 --> A5[KTU5_MCB_DISTRIBUTION_BOARD_SPN]
    A5 --> A6A[KTU6A_MCB6A_LIGHTING]
    A5 --> A6B[KTU6B_MCB16A_SOCKETS]
    A5 --> A6C[KTU6C_MCB20A_AC]
    A5 --> A6D[KTU6D_MCB32A_GEYSER]
    A6A --> A7A[KTU7A_LED_FANS_TUBELIGHTS]
    A6B --> A7B[KTU7B_PLUG_SOCKETS_GENERAL]
    A6C --> A7C[KTU7C_SPLIT_AC_1TON]
    A6D --> A7D[KTU7D_STORAGE_GEYSER_2KW]
    
    A1 -.PHASE_RED.-> A2
    A1 -.NEUTRAL_BLACK.-> A2
    A2 -.NEUTRAL_LINK.-> A5
    A4 -.EARTH_GREEN.-> A5
    A5 -.EARTH_BUS.-> A7A
    A5 -.EARTH_BUS.-> A7B
    A5 -.EARTH_BUS.-> A7C
    A5 -.EARTH_BUS.-> A7D
```

## 4.2 Detailed Wiring Architecture — Terminal-Level Block View
