This Mermaid diagram maps the **terminal-to-terminal connections** at each component, showing where Phase (P), Neutral (N), and Earth (E) physically terminate.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KTUSRC[KTU_SRC_INCOMING_SUPPLY]
        K1[KTU1A_PHASE_L_RED]
        K2[KTU1B_NEUTRAL_N_BLUE]
    end
    
    subgraph KTUMTR[KTU_MTR_ENERGY_METER]
        M1[KTU2A_TERMINAL_1_PHASE_IN]
        M2[KTU2B_TERMINAL_3_NEUTRAL_IN]
        M3[KTU2C_TERMINAL_2_PHASE_OUT]
        M4[KTU2D_TERMINAL_4_NEUTRAL_OUT]
    end
    
    subgraph KTUSW[KTU_SW_MAIN_SWITCH_DP]
        S1[KTU3A_LINE_IN_TOP]
        S2[KTU3B_NEUTRAL_IN_TOP]
        S3[KTU3C_LINE_OUT_BOTTOM]
        S4[KTU3D_NEUTRAL_OUT_BOTTOM]
    end
    
    subgraph KTURL[KTU_RL_ELCB_RCCB_30MA]
        R1[KTU4A_L_IN]
        R2[KTU4B_N_IN]
        R3[KTU4C_L_OUT]
        R4[KTU4D_N_OUT]
        R5[KTU4E_EARTH_TERMINAL]
    end
    
    subgraph KTUDB[KTU_DB_MCB_DISTRIBUTION_BOARD]
        D1[KTU5A_PHASE_BUSBAR]
        D2[KTU5B_NEUTRAL_LINK]
        D3[KTU5C_EARTH_BUS]
        D4[KTU5D_MCB_6A]
        D5[KTU5E_MCB_16A]
        D6[KTU5F_MCB_25A]
    end
    
    subgraph KTULO[KTU_LO_FINAL_LOADS]
        L1[KTU6A_LIGHTS_100W]
        L2[KTU6B_SOCKETS_300W]
        L3[KTU6C_AC_1200W]
    end
    
    K1 --> M1
    K2 --> M2
    M3 --> S1
    M4 --> S2
    S3 --> R1
    S4 --> R2
    R3 --> D1
    R4 --> D2
    R5 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D1 --> D5
    D1 --> D6
    D4 --> L1
    D5 --> L2
    D6 --> L3
    D2 --> L1
    D2 --> L2
    D2 --> L3
    D3 --> L1
    D3 --> L2
    D3 --> L3
```

## 4.3 Single-Line Schematic Representation (Text-Based, ASCII)

For KTU record-submission, the following single-line diagram is conventionally drawn:

```
   KSEB 230V 50Hz
      |
      |
   [ KWH METER ]
      |
   [ MAIN SWITCH DP ]  <-- Master ON/OFF
      |
   [ ELCB / RCCB 30mA ] <-- Earth leakage protection
      |
   [ SPN DISTRIBUTION BOARD ]
      |       |        |         |
   [6A MCB] [16A MCB] [20A MCB] [32A MCB]
      |       |        |         |
   LIGHTING  SOCKET    AC       GEYSER
   CIRCUIT   CIRCUIT   POINT    POINT
      |       |        |         |
   ===================================
   (Earth bus - green - bonded to all earth pins)
   ===================================
```

## 4.4 Functionality Mapping Table (Engineering Justification)

| Block | Engineering Function | Failure Without It |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Meter** | Billing + monitoring of kWh consumption | Cannot bill consumer; no energy audit |
| **Main Switch** | Single-point isolation for maintenance | Electrician cannot safely work; risk of arc-flash |
| **ELCB/RCCB** | Life protection against electric shock | Electrocution / fatality on insulation failure |
| **MCB DB** | Selective protection of sub-circuits | One fault trips entire house; no discrimination |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define MCB. State two advantages of an MCB over a conventional rewireable fuse.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** is an automatically operated electrical switch designed to protect low-voltage electrical circuits from damage caused by overcurrent due to overload or short circuit. It uses a **bimetallic strip** for delayed overload tripping and a **solenoid** for instantaneous short-circuit tripping.

**Two advantages over a rewireable fuse:**
1. **Resettable** — The MCB can be reset by a simple dolly operation after the fault is cleared; no replacement of a fuse wire is required. (1½ marks)
2. **Faster, more accurate tripping** — MCB trips within **< 100 ms** during short circuits and follows a precise **B/C/D trip curve** per IS 60898, whereas fuses have variable characteristics depending on operator skill in setting the wire. (1½ marks)

*(Bonus point for KTU: MCB has a defined breaking capacity (e.g., 10 kA), whereas fuses have inconsistent breaking capacity.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the function of an ELCB? Why is 30 mA considered the standard sensitivity for domestic ELCBs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Function of ELCB:** An Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker (ELCB) — modernly called **RCCB (Residual Current Circuit Breaker)** — detects any *imbalance* between the line current and the neutral current. This imbalance is the *residual current* flowing to earth, possibly through a human body. When the residual current exceeds the rated sensitivity $I_{\Delta n}$, the ELCB disconnects the supply in **< 40 ms**, preventing fatal electrocution. (1½ marks)

**Why 30 mA:** Human physiological studies show that the *let-go threshold* for AC 50 Hz is **10–20 mA**, and **ventricular fibrillation** (often fatal) begins at currents above **30 mA** if sustained for more than **0.5 seconds**. A 30 mA ELCB trips in less than 40 ms, ensuring the duration × current product stays below the dangerous threshold of the **IEC 60479** body-current vs. time curve. Hence **30 mA is the universally accepted sensitivity for personal protection in domestic installations**. (1½ marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Never confuse ELCB and MCB functions.** ELCB detects *earth leakage* (mA range), MCB detects *overload/short-circuit* (A range). Mixing these up is the #1 reason students lose 4–6 marks in KTU valuation.
> - **Always show the calculation step** for MCB and cable selection. Skipping the numerical work (e.g., jumping to "use 2.5 mm²" without showing the voltage-drop calculation) leads to **2-mark deduction per KTU answer-key penalty**.
> - **Draw the diagram FIRST**, then explain. A neat labelled single-line diagram carries **2–3 marks** independently. Examiners reward diagrams even if the written explanation is weak.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** With a neat single-line diagram, explain the wiring of a single-phase power distribution arrangement using MCB distribution board with ELCB, main switch, and energy meter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a single-phase induction-type energy meter with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Single-line diagram (3 marks):**
The diagram is drawn as shown in Section 4.3 (ASCII single-line schematic) with clear labels:
- KSEB incoming → Energy Meter → Main Switch (DP) → ELCB (30 mA) → MCB DB → 4 sub-circuits (6A, 16A, 20A, 32A MCBs) → Loads (Lights, Sockets, AC, Geyser). Earth bus is shown running parallel and connected to all earth pins.

**Step 2 — Sequential description (4 marks):**
The wiring sequence is:
1. The 230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase AC incoming supply from the utility is first routed through the **single-phase electromagnetic induction-type energy meter** for kWh measurement. The phase (red) and neutral (blue) wires terminate at the input terminals of the meter. (1 mark)
2. The output of the energy meter is connected to the **DP main switch / isolator**, which is the master ON/OFF for the entire installation. During maintenance, this switch is opened first to de-energize the entire premises. (1 mark)
3. From the main switch output, phase and neutral are fed to the **ELCB / RCCB (30 mA sensitivity)**. The ELCB is also connected to the **earth electrode** through the earth bus. Any residual current $\geq 30$ mA flowing through earth (e.g., due to insulation failure or human contact) trips the ELCB within 40 ms, saving life. (1 mark)
4. The ELCB output feeds the **MCB Distribution Board**. The phase output is connected to a **phase bus-bar**, and the neutral to a **removable neutral link**. Earth is connected to a separate **earth bus-bar**. (1 mark)
5. Individual **SP MCBs** (6 A for lighting, 16 A for general sockets, 20–25 A for AC, 32 A for geyser) are mounted on the DIN rail and connected to the bus-bar on the input side. Their outputs feed the respective sub-circuits. Each MCB protects its sub-circuit against overload and short circuit independently. (1 mark)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A single-phase induction-type energy meter consists of:
- **Current coil (CC):** A few turns of thick wire wound on a laminated iron core, connected *in series* with the line, producing a flux $\Phi_1 \propto I$. (1 mark)
- **Voltage coil (VC):** Many turns of thin wire wound on a laminated iron core, connected *in parallel* across L–N, producing a flux $\Phi_2 \propto V$. (1 mark)
- **Aluminum disc:** Mounted on a spindle, free to rotate between the two electromagnets. Eddy currents are induced in it by the alternating fluxes. (½ mark)
- **Permanent brake magnet:** A small permanent magnet placed near the disc; provides a retarding torque proportional to disc speed. (½ mark)
- **Register / counter:** A gear-train connected to the spindle that counts the number of revolutions and displays cumulative kWh on a cyclometer dial. (½ mark)

**Working (3 marks):**
The two fluxes $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ are alternating and displaced in space (typically 90° due to the shaded-pole arrangement in the voltage coil). The interaction of these fluxes with the eddy currents in the aluminum disc produces a **deflecting torque**:

$$
T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\varphi = P\ (instantaneous\ active\ power)
$$

The permanent brake magnet provides a **braking torque**:

$$
T_b \propto N\ (disc\ speed\ in\ rev/s)
$$

At equilibrium, $T_d = T_b$, so the **disc speed is directly proportional to the active power**. The number of revolutions in time $t$ is therefore proportional to **energy consumed** ($P \times t$), which the register displays in **kWh**. (1 mark)

> **KTU Specific:** The energy meter is always supplied and sealed by the **utility (KSEB)**; the consumer is not permitted to open or break its seal. (½ mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** List the safety precautions to be observed while wiring a power distribution board. Explain the procedure for selecting the proper MCB rating for a given load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare MCB and ELCB in terms of function, trip mechanism, and application. A household has a total connected load of 4.5 kW, supply 230 V single-phase, and the consumer wants a power factor of 0.9 lagging. Calculate the rating of the main MCB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Safety Precautions (4 marks):**

1. **De-energize before working** — Always switch off the main switch and, if possible, the KSEB supply before starting any wiring. Use a *lock-out tag-out (LOTO)* procedure. (½ mark)
2. **Use proper PPE** — Insulated gloves, insulated tools, safety shoes, and protective eyewear. Never work with wet hands. (½ mark)
3. **Maintain proper color coding** — Phase: Red/Brown, Neutral: Black/Blue, Earth: Green/Green-Yellow. Mixing these is a major hazard. (½ mark)
4. **Use correct cable size** — As per the current rating (per IS 3961) and voltage drop (≤ 3%). Do not undersize cables. (½ mark)
5. **Earth everything** — Connect the earth bus to the earth electrode (earth pit) with R_earth ≤ 5 Ω. Every metallic body of appliance must be earthed. (½ mark)
6. **Tighten all terminals** properly; loose connections cause heating, fire, and voltage drop. Use a *torque wrench* for bus-bar joints. (½ mark)
7. **Insulation test** — Use a **500 V megger** to measure insulation resistance between conductors and earth. The value must be **≥ 1 MΩ**. (½ mark)
8. **Continuity test** for the earth wire — the earth continuity must be verified end-to-end with low resistance (typically < 0.5 Ω). (½ mark)

**MCB Rating Selection Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Compute total connected load** $P_{total}$ in watts. (½ mark)
2. **Compute total current** $I = P_{total} / (V \cos\varphi)$. (½ mark)
3. **Apply diversity factor** ($D = 0.5–0.7$ for domestic, $0.7–0.8$ for commercial) → $I_{design} = I \times D$. (½ mark)
4. **Select standard MCB rating** = next higher standard rating from $I_{design}$. (½ mark)
5. **Verify breaking capacity** of MCB ≥ prospective short-circuit current at that point (typically 6 kA or 10 kA). (½ mark)
6. **Select trip curve** — B-curve for lighting, C-curve for motors/socket, D-curve for heavy inductive loads. (½ mark)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Feature | MCB | ELCB / RCCB |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Overload + short-circuit protection | Earth-leakage / residual-current protection |
| Detects | Current > rated amperes | Imbalance $I_L - I_N \geq I_{\Delta n}$ |
| Trip range | Amperes (A) | Milliamperes (mA) |
| Trip time | B-curve: <100 ms; D-curve: <10 ms | < 40 ms at 5× IΔn |
| Application | Per sub-circuit | Common, for whole installation |
| Standard | IS 60898 | IS 12640 / IEC 61008 |

**Numerical (4 marks):**

**Given:** $P = 4.5\ kW = 4500\ W$, $V = 230\ V$, $\cos\varphi = 0.9$.

**Step 1: Compute line current**

$$
I = \frac{P}{V \cdot \cos\varphi} = \frac{4500}{230 \times 0.9} = \frac{4500}{207} \approx 21.74\ A
$$

**Step 2: Apply a 20% safety margin (for future load addition)**

$$
I_{design} = 1.2 \times 21.74 \approx 26.09\ A
$$

**Step 3: Select next higher standard MCB rating**

Standard ratings: 6, 10, 16, 20, 25, **32 A** → Select **32 A SP MCB (C-curve)**.

**Step 4: Verify ELCB upstream**

ELCB / RCCB should be rated **40 A or 63 A, 30 mA sensitivity** (per IS 12640) so that it does not nuisance-trip on full load but trips on earth leakage.

**Final answer:** **32 A, SP, C-curve MCB** at the main input. (½ mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section for the night-before exam!**

- [ ] **Sequence is sacred:** Energy Meter → Main Switch → ELCB → MCB DB → Loads. Never rearrange.
- [ ] **MCB trips on overload/short circuit** (Amps range); **ELCB trips on earth leakage** (mA range).
- [ ] **30 mA ELCB** for personal protection; **300 mA ELCB** for fire protection.
- [ ] **Standard voltage in India: 230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase.** Three-phase is 415 V.
- [ ] **Color code:** Phase-Red/Brown, Neutral-Black/Blue, Earth-Green (or Green-Yellow).
- [ ] **Voltage drop must be ≤ 3%** of declared voltage in any sub-circuit (IS 732).
- [ ] **Standard MCB ratings:** 6, 10, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50, 63 A.
- [ ] **Standard copper cable sizes:** 1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0, 10.0 mm² (PVC insulated).
- [ ] **Earth resistance** must be ≤ 5 Ω for domestic (per IS 3043).
- [ ] **Energy meter constant K (rev/kWh)** is printed on the meter nameplate; useful in calibration.
- [ ] **P = V × I × cos φ** is the single-phase AC power formula — the most tested equation.
- [ ] **Diversity factor** (0.5–0.7 domestic) must be applied before MCB selection.
- [ ] **Insulation resistance** measured by megger must be **≥ 1 MΩ** between conductors and earth.
- [ ] **MCB trip curves:** B (resistive), C (general/motor), D (heavy inductive).
- [ ] **RCCB is the modern name for ELCB** — both are the same device; KTU accepts both.
- [ ] **In energy meter, deflecting torque $T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\varphi$**, and braking torque $T_b \propto N$. At equilibrium, $N \propto P$, and revolutions in time $t$ represent energy.
- [ ] **Always draw the single-line diagram FIRST in KTU exams** — it carries independent marks.
- [ ] **Never skip numerical steps** — show $I = P/V$, $A_{min} = \rho L / R$, etc., explicitly.
- [ ] **R_earth ≤ V_safe / I_Δn** = 50 / 0.030 ≈ 1666 Ω, but practically enforce ≤ 5 Ω.
- [ ] **Bus-bar of MCB DB carries phase**; **Neutral link carries neutral**; **Earth bus carries earth** — three separate conductors, all interlinked only at the main ELCB / earth pit.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of step-up and step-down transformers; measurement and representation of voltage and waveform using CRO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Transformer — Formal Academic Definition

A **transformer** is a static (stationary, no moving parts) electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current (AC) circuit to another through the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency. It can step up (increase) or step down (decrease) the voltage level while correspondingly adjusting the current, governed by the **turns ratio** of its windings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** A transformer is an electrical machine that converts AC electrical energy from one voltage level to another, working only with AC supply. It consists of two or more coupled windings wound over a common laminated magnetic core.

---

## 1.2 Step-Up vs Step-Down Transformer — Tabular Overview

| Parameter | Step-Up Transformer | Step-Down Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Increases voltage from primary to secondary | Decreases voltage from primary to secondary |
| **Turns Ratio ($N_s / N_p$)** | Greater than 1 (i.e., $N_s > N_p$) | Less than 1 (i.e., $N_s < N_p$) |
| **Primary Winding** | Fewer turns, **thicker** wire (high current side) | More turns, **thinner** wire |
| **Secondary Winding** | More turns, **thinner** wire (low current side) | Fewer turns, **thicker** wire |
| **Voltage Relation** | $V_s > V_p$ | $V_s < V_p$ |
| **Current Relation** | $I_s < I_p$ | $I_s > I_p$ |
| **Real-world Examples** | Microwave oven, X-ray machines, CRT TV, power distribution substation (after generation) | Mobile chargers, doorbell circuits, AC adapters, distribution transformers to homes |

---

## 1.3 Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) — Formal Academic Definition

A **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)** is a versatile electronic test instrument that visually displays and analyses the **waveform** of electrical signals (typically voltage versus time) on a phosphor-coated screen, using a focused beam of electrons. It allows direct measurement of **voltage amplitude, frequency, period, phase difference**, and waveform shape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Concept:** The CRO is the *“eye”* of an electronics/electrical engineer. Unlike a multimeter which only gives numerical RMS or average values, the CRO shows the **entire shape of the signal**, making it indispensable for waveform analysis.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### 1.4.1 Transformer — The "Water Pressure Analogy"
Imagine water flowing through a pipe:
- A **narrow pipe** with high pressure (high voltage, low current) is like a **step-up transformer output** — water rushes out fast but in small volume.
- A **wide pipe** with low pressure (low voltage, high current) is like a **step-down transformer output** — water flows slowly but in large volume.
- The transformer "renegotiates" the pressure–flow trade-off without wasting the total energy (ideally).

### 1.4.2 CRO — The "Heart Monitor Analogy"
A CRO is to electrical signals what a **medical ECG (Electrocardiogram) machine** is to the human heart. Just as a doctor can read a patient's heartbeat pattern from the wavy line on the ECG, an engineer can study the "health" of an electronic circuit from the waveform displayed on the CRO screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnetic Flux ($\phi$):** The invisible "magnetic river" linking the primary and secondary coils. Unit: **Weber (Wb)**. For all KTU numericals, assume flux varies sinusoidally: $\phi = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$.
> **Frequency ($f$):** Standard Indian AC supply: **50 Hz**. This is a constant value you must remember for KTU problems.
> **Permeability of free space ($\mu_0$):** $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$.

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experiment tests **CO1: Understanding of basic electrical machines and measurement instruments**. As per KTU 2024 scheme, the workshop is evaluated continuously (internals) for **50 marks** and the end-semester practical exam carries **50 marks**. Familiarity with transformer terminals, identifying step-up/step-down by visual inspection, and using CRO knobs correctly are the most frequently asked viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Transformer — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a transformer is rooted in **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** (1831) and **Lenz's Law**.

### Logical Flow of Operation:

1. **AC Supply to Primary:** When an alternating voltage $V_p$ is applied to the primary winding, an alternating current $I_p$ flows through it.
2. **Production of Alternating Flux:** This alternating current produces an **alternating magnetic flux** ($\phi$) in the laminated iron core. The core is laminated (thin sheets insulated from each other) to **minimise eddy current losses**.
3. **Linking of Flux:** The core guides this alternating flux such that it links with the secondary winding with near 100% efficiency (ideal coupling).
4. **EMF Induction in Secondary:** By Faraday's law, the changing flux induces an EMF in the secondary winding:
   $$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$
5. **Load Current Flow:** If the secondary is connected to a load, this induced EMF drives a current $I_s$ through it, delivering power to the load.
6. **Magnetising Current:** A small portion of $I_p$ (called the **magnetising current**, $I_m$) is used only to maintain the core flux. It does no useful work.
7. **Conservation of Power (Ideal):** For an ideal transformer, input power = output power:
   $$V_p I_p = V_s I_s$$

---

## 2.2 Constructional Details (Workshop Familiarisation)

### 2.2.1 Core
- Made of **silicon steel laminations** (each ~0.35 mm thick, insulated by varnish).
- Two common shapes: **Core-type** (windings surround a part of the core) and **Shell-type** (core surrounds the windings).
- Lamination reduces **eddy current losses**; silicon steel reduces **hysteresis losses**.

### 2.2.2 Windings
- **Primary:** Connected to the AC source.
- **Secondary:** Connected to the load.
- Made of **enamel-coated copper** wire (enamel prevents shorting between adjacent turns).
- Helical, sandwich, or disc type depending on rating.

### 2.2.3 Other Parts
- **Tank and oil:** For cooling in large transformers.
- **Bushings:** Insulators for terminals.
- **Tap changer:** To adjust turns ratio slightly.
- **Breather (Silica gel):** Absorbs moisture from air entering the tank.

---

## 2.3 Ideal Transformer — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU numericals for this experiment assume the **ideal transformer** unless explicitly stated. Ideal means: no winding resistance, no leakage flux, no core losses, 100% efficiency.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turns Ratio ($K$ or $a$) | $K = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Ratio of secondary to primary turns |
| 2 | Voltage Ratio | $\dfrac{V_s}{V_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = K$ | EMF equation (Faraday's law applied to both windings) |
| 3 | Current Ratio | $\dfrac{I_p}{I_s} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = K$ | From power conservation |
| 4 | Power Conservation (Ideal) | $V_p I_p = V_s I_s$ | Input power equals output power |
| 5 | Sinusoidal Flux | $\phi = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$ | Flux varies sinusoidally |
| 6 | Induced EMF (RMS) | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_{max}$ | **EMF equation of a transformer** (most important) |
| 7 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Relates $f$ to $\omega$ |
| 8 | Transformer Efficiency ($\eta$) | $\eta = \dfrac{V_s I_s \cos\phi_s}{V_s I_s \cos\phi_s + P_{losses}} \times 100\%$ | Practical efficiency |
| 9 | CRO Voltage Measurement | $V_{pp} = (\text{Vertical Divisions}) \times (\text{VOLTS/DIV setting})$ | Peak-to-peak voltage |
| 10 | CRO Time Period | $T = (\text{Horizontal Divisions}) \times (\text{TIME/DIV setting})$ | Period of one cycle |
| 11 | Frequency (from CRO) | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Inverse of period |
| 12 | CRO Instantaneous Voltage | $v(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | General sinusoidal waveform |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4.44 Factor — Why 4.44?**
> The constant 4.44 in the EMF equation comes from: $4.44 = 2\pi / \sqrt{2} \times \text{form factor}$ which is approximately 4.44 for a pure sine wave. KTU may ask: "Why not use $2\pi$?" — The answer: 4.44 converts peak flux $\phi_{max}$ into average/rms and incorporates the form factor (1.11) of a sine wave.

---

## 2.4 KTU 2024 Concept: Identifying Step-Up vs Step-Down Without Meters

In the workshop, students often must identify a transformer **only by physical inspection** (a common viva question). Use the table:

| Observation | Step-Up | Step-Down |
|---|---|---|
| Wire thickness of secondary vs primary | **Thinner** (handles less current) | **Thicker** (handles more current) |
| Number of turns visible on secondary | **More** | **Fewer** |
| Core size for same VA rating | Generally **larger** core for same kVA | Smaller core possible |
| Resistance of secondary (DC ohmmeter) | **Higher** (more turns, thinner wire) | **Lower** |

---

## 2.5 Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) — Deep Theory

### 2.5.1 Block Diagram Components (To be cross-referenced with Section 4)

The major subsystems of a CRO are:

1. **Electron Gun Assembly:** Produces a focused, accelerated beam of electrons.
   - **Cathode (K):** Heated filament (indirectly heated) emits electrons via **thermionic emission**.
   - **Control Grid (G):** Negative potential w.r.t. cathode; controls **beam intensity (brightness)**.
   - **Focusing Anode (A1)** and **Accelerating Anode (A2):** Focus the beam to a fine dot on the screen.
2. **Deflection System:** Two pairs of plates.
   - **Y-plates (Vertical Deflection Plates):** Deflect beam **up/down** proportional to input voltage. Mounted horizontally with plates on top and bottom.
   - **X-plates (Horizontal Deflection Plates):** Deflect beam **left/right** proportional to time-base voltage.
3. **Fluorescent Screen:** Coated with **phosphor** (e.g., P31 green, P7 blue-short persistence). When electrons strike, they emit light.
4. **Time Base Generator (Sweep Generator):** Produces a **sawtooth waveform** that sweeps the beam horizontally at a constant rate, creating the time axis.
5. **Vertical Amplifier:** Amplifies weak input signals to a level sufficient to deflect the beam visibly.
6. **Horizontal Amplifier:** Amplifies the sawtooth signal before applying to X-plates.
7. **Trigger Circuit:** Synchronises the sweep start with the input signal for a stable display.
8. **Power Supply:** Provides DC voltages to all electrodes.

### 2.5.2 Working of a CRO

- The electron gun emits a narrow, focused beam of high-velocity electrons.
- This beam strikes the phosphor screen producing a bright spot at the centre.
- When an AC input voltage is applied to the **Y-plates**, the spot moves up and down.
- Simultaneously, the **time base** applies a linear ramp (sawtooth) to the **X-plates**, sweeping the spot left to right at constant speed.
- As the spot returns quickly to the left (during the flyback of the sawtooth), the input is also returning, so a **stable waveform** is displayed.
- **Triggering** ensures the sweep starts at the same point of the input waveform each cycle, preventing the display from drifting.

### 2.5.3 Important CRO Front Panel Controls (Viva Essentials)

| Control | Function |
|---|---|
| **INTENSITY (or BRIGHT)** | Controls the brightness of the trace by varying grid voltage. |
| **FOCUS** | Adjusts the sharpness of the spot. |
| **VERTICAL POSITION** | Shifts the waveform up/down. |
| **HORIZONTAL POSITION** | Shifts the waveform left/right. |
| **VOLTS/DIV** | Sets vertical scale (sensitivity). E.g., 1 V/div means each vertical cm = 1 V. |
| **TIME/DIV (or TIME/CM)** | Sets horizontal scale (sweep speed). E.g., 1 ms/div means each horizontal cm = 1 ms. |
| **AC/DC/GND switch** | Selects input coupling: AC (blocks DC), DC (passes all), GND (grounds input, sets reference). |
| **TRIGGER LEVEL** | Sets the voltage at which the sweep starts. |
| **X-Y Mode** | Disables time base; an external signal drives the X-axis. Used for **Lissajous figures** to measure frequency and phase. |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Tool Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Power Grid (Generation → Home) | **Step-Up** at generation, **Step-Down** at distribution | Reduces $I^2R$ transmission losses over long distances. |
| Mobile Phone Charging | **Step-Down** + Rectifier | Converts 230 V AC to ~5 V DC for the phone battery. |
| Microwave Oven (Magnetron supply) | **Step-Up** (to ~2000 V) | High voltage needed to accelerate electrons in the magnetron. |
| CRO in Lab | **Voltage and waveform measurement** | The only instrument that shows the **shape** of the signal, not just a number. |
| Audio Equipment Testing | **CRO (X-Y mode)** | Lissajous figures check phase and distortion. |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of a Transformer

This is the most important derivation in the entire workshop module and is **frequently asked for 7 marks** in KTU ESE.

### Given:
- $N$ = Number of turns of the winding
- $\phi_{max}$ = Maximum value of flux in the core (in Webers)
- $f$ = Frequency of the AC supply (in Hz)
- $\phi$ varies sinusoidally: $\phi(t) = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Write the flux as a function of time.

The flux in the core is sinusoidal, so we write:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.

According to Faraday's Law, the EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns is:

$$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate the flux expression.

$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \left[ \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t) \right] = \phi_{max} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into Faraday's equation.

$$e = -N \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

$$e = N \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot \omega \cdot \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Identify the maximum value of the induced EMF.

The maximum value occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = \pm 1$:

$$E_{max} = N \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot \omega$$

**Step 6:** Express angular frequency $\omega$ in terms of supply frequency $f$.

We know that $\omega = 2\pi f$, so substitute:

$$E_{max} = N \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot 2\pi f$$

$$E_{max} = 2\pi f N \phi_{max}$$

**Step 7:** Convert peak (maximum) EMF to RMS value.

For a sinusoidal waveform, the RMS value is the peak value divided by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$E_{rms} = \frac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f N \phi_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 8:** Simplify the constant.

The constant $\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}}$ simplifies numerically to:

$$\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\pi \times 0.7071 = 4.44 \text{ (approximately)}$$

**Step 9:** Write the final EMF equation.

$$\boxed{E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_{max}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **EMF equation of a transformer**, valid for both primary and secondary windings. For the primary: $E_p = 4.44 f N_p \phi_{max}$ and for the secondary: $E_s = 4.44 f N_s \phi_{max}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Transformation Ratio

**Step 1:** Write EMF equation for primary and secondary.

$$E_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_{max}$$

$$E_s = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_{max}$$

**Step 2:** Divide the two equations (since frequency and flux are common for an ideal transformer).

$$\frac{E_s}{E_p} = \frac{4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_{max}}{4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_{max}}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel common terms.

$$\frac{E_s}{E_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

**Step 4:** In an ideal transformer, primary voltage ≈ back-EMF, so $V_p \approx E_p$ and $V_s = E_s$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{V_s}{V_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} = K}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Current Transformation Ratio (Using Power Conservation)

**Step 1:** For an ideal transformer, input power = output power.

$$P_{input} = P_{output}$$

**Step 2:** Express power in terms of voltage and current (assuming unity power factor for ideal case).

$$V_p \, I_p = V_s \, I_s$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the voltage ratio from the previous derivation.

$$V_p \, I_p = V_p \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot I_s$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $V_p$ on both sides.

$$I_p = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot I_s$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange.

$$\boxed{\frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} = K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **inverse relationship**:
> - **Step-Up** ($N_s > N_p$, so $K > 1$): Voltage increases, current decreases.
> - **Step-Down** ($N_s < N_p$, so $K < 1$): Voltage decreases, current increases.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 1 (KTU Typical 7-Mark Question)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> A single-phase transformer has 800 turns on the primary and 200 turns on the secondary. The primary is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. Calculate:
> (i) Turns ratio
> (ii) Secondary voltage
> (iii) Secondary current when the primary draws 5 A
> (iv) Identify whether the transformer is step-up or step-down.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $N_p = 800$ turns
- $N_s = 200$ turns
- $V_p = 230 \text{ V}$
- $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$
- $I_p = 5 \text{ A}$

**Part (i): Turns Ratio**

$$K = \frac{N_s}{N_p} = \frac{200}{800} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Part (ii): Secondary Voltage**

$$\frac{V_s}{V_p} = K = 0.25$$

$$V_s = 0.25 \times 230 = 57.5 \text{ V}$$

**Part (iii): Secondary Current**

$$\frac{I_p}{I_s} = K = 0.25$$

$$I_s = \frac{I_p}{0.25} = \frac{5}{0.25} = 20 \text{ A}$$

**Part (iv): Identification**

Since $V_s = 57.5 \text{ V} < V_p = 230 \text{ V}$, this is a **step-down transformer**. Also confirmed because $N_s < N_p$ (200 < 800).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $K = N_s / N_p$ with $K = N_p / N_s$. If you take the ratio as $N_p / N_s = 4$, then a *step-down* would falsely appear as step-up. **Always follow the convention**: $K$ is secondary-to-primary.

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 2 (CRO Measurement Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**
> A sinusoidal waveform displayed on a CRO shows a peak-to-peak vertical deflection of 4.2 divisions with the VOLTS/DIV switch set at 2 V/div. One complete cycle occupies 5 divisions horizontally with the TIME/DIV switch set at 5 ms/div. Calculate:
> (i) Peak-to-peak voltage
> (ii) RMS voltage
> (iii) Period of the waveform
> (iv) Frequency

### Solution:

**Given:**
- Vertical deflection = 4.2 divisions
- VOLTS/DIV = 2 V/div
- Horizontal deflection = 5 divisions
- TIME/DIV = 5 ms/div

**Part (i): Peak-to-Peak Voltage**

$$V_{pp} = (\text{vertical divisions}) \times (\text{VOLTS/DIV})$$

$$V_{pp} = 4.2 \times 2 = 8.4 \text{ V}$$

**Part (ii): RMS Voltage**

For a pure sine wave:

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

First, $V_m = V_{pp} / 2 = 8.4 / 2 = 4.2 \text{ V}$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{4.2}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4.2}{1.414} = 2.97 \text{ V}$$

**Part (iii): Period**

$$T = (\text{horizontal divisions}) \times (\text{TIME/DIV})$$

$$T = 5 \times 5 \text{ ms} = 25 \text{ ms} = 0.025 \text{ s}$$

**Part (iv): Frequency**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.025} = 40 \text{ Hz}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Voltage, Current, and Turns in Both Configurations

| Configuration | $N_p$ vs $N_s$ | $K = N_s / N_p$ | $V_s$ vs $V_p$ | $I_s$ vs $I_p$ | Power In vs Out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Step-Up | $N_s > N_p$ | $K > 1$ | $V_s > V_p$ | $I_s < I_p$ | $P_{in} = P_{out}$ |
| Ideal Step-Down | $N_s < N_p$ | $K < 1$ | $V_s < V_p$ | $I_s > I_p$ | $P_{in} = P_{out}$ |
| Isolation (1:1) | $N_s = N_p$ | $K = 1$ | $V_s = V_p$ | $I_s = I_p$ | $P_{in} = P_{out}$ |

---

## 3.7 Derivation of the Lissajous Figure Relationship (Bonus 14-Mark Topic)

When the CRO is operated in **X-Y mode**, the X-plates are driven by a known reference signal of frequency $f_y$, and the Y-plates by the unknown signal of frequency $f_x$. The pattern formed is a **Lissajous figure**.

**Key Formulas:**

$$f_x = f_y \times \frac{N_x}{N_y}$$

where $N_x$ = number of times the pattern touches the **vertical (X) edge** (i.e., number of peaks along X), and $N_y$ = number of times the pattern touches the **horizontal (Y) edge** (i.e., number of peaks along Y).

**Phase difference from ellipse:**

$$\sin(\phi) = \frac{Y_{intercept}}{Y_{max}} = \frac{X_{intercept}}{X_{max}}$$

where the intercepts are measured on the central horizontal/vertical axis of the ellipse.

> [!NOTE]
> This is **rarely** asked in the workshop exam (mostly in core electronics papers), but the CRO section of this experiment often includes a 3-mark viva on Lissajous figures.

```mermaid
graph TD

    A2 --> A3[Laminated Silicon Steel Core<br/>Guides Alternating Flux phi]
    A3 --> A4[Secondary Winding<br/>Ns turns]

    A3 -.mutual induction.-> A4
    A2 -.flux linkage.-> A3

    style A5 fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#dc143c,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- AC Source → energises the **Primary** winding.
- Primary current creates alternating magnetic flux in the **Core**.
- The **Secondary** is magnetically coupled to the Primary (no physical wire connection).
- Mutual induction transfers energy to the **Load**.

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model]**
>
> **Q1.** Define a transformer and state the principle on which it works.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A transformer is a static electromagnetic device that transfers AC electrical energy from one circuit to another without changing the frequency. It works on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** between two coils linked by a common magnetic core. When AC is supplied to the primary, the resulting alternating flux induces an EMF in the secondary winding per Faraday's law. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark principle + 1 mark brief explanation)*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **Q2.** List any three front panel controls of a CRO and state their functions.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **INTENSITY** — Controls the brightness of the trace on the screen by adjusting the grid voltage in the electron gun. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **FOCUS** — Adjusts the sharpness/fineness of the spot on the screen by varying the focusing anode voltage. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **VOLTS/DIV** — Sets the vertical sensitivity; each vertical division corresponds to a specific voltage (e.g., 1 V/div). *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Transformer Focused

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a transformer, starting from Faraday's law. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 5 kVA, 2300/230 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 800 turns on the primary. Calculate:
> (i) Number of turns on the secondary
> (ii) Full-load primary and secondary currents
> (iii) Maximum flux in the core
> *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer to Question A:

#### Part (a) — EMF Equation Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:** Ideal transformer, sinusoidal flux, no losses, perfect magnetic coupling.

**Step 1:** Let flux in the core vary sinusoidally:
$$\phi(t) = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply Faraday's Law for N turns:
$$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate:
$$e = -N \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t) = N \phi_{max} \omega \sin(\omega t - \pi/2)$$

**Step 4:** Maximum EMF occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$:
$$E_{max} = N \phi_{max} \omega = 2\pi f N \phi_{max}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to RMS using the $1/\sqrt{2}$ factor:
$$E_{rms} = \frac{2\pi f N \phi_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_{max}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_{max}}$$

**Mark Distribution:** (Assumptions 1, Equation setup 1, Differentiation 1, Peak form 1, RMS conversion 2, Final box 1)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $S = 5 \text{ kVA} = 5000 \text{ VA}$
- $V_p = 2300 \text{ V}$, $V_s = 230 \text{ V}$
- $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$
- $N_p = 800$ turns

**Part (i): Number of turns on secondary**

Using voltage ratio:
$$\frac{V_s}{V_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

$$N_s = N_p \times \frac{V_s}{V_p} = 800 \times \frac{230}{2300} = 80 \text{ turns}$$

**Part (ii): Full-load currents**

For a single-phase transformer, $S = V \times I$:

$$I_p = \frac{S}{V_p} = \frac{5000}{2300} = 2.174 \text{ A}$$

$$I_s = \frac{S}{V_s} = \frac{5000}{230} = 21.74 \text{ A}$$

**Verification:** $V_p I_p = 2300 \times 2.174 = 5000 \text{ VA}$ ✓ and $V_s I_s = 230 \times 21.74 = 5000 \text{ VA}$ ✓

**Part (iii): Maximum flux in the core**

Using the EMF equation for the primary:
$$V_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_{max}$$

$$\phi_{max} = \frac{V_p}{4.44 \, f \, N_p} = \frac{2300}{4.44 \times 50 \times 800}$$

$$\phi_{max} = \frac{2300}{177600} = 0.01295 \text{ Wb} = 12.95 \text{ mWb}$$

**Final Answer:** $N_s = 80$ turns, $I_p = 2.174$ A, $I_s = 21.74$ A, $\phi_{max} = 12.95$ mWb.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — CRO Focused (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO). *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A sine wave is displayed on a CRO. The waveform has a peak-to-peak vertical span of 6 divisions with the VOLTS/DIV switch at 0.5 V/div, and one cycle spans 4 divisions with the TIME/DIV switch at 2 μs/div. Calculate:
> (i) Peak-to-peak and RMS voltage
> (ii) Period and frequency
> (iii) Write the instantaneous voltage expression
> *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer to Question B:

#### Part (a) — CRO Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction (refer to block diagram in Section 4):**

The CRO consists of the following major blocks:

1. **Electron Gun:** Produces a focused beam of electrons via thermionic emission from a heated cathode. The cathode (K), control grid (G), focusing anode (A1), and accelerating anode (A2) work together to produce a sharp, high-velocity electron beam. *(1 mark)*

2. **Deflection System:** Two pairs of plates:
   - **Y-plates** (horizontal plates) deflect the beam vertically based on input signal.
   - **X-plates** (vertical plates) deflect horizontally based on time-base voltage. *(1 mark)*

3. **Phosphor Screen:** Coated with phosphor (P31 for green, P7 for blue), it glows when struck by the electron beam. *(0.5 marks)*

4. **Time Base Generator:** Produces a sawtooth voltage that sweeps the beam linearly across the screen. *(0.5 marks)*

5. **Vertical and Horizontal Amplifiers:** Boost weak input signals. *(0.5 marks)*

6. **Trigger Circuit:** Synchronises the sweep with the input signal for a stable display. *(0.5 marks)*

**Working:**

The electron gun emits a focused, high-velocity beam. When the input signal is applied to the Y-plates, the beam is deflected vertically in proportion to the instantaneous voltage. Simultaneously, the time-base sawtooth signal applied to the X-plates sweeps the beam horizontally at a constant rate. The combination produces a 2-D plot of **voltage (Y) vs. time (X)** on the screen. The flyback of the sawtooth quickly returns the beam to the left to begin the next trace. The trigger circuit ensures that the sweep is synchronised with the input, producing a stable display. *(2.5 marks for the working explanation)*

#### Part (b) — CRO Measurement Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Vertical deflection = 6 divisions
- VOLTS/DIV = 0.5 V/div
- Horizontal deflection = 4 divisions (one cycle)
- TIME/DIV = 2 μs/div

**Part (i): Peak-to-Peak and RMS Voltage**

$$V_{pp} = 6 \times 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

$$V_m = \frac{V_{pp}}{2} = \frac{3.0}{2} = 1.5 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.5}{1.414} = 1.061 \text{ V}$$

**Part (ii): Period and Frequency**

$$T = 4 \times 2 \, \mu s = 8 \, \mu s = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.25 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 125 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (iii): Instantaneous Voltage Expression**

The general form for a sine wave is:
$$v(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$$

Substituting $V_m = 1.5$ V and $f = 125,000$ Hz:

$$\boxed{v(t) = 1.5 \sin(2\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{5} \times t) = 1.5 \sin(7.854 \times 10^{5} \, t) \text{ V}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **The "4.44 vs 2π" Trap (3 marks):** Students often write $E = 2\pi f N \phi_{max}$ (peak value) and forget to convert to RMS. The **RMS equation** is $E = 4.44 f N \phi_{max}$. Always specify whether you are writing peak or RMS, and use the correct constant. *(Loss: 2-3 marks)*
>
> 2. **Turns Ratio Direction (2 marks):** KTU convention is $K = N_s / N_p$. Reversing this ($K = N_p / N_s$) gives wrong step-up/step-down identification. Always double-check.
>
> 3. **CRO Knob Misreading (1-2 marks):** Reading $V_{pp}$ instead of $V_m$ (or vice versa) is a classic error. Remember: **$V_{pp}$ is full vertical span, $V_m$ is half**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Units (1 mark):** If TIME/DIV is in μs/div, the period comes out in μs. Convert to seconds (×10⁻⁶) **before** calculating $f = 1/T$.
>
> 5. **Drawing a CRO without labelling the X and Y plates (1 mark):** A block diagram without labels is incomplete. Always annotate: "Y-plates — vertical deflection", "X-plates — horizontal deflection".
>
> 6. **Confusing the "GND" coupling switch with actual ground (1 mark):** GND in CRO simply disconnects the input and grounds the amplifier; the circuit ground is not physically connected to earth.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Summary — Memorise Before Exam:**

### Transformer
- ☐ A **static** AC device working on **mutual induction** (Faraday's law).
- ☐ **EMF equation (RMS):** $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_{max}$ — The single most important formula.
- ☐ **Voltage ratio:** $V_s / V_p = N_s / N_p = K$ (turns ratio).
- ☐ **Current ratio:** $I_p / I_s = K$ (inverse to voltage).
- ☐ **Power conservation (ideal):** $V_p I_p = V_s I_s$.
- ☐ **Step-up:** $N_s > N_p$, $K > 1$, voltage rises, current falls.
- ☐ **Step-down:** $N_s < N_p$, $K < 1$, voltage falls, current rises.
- ☐ **Core:** Laminated silicon steel; lamination = less eddy current loss; silicon = less hysteresis loss.
- ☐ **Identified by physical inspection:** Secondary wire thinner (step-up) or thicker (step-down).

### Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)
- ☐ **Purpose:** Display voltage vs. time waveform on a phosphor screen.
- ☐ **Electron gun parts:** Cathode (K) → Grid (G, intensity) → Anodes (A1 focus, A2 accelerate).
- ☐ **Deflection plates:** Y-plates (vertical) deflect up/down; X-plates (horizontal) deflect left/right.
- ☐ **Time base:** Sawtooth waveform sweeps the beam at constant speed; creates time axis.
- ☐ **Triggering:** Synchronises sweep with input for a stable, non-drifting display.
- ☐ **Voltage measurement:** $V_{pp} = (\text{vertical div}) \times (\text{VOLTS/DIV})$.
- ☐ **Time measurement:** $T = (\text{horizontal div}) \times (\text{TIME/DIV})$.
- ☐ **Frequency:** $f = 1/T$.
- ☐ **RMS conversion (sine):** $V_{rms} = V_{pp} / (2\sqrt{2})$.
- ☐ **Front panel essentials:** INTENSITY, FOCUS, VOLTS/DIV, TIME/DIV, POSITION (H and V), TRIGGER, AC/DC/GND, X-Y mode.
- ☐ **Standard AC mains in India:** 230 V, 50 Hz — remember for numericals.

### KTU Viva Quick-Fire Questions
1. *Why is the core laminated?* → To reduce eddy current losses.
2. *Why is silicon steel used?* → High permeability, low hysteresis loss.
3. *Can a transformer work on DC?* → No. DC produces constant flux, no induced EMF.
4. *What is the function of the grid in CRO?* → To control the intensity (brightness) of the trace.
5. *Why is the time-base waveform sawtooth?* → To make the beam sweep at constant speed (linear time axis).
6. *What is meant by "triggering"?* → Synchronising the sweep start with the input signal.
7. *Why is the CRO screen coated with phosphor?* → To emit visible light when struck by the electron beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for remembering transformer ratios:** "**N**ew **S**econdary over **P**rimary" → "**NS**P" → always $K = N_s / N_p$.
> **Mnemonic for CRO knobs:** "**I**'ll **F**ind **V**ery **T**riggered **P**eople **A**t **G**round" → Intensity, Focus, Volts/Div, Time/Div, Trigger, Position, AC/DC, GND.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation of rheostats; measurement of potential across resistance elements using a DC circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS  ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of rheostats; measurement of potential across resistance elements using a DC circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Rheostat — Formal KTU Definition

A **rheostat** is a two-terminal **variable resistor** whose resistance can be adjusted manually by sliding or rotating a wiper contact along a continuous resistance element. It is used to control the magnitude of current in a DC circuit without interrupting the supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — GXESL106):** A rheostat is treated as a *continuously variable* resistor (as opposed to a *stepped* resistor like a resistance box). The student must learn to identify its **three terminals**, distinguish between its use as a *series current controller* and as a *potential divider (potentiometer)*, and operate it safely within its **rated current** and **rated wattage**.

### 1.1.1 Construction in Brief

A typical wire-wound rheostat consists of:
1. An **insulating ceramic / porcelain former** (tube).
2. **Resistance wire** (nichrome / constantan) uniformly wound on the former.
3. A **sliding wiper (slider)** that traverses the wire turns.
4. **Two fixed end terminals** $T_1$ and $T_2$ at the ends of the winding.
5. A **movable wiper terminal** $T_W$ (sometimes a third terminal on rotary types).
6. A **metal / Bakelite housing** with heat-dissipation fins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wire-wound rheostats** are used for high current / high power applications (motors, heaters). **Carbon-track rheostats** are used for low-power, precision laboratory work because their resistance varies *linearly* with slider displacement.

### 1.1.2 Types of Rheostats (Classification Table)

| Type | Resistance Element | Power Range | Typical Use |
|------|-------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Wire-wound (sliding, tubular)** | Nichrome wire on ceramic tube | 25 W – 500 W | Motor speed control, lamp dimming |
| **Wire-wound (rotary, disc type)** | Resistance wire on a flat ring | 10 W – 300 W | Fan regulators, lab experiments |
| **Carbon-film rotary** | Carbon composition track | 0.25 W – 2 W | Electronic circuits, volume controls |
| **Liquid (electrolyte) rheostat** | Salt-water column with movable plates | kW range | Industrial motor starters (obsolete) |
| **Digital / Electronic rheostat** | FET / power-MOSFET array | Programmable | Modern lab power supplies |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a **water pipeline** carrying water (current) from a tank (battery). If you place a **partially-closed valve** in the pipe, you restrict the flow of water.

- **Wider opening** = less restriction = **less resistance** = **more current**.
- **Narrower opening** = more restriction = **more resistance** = **less current**.

A **rheostat is exactly this valve** in an electrical circuit.

> [!TIP]
> A second, even more relatable analogy: a rheostat behaves like the **dimmer switch** in your home — turn it one way, the bulb (load) glows brightly (more current); turn it the other way, the bulb dims (less current). Internally, the dimmer is varying resistance, which changes the current through the bulb, which changes its power dissipation $P = I^2 R$, which changes its brightness.

---

## 1.3 DC Circuit — Potential Measurement (Formal Definition)

**Electric potential difference (V)** between two points **A** and **B** in a DC circuit is defined as the **work done per unit charge** in moving a positive test charge from A to B against the electric field.

$$V_{AB} = \frac{W_{AB}}{Q} \quad \text{(unit: Volt, V)}$$

In a purely resistive DC circuit, this potential difference equals the **voltage drop** across the resistor as given by **Ohm's Law**:

$$V = I \cdot R$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Measurement rule:** A voltmeter is **always connected in parallel (shunt)** with the element whose voltage is to be measured — never in series. An ideal voltmeter has **infinite internal resistance**, so it draws **zero current** and does not disturb the circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping — KTU 2024:**
> - **CO1:** Identify common electrical workshop tools and components (rheostat, voltmeter, ammeter, resistors).
> - **CO2:** Construct simple DC series–parallel circuits and measure voltage / current safely.
> - **CO3:** Verify Ohm's Law and the voltage-divider rule experimentally.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Rheostat

The working of a rheostat can be understood in **three structured logic steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Resistance Element:** A uniform resistance wire (e.g., nichrome, resistivity $\rho \approx 1.10 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!m$) is wound on an insulating former. The total resistance between the two end terminals is
$$R_{total} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$
where $L$ is the effective wire length and $A$ its cross-section.

2. **Step 2 — Wiper Action:** When the slider is moved, the **effective length** of wire between the *used* terminal (say $T_1$) and the wiper changes. Since $R \propto L$, the resistance **$R$ between $T_1$ and $T_W$** varies linearly with slider position.

3. **Step 3 — Circuit Effect:** The rheostat, connected in series with a load $R_L$, forms the loop
$$I = \dfrac{V_s}{R_{rheostat} + R_L}$$
As $R_{rheostat}$ is increased from **0 Ω** to its **maximum rated value**, the current $I$ decreases smoothly from a maximum to a minimum (often zero). This is the **current-control action**.

> **Real-world utility:** This same principle is used in **ceiling fan regulators** (rotary rheostat), **theatre light dimmers**, **laboratory DC power supplies** (current-limit knob), and **starter resistors for DC motors**.

---

## 2.2 Rheostat Connection Modes

| Mode | Terminals Used | Function | Typical Application |
|------|----------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Two-terminal (series controller)** | $T_1$ and $T_W$ (wiper shorted to $T_2$ is optional) | Variable series resistance → varies current | Motor speed control, lamp dimming |
| **Three-terminal (potentiometer / potential divider)** | $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_W$ all used | Output voltage between $T_W$ and one end varies from 0 to $V_s$ | Volume controls, sensor calibration, reference voltage generation |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake is to leave **all three terminals open / floating**. In the two-terminal connection, the unused end-terminal **must be left disconnected** (or sometimes shorted to the wiper) — never both end terminals floating.

---

## 2.3 Voltmeter — Connection Rules

A voltmeter measures the **potential difference between two nodes** of a circuit. The **golden rules** are:

1. **Always in parallel** (across the element).
2. **Polarity observed**: Red lead → higher potential, Black lead → lower potential.
3. **Range first, then connect**: Set the voltmeter to the **highest range**, connect, then step down.
4. An ideal voltmeter has **$R_V \to \infty$**; a practical moving-coil voltmeter has $R_V \geq 20\ \mathrm{k\Omega/V}$.

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula, condition, and unit you will need for Module Experiment 2 in the KTU 2024 scheme examination.

| # | Quantity / Law | Formula | Units | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ohm's Law** | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Valid for *linear* ohmic conductors at constant temperature. |
| 2 | **Power dissipated in a resistor** | $P = V \cdot I \;=\; I^{2} R \;=\; \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | W (Watt) | Use whichever form is convenient. |
| 3 | **Resistance of a wire** | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | $\Omega$ | $\rho$ = resistivity ($\Omega\!\cdot\!m$), $L$ = length (m), $A$ = area (m²). |
| 4 | **Series combination** | $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots$ | $\Omega$ | Same current flows through all. |
| 5 | **Parallel combination** | $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dots$ | $\Omega$ | Same voltage across all. |
| 6 | **Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)** | $V_k = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{\sum R_i}$ | V | $R_k$ is the resistor across which $V_k$ is to be found. |
| 7 | **Current Divider Rule (CDR)** | $I_k = I_{total} \cdot \dfrac{R_{eq}}{R_k}$ | A | Valid for two parallel branches; $R_{eq}$ is the *parallel* equivalent. |
| 8 | **Maximum current through rheostat** | $I_{max} = \dfrac{V_s}{R_{rh(min)} + R_L}$ | A | When rheostat is at its **minimum** value. |
| 9 | **Minimum current through rheostat** | $I_{min} = \dfrac{V_s}{R_{rh(max)} + R_L}$ | A | When rheostat is at its **maximum** value. |
| 10 | **Wattage rating of rheostat** | $P_{rated} = I_{rated}^{2} \cdot R_{max}$ | W | Must **never be exceeded** in operation. |
| 11 | **Voltmeter loading error** | $V_{measured} = V_{true} \cdot \dfrac{R_V}{R_V + R_{th}}$ | V | $R_{th}$ = Thevenin resistance seen by voltmeter. |
| 12 | **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** | $\sum V_{loop} = 0$ | V | Sum of EMFs = sum of IR drops around any closed loop. |
| 13 | **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** | $\sum I_{node} = 0$ | A | Sum of currents entering = sum leaving a node. |

### 2.4.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ceiling Fan Regulator:** A wire-wound rheostat in series with the fan motor winding. As resistance decreases, the voltage across the motor increases → fan speed increases.
- **Battery Testing:** A rheostat is used as a **load bank** to draw a controlled, known current from a battery while its terminal voltage is measured.
- **Sensor Calibration:** A potentiometer (rheostat in 3-terminal mode) provides a precise adjustable reference voltage to calibrate transducers and instrumentation amplifiers.
- **Educational Labs:** Verify Ohm's law, VDR, and CDR; calibrate ammeters and voltmeters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)

**Statement:** In a series circuit supplied by a single source $V_s$, the voltage across any resistor $R_k$ is proportional to its share of the total series resistance.

**Derivation:**

Consider $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ connected in series across a DC source $V_s$.

**Step 1 — Total resistance:** Because series resistances add directly,

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots + R_n$$

**Step 2 — Loop current (KVL):** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the only loop,

$$V_s - I R_1 - I R_2 - \dots - I R_n = 0$$

Solving for the current $I$:

$$I = \frac{V_s}{R_1 + R_2 + \dots + R_n} = \frac{V_s}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage across $R_k$:** By Ohm's law, the potential drop across the $k$-th resistor is

$$V_k = I \cdot R_k$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I$:**

$$V_k = \left(\frac{V_s}{R_{total}}\right) \cdot R_k = V_s \cdot \frac{R_k}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 5 — Final form (the Voltage Divider Rule):**

$$\boxed{\,V_k \;=\; V_s \cdot \frac{R_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}\,}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** VDR is the *most-used* network reduction formula in instrument design, sensor biasing, and reference-voltage generation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Current Divider Rule (CDR) — Two-Branch Case

Consider two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ in parallel, fed by total current $I_T$.

**Step 1 — Voltage across the parallel combination:**

$$V = I_1 R_1 = I_2 R_2$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent resistance:**

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Total current:**

$$I_T = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = V \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Current through $R_1$:**

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1} = \frac{I_T \cdot R_{eq}}{R_1} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_1 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,}\qquad\boxed{\,I_2 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,}$$

> **Note the inversion:** The current through a branch is **inversely proportional** to its resistance. The *smaller* resistor carries the *larger* share of the current.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Series VDR with Three Resistors

**Problem:** Three resistors $R_1 = 10\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 20\ \Omega$, and $R_3 = 30\ \Omega$ are connected in series across a $12\ \mathrm{V}$ DC battery. Calculate:
(a) the loop current $I$,
(b) the potential difference across each resistor,
(c) the total power consumed.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 10 + 20 + 30 = 60\ \Omega$$

**Loop current (Ohm's Law):**

$$I = \frac{V_s}{R_{total}} = \frac{12\ \mathrm{V}}{60\ \Omega} = 0.20\ \mathrm{A}$$

**(b) Voltage drops (VDR or Ohm's law):**

$$V_1 = I \cdot R_1 = 0.20 \times 10 = 2.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

$$V_2 = I \cdot R_2 = 0.20 \times 20 = 4.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

$$V_3 = I \cdot R_3 = 0.20 \times 30 = 6.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

**Check (KVL):** $V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12\ \mathrm{V} = V_s$ ✓

**(c) Total power:**

$$P_{total} = V_s \cdot I = 12 \times 0.20 = 2.4\ \mathrm{W}$$

(Alternatively: $P = I^2 R_{total} = (0.20)^2 \times 60 = 0.04 \times 60 = 2.4\ \mathrm{W}$ ✓)

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Rheostat in Series with a Load

**Problem:** A rheostat of maximum value $100\ \Omega$ is connected in series with a load $R_L = 50\ \Omega$ across a $20\ \mathrm{V}$ DC source. Find the circuit current when the rheostat is set to (i) $0\ \Omega$, (ii) $50\ \Omega$, (iii) $100\ \Omega$. Also find the voltage across the load in each case.

**Solution:**

The governing equation is

$$I = \frac{V_s}{R_{rh} + R_L} \quad\text{and}\quad V_L = I \cdot R_L$$

| Case | $R_{rh}\ (\Omega)$ | $R_L\ (\Omega)$ | $R_{total}\ (\Omega)$ | $I = V_s/R_{total}$ (A) | $V_L = I \cdot R_L$ (V) |
|------|----|----|----|----|----|
| (i)  | 0   | 50 | 50  | $20/50 = 0.400$ | $0.400 \times 50 = 20.0$ |
| (ii) | 50  | 50 | 100 | $20/100 = 0.200$ | $0.200 \times 50 = 10.0$ |
| (iii)| 100 | 50 | 150 | $20/150 = 0.133$ | $0.133 \times 50 = 6.67$ |

**Observation:** As the rheostat resistance is increased, both the current and the load voltage decrease monotonically. This is the **current-limiting action** of a series rheostat.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour vs. Rheostat Setting

| Slider Position | Effective $R_{rh}$ | Circuit Current $I$ | Load Voltage $V_L$ | Rheostat Voltage $V_{rh}$ | Power in Load $P_L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fully at $T_1$ end | $0\ \Omega$ | **Maximum** $I_{max} = V_s/R_L$ | $V_s$ | $0$ | **Maximum** $V_s^{2}/R_L$ |
| Mid-position | $R_{rh,max}/2$ | Intermediate | $V_s \cdot R_L/(R_L + R_{rh}/2)$ | $I \cdot R_{rh}/2$ | Intermediate |
| Fully at $T_2$ end | $R_{rh,max}$ | **Minimum** $V_s/(R_L + R_{rh,max})$ | Smallest | Largest | Smallest |

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check formula:** $V_L + V_{rh} = V_s$ at every instant (KVL). Always use this to cross-verify any measured pair of voltmeter readings.

---

## 3.6 Voltmeter Loading Error — Quantitative Derivation

When a non-ideal voltmeter of internal resistance $R_V$ is connected across a resistor $R_k$, the meter itself forms a parallel branch, so the *measured* voltage is slightly less than the *true* voltage.

**True voltage (no meter):** $V_{true} = I \cdot R_k$ (with $I = V_s / R_{total}$)

**Measured voltage (with meter):** The effective resistance at the point becomes $R_k \parallel R_V$:

$$V_{meas} = I' \cdot (R_k \parallel R_V) \quad \text{where } I' = \frac{V_s}{R_{total} - R_k + (R_k \parallel R_V)}$$

A simpler form is obtained by Thevenin analysis:

$$\boxed{\,V_{meas} \;=\; V_{true} \cdot \frac{R_V}{R_V + R_{th}}\,}$$

where $R_{th}$ is the Thevenin resistance seen from the voltmeter terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** Choose $R_V \geq 100 \cdot R_{th}$ to keep loading error below **1 %**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Construction of a Wire-Wound Rheostat

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[End Terminal T1] --> B[Resistance Wire Element]
    B --> C[End Terminal T2]
    D[Sliding Wiper Contact] --> B
    D --> E[Wiper Terminal TW]
    F[Ceramic Insulating Former] --- B
    G[Metal Guide Rod] --- D
    H[Bakelite Housing] --- A
    H --- C
    style A fill:#ffe6e6
    style B fill:#fff4e6
    style C fill:#e6ffe6
    style D fill:#e6f3ff
    style E fill:#f3e6ff
    style F fill:#f0f0f0
    style G fill:#f0f0f0
    style H fill:#d9d9d9
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Circuit Schematic of Module Experiment 2

```mermaid
graph LR
    V[DC Source 12V] --> S[SPST Switch]
    S --> RH[Rheostat Rrh 0 to 100 ohm]
    RH --> R1[Resistor R1 10 ohm]
    R1 --> R2[Resistor R2 20 ohm]
    R2 --> R3[Resistor R3 30 ohm]
    R3 --> G[Return to Source Negative]
    VM1[Voltmeter V1 across R1] -.parallel.-> R1
    VM2[Voltmeter V2 across R2] -.parallel.-> R2
    VM3[Voltmeter V3 across R3] -.parallel.-> R3
    VM4[Voltmeter VS across Source] -.parallel.-> V
    style V fill:#ffd966
    style S fill:#b4c7e7
    style RH fill:#f4b183
    style R1 fill:#c5e0b4
    style R2 fill:#c5e0b4
    style R3 fill:#c5e0b4
    style VM1 fill:#fff2cc
    style VM2 fill:#fff2cc
    style VM3 fill:#fff2cc
    style VM4 fill:#fff2cc
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow Diagram — Experiment Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Components: Rheostat, Resistors, DC Source, Voltmeter, Connecting Wires] --> S2[Step 2 Inspect Rheostat: Note Rated Resistance, Rated Current, Terminal Identification]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Set Up Series Circuit on Breadboard as per Circuit Diagram]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Connect Voltmeter in Parallel across Each Resistor and across the Source]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Keep Switch Open and Verify All Connections with Trainer]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Close Switch and Set Rheostat to a Known Position]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Record Voltmeter Readings across Each Element]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Verify KVL: Sum of VR drops equals Source Voltage]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Vary Rheostat Position and Tabulate a New Set of Readings]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Calculate Theoretical Values Using VDR and Compare with Measured Values]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Calculate Percentage Error and Draw Inferences]
    style S1 fill:#c5e0b4
    style S2 fill:#c5e0b4
    style S3 fill:#fff2cc
    style S4 fill:#fff2cc
    style S5 fill:#f4b183
    style S6 fill:#f4b183
    style S7 fill:#b4c7e7
    style S8 fill:#b4c7e7
    style S9 fill:#b4c7e7
    style S10 fill:#d9d9d9
    style S11 fill:#d9d9d9
```

## 4.4 Two-Terminal vs Three-Terminal Rheostat — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TwoTerminalMode[Two Terminal Series Controller]
        T1A[Terminal T1] --> RWA[Effective Resistance Rrh between T1 and Wiper]
        RWA --> TWA[Wiper Terminal TW]
        TWA --> T2A[Terminal T2 is Left Open or Shorted to Wiper]
    end
    subgraph ThreeTerminalMode[Three Terminal Potential Divider]
        T1B[Terminal T1] --> RWB[Full Resistance Element R full]
        RWB --> T2B[Terminal T2]
        TWB[Wiper Terminal TW] --> RWB
        VOUT[Variable Output Voltage between TW and T1] -.taken from.-> TWB
    end
    style TwoTerminalMode fill:#fce4d6
    style ThreeTerminalMode fill:#deebf7
```

> [!TIP]
> The block diagrams above are the **KTU board-exam-acceptable** representations of physical circuit diagrams. For full marks in the practical record, also draw the **standardised schematic symbols** (zigzag for resistor, rectangle-with-arrow for rheostat, circle-with-V for voltmeter) alongside these block diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a rheostat. Mention its two main types and any two practical applications.  `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module Experiment 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rheostat** is a **two-terminal variable resistor** used to control the magnitude of current in a DC circuit by manually varying its effective resistance. **(1 mark)**

The two main types are:
1. **Wire-wound rheostat** — resistance wire (e.g., nichrome) wound on a ceramic former; used for high-power applications. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Carbon-film rheostat** — carbon composition track on an insulating base; used for low-power, precision applications. **(0.5 mark)**

Two practical applications:
1. **Speed control of DC motors / fan regulators.** **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Current-limit knob in laboratory DC power supplies / dimmer switch for lamps.** **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Q2. State Ohm's Law. How is a voltmeter connected in a circuit to measure the potential difference across a resistor?  `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module Experiment 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Ohm's Law:** *The potential difference across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided physical conditions (especially temperature) remain constant.*
$$V = I \cdot R \qquad \text{where } V \text{ in volts, } I \text{ in amperes, } R \text{ in ohms.}$$  **(1.5 marks)**

**Voltmeter connection:**
- A voltmeter is **always connected in parallel** (across the two terminals) of the resistor whose voltage is to be measured. **(1 mark)**
- The **red (positive) lead** goes to the **higher potential** terminal and the **black (negative) lead** to the **lower potential** terminal. **(0.5 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Many students write "voltmeter is connected in *series*". This is the most common single-sentence mistake and leads to immediate loss of 1 mark. Always re-state: **voltmeter = parallel, ammeter = series.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer *either* the full Question A *or* the full Question B. Both choices are placed here for comprehensive practice.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module Experiment 2]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **wire-wound rheostat**. State its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC circuit consists of a $24\ \mathrm{V}$ source connected to three resistors $R_1 = 6\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 12\ \Omega$, and $R_3 = 18\ \Omega$ in **series**. Calculate:
   (i) the total resistance and circuit current,
   (ii) the potential difference across each resistor using the Voltage Divider Rule,
   (iii) the power dissipated in $R_2$ alone. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Construction and Working of a Wire-Wound Rheostat (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
1. A **ceramic / porcelain insulating tube** acts as the former. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Nichrome or constantan resistance wire** of uniform cross-section is wound uniformly over the former. *(0.5 mark)*
3. The two ends of the winding are brought out as **fixed terminals $T_1$ and $T_2$**. *(0.5 mark)*
4. A **sliding wiper (brass contact)** moves along a polished metal rod parallel to the winding; the wiper is connected to a third terminal $T_W$. *(1 mark)*
5. The whole assembly is enclosed in a **metal / Bakelite housing** with **ventilation fins** for heat dissipation. *(0.5 mark)*
6. Refer to the **block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes for the schematic. *(0.5 mark for diagram, mandatory for full marks)*

**Working (2 marks):**
When the slider is moved along the winding, the **effective length of resistance wire** between the used terminals (say $T_1$ and $T_W$) changes. Since $R = \rho L/A$, the resistance varies in direct proportion to the active length. Connected in series with a load, the rheostat then **controls the current** as
$$I = \frac{V_s}{R_{rh} + R_L}$$
As $R_{rh}$ increases from 0 to its maximum value, $I$ decreases smoothly. **(2 marks)**

**Advantages (0.5 mark):**
- High power-handling capacity; can withstand overloads for short durations; mechanically robust.

**Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Resolution is limited (changes in steps equal to one turn of wire); not suitable for very low resistances; produces discrete resistance jumps.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total resistance and circuit current (2 marks):**

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 6 + 12 + 18 = 36\ \Omega$$

$$I = \frac{V_s}{R_{total}} = \frac{24\ \mathrm{V}}{36\ \Omega} = 0.667\ \mathrm{A}$$

**(ii) Potential difference across each resistor using VDR (3 marks):**

$$V_1 = V_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_{total}} = 24 \cdot \frac{6}{36} = 4.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

$$V_2 = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_{total}} = 24 \cdot \frac{12}{36} = 8.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

$$V_3 = V_s \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_{total}} = 24 \cdot \frac{18}{36} = 12.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

**Check (KVL):** $V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 4 + 8 + 12 = 24\ \mathrm{V} = V_s$ ✓

**(iii) Power dissipated in $R_2$ (2 marks):**

$$P_2 = I^{2} \cdot R_2 = (0.667)^{2} \times 12 = 0.444 \times 12 = 5.33\ \mathrm{W}$$

(Or equivalently: $P_2 = V_2 \cdot I = 8.0 \times 0.667 = 5.33\ \mathrm{W}$ ✓)

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module Experiment 2]`

**(a)** Explain, with a neat circuit diagram, the **procedure to measure the potential difference across each of three series-connected resistors** in a DC circuit using a **voltmeter and a rheostat**. Mention the **precautions** to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $200\ \Omega$ rheostat is connected in series with a $100\ \Omega$ load across a $30\ \mathrm{V}$ DC supply. Find:
   (i) the maximum and minimum current that can be drawn,
   (ii) the load voltage when the rheostat is set to $50\ \Omega$ and $150\ \Omega$,
   (iii) the power wasted in the rheostat when it is set to $100\ \Omega$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Procedure to measure potential difference (7 marks):**

**Apparatus (1 mark):** DC source (0–30 V), rheostat (e.g., 0–200 $\Omega$, rated ≥ 0.5 A), three resistors (e.g., 10 $\Omega$, 20 $\Omega$, 30 $\Omega$ each rated ≥ 5 W), DC voltmeter (0–30 V), connecting wires, SPST switch.

**Circuit diagram (2 marks):** Draw the series loop: Source + → Switch → Rheostat → $R_1$ → $R_2$ → $R_3$ → Source −. Show voltmeter *V1* across $R_1$, *V2* across $R_2$, *V3* across $R_3$, and *VS* across the source. (Refer to the block schematic in Section 4.2.)

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Identify and label the three terminals of the rheostat ($T_1, T_2, T_W$). Use $T_1$ and $T_W$ for the two-terminal connection. *(0.5 mark)*
2. Connect the components in series on the breadboard as per the circuit diagram. *(0.5 mark)*
3. Set the voltmeter to its **highest range** (e.g., 30 V). *(0.5 mark)*
4. With the switch **open**, verify the polarity of the source and the connections. *(0.5 mark)*
5. Close the switch and set the rheostat to a mid-position. *(0.5 mark)*
6. Measure $V_1$, $V_2$, $V_3$ and $V_S$ and tabulate. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Verify KVL:** $V_1 + V_2 + V_3 \approx V_S$. *(0.5 mark)*

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Voltmeter must be connected in *parallel*, never in series.
- Polarity must be observed (red to higher potential).
- Do not exceed the rated wattage of the rheostat.
- Set the meter to the highest range first, then reduce.
- Switch off the supply before changing connections.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $V_s = 30\ \mathrm{V}$, $R_L = 100\ \Omega$, $R_{rh} \in [0, 200]\ \Omega$.

**(i) Maximum and minimum current (2 marks):**

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_s}{R_{rh(min)} + R_L} = \frac{30}{0 + 100} = 0.300\ \mathrm{A}\quad\text{(at }R_{rh}=0\text{)}$$

$$I_{min} = \frac{V_s}{R_{rh(max)} + R_L} = \frac{30}{200 + 100} = 0.100\ \mathrm{A}\quad\text{(at }R_{rh}=200\text{)}$$

**(ii) Load voltage at $R_{rh} = 50\ \Omega$ and $150\ \Omega$ (3 marks):**

At $R_{rh} = 50\ \Omega$:

$$I = \frac{30}{50 + 100} = \frac{30}{150} = 0.200\ \mathrm{A}$$

$$V_L = I \cdot R_L = 0.200 \times 100 = 20.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

At $R_{rh} = 150\ \Omega$:

$$I = \frac{30}{150 + 100} = \frac{30}{250} = 0.120\ \mathrm{A}$$

$$V_L = 0.120 \times 100 = 12.0\ \mathrm{V}$$

**(iii) Power wasted in the rheostat at $R_{rh} = 100\ \Omega$ (2 marks):**

$$I = \frac{30}{100 + 100} = 0.150\ \mathrm{A}$$

$$P_{rh} = I^{2} \cdot R_{rh} = (0.150)^{2} \times 100 = 0.0225 \times 100 = 2.25\ \mathrm{W}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Wrong voltmeter connection (–2 marks):** Writing or drawing the voltmeter in *series* with a resistor. Always parallel, with correct polarity.
> 2. **Missing the third terminal of the rheostat (–1 mark):** Many students treat a potentiometer-connected rheostat as a 2-terminal device. In the 3-terminal "potential divider" mode, all three terminals must be shown.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify KVL (–1 mark):** The experiment record *must* include a KVL verification row: $V_S = V_1 + V_2 + V_3$. Examiner looks for this explicitly.
> 4. **Not tabulating observations (–1 mark):** A neat table with columns *Rheostat position, $V_S$, $V_1$, $V_2$, $V_3$, $I_{calc}$* is compulsory for full marks.
> 5. **Missing the graph/plot (–1 mark):** A plot of $V_L$ vs. $R_{rh}$ is often a viva question. The curve is **decreasing and non-linear (hyperbolic)** — not a straight line.
> 6. **Unit errors (–0.5 mark each):** Writing "V = 12 A" instead of "V = 12 V", or omitting units in the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **rheostat** is a *two-terminal variable resistor*; it can also be used in *three-terminal potentiometer mode*.
- ✅ The resistance element is usually **nichrome wire** (high resistivity, low temperature coefficient).
- ✅ Wire-wound types are for **high power**; carbon-film types are for **precision low-power** work.
- ✅ A **voltmeter is always connected in parallel** (high internal resistance, ideally infinite).
- ✅ An **ammeter is always connected in series** (low internal resistance, ideally zero).
- ✅ The governing equation for any series DC loop is $\,I = V_s / R_{total}\,$.
- ✅ **Voltage Divider Rule:** $\,V_k = V_s \cdot R_k / \sum R_i\,$.
- ✅ **Current Divider Rule:** $\,I_k = I_T \cdot R_{eq} / R_k\,$ (inversely proportional to branch resistance).
- ✅ **KVL sanity check:** $\sum V_{drops} = V_{source}$ in every closed loop.
- ✅ **KCL sanity check:** $\sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out}$ at every node.
- ✅ **Power formulae:** $P = VI = I^2R = V^2/R$ — pick the form with the *known* quantities.
- ✅ **Rheostat safety:** Never exceed its **rated current**; never operate it for long at very low resistance (it acts as a short and overheats).
- ✅ **Voltmeter loading error:** Always keep $R_V \geq 100 \times R_{th}$ to ensure < 1 % error.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Favourites:**
  - "What happens if you reverse the source polarity?" → All measured voltages reverse sign; the magnitudes remain the same.
