**Define the term "datasheet" as applicable to electronic components. List any four key parameters found in the absolute maximum ratings section of a BJT datasheet.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **datasheet** is a technical document published by a semiconductor manufacturer that provides comprehensive information on a component's electrical characteristics, mechanical dimensions, thermal limits, and recommended operating conditions. It is the primary reference for circuit design and component selection.

**Four key parameters in the Absolute Maximum Ratings of a BJT (e.g., BC547):**

1. **$V_{CEO(max)}$** — Maximum collector-emitter voltage with base open = **45 V**
2. **$I_{C(max)}$** — Maximum continuous collector current = **100 mA**
3. **$P_{D(max)}$** — Maximum power dissipation at $T_A = 25\,°C$ = **500 mW**
4. **$T_{J(max)}$** — Maximum junction temperature = **150 °C**
5. *(Any four are acceptable)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the resistor color code for the bands Yellow-Violet-Orange-Gold. State the value with tolerance and the range of acceptable resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

- **1st band** Yellow = digit **4**
- **2nd band** Violet = digit **7**
- **3rd band** Orange = multiplier $\times 10^{3} = 1000$
- **4th band** Gold = tolerance $\pm 5\%$

$$
R = 47 \times 10^{3} \; \Omega = 47 \, k\Omega \pm 5\%
$$

$$
\Delta R = 47 \times 0.05 = 2.35 \, k\Omega
$$

$$
R_{min} = 44.65 \, k\Omega, \quad R_{max} = 49.35 \, k\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{44.65 \, k\Omega \leq R \leq 49.35 \, k\Omega}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 scheme mandates **internal choice** for every Part B question. Two alternatives are provided below; the student answers **either** A **or** B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the BIS/IEEE symbols for the following components and label their terminals: **(7 Marks)**
1. NPN BJT (e.g., BC547)
2. PNP BJT (e.g., BC557)
3. Zener Diode
4. N-channel Enhancement MOSFET
5. Electrolytic Capacitor
6. Variable Resistor (Potentiometer)
7. Op-Amp (single, e.g., LM741)

**(b)** A BJT (BC547) is used as a switch to drive a relay coil of 12 V / 100 mA. The datasheet gives $h_{FE(min)} = 110$. The microcontroller GPIO provides $V_{OH} = 5 \, V$ and can source $I_{OH(max)} = 20 \, mA$. The base resistor $R_B$ is connected from the GPIO to the base. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**
1. The minimum base current required to saturate the transistor.
2. The value of $R_B$ to ensure saturation.
3. Choose the nearest E12 standard value of $R_B$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Minimum base current to saturate.

$$
I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{h_{FE(min)}} = \frac{100 \, mA}{110} \approx 0.909 \, mA
$$

**Step 2:** Apply KVL: $V_{OH} = I_B \cdot R_B + V_{BE(on)}$

$$
R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE(on)}}{I_{B(min)}} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{0.909 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
R_B = \frac{4.3}{0.909 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 4730 \; \Omega = 4.73 \, k\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Nearest E12 value = **4.7 kΩ**

**Step 4: Sanity check** — GPIO source current: $I_{OH} = 4.3 / 4700 \approx 0.915 \, mA \ll 20 \, mA$ → Safe.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{I_{B(min)} \approx 0.91 \, mA, \quad R_B = 4.7 \, k\Omega \text{ (E12 standard)}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A datasheet for the **7805 voltage regulator** lists the following: $V_{in(max)} = 35 \, V$, $V_{out} = 5.0 \, V$ (typ), $I_{out(max)} = 1 \, A$, dropout voltage $V_{drop} = 2.0 \, V$, $R_{\theta JA} = 50 \, °C/W$, $T_{J(max)} = 125 \, °C$. The regulator is used with $V_{in} = 15 \, V$ and $I_{load} = 750 \, mA$ at $T_A = 45 \, °C$. Compute: **(7 Marks)**
1. Power dissipated in the regulator.
2. Junction temperature without a heatsink.
3. Whether a heatsink is required (justify).

**(b)** With reference to the BIS standard (IS 2032), list the symbols and explain the function of any four protective / signal indicators used in electronic schematics. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Power dissipation**

$$
P_{diss} = \left( V_{in} - V_{out} \right) \times I_{load} = \left( 15 - 5 \right) \times 0.75 = 7.5 \, W
$$

**Step 2: Junction temperature without heatsink**

$$
T_J = T_A + P_{diss} \times R_{\theta JA} = 45 + 7.5 \times 50 = 45 + 375 = 420 \, °C
$$

**Step 3: Comparison with $T_{J(max)}$**

$$
T_J = 420 \, °C \gg T_{J(max)} = 125 \, °C
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{A heatsink is MANDATORY. Without it, the device will thermally fail.}}
$$

**Step 4: Required total thermal resistance**

$$
R_{\theta JA(allowed)} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_{diss}} = \frac{125 - 45}{7.5} = 10.67 \, °C/W
$$

**Step 5: Required heatsink rating** (assuming $R_{\theta JC} = 5\,°C/W$, $R_{\theta CS} = 1\,°C/W$)

$$
R_{\theta HS} = 10.67 - 5 - 1 = 4.67 \, °C/W
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Choose a TO-220 heatsink rated at } 4 \, °C/W \text{ or lower.}}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Symbol | BIS Drawing | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuse** | Rectangular box with two leads and a wavy line inside | Protective device that breaks the circuit when current exceeds a rated value |
| **Lamp / Indicator** | Circle with a cross (✕) inside | Visual signal of power ON or fault condition |
| **Bell / Buzzer** | Dome-shaped semicircle with two leads | Audio signal indicator |
| **Earth Ground** | Three decreasing horizontal lines `⏚` | Safety connection to earth (chassis ground) |
| **Antenna** | Inverted triangle `▽` on a lead | Wireless signal input/output |
| **Speaker** | Rectangle with a triangular arrow inside | Audio output transducer |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing the resistor color code in the **wrong order** (e.g., forgetting to start from the band closest to the lead). Marks are **deducted fully** if the order is reversed.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the **multiplier band (3rd)** with the **tolerance band (4th/5th)**. Always remember: in a 4-band resistor, the **4th band is separated** (or gold/silver).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **$V_{CEO}$ vs $V_{CBO}$**. $V_{CEO}$ (collector-emitter, base open) is **always lower** than $V_{CBO}$ (collector-base, emitter open). Using the wrong one in design leads to transistor breakdown.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Drawing the **zener diode symbol** with the arrow pointing the wrong way (zener is reverse-biased in operation; the bar is the **cathode**, not anode).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In the 7805 problem, students often **forget to add a heatsink** or use $T_A = 25\,°C$ instead of the given ambient. Always use the value **given in the question**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Workshop Exam**

- ✅ **BIS = IS 2032** (Indian standard), **IEEE = IEEE Std 315** (American/global). Both are accepted; BIS is preferred in KTU papers.
- ✅ **Resistor symbol in India = Zig-zag**; **abroad = Rectangle**. Both are valid in KTU.
- ✅ **BJT arrow direction rule:** Arrow **OUT** = NPN, Arrow **IN** = PNP. (Mnemonic: "**N**PN — **N**ot Pointing i**N**")
- ✅ **Zener diode** = diode with a **Z-shaped bar** at the cathode. Always used in **reverse bias**.
- ✅ **MOSFET** has a **broken channel line** for enhancement type; **continuous line** for depletion type.
- ✅ **Polarized capacitor** symbol has a **curved plate (–)** and a **straight plate (+)**. The longer lead is +.
- ✅ **Resistor color code order** = Significant1, Significant2, Multiplier, Tolerance. Tolerance band is **gold or silver** (in 4-band) or **separated by a gap** (in 5-band).
- ✅ **Capacitor code** "104" means $10 \times 10^4 \, pF = 100 \, nF$, **NOT** 104 ohms.
- ✅ **Datasheet sections to read first:** (1) Absolute Maximum Ratings, (2) Pin Configuration, (3) Electrical Characteristics.
- ✅ **NEVER exceed absolute maximum ratings** — not even briefly. The ratings are for continuous operation.
- ✅ **Heat sink rule:** $P_{diss} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_{load}$ for linear regulators. Always check $T_J$ vs $T_{J(max)}$.
- ✅ **Voltage regulator minimum input** = $V_{out} + V_{drop}$. For 7805, this is at least **7 V**, ideally **9–12 V** for efficiency.
- ✅ **In schematics, never cross wires without a dot** (junction) or a **jump arc** (no-connection).
- ✅ **Reference designators** follow a standard: R = Resistor, C = Capacitor, L = Inductor, D = Diode, Q = Transistor, U = IC, SW = Switch, F = Fuse, J = Connector, T = Transformer.
- ✅ **Net labels** (`5V`, `GND`, `CLK`) replace long wires for clarity — preferred for KTU diagrams.
- ✅ For **KTU record book**, always include: **Title, Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Circuit Diagram (with symbols), Procedure, Observation, Result, Conclusion.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation and application of testing instruments and commonly used tools (Multimeter, Function generator, Power supply, DSO, Soldering iron, Desoldering pump, Pliers, Cutters, Wire strippers, Screw drivers, Tweezers, Crimping tool, Hot air soldering and de-soldering station)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation and application of testing instruments and commonly used tools (Multimeter, Function generator, Power supply, DSO, Soldering iron, Desoldering pump, Pliers, Cutters, Wire strippers, Screw drivers, Tweezers, Crimping tool, Hot air soldering and de-soldering station)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Testing Instruments and Workshop Tools

**Testing Instruments** are precision electronic devices designed to measure, generate, monitor, or display electrical quantities such as voltage, current, resistance, frequency, and waveforms. They are the *eyes and ears* of an electronics engineer — without them, circuits become invisible.

**Workshop Tools** are hand-operated or semi-automatic devices used for fabrication, assembly, repair, and modification of electronic circuits and electrical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment trains the student to *recognise, select, and correctly apply* each instrument/tool for a given electrical or electronics task. Familiarisation (knowing parts, controls, ratings) and application (using them to measure/build/fault-find) are the two assessment outcomes evaluated in the lab viva and record.

### 1.2 Classification of Equipment

The equipment listed in the syllabus is grouped into **two broad functional categories**:

| Category | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Measurement / Test Instruments** | Quantify electrical parameters | Multimeter, Function Generator, Power Supply, DSO |
| **Fabrication / Assembly Tools** | Physically build/repair circuits | Soldering iron, Desoldering pump, Pliers, Cutters, Wire strippers, Screw drivers, Tweezers, Crimping tool, Hot air station |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Toolkit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an electronics lab as a *doctor's clinic*. The **Multimeter** is the stethoscope (checks vital signs — V, I, R). The **Function Generator** is the prescription (delivers controlled stimuli — sine, square, ramp). The **Power Supply** is the IV drip (provides life-sustaining energy at controlled levels). The **DSO** is the MRI scan (visualises the hidden waveform with time precision). The **Soldering Iron** is the scalpel (precise cutting and joining of metal), and the **Pliers/Cutters/Strippers** are the supporting surgical instruments. Without knowing what each tool does, even the best doctor cannot cure the patient.

### 1.4 Standard Safety and Rating Metrics

- **Voltage Rating:** AC up to **750 V**, DC up to **1000 V** (typical CAT-III DMM)
- **Current Rating:** up to **10 A** continuous, **20 A** for 30 s
- **Soldering Iron Temperature:** **200 °C – 480 °C** (typically set to **350 °C** for leaded solder, **380 °C** for lead-free)
- **Hot Air Station Temperature:** **100 °C – 500 °C** with airflow **1–10 L/min**
- **DSO Bandwidth:** **50 MHz – 200 MHz** for general-purpose labs
- **Insulation:** **1000 V** double-insulated probes

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Mandate:** Always start on the *highest range* of an unknown quantity, then step down. Never measure resistance on a powered circuit. Never use a soldering iron near flammable materials.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Digital Multimeter (DMM)

**Working Principle:** A DMM uses an *integrated ADC* (typically dual-slope type) to convert analog inputs into digital readings. A rotary switch routes the input through appropriate *shunt resistors* (for current), *voltage dividers* (for voltage), or *constant-current source* (for resistance measurement via Ohm's law).

**Key Specifications:**
- **Display:** 3½ digit (1999 counts) or 4½ digit (19999 counts)
- **Resolution:** 1 mV, 0.1 Ω, 0.1 µA
- **Input Impedance:** **10 MΩ** (voltage mode — prevents circuit loading)
- **Accuracy:** ±(0.5% rdg + 2 dgt)

**Applications:** Continuity test, diode test, transistor hFE test, voltage/current/resistance measurement, capacitance (in advanced DMMs).

### 2.2 Function Generator

**Working Principle:** Based on the **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** architecture, a function generator produces sine, square, triangle, ramp, and arbitrary waveforms by reading pre-stored digital samples from a lookup table at a clock-controlled rate. The output frequency is:

$$f_{out} = \frac{f_{clock}}{2^N} \times M$$

where $N$ is the accumulator bit-width and $M$ is the frequency tuning word.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Frequency Range:** **0.1 Hz to 20 MHz** (typical lab unit)
- **Amplitude:** **10 mVpp to 20 Vpp** (open circuit), **50 Ω** output impedance
- **Waveforms:** Sine, Square, Triangle, Ramp, TTL/CMOS sync
- **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion):** < **1%** for sine

**Applications:** Testing amplifier frequency response, Bode plots, PWM generation, clock signals, filter characterisation.

### 2.3 DC Power Supply (RPS — Regulated Power Supply)

**Working Principle:** A linear DC power supply follows the sequence: *Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator → Output*. A series-pass transistor (or IC regulator like **LM317**, **LM7805**) maintains a constant output despite input/load variations. Modern lab supplies use *SMPS topology* for higher efficiency.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Output Voltage:** **0 – 30 V** variable (single or dual tracking)
- **Current Limit:** **0 – 5 A** (adjustable CC/CV mode)
- **Ripple:** < **1 mVrms**
- **Line/Load Regulation:** < **0.01%**

**Applications:** Powering breadboard circuits, charging batteries, component testing, providing reference voltages.

### 2.4 Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)

**Working Principle:** A DSO samples the input signal using a high-speed **ADC**, stores the digitised values in memory, and reconstructs the waveform on an LCD. The *sample rate* $f_s$ must obey the **Nyquist criterion**:

$$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$$

In practice, $f_s \geq 5 \times f_{max}$ for faithful reproduction. Triggering (edge, pulse, video, logic) synchronises the sweep.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Bandwidth:** **100 MHz** (typical)
- **Sample Rate:** **1 GSa/s** (Giga-Samples per second)
- **Memory Depth:** **1 Mpts** per channel
- **Channels:** **2 or 4**

**Applications:** Waveform observation, time/frequency measurements, rise-time analysis, signal integrity debugging, FFT (spectrum) analysis.

### 2.5 Soldering Iron and Hot Air Station

**Soldering Iron:** A *resistive heating element* (nichrome/cermet) raises the tip temperature to melt solder (a tin-lead or lead-free alloy with flux core). Heat transfer follows:

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

where $m$ is the joint mass, $c$ is specific heat of solder, $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise.

**Hot Air Station:** A brushless blower drives heated air through a nozzle. Used for **SMD rework** — reflowing solder paste on multi-pin components (QFP, BGA, SOIC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lead-Free Solder (SAC305 — Sn 96.5%, Ag 3%, Cu 0.5%):** Melting point **217 °C** — hence higher iron temperature (~**380 °C**). Leaded (Sn-Pb 60/40): Melting point **183 °C** — iron at **350 °C**.

### 2.6 Hand Tools — Quick Reference

| Tool | Primary Use | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Pliers (Needle-nose)** | Bending wires, holding components | Long tapered jaws |
| **Cutters (Diagonal / Flush)** | Cutting component leads, trimming wires | Sharpened angled jaws |
| **Wire Strippers** | Removing insulation without nicking conductor | Sized notches (AWG 10–30) |
| **Screw Drivers (Phillips / Slotted / Torx)** | Fastening screws in enclosures, PCBs | Insulated handles (1000 V) |
| **Tweezers (ESD-safe)** | Placing SMD components, holding small parts | Anti-static coating |
| **Crimping Tool** | Attaching lugs, connectors to wires | Ratcheting mechanism |
| **Desoldering Pump (Solder Sucker)** | Removing molten solder from through-hole joints | Spring-loaded plunger, Teflon tip |
| **Desoldering Braid (Wick)** | Removing solder via capillary action | Fine copper braid with flux |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any numerical/viva question.**

| S.No. | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = I \times R$ | Ohm's Law (Multimeter basis) | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | $P = V \times I = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Power dissipation | W |
| 3 | $f_{out} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{2^N} \times M$ | DDS output frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 5 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{T}{div} \times div_{count}$ | Time interval from DSO | s |
| 6 | $V_{pp} = 2\sqrt{2} \times V_{rms}$ | Peak-peak to RMS (sine) | V |
| 7 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | Average of full-wave rectified sine | V |
| 8 | $V_{ripple} = \dfrac{I_{load}}{f \times C}$ | Power supply ripple (single-stage) | V |
| 9 | $V_{out} = 1.25 \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | LM317 adjustable regulator | V |
| 10 | $T_{rise} = \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ | Rise time from oscilloscope bandwidth | s |

> **Note on LaTeX Safety:** Vertical bars inside tables are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX source to avoid breaking markdown rendering. For example, $|V_{out}|$ is written as `\vert V_{out} \vert` when appearing inside a table cell.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multimeter** → Field servicing of power systems, PCB troubleshooting
- **Function Generator** → Communication lab, IoT sensor excitation, audio testing
- **Power Supply** → Every R&D bench, product validation, battery simulation
- **DSO** → Embedded firmware bring-up, RF/microwave analysis, power electronics
- **Soldering/Hot Air** → PCB prototyping, mass production rework, SMD repair
- **Hand Tools** → Cable harness assembly (automotive/aerospace), enclosure build

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DSO Time and Voltage Measurement from Display

**Problem:** A DSO displays a square wave with the *Time/Div* knob set to **2 µs/div** and the wave occupies **4 horizontal divisions** for one complete cycle. The *Volts/Div* is set to **5 V/div** and the wave peak-to-peak occupies **3 vertical divisions**. Calculate the frequency and peak-to-peak voltage.

**Step 1 — Period of the waveform:**

$$T = (\text{Time/div}) \times (\text{number of horizontal divisions})$$

$$T = 2 \, \mu s/div \times 4 \, div = 8 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-6}} \, Hz$$

$$f = 0.125 \times 10^{6} \, Hz = 125 \, kHz$$

**Step 3 — Peak-to-Peak Voltage:**

$$V_{pp} = (\text{Volts/div}) \times (\text{number of vertical divisions})$$

$$V_{pp} = 5 \, V/div \times 3 \, div = 15 \, V$$

**Step 4 — RMS Voltage (for sine conversion if it were a sine):**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{15}{2.828} = 5.30 \, V$$

This systematic *div × setting* method is the **only** accepted KTU valuation step for any DSO-based question.

### 3.2 Derivation: LM317 Variable Power Supply Output

**Problem:** Design an LM317-based adjustable supply with $R_1 = 240 \, \Omega$ and $R_2 = 720 \, \Omega$. What is the output voltage?

**Step 1 — Standard LM317 output equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \left( 1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1} \right) + I_{adj} \times R_2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the typical reference voltage $V_{ref} = 1.25 \, V$ and $I_{adj} \approx 50 \, \mu A$ (negligible across 720 Ω):**

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \left( 1 + \frac{720}{240} \right) + (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 720$$

**Step 3 — Compute the main term:**

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \times (1 + 3) + 0.036$$

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \times 4 + 0.036$$

$$V_{out} = 5.0 + 0.036 = 5.036 \, V$$

**Step 4 — Result:** The supply outputs approximately **5.04 V**, ideal for digital logic TTL rails.

### 3.3 Derivation: Soldering Iron Joint Quality Heat Calculation

**Problem:** A solder joint uses **0.5 g** of tin-lead solder to join a component lead. The iron is at **350 °C** and the joint reaches **200 °C**. Specific heat of solder $c = 176 \, J/(kg \cdot °C)$. Find the heat energy required.

**Step 1 — Heat equation:**

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass to kg:**

$$m = 0.5 \, g = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, kg = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, kg$$

**Step 3 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = 350 - 200 = 150 \, °C$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$Q = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 176 \times 150$$

$$Q = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 26400$$

$$Q = 13.2 \, J$$

**Step 5 — Practical implication:** A 40 W iron supplies **40 J/s**, so this joint's heat requirement is met in **0.33 s** — but allowing for thermal losses, *typical joint time is 2–4 s*.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Tool for a Task

| Engineering Task | Correct Instrument / Tool | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Measure DC voltage of a 9 V battery | **DMM (DC V range)** | High input impedance (10 MΩ), accurate |
| Observe the shape of a 50 kHz square wave | **DSO (AC coupled, 100 MHz BW)** | Time-domain waveform, Nyquist satisfied |
| Generate a 1 kHz sine wave for audio test | **Function Generator** | Continuous wave, low THD |
| Power a microcontroller at 5 V | **RPS (constant voltage mode)** | Stable, low ripple, current-limited |
| Remove a faulty through-hole IC | **Desoldering Pump + Soldering Iron** | Melts solder, then vacuum extracts it |
| Place an 0805 resistor on a PCB | **ESD-safe Tweezers** | Fine tip, anti-static |
| Cut a component lead flush with PCB | **Diagonal Cutter (flush type)** | Shear action, minimal stress |
| Strip 1.5 mm² copper wire | **Wire Stripper (AWG 16 notch)** | Sized for the conductor |
| Attach a spade lug to a 4 mm² wire | **Crimping Tool + Ratchet** | Gas-tight, vibration-proof joint |
| Rework a 64-pin QFP microcontroller | **Hot Air SMD Rework Station** | Even heat, no direct contact |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Multimeter Resistance Measurement (Four-Wire vs Two-Wire)

**Two-Wire Method (DMM internal):**

$$R_{measured} = R_{lead1} + R_{DUT} + R_{lead2}$$

**Four-Wire (Kelvin) Method — for low resistances < 1 Ω:**

$$V_{sense} = I_{source} \times R_{DUT}$$

$$R_{DUT} = \frac{V_{sense}}{I_{source}}$$

The voltage-sensing leads carry negligible current, eliminating lead resistance error.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Digital Multimeter Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Input Signal V or I or R] --> nodeB[Function Selector Rotary Switch]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Attenuator or Shunt Network]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Integrated ADC Dual Slope]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Microcontroller Decoder]
    nodeE --> nodeF[LCD or 7 Segment Display]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Reference Voltage Source]
    nodeG --> nodeD
    nodeH[Battery 9V] --> nodeI[Internal DC DC Converter]
    nodeI --> nodeD
```

> The **attenuator/shunt network** scales the input to ADC range. The **dual-slope ADC** integrates the input for a fixed time, then de-integrates against a reference — giving excellent noise rejection at mains frequency (50/60 Hz).

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Function Generator (DDS-Based)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Reference Crystal Oscillator] --> nodeB[Phase Accumulator N bit]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Lookup Table ROM Sine Samples]
    nodeC --> nodeD[DAC Digital to Analog]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Reconstruction Filter Low Pass]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Output Amplifier and Attenuator]
    nodeF --> nodeG[BNC Output Connector 50 Ohm]
    nodeH[Front Panel Frequency and Amplitude Knobs] --> nodeB
    nodeH --> nodeF
```

> The **Phase Accumulator** increments by the tuning word $M$ each clock cycle. Modulo-$2^N$ overflow gives the wave's phase wrap. The **Lookup Table** converts phase to amplitude, which the **DAC** converts to analog.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Linear Regulated DC Power Supply

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> nodeB[Step Down Transformer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Bridge Rectifier 4 Diodes]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Smoothing Capacitor C]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Series Pass Regulator LM317]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Output 0 to 30V Variable]
    nodeG[Reference Divider Resistors] --> nodeE
    nodeH[Current Sense Resistor] --> nodeI[Current Limit Comparator]
    nodeI --> nodeE
    nodeJ[Output Sampling] --> nodeI
```

> The **Current Limit Comparator** monitors the shunt voltage; if it exceeds ~0.6 V, the regulator is forced into constant-current (CC) mode — protecting the load.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: DSO Signal Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Input BNC Connector] --> nodeB[Attenuator Probe Comp Network]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Vertical Amplifier]
    nodeC --> nodeD[High Speed ADC]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Acquisition Memory FIFO]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Trigger Circuit]
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG[Display Processor and Waveform Reconstruction]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Color LCD Display]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Measurement Engine Vrms Freq Period Rise Time]
```

> The **trigger circuit** detects a user-defined event (rising edge at 1.5 V) and synchronises memory write so the waveform is stable on screen.

### 4.5 Workflow Diagram: Soldering Operation Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Start] --> nodeB[Clean Tip on Wet Sponge]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Tin the Tip Apply Solder]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Heat Joint Pad and Lead Simultaneously]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Solder to Opposite Side of Joint]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Solder Flows and Wets Both Surfaces}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Remove Solder Then Iron]
    nodeF -->|No| nodeH[Add Flux and Continue Heating]
    nodeH --> nodeF
    nodeG --> nodeI[Hold Joint Still Until Solid]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Inspect for Shiny Concave Fillet]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[End]
```

### 4.6 Tool Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Task Identification] --> nodeB{Measure Quantify Test or Fabricate Build}
    nodeB -->|Measure| nodeC[Quantity Type]
    nodeC -->|Voltage Current Resistance| nodeD[Multimeter]
    nodeC -->|Waveform Time Frequency| nodeE[DSO]
    nodeC -->|Generate Signal| nodeF[Function Generator]
    nodeB -->|Fabricate| nodeG[Action Type]
    nodeG -->|Join Solder| nodeH[Iron or Hot Air]
    nodeG -->|Cut Strip| nodeI[Cutters or Strippers]
    nodeG -->|Hold Place| nodeJ[Pliers or Tweezers]
    nodeG -->|Fasten Crimp| nodeK[Screwdriver or Crimper]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Always *measure before you fabricate*. Verify supply rails with the DMM before powering a board. Use the DSO to confirm the function generator's actual output amplitude — never trust the front-panel readout for critical values.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]** *List the major controls on the front panel of a Digital Multimeter and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**
A typical DMM front panel includes:
1. **Rotary Selector Switch** — selects the function (V, A, Ω, Hz, diode, continuity) and range.
2. **Display (LCD)** — 3½ or 4½ digit readout of the measured value.
3. **COM (Common) Terminal** — negative/ground probe input (black lead).
4. **V-Ω-Hz Terminal** — positive probe input for voltage, resistance, frequency (red lead).
5. **10 A or 20 A Terminal** — dedicated high-current input with internal fuse.
6. **HOLD Button** — freezes the current reading on the display.
7. **RANGE Button** — toggles between auto and manual range selection.
**(3 marks – all seven points)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the difference between a soldering iron and a hot-air soldering station? When is each preferred?*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Soldering Iron | Hot-Air Station |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Transfer** | Direct contact via metallic tip | Convected heated air through nozzle |
| **Best For** | Through-hole, single-pin SMD, wires | Multi-pin SMD (QFP, SOIC, BGA) |
| **Precision** | Very high (tip size controllable) | Moderate (nozzle size matters) |
| **Risk** | Lifted pads if overheated | Blowing adjacent components away |
| **Typical Use** | General lab assembly | Rework / reflow / desoldering SMD |

A soldering iron is preferred for **through-hole joints and single SMD pads**; a hot-air station is essential for **SMD ICs with many pins**. **(3 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**PART B — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> *a) (7 marks) With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a Digital Storage Oscilloscope. List its key specifications and state two applications.*
>
> *b) (7 marks) A square wave of 50 kHz frequency is displayed on a DSO with the Time/Div set to 5 µs/div. How many horizontal divisions will one complete cycle occupy? If the peak-to-peak voltage reads 4 divisions at 0.5 V/div, calculate the RMS voltage assuming the signal is sinusoidal in shape.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of a DSO — Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):**

The block diagram was shown in Section 4.4. The description:

1. **Input & Attenuator:** The signal enters via a BNC probe. The attenuator network reduces high voltages to ADC-safe levels; the probe's compensation capacitor is adjusted to flatten frequency response. **(1 mark)**
2. **Vertical Amplifier:** Amplifies the attenuated signal to span the ADC's input range. **(1 mark)**
3. **High-Speed ADC:** Samples the analog waveform at rate $f_s$ (e.g., 1 GSa/s). Nyquist requires $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. **(1 mark)**
4. **Acquisition Memory:** Digital samples are stored in a FIFO buffer (e.g., 1 Mpts depth). **(0.5 marks)**
5. **Trigger Circuit:** Detects a chosen event (rising/falling edge, pulse width) and synchronises the start of memory write — so the wave is stable on screen. **(1 mark)**
6. **Display Processor:** Reads memory, scales, and paints the waveform on the LCD; also drives the measurement engine. **(0.5 marks)**
7. **Key Specifications:** Bandwidth (100 MHz), Sample Rate (1 GSa/s), Memory Depth (1 Mpts), Channels (2/4), Display (color TFT). **(1 mark)**
8. **Applications:** (i) Time and frequency measurement of periodic signals. (ii) Observing transient glitches and rise-time analysis. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Period of 50 kHz wave:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^3} = 20 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Divisions occupied at 5 µs/div:**

$$\text{Divisions} = \frac{T}{\text{Time/div}} = \frac{20 \, \mu s}{5 \, \mu s/div} = 4 \, divisions$$

**Step 3 — Peak-to-Peak Voltage:**

$$V_{pp} = 0.5 \, V/div \times 4 \, div = 2.0 \, V$$

**Step 4 — Peak Voltage:**

$$V_m = \frac{V_{pp}}{2} = 1.0 \, V$$

**Step 5 — RMS Voltage (sine):**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.0}{1.414} = 0.707 \, V$$

**Final Answer:** **4 divisions per cycle; $V_{rms} = 0.707 \, V$**. **(7 marks — 1 each for steps 1–5 plus 2 for clear presentation and correct units)**

---

**PART B — Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> *a) (7 marks) Explain the working principle of a Regulated DC Power Supply with a neat block diagram. Define Line Regulation and Load Regulation.*
>
> *b) (7 marks) With reference to a soldering station, list the temperature settings for (i) lead-based solder, (ii) lead-free solder, (iii) desoldering. State the safety precautions to be observed while using a soldering iron.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Regulated DC Power Supply (7 marks):**

The block diagram was shown in Section 4.3. The description:

1. **Transformer:** Steps down 230 V AC mains to a low AC voltage (e.g., 18 V). Provides isolation. **(1 mark)**
2. **Bridge Rectifier:** Four diodes in bridge configuration perform full-wave rectification. **(1 mark)**
3. **Filter Capacitor:** Large electrolytic (e.g., 2200 µF) smooths the rectified DC; reduces ripple to < 1 mV. **(1 mark)**
4. **Series-Pass Regulator (LM317 / 7805):** Adjusts its conduction to maintain constant $V_{out}$ despite line or load variations. **(1 mark)**
5. **Feedback Network:** Samples $V_{out}$ and compares with internal reference (1.25 V for LM317). **(1 mark)**
6. **Current Limiting Circuit:** Senses current via shunt resistor; folds back to CC mode on overload. **(1 mark)**
7. **Definitions:** **(1 mark)**
   - **Line Regulation** = $\dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$ — output stability against input variation.
   - **Load Regulation** = $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ — output stability against load current change.

**(b) Soldering Station — Temperatures and Safety (7 marks):**

| Task | Solder Type | Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Lead-based Sn-Pb (60/40) | Eutectic | **300 – 350 °C** |
| (ii) Lead-free SAC305 | Sn-Ag-Cu | **370 – 400 °C** |
| (iii) Desoldering (through-hole) | Any | **350 – 380 °C** with active suction |

**(1 mark each row)**

**Safety Precautions (4 marks):**
1. Wear safety goggles and use an **ESD wrist strap** to prevent component damage. **(0.5)**
2. Ensure **fume extraction** is on — lead and flux fumes are toxic. **(0.5)**
3. **Never** touch the tip (reaches 400 °C); always return the iron to its stand. **(0.5)**
4. Disconnect power before replacing tips; allow to cool naturally — do not quench. **(0.5)**
5. Work in a **well-ventilated area**; do not solder on live circuits. **(0.5)**
6. Keep the iron tip **tinned** (coated with solder) when not in use to prevent oxidation. **(0.5)**
7. Dispose of lead-contaminated sponges and dross in **hazardous waste** bins. **(0.5)**
8. Never solder a battery directly — risk of explosion. **(0.5)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in Lab Record & Viva:**
> 1. **Confusing RMS and Peak Values** — Students often quote $V_m$ instead of $V_{rms}$ when asked for "voltage" on the DSO. Always clarify which one.
> 2. **Wrong Probe Compensation** — A poorly compensated 10× probe shows distorted square waves. Examiners specifically test this in the practical.
> 3. **Using Multimeter on Ohms with Power ON** — Instant damage to the meter fuse and false readings. Examiners deduct **full marks** for procedural errors.
> 4. **Skipping the Block Diagram** — Drawing a labelled block diagram is worth **2 of 7 marks** in any "explain the working" question.
> 5. **Mixing up Through-Hole and SMD Soldering Temperatures** — Examiners test this frequently; remember **350 °C for leaded, 380 °C for lead-free**.
> 6. **Not stating units** — A correct number without **V, A, Hz, °C** loses half a mark per quantity.
> 7. **Forgetting the safety/precaution paragraph** — The 2024 Scheme rubric includes a *dedicated* line for safety practices; missing it means losing 1–2 marks even with a perfect answer.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorise before lab exam):**
> - **DMM** = 10 MΩ input impedance, never measure Ω on powered circuit.
> - **Function Generator** = 50 Ω output impedance, voltage halves when terminated with 50 Ω load.
> - **Power Supply** = Constant Voltage (CV) and Constant Current (CC) modes; **LM317** → 1.25 V reference.
> - **DSO** = $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist); rise time = 0.35/BW.
> - **Soldering Iron** = 350 °C (leaded), 380 °C (lead-free); tin the tip before and after.
> - **Hot Air Station** = 100–500 °C, use correct nozzle size; preheat nearby components with aluminium foil shield.
> - **Desoldering Pump** = prime the spring; press the plunger *before* heating the joint.
> - **Wire Strippers** = match the AWG notch to the wire gauge; never nick the conductor.
> - **Crimping Tool** = ratchet must release only at correct pressure; cycle the tool each time.
> - **Tweezers** = ESD-safe, anti-magnetic (for ferrous components).
> - **Screwdrivers** = insulated to 1000 V; correct head type (Phillips #1, #2 / Slotted 3 mm, 5 mm).
> - **Pliers** = needle-nose for lead bending, side-cutters for trimming.
> - **General safety**: Mains OFF for resistance measurement; ESD strap ON for ICs; goggles ON for soldering.

> **Final KTU Tip:** In the lab record, *always* include the equipment used (model & specification), the procedure, a labelled block diagram, observation table, sample calculation, and the result with units. The 2024 Scheme evaluation explicitly maps marks to each of these sections.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of electronic components using multimeter (Resistor, Capacitor, Diode, Transistor, JFET)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Testing of electronic components using multimeter (Resistor, Capacitor, Diode, Transistor, JFET)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Digital Multimeter (DMM)?

A **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** is a precision electronic measuring instrument that combines multiple measurement functions—voltage (V), current (A), and resistance (Ω)—into a single handheld unit. In the context of the KTU 2024 workshop, the DMM is used as the primary diagnostic tool for verifying the health, polarity, and electrical characteristics of discrete electronic components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A DMM can measure **DC Voltage (VDC)**, **AC Voltage (VAC)**, **DC Current (IDC)**, **Resistance (R)**, **Continuity**, **Capacitance (C)**, and can test **Diodes** and **Transistors** using specialized modes.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor with a stethoscope** checking a patient's health. A multimeter acts as the **"stethoscope of an electronics engineer."** Just as a doctor checks heartbeat, blood pressure, and reflexes, the multimeter checks whether a resistor is "breathing" the right resistance, whether a diode "allows blood (current) to flow in one direction only," and whether a capacitor is "storing and releasing energy properly." Without this tool, an engineer would be working "blind" inside a circuit.

---

### 1.2 Electronic Components Under Test — Formal Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> The following definitions are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** workshop vocabulary.

#### 1.2.1 Resistor
A **Resistor** is a passive two-terminal electronic component that implements electrical resistance as a circuit element. It restricts the flow of current in accordance with **Ohm's Law** (V = IR).

#### 1.2.2 Capacitor
A **Capacitor** is a passive two-terminal component that stores electrical energy in an electric field. Its capacitance **C** is measured in **Farads (F)**, and it blocks DC while passing AC (in ideal conditions).

#### 1.2.3 Diode
A **Diode** is a semiconductor device with two terminals (anode and cathode) that conducts current primarily in **one direction** (forward bias) and blocks it in the opposite direction (reverse bias). It exhibits a typical forward voltage drop of **0.7 V (Silicon)** or **0.3 V (Germanium)**.

#### 1.2.4 Transistor (BJT)
A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal semiconductor device (Emitter, Base, Collector) used for amplification or switching. Two principal types: **NPN** and **PNP**. It contains two PN junctions.

#### 1.2.5 JFET (Junction Field-Effect Transistor)
A **JFET** is a three-terminal semiconductor device (Gate, Drain, Source) that controls current flow using an electric field. The three terminals are **Gate (G)**, **Drain (D)**, and **Source (S)**. JFETs are voltage-controlled, high-input-impedance devices.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Voltage Drop (Si Diode) | $V_F$ | **≈ 0.7 V** |
| Forward Voltage Drop (Ge Diode) | $V_F$ | **≈ 0.3 V** |
| Standard DMM Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | **10 MΩ** |
| BJT Base-Emitter Voltage | $V_{BE}$ | **≈ 0.7 V** |
| JFET Gate-Source Cutoff Voltage | $V_{GS(off)}$ | **Typically −1 V to −10 V (N-channel)** |
| Ohmic Value of Healthy Resistor | $R$ | **Read directly in Ohms (Ω)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A good resistor shows the **same value** when measured from either terminal. A good diode shows **low resistance in one direction** and **high resistance (OL) in the other**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principle Behind Multimeter Testing

The DMM testing of components relies on injecting a **small, known test current** or **voltage** from the multimeter's internal battery and measuring the resulting response. For example:
- In **resistance mode**, the DMM applies a small DC voltage and measures the current using **Ohm's Law** to compute resistance.
- In **diode mode**, the DMM applies enough current to forward-bias the junction and displays the forward voltage drop.

---

### 2.2 Resistor Testing — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Disconnect the resistor from the circuit (to avoid parallel paths).
**Step 2:** Set the DMM to **Ω (resistance) mode** and select the appropriate range.
**Step 3:** Connect the **red probe** to one terminal and the **black probe** to the other.
**Step 4:** Read the displayed value and compare it with the **color-coded nominal value** (with tolerance).

> [!NOTE]
> If the displayed value is **∞ (OL)** → the resistor is **open (burnt)**. If the displayed value is **0 Ω** → the resistor is **shorted**.

#### Color Code Identification (Standard 4-Band Resistor)

The resistance value is calculated as:
$$R = (A \times 10 + B) \times 10^C \, \Omega$$

Where:
- **A** = 1st band (first significant digit)
- **B** = 2nd band (second significant digit)
- **C** = 3rd band (multiplier as power of 10)
- **4th band** = Tolerance (±5% = Gold, ±10% = Silver)

---

### 2.3 Capacitor Testing — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** **Discharge** the capacitor completely by shorting its leads (important for safety and accurate reading).
**Step 2:** Set the DMM to **capacitance mode (symbol: ⊣⊢)**.
**Step 3:** Connect the probes across the capacitor terminals.
**Step 4:** Read the value and compare with the **rated value** (with tolerance, typically ±20% for electrolytic).

#### Charging Behavior Test (for analog DMM)
If a capacitance-mode DMM is unavailable, an analog multimeter in high-resistance mode can be used:
- A **good capacitor** initially shows a **low resistance** that **gradually rises to infinity (∞)**.
- A **shorted capacitor** shows **constant 0 Ω**.
- An **open capacitor** shows **immediate ∞ (no charging curve)**.

---

### 2.4 Diode Testing — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Set the DMM to **diode mode (symbol: ▶|)**.
**Step 2:** Connect the **red (+) probe to the anode** and the **black (−) probe to the cathode** → this is the **forward bias** condition.
**Step 3:** Read the **forward voltage drop**:
- **Silicon diode:** **0.5 V to 0.8 V** (typically **0.7 V**)
- **Germanium diode:** **0.2 V to 0.4 V** (typically **0.3 V**)
**Step 4:** Reverse the probes → DMM should display **OL (open loop / over limit)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A good diode shows **low forward voltage drop in one direction** and **OL in the reverse direction**. A **shorted diode** shows **≈ 0 V in both directions**. An **open diode** shows **OL in both directions**.

---

### 2.5 Transistor (BJT) Testing — Step-by-Step Logic

A BJT contains **two PN junctions** (Base-Emitter and Base-Collector). Therefore, it can be treated as **two diodes** during testing.

**Step 1:** Identify the **type (NPN or PNP)** and the **leads (E, B, C)** using the DMM's diode mode.
**Step 2:** Test the **Base-Emitter (BE) junction**:
- **NPN:** Red to Base, Black to Emitter → Forward drop ≈ **0.7 V**
- **PNP:** Black to Base, Red to Emitter → Forward drop ≈ **0.7 V**
**Step 3:** Test the **Base-Collector (BC) junction** (similar to above).
**Step 4:** Test the **Collector-Emitter (CE) junction** in both directions → should show **OL** (no conduction in both polarities for a healthy BJT in the absence of base current).

#### Identification Trick for BJT
- The **Base** is the **common terminal** of the two internal diodes.
- The terminal that gives **two forward-biased readings** when the red probe is placed on it is the **Base of an NPN transistor**.
- The terminal that gives **two forward-biased readings** when the black probe is placed on it is the **Base of a PNP transistor**.

---

### 2.6 JFET Testing — Step-by-Step Logic

A JFET has three terminals: **Gate (G)**, **Drain (D)**, and **Source (S)**. The Gate is connected to the channel through a **reverse-biased PN junction**.

**Step 1:** Set the DMM to **diode mode**.
**Step 2:** Identify the **Gate** terminal:
- The **Gate-to-Channel** junction behaves like a diode.
- One forward-biased reading and one reverse-biased (OL) reading reveals the Gate pin.
**Step 3:** The remaining two pins are the **Drain and Source**. Most JFETs have a **symmetrical Drain-Source path**, so it is hard to differentiate D from S by simple diode test alone. The DMM should show a **low resistance (or a small voltage drop)** between D and S in both directions.
**Step 4:** Test the **Gate-to-Source (GS)** and **Gate-to-Drain (GD)** junctions:
- One direction: **OL (reverse bias)**
- Other direction: **Forward voltage drop ≈ 0.5 V to 0.7 V**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the JFET with a BJT. Remember — a JFET has a **very high Gate-to-Source resistance** in the reverse direction, while a BJT's Collector-Emitter shows OL only because it requires base current.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | Test Mode | Expected Reading (Forward) | Expected Reading (Reverse) | Diagnosis - Good | Diagnosis - Bad |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Resistor** | Ω mode | Matches color code ± tolerance | Same (polarity-free) | Within tolerance | OL = Open, 0 = Shorted |
| **Capacitor (DMM with C mode)** | Capacitance mode | Matches rated value ± tolerance | Same | Within tolerance | OL = Open, 0 = Shorted |
| **Diode (Si)** | Diode mode | **0.5 V to 0.8 V** | **OL** | One-way conduction | 0V both = Shorted, OL both = Open |
| **Diode (Ge)** | Diode mode | **0.2 V to 0.4 V** | **OL** | One-way conduction | 0V both = Shorted, OL both = Open |
| **BJT (NPN)** | Diode mode | **0.7 V (B-E, B-C)** | **OL (E-B, C-B), OL (C-E)** | Two-diode behavior | Shorts or opens |
| **BJT (PNP)** | Diode mode | **0.7 V (E-B, C-B)** | **OL (B-E, B-C), OL (C-E)** | Two-diode behavior | Shorts or opens |
| **JFET (N-channel)** | Diode mode | **≈ 0.5 V to 0.7 V (G-S, G-D)** | **OL (S-G, D-G)** | Diode + high-Ω D-S | Shorts or opens |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU valuation key** often awards marks for **stating the expected numerical value** (e.g., "0.7 V for silicon diode") along with the correct interpretation.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB Repair & Troubleshooting:** Multimeter-based component testing is the **first-line diagnostic** for identifying burnt/failed components on printed circuit boards.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** Production lines use automated DMM-based testing to **bin resistors and verify diodes** before soldering.
- **Educational Labs:** Helps students visualize **PN junction behavior**, **forward/reverse bias**, and **semiconductor physics** without expensive curve tracers.
- **Field Service:** Every electronics technician carries a DMM as a **universal diagnostic instrument**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resistor Color Code to Numerical Value

**Given:** A resistor with color bands: **Yellow – Violet – Red – Gold**

**Step 1:** Identify each band's numerical meaning.
- **Yellow = 4** (1st digit)
- **Violet = 7** (2nd digit)
- **Red = 2** (multiplier = $10^2$)
- **Gold = ±5%** (tolerance)

**Step 2:** Apply the standard resistor value formula.

$$R = (A \times 10 + B) \times 10^C$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= (4 \times 10 + 7) \times 10^{2} \, \Omega \\
R &= (40 + 7) \times 100 \, \Omega \\
R &= 47 \times 100 \, \Omega \\
R &= 4700 \, \Omega \\
R &= 4.7 \, \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the tolerance.

$$R_{actual} = 4.7 \, \text{k}\Omega \pm 5\%$$

**Tolerance Range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= 4700 - (0.05 \times 4700) = 4465 \, \Omega \\
R_{max} &= 4700 + (0.05 \times 4700) = 4935 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When this resistor is tested using a DMM in resistance mode, the displayed value should lie between **4465 Ω and 4935 Ω** for it to be considered "healthy."

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: BJT Terminal Identification Using Diode Mode

The BJT contains two internal PN junctions. The following matrix helps identify the **Base terminal** and the **type (NPN/PNP)**.

| Test Condition | Red Probe | Black Probe | Expected Reading (Si) | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trial 1 | Pin 1 | Pin 2 | 0.7 V | Forward biased |
| Trial 1 (Reverse) | Pin 2 | Pin 1 | OL | Reverse biased → **Pin 1 = Anode of a junction** |
| Trial 2 | Pin 1 | Pin 3 | 0.7 V | Forward biased |
| Trial 2 (Reverse) | Pin 3 | Pin 1 | OL | Reverse biased → **Pin 1 = Anode of a junction** |

**Inference:** Since **Pin 1** acts as the common **Anode**, the BJT is a **PNP transistor**, and **Pin 1 is the Base**.

**If Pin 1 acts as the common Cathode (OL in reverse when red is on it), then it is an NPN transistor.**

---

### 3.3 Capacitor Time Constant Analysis (Charging Behavior)

When an analog multimeter is used to test a capacitor in high-resistance mode, the reading depends on the **RC time constant** of the meter and the capacitor.

**Formula for the RC Charging Curve:**

$$V_C(t) = V_{supply} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

Where:
- $V_C(t)$ = Voltage across the capacitor at time $t$
- $V_{supply}$ = DMM's internal battery voltage
- $R$ = Internal resistance of the DMM
- $C$ = Capacitance of the capacitor under test
- $e$ = Euler's number ≈ **2.71828**

**Practical DMM Observation:** The meter shows a **decreasing resistance reading** as the capacitor charges, settling at **OL (infinity)** for a healthy capacitor.

> [!NOTE]
> A healthy electrolytic capacitor of 100 μF tested with a typical analog DMM (internal R = 1 MΩ) will show full charging in approximately $5\tau = 5RC = 5 \times 10^6 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 500$ seconds, but in practice, meters use lower resistances for fast testing.

---

### 3.4 JFET Pin Identification Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Set DMM to **diode mode**.

**Step 2:** Find the **Gate pin** by testing all combinations of three pins (6 combinations in total).

**Step 3:** Identify the **Gate** as the pin that:
- Shows a **forward voltage drop (≈ 0.5 V to 0.7 V)** with one other pin (channel).
- Shows **OL** with the same other pin when probes are reversed.

**Step 4:** Once the **Gate** is identified, the other two pins are the **Drain and Source**.

**Step 5:** The **Drain-Source path** typically shows:
- **Low resistance (a few hundred ohms)** in both directions.
- This is because the JFET's channel is a continuous piece of semiconductor material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **N-channel JFET**, the Gate is the **P-type material** and the channel (D-S) is the **N-type material**. The PN junction between G and channel behaves exactly like a diode.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: DMM Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["DMM Main Unit"] --> nodeB["Selector Switch"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Voltage Mode VDC VAC"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Current Mode ADC"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Resistance Mode OHM"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Diode Test Mode"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Capacitance Mode"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Continuity Buzzer"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["ADC Converter"]
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["LCD Display"]
    nodeA --> nodeK["Red Probe Positive"]
    nodeA --> nodeL["Black Probe COM Negative"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the internal signal flow of a typical DMM. The selector switch routes the input signal to the appropriate measurement circuit, and the ADC digitizes the result for LCD display.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Diode Testing Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Start: Set DMM to Diode Mode"]) --> connect1["Connect Red Probe to Anode"]
    connect1 --> connect2["Connect Black Probe to Cathode"]
    connect2 --> read1["Read Forward Voltage Drop"]
    read1 --> check1{"Is Reading 0.5V to 0.8V?"}
    check1 -- "Yes" --> reverse["Reverse Probes"]
    check1 -- "No" --> shortcheck["Reading 0V means SHORTED Diode"]
    shortcheck --> stop(["Stop: Component Faulty"])
    reverse --> read2["Read Reverse Voltage Drop"]
    read2 --> check2{"Is Reading OL?"}
    check2 -- "Yes" --> good(["GOOD Diode: One-Way Conduction Confirmed"])
    check2 -- "No" --> opencheck["Reading 0V means SHORTED Diode"]
    opencheck --> stop
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU standard testing procedure** for a diode. The decision diamonds represent the **binary outcomes** used in board exam answers.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: BJT Terminal Identification Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Start: Set DMM to Diode Mode"]) --> trial["Test Each Pin Pair Combinations"]
    trial --> findcommon["Find the Pin with Two Forward-Bias Readings"]
    findcommon --> checkpol{"Probe Polarity on Common Pin?"}
    checkpol -- "Red Probe on Common Pin" --> npn["Common Pin is BASE - Transistor is NPN"]
    checkpol -- "Black Probe on Common Pin" --> pnp["Common Pin is BASE - Transistor is PNP"]
    npn --> identify1["Remaining Two Pins: Emitter and Collector"]
    pnp --> identify2["Remaining Two Pins: Emitter and Collector"]
    identify1 --> testCE["Test C-E in Both Directions: Should Show OL"]
    identify2 --> testCE2["Test C-E in Both Directions: Should Show OL"]
    testCE --> good(["GOOD BJT: Two Diode Junctions Confirmed"])
    testCE2 --> good
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: JFET Internal Structure (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeG["Gate Terminal G"] --- nodeCh["Channel N-Type Semiconductor Material"]
    nodeD["Drain Terminal D"] --- nodeCh
    nodeS["Source Terminal S"] --- nodeCh
    nodeG -. "PN Junction Diode" .-> nodeCh
```

> [!NOTE]
> This conceptual diagram illustrates the **single-piece channel** of a JFET, with the Gate forming a **PN junction** with the channel. This is why a JFET tests like **one diode (G-S or G-D) plus a low-resistance path (D-S)**.

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Component Testing Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Start: Identify Component"]) --> resistor{"Is it a Resistor?"}
    resistor -- "Yes" --> rt["Test: Use Ohms Mode"]
    rt --> rcheck{"Reading matches color code?"}
    rcheck -- "Yes" --> rgood["GOOD Resistor"]
    rcheck -- "No" --> rbad["FAULTY: Check for Open or Shorted"]
    resistor -- "No" --> cap{"Is it a Capacitor?"}
    cap -- "Yes" --> ct["Test: Use Capacitance Mode"]
    ct --> ccheck{"Reading matches rated value?"}
    ccheck -- "Yes" --> cgood["GOOD Capacitor"]
    ccheck -- "No" --> cbad["FAULTY: Check for Leakage or Open"]
    cap -- "No" --> diode{"Is it a Diode?"}
    diode -- "Yes" --> dt["Test: Use Diode Mode"]
    dt --> dcheck{"Forward 0.7V and Reverse OL?"}
    dcheck -- "Yes" --> dgood["GOOD Diode"]
    dcheck -- "No" --> dbad["FAULTY Diode"]
    diode -- "No" --> trans{"Is it a Transistor BJT or JFET?"}
    trans -- "Yes" --> tt["Test: Diode Mode for PN Junctions"]
    tt --> tcheck{"Two Diode Junctions Present?"}
    tcheck -- "Yes" --> tgood["GOOD Transistor"]
    tcheck -- "No" --> tbad["FAULTY Transistor"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Explain how a digital multimeter is used to test a silicon diode. State the expected readings in both bias conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. Set the DMM to **diode test mode** (▶| symbol).
2. Connect the **red (positive) probe to the anode** and the **black (negative) probe to the cathode** of the diode (forward bias condition).
3. Read the displayed value.
4. Reverse the probes to apply reverse bias.

**Expected Readings:**
- **Forward bias:** Display shows the **forward voltage drop ≈ 0.7 V** (for a healthy silicon diode).
- **Reverse bias:** Display shows **OL (open loop)** indicating no conduction.

**Conclusion:** A healthy silicon diode exhibits **unidirectional conduction** with a forward drop of **0.7 V** and **infinite resistance** in reverse bias. If both readings show 0 V, the diode is **shorted**; if both show OL, the diode is **open (burnt)**.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**How do you identify the Base terminal of an unknown BJT using a digital multimeter? State the inference rule.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. Set the DMM to **diode test mode**.
2. Test all three pin combinations (3 pairs × 2 polarities = 6 measurements).
3. Identify the **pin that gives a forward voltage drop (≈ 0.7 V) with both of the other two pins** (in one consistent probe polarity).

**Inference Rule:**
- If the **red probe is on the common pin** during both forward readings → the transistor is **NPN**, and the common pin is the **Base**.
- If the **black probe is on the common pin** during both forward readings → the transistor is **PNP**, and the common pin is the **Base**.

**Why:** The BJT contains **two PN junctions** sharing the Base. The common pin (Base) acts as the **anode for both junctions in an NPN** and as the **cathode for both junctions in a PNP**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe with a step-by-step procedure, how a digital multimeter is used to test the following components: **(7 Marks)**
  - **(i)** A **resistor** (with color code **Brown – Black – Red – Silver**)
  - **(ii)** A **capacitor** (rated 100 μF, electrolytic)
  - **(iii)** A **PNP transistor**

**(b)** Tabulate the expected DMM readings for a healthy and a faulty component in each of the above cases. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) Testing the Resistor (Brown – Black – Red – Silver):**

**Step 1:** Identify the color code.
- Brown = **1**, Black = **0**, Red = multiplier $10^2$, Silver = ±10% tolerance.

**Step 2:** Calculate the nominal value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= (1 \times 10 + 0) \times 10^{2} \, \Omega \\
R &= 10 \times 100 = 1000 \, \Omega = 1 \, \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Disconnect the resistor from the circuit to avoid **parallel paths**.

**Step 4:** Set the DMM to **resistance mode (Ω)** with appropriate range (2 kΩ).

**Step 5:** Connect probes across the resistor terminals (polarity-independent).

**Step 6:** Read the value and verify it lies within **900 Ω to 1100 Ω** (1 kΩ ± 10%).

---

**(a)(ii) Testing the 100 μF Electrolytic Capacitor:**

**Step 1:** **Discharge** the capacitor by shorting the leads (using a resistor for safety).

**Step 2:** Set the DMM to **capacitance mode (⊣⊢)**.

**Step 3:** Observe the **polarity marking** on the electrolytic capacitor (long lead = positive for through-hole, stripe = negative).

**Step 4:** Connect the **red probe to the positive terminal** and the **black probe to the negative terminal**.

**Step 5:** Read the displayed capacitance value. For a healthy capacitor, the value should be close to **100 μF** (typically within ±20% = 80 μF to 120 μF).

---

**(a)(iii) Testing the PNP Transistor:**

**Step 1:** Set the DMM to **diode test mode**.

**Step 2:** Identify the **Base** by finding the pin where the **black probe** gives forward voltage drop with both other pins.

**Step 3:** Test the **Base-Emitter (B-E) junction**: Black to Base, Red to Emitter → expect **0.7 V** forward, **OL** reverse.

**Step 4:** Test the **Base-Collector (B-C) junction**: Black to Base, Red to Collector → expect **0.7 V** forward, **OL** reverse.

**Step 5:** Test the **Collector-Emitter (C-E) junction** in both polarities → expect **OL** in both (no base current supplied).

---

**(b) Tabulation of Expected Readings:**

| Component | Healthy Reading (Forward) | Healthy Reading (Reverse) | Faulty - Shorted | Faulty - Open |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Resistor (1 kΩ)** | **1 kΩ ± 10%** (e.g., 980 Ω) | Same (polarity-free) | **0 Ω** | **OL** |
| **Capacitor (100 μF)** | **80 μF to 120 μF** | Same (polarity-free) | **0 μF or 0 Ω** | **OL or 0 μF** |
| **PNP BJT (B-E junction)** | **0.7 V** (Black to B, Red to E) | **OL** (Red to B, Black to E) | **0 V in both** | **OL in both** |
| **PNP BJT (B-C junction)** | **0.7 V** (Black to B, Red to C) | **OL** (Red to B, Black to C) | **0 V in both** | **OL in both** |
| **PNP BJT (C-E junction)** | **OL** | **OL** | **0 V in both** | **OL in both** |

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the procedure for testing a **JFET** using a digital multimeter. Explain how the Gate pin is identified and the expected readings at each junction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the testing procedures of a **BJT** and a **JFET** using a multimeter. Why can't a multimeter alone fully determine the functionality of a JFET in a circuit? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) JFET Testing Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Set the DMM to **diode test mode**.

**Step 2:** Test all three combinations of pin pairs (D-G, D-S, G-S) in both polarities (total 6 measurements).

**Step 3:** Identify the **Gate pin**:
- The Gate is the pin that shows a **forward voltage drop (≈ 0.5 V to 0.7 V)** with one of the other pins and **OL** with the same pin in the reverse direction.
- The remaining two pins are the **Drain (D)** and **Source (S)**.

**Step 4:** Test the **Gate-Source (G-S) junction**:
- Forward: **0.5 V to 0.7 V**
- Reverse: **OL**

**Step 5:** Test the **Gate-Drain (G-D) junction**:
- Forward: **0.5 V to 0.7 V**
- Reverse: **OL**

**Step 6:** Test the **Drain-Source (D-S) path** in both directions:
- Both directions should show a **low resistance (typically a few hundred ohms)**, indicating a continuous channel.

**Inference Table:**

| Pin Pair | Forward | Reverse | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| G-S | 0.5–0.7 V | OL | PN junction present |
| G-D | 0.5–0.7 V | OL | PN junction present |
| D-S | Low Ω | Low Ω | Continuous channel (no junction) |

---

**(b) Differences between BJT and JFET Testing:**

| Parameter | BJT (Bipolar) | JFET (Field-Effect) |
|---|---|---|
| **Junction Count** | Two PN junctions (B-E and B-C) | One PN junction (G to channel) + continuous channel |
| **Base/Gate Identification** | Common Anode (NPN) or Common Cathode (PNP) of two diodes | Common Cathode (N-channel) or Anode (P-channel) of one diode |
| **C-E / D-S Path** | **OL in both directions** (open switch) | **Low resistance in both directions** (continuous channel) |
| **Control Mechanism** | Current-controlled (Base current needed) | Voltage-controlled (Gate voltage controls channel) |
| **Diode Mode Test** | Two distinct forward readings | One forward reading (G-S or G-D) + low-Ω channel |
| **Functionality Check** | DMM test is mostly sufficient | DMM cannot verify the **amplification/transconductance** property |

**Why DMM alone cannot fully test a JFET:**

A DMM is a **static testing tool** that checks for the presence of PN junctions and basic conduction paths. However, a JFET's primary function is to act as a **voltage-controlled current source**, where the **Gate-to-Source voltage ($V_{GS}$)** controls the **Drain current ($I_D$)**. The DMM cannot:
1. Measure the **transconductance ($g_m$)** of the JFET.
2. Verify the **pinch-off voltage ($V_{GS(off)}$)**.
3. Confirm the JFET's ability to **amplify small signals** under operating conditions.
4. Test dynamic behavior such as **switching speed** and **frequency response**.

To fully characterize a JFET, an engineer needs a **curve tracer** or a **test circuit with a variable DC power supply** to plot the $I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$ characteristic curves.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write "the multimeter gives the resistance value" for a diode. **Wrong!** A diode is tested in **diode mode**, not resistance mode, because the DMM's resistance mode applies too low a voltage to forward-bias the junction.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **NPN with PNP** during base identification. Always state the **probe polarity on the Base** explicitly.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "OL" for the C-E path of a BJT without mentioning **"in the absence of base current"** — this is the key reason the C-E path is open.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** For JFET, students often write "D-S shows OL." **Wrong!** A JFET's D-S is a **continuous channel of semiconductor material** and shows **low resistance in both directions**.
> 5. **Mark Loss Zone:** Drawing **unlabeled diagrams** or missing the **expected numerical values** (e.g., 0.7 V for Si diode) leads to a 2–3 mark deduction per question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Resistor:** Test in **Ω mode**; reading should match the **color code value** within **tolerance**.
- ✅ **Capacitor:** Test in **capacitance mode**; **discharge first** for safety; electrolytic capacitors are **polarity-sensitive**.
- ✅ **Diode (Silicon):** Forward drop **≈ 0.7 V**; reverse = **OL**. **One-way conduction** is the key behavior.
- ✅ **Diode (Germanium):** Forward drop **≈ 0.3 V**; reverse = **OL**.
- ✅ **NPN BJT:** Red probe on Base gives **two forward readings**; C-E shows **OL** in both directions.
- ✅ **PNP BJT:** Black probe on Base gives **two forward readings**; C-E shows **OL** in both directions.
- ✅ **JFET:** Gate identified by **one forward-biased junction with channel**; D-S shows **low resistance in both directions**.
- ✅ **Always disconnect components** from the circuit before testing to avoid **parallel path errors**.
- ✅ **Expected forward voltage for Si PN junction = 0.7 V**; this is a **universal value** in all DMM tests.
- ✅ **OL on DMM = Open Loop = Infinite resistance**; **0 V in diode mode = short circuit**.
- ✅ **BJT = current-controlled, two junctions, C-E open in DMM test.**
- ✅ **JFET = voltage-controlled, one junction, D-S conductive in DMM test.**
- ✅ For **full functionality testing** of transistors, a **curve tracer** or **test circuit** is required — DMM only checks **static junction integrity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always include a **neatly labeled circuit diagram** or **tabular comparison** in your answer. KTU examiners reward **structured answers** with clear headings, sub-points, and a **summary table** at the end. Allocate **3–4 minutes** for a 3-mark question and **12–15 minutes** for a 14-mark question in your exam strategy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Printed circuit boards (PCB) - types, single sided, double sided, processing methods; design and fabrication of a single-sided PCB for a simple circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Printed circuit boards (PCB) - types, single sided, double sided, processing methods; design and fabrication of a single-sided PCB for a simple circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Printed Circuit Board (PCB):** A flat, rigid (or flexible) insulating substrate laminated with a thin layer of conductive copper foil, on which electrical components are mounted and electrically interconnected using etched copper traces, pads, and vias, following a predetermined circuit diagram.

A PCB mechanically supports and electrically connects electronic components using conductive pathways etched from copper sheets laminated onto a non-conductive substrate. The conductive tracks replace the conventional point-to-point wiring, providing a compact, reliable, and reproducible interconnection medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the workshop course **GXESL106**, the focus is on understanding PCB **types**, comparing **single-sided vs double-sided** boards, recognizing common **processing methods**, and executing the **design and fabrication of a single-sided PCB for a simple circuit** (typically a rectifier, LED indicator, or astable multivibrator).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The City Road Network

Think of a PCB as the **road network of a planned city**:

| PCB Element | City Analogy | Function |
|-------------|--------------|----------|
| **Copper trace** | Asphalt road | Conducts electricity (cars = electrons) |
| **Substrate (FR-4)** | Ground/earth | Provides mechanical foundation and insulation |
| **Component pads** | Bus stops / parking bays | Connection points where "passengers" (components) board |
| **Vias** | Flyovers / underpasses | Allow roads to cross between layers without colliding |
| **Solder mask** | Lane markings | Insulates and protects the copper from shorts (accidents) |
| **Silkscreen** | Street name boards | Labels for components (R1, C2, D3) |

Just as a city planner decides the road layout before construction, an electronics designer lays out the PCB artwork *before* fabrication. The PCB is essentially the **"blacktop highway" of modern electronics**, found in everything from your smartphone to a satellite.

## 1.3 Standard PCB Metrics (Industry-Recognized Values)

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and units are universally used in PCB design and fabrication. They appear frequently in KTU lab viva questions.

- **Standard board thickness:** **1.6 mm** (most common), with variants of 0.8 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.2 mm, 2.0 mm, and 2.4 mm.
- **Copper foil thickness:** Expressed in **oz/ft² (ounces per square foot)**, where **1 oz/ft² = 35 µm = 1.35 mil** thickness.
- **Standard copper weights:** **1 oz** (35 µm) and **2 oz** (70 µm) for signal layers.
- **Dielectric constant (Dk) of FR-4:** **4.2 – 4.6** at 1 MHz.
- **Minimum trace width (hobby/professional):** **0.2 mm (8 mil)** for DIY, **0.1 mm (4 mil)** for industrial.
- **Drill-to-copper clearance:** Typically **0.3 mm (12 mil)** minimum.
- **Working temperature of FR-4:** Up to **130 °C – 150 °C** (Tg rating).

## 1.4 Classification of PCBs — Overview

PCBs are broadly classified based on **two criteria**:

**(A) By Number of Conductive Layers:**

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Single-Sided PCB** | Copper on **one side** only; components on the other | Toys, calculators, simple power supplies |
| **Double-Sided PCB** | Copper on **both sides**, connected by **plated through-holes (vias)** | Consumer electronics, amplifiers, microcontrollers |
| **Multi-Layer PCB** | 4, 6, 8, or more alternating copper/dielectric layers | Smartphones, computers, high-speed digital systems |
| **Flexible PCB (Flex)** | Polyimide substrate, can bend | Cameras, wearables, medical probes |
| **Rigid-Flex PCB** | Combination of rigid and flexible regions | Aerospace, foldable devices, military hardware |

**(B) By Substrate Material:**

| Substrate | Property | Use Case |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **FR-2 (Phenolic paper)** | Low cost, brown, poor mechanical strength | Low-frequency toys, radios |
| **FR-4 (Glass epoxy)** | Excellent strength, flame retardant, green | **Industry standard** for almost all PCBs |
| **CEM-1, CEM-3** | Composite epoxy material | Mid-range consumer products |
| **Polyimide** | High temperature, flexible | Aerospace, flex PCBs |
| **Aluminum / Metal-core** | High thermal conductivity | LED lighting, power modules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU GXESL106 Workshop:** Students are expected to design and fabricate a **single-sided PCB on FR-2 or FR-4 substrate** with **1 oz copper**, using either the **toner-transfer (iron-on)** or **UV photoresist** method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Single-Sided PCB — Detailed Anatomy

A single-sided PCB is the **simplest and most economical** type of PCB. It has:

- **Bottom (solder) side:** Continuous copper foil (35 µm / 1 oz) laminated to substrate.
- **Top (component) side:** Bare substrate (FR-4) where components are mounted and leads pass through drilled holes to be soldered on the bottom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "single-sided"?** Because the **circuit pattern exists on only ONE side** of the board. All component leads go *through* the board to the copper side for soldering. The other side hosts the components and the silkscreen legend.

### Construction Stack-up of a Single-Sided PCB

From top to bottom:

1. **Silkscreen layer** (white legend) — optional
2. **Solder mask layer** (green lacquer) — optional but recommended
3. **Substrate (FR-4, 1.6 mm)** — dielectric insulator
4. **Copper layer (1 oz / 35 µm)** — etched to form traces
5. **Bottom silkscreen** (rare)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the top side has no copper, **all routing must occur on the bottom side**. This makes single-sided designs inherently **limited in routing density** and prone to **jumpers (zero-ohm resistors or wire links)** when traces must cross.

## 2.2 Double-Sided PCB — Detailed Anatomy

A double-sided PCB has **copper laminated on BOTH sides** of the substrate. The two sides are electrically connected through:

- **Plated Through-Holes (PTHs):** Holes drilled and electroplated with copper to form conductive barrels.
- **Vias:** Small PTHs used purely for routing (signal, ground, or power).
- **Through-hole components:** Component leads pass through and are soldered on both sides.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"plating" step is the critical differentiator** between a regular double-sided board and a true double-sided PCB. Without electroplating, you cannot achieve reliable inter-layer connections.

### Construction Stack-up of a Double-Sided PCB

From top to bottom:
1. Top silkscreen
2. Top solder mask
3. Top copper layer (1 oz) — etched pattern
4. Substrate (FR-4 core, ~1.0 – 1.2 mm)
5. Bottom copper layer (1 oz) — etched pattern
6. Bottom solder mask
7. Bottom silkscreen

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Bridge Crossings:** A single-sided PCB is like a city where all roads are flat and can never cross — you need flyovers. A double-sided PCB gives you a "second level" of roads connected by bridges (vias), which dramatically reduces the need for jumpers.

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix — Single vs Double Sided

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU viva favorite** comparison question. Memorize the key differences.

| Parameter | Single-Sided PCB | Double-Sided PCB |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Copper layers** | 1 | 2 |
| **Routing density** | Low (often needs jumpers) | High (vias enable crossings) |
| **Component mounting** | Only through-hole, on top side | Through-hole + SMD on both sides |
| **Inter-layer connection** | Not possible (traces must not cross) | Plated through-holes (vias) |
| **Cost** | **Low** (~30–50% of double-sided) | Moderate to high |
| **Fabrication complexity** | Simple (no plating) | Complex (requires electroless + electroplating) |
| **Drilling & plating** | Drilling only (no plating) | Drilling + electroless Cu + electrolytic Cu plating |
| **Typical applications** | LED chasers, power supplies, toys | Microcontroller boards, audio amplifiers, SMPS |
| **Signal integrity** | Limited; no ground plane | Better; one side can be dedicated ground plane |
| **Suitable for high-speed design** | No | Yes (with controlled impedance) |

## 2.4 PCB Processing Methods — The Three Industrial Families

### (A) Subtractive Method (Most Common)

> [!NOTE]
> The **subtractive method** is the **dominant industrial process** and the method implied in your KTU workshop. The PCB starts with a **fully copper-clad board**, and the unwanted copper is **chemically etched away**, leaving only the desired trace pattern.

**Process Flow:**
1. Cut blank copper-clad laminate to size.
2. Clean the copper surface (scouring powder / Scotch-Brite).
3. Apply etch resist (toner, photoresist, or screen-printed ink) to the trace pattern.
4. Etch in ferric chloride (FeCl₃) or cupric chloride (CuCl₂) solution.
5. Strip the etch resist.
6. Drill mounting holes.
7. Apply solder mask (optional).
8. Apply silkscreen (optional).
9. Cut to final shape and finish.

**Merits:** Simple, cheap, well-suited for prototypes and low-volume production.
**Demerits:** Wastes copper; undercutting can occur; not ideal for fine pitch (< 0.2 mm trace).

### (B) Additive Method

Copper is **deposited only where needed** on a bare substrate using electroless plating, with the rest of the board masked. **No etching required.**

**Merits:** No undercutting, copper-saving, finer pitch possible.
**Demerits:** Slow deposition process, expensive chemicals, less common.

### (C) Semi-Additive Method (Modified Semi-Additive Process — mSAP)

Combines both: a thin layer of electroless copper is added to a thin-clad laminate, then pattern-plated electrolytically to thicken, and finally flash-etched.

**Merits:** Ultra-fine pitch (down to 25 µm lines), used in IC substrates and HDI boards.
**Demerits:** Highly capital-intensive; not used in academic labs.

## 2.5 Materials Used in PCB Fabrication

| Material | Role | Notes |
|----------|------|-------|
| **FR-4 laminate** | Substrate (base) | Glass-fiber reinforced epoxy, Tg 130–180 °C |
| **Copper foil** | Conductive layer | 1 oz (35 µm) standard; rolled or electrodeposited |
| **Ferric chloride (FeCl₃)** | Etchant | Brown liquid; **stains everything — handle with gloves** |
| **Cupric chloride (CuCl₂)** | Etchant | Faster than FeCl₃, regenerable |
| **Sodium persulfate (Na₂S₂O₈)** | Etchant | Cleaner, transparent; good for small boards |
