| **Positive photoresist** | UV-sensitive etch resist | Hardens on exposure; unaffected areas etched away |
| **Negative photoresist** | UV-sensitive etch resist | Soluble in developer; exposed areas become hard |
| **Toner (laser printer)** | DIY etch resist | Iron-on toner transfer method |
| **Solder mask (liquid photoimageable)** | Protective lacquer | Green / blue / red; prevents solder bridges |
| **Flux (rosin-based)** | Cleaning agent | Removes oxide from copper before soldering |
| **Lead-free solder (SAC305)** | Joining material | Sn 96.5% / Ag 3.0% / Cu 0.5%, melts at 217 °C |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — PCB Design & Fabrication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas and constants are the ones most likely to appear in a KTU theory question or viva for this topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

### Trace Current-Carrying Capacity (IPC-2152 Modified Equation)

The relationship between the **current a trace can carry**, the **temperature rise**, and the **trace cross-sectional area** is given by:

$$
I = k \cdot (\Delta T)^{b} \cdot (A)^{c}
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $I$ | Current carrying capacity | Amperes (A) |
| $\Delta T$ | Allowable temperature rise above ambient | °C |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of trace | mil² (sq. mil) |
| $k, b, c$ | Empirically determined constants (depend on trace location) | dimensionless |

**Standard empirical constants (from IPC-2152):**

| Trace Location | $k$ | $b$ | $c$ |
|----------------|-----|-----|-----|
| **External (outer layer)** | 0.048 | 0.44 | 0.725 |
| **Internal (inner layer)** | 0.024 | 0.44 | 0.725 |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU problems, the simplified form commonly used is: **A = (I / 0.048)^(1/0.725) / (ΔT)^(0.44/0.725)** (external traces), giving the required cross-section in mil².

### Cross-Sectional Area of a Trace

$$
A = W \times T
$$

Where:
- $W$ = trace width (mils or mm)
- $T$ = copper thickness (mils or mm, typically 1.35 mil for 1 oz Cu)

### Conversion Between oz/ft² and µm

$$
1 \, \text{oz/ft}^2 = 35 \, \mu\text{m} = 1.35 \, \text{mil}
$$

### Etch Time Estimation

$$
t_{\text{etch}} = \frac{T_{\text{Cu}}}{R_{\text{etch}}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{etch}}$ = etching time (minutes)
- $T_{\text{Cu}}$ = copper thickness to be removed (mil or µm)
- $R_{\text{etch}}$ = etch rate of the etchant (typical **1 – 2 mil/min for FeCl₃ at 40 °C**)

### Drill Size for Plated Through-Holes (PTH)

$$
D_{\text{drill}} = D_{\text{finished hole}} + 2 \times t_{\text{plating}}
$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{drill}}$ = drill bit diameter (mm)
- $D_{\text{finished hole}}$ = required finished hole diameter (mm)
- $t_{\text{plating}}$ = thickness of copper plating (typically **25 – 35 µm** per side)

### Sheet Resistance of Copper (Useful for Power Calculations)

$$
R = \frac{\rho \times L}{A}
$$

Where $\rho$ for copper = **$1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$** at 20 °C.

### Annular Ring Width

$$
W_{\text{annular}} = \frac{D_{\text{pad}} - D_{\text{drill}}}{2}
$$

Where:
- $W_{\text{annular}}$ = annular ring (mm), minimum **0.2 mm (8 mil)** preferred
- $D_{\text{pad}}$ = pad diameter (mm)
- $D_{\text{drill}}$ = drill diameter (mm)

### Trace Spacing vs Voltage (Creepage Guideline)

$$
d_{\text{min}} = 0.6 \, \text{mm} + 0.005 \times V_{\text{peak}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Rule of thumb: For each volt above 12 V, add **0.005 mm** of clearance. Always check IPC-2221 for production-grade designs.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

PCBs are the **backbone of every modern electronic device**:

- **Consumer electronics:** Smartphones (10–12 layer HDI boards), laptops, TVs.
- **Automotive:** Engine control units (ECUs), ADAS, infotainment — must withstand vibration and 150 °C.
- **Aerospace & defense:** Rigid-flex PCBs in satellites and avionics — survive radiation and thermal cycling.
- **Medical devices:** Pacemakers, MRI machines, hearing aids — biocompatible substrates required.
- **Industrial automation:** PLCs, motor drives, robotics — high-current, high-voltage PCBs.
- **IoT & wearables:** Flexible PCBs in smartwatches, fitness bands.

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU workshop PCB you fabricate (e.g., a 5V regulated power supply with bridge rectifier + filter capacitor + 7805 + LED indicator)** uses a single-sided FR-4 board, 1 oz copper, and is a perfect miniature example of how every industrial PCB starts its life.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Trace Width from Current Rating (IPC-2152)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Design a PCB trace on a **single-sided FR-4 board (1 oz Cu, external trace)** to carry **2 A** of continuous current with a maximum temperature rise of **20 °C** above ambient. Calculate the required minimum trace width in mm and mils.

**Given:**
- Current $I = 2$ A
- Allowable temperature rise $\Delta T = 20$ °C
- Copper weight = 1 oz (thickness $T = 1.35$ mil)
- External trace: $k = 0.048$, $b = 0.44$, $c = 0.725$

**The governing equation (IPC-2152 external trace):**

$$
I = 0.048 \cdot (\Delta T)^{0.44} \cdot (A)^{0.725}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for the required cross-sectional area A:**

Rearranging for $A$:

$$
A^{0.725} = \frac{I}{0.048 \cdot (\Delta T)^{0.44}}
$$

$$
A = \left[ \frac{I}{0.048 \cdot (\Delta T)^{0.44}} \right]^{\frac{1}{0.725}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
A = \left[ \frac{2}{0.048 \cdot (20)^{0.44}} \right]^{\frac{1}{0.725}}
$$

Compute the denominator first:

$$
(20)^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln(20)} = e^{0.44 \cdot 2.9957} = e^{1.3181} = 3.7368
$$

$$
0.048 \cdot 3.7368 = 0.17937
$$

Thus:

$$
A = \left[ \frac{2}{0.17937} \right]^{1.3793}
$$

$$
A = (11.1506)^{1.3793}
$$

Taking natural log and exponentiating:

$$
\ln(A) = 1.3793 \cdot \ln(11.1506) = 1.3793 \cdot 2.4109 = 3.3249
$$

$$
A = e^{3.3249} = 37.79 \, \text{mil}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Convert cross-sectional area to trace width:**

Since $A = W \times T$, and $T = 1.35$ mil for 1 oz copper:

$$
W = \frac{A}{T} = \frac{37.79}{1.35} = 28.0 \, \text{mil}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert mils to millimeters:**

$$
1 \, \text{mil} = 0.0254 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
W = 28.0 \times 0.0254 = 0.711 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A minimum trace width of **~0.71 mm (28 mil)** is required to carry 2 A with a 20 °C temperature rise on a 1 oz external copper layer. Designers typically round up to **1.0 mm** for safety margin and ease of fabrication.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Etching Time Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A 1 oz/ft² (35 µm / 1.35 mil) copper-clad FR-4 board is to be etched in ferric chloride at **40 °C**, where the etch rate is known to be **1.5 mil/min**. Calculate the minimum time required to fully etch away the unwanted copper. Also find the etch time if the etchant is cooled to 25 °C, where the etch rate drops to 0.6 mil/min.

**Given:**
- Copper thickness $T_{\text{Cu}} = 1.35$ mil
- Etch rate at 40 °C: $R_{40} = 1.5$ mil/min
- Etch rate at 25 °C: $R_{25} = 0.6$ mil/min

**Step 1 — Apply the etch time formula:**

$$
t_{\text{etch}} = \frac{T_{\text{Cu}}}{R_{\text{etch}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute at 40 °C:**

$$
t_{40} = \frac{1.35 \, \text{mil}}{1.5 \, \text{mil/min}} = 0.9 \, \text{min} = 54 \, \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute at 25 °C:**

$$
t_{25} = \frac{1.35 \, \text{mil}}{0.6 \, \text{mil/min}} = 2.25 \, \text{min} = 135 \, \text{seconds}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Etch rate roughly **doubles for every 10 °C** rise (Arrhenius behavior). Cooling the bath slows etching dramatically and risks **under-etching (incomplete pattern)**. KTU lab manuals typically recommend a warm bath (35 – 45 °C) with gentle agitation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Drill Size for a Plated Through-Hole

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A double-sided PCB requires a finished PTH of **0.8 mm** diameter. The plating bath deposits **30 µm (1.18 mil)** of copper on each side of the hole wall. What drill bit size should be used?

**Given:**
- Required finished hole $D_{\text{finished}} = 0.8$ mm
- Plating thickness per side $t_{\text{plating}} = 30$ µm = 0.030 mm

**Step 1 — The drill must account for copper deposited on BOTH sides of the hole wall:**

$$
D_{\text{drill}} = D_{\text{finished}} + 2 \times t_{\text{plating}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
D_{\text{drill}} = 0.8 + 2 \times 0.030
$$

$$
D_{\text{drill}} = 0.8 + 0.060 = 0.86 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** Since standard drills come in 0.1 mm increments, a **0.9 mm drill** would be selected. Always **round UP** the drill size, never down, or the finished hole will be too small for component leads.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Annular Ring Verification

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A through-hole pad has a diameter of **2.0 mm** and is drilled with a **0.9 mm** drill bit. Verify if the annular ring meets the IPC minimum of **0.2 mm (8 mil)**.

**Step 1 — Apply the annular ring formula:**

$$
W_{\text{annular}} = \frac{D_{\text{pad}} - D_{\text{drill}}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
W_{\text{annular}} = \frac{2.0 - 0.9}{2} = \frac{1.1}{2} = 0.55 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with IPC minimum:**

$$
0.55 \, \text{mm} > 0.2 \, \text{mm} \quad \checkmark \, \text{ (Passes)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Annular ring is critical for **plating adhesion and solder joint strength**. Insufficient annular ring causes **"tombstoning"** and **"broken barrel"** failures during thermal stress.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — PCB Type vs Selection Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix helps choose the right PCB type for any given design (a common KTU design question).

| Selection Criterion | Single-Sided | Double-Sided | Multi-Layer | Flex |
|--------------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|------|
| **Lowest cost** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ |
| **Easiest fabrication** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★ |
| **High component density** | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
| **High-speed signals** | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Mechanical flexibility** | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Suitability for SMD** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good |
| **EMI/EMC performance** | Poor | Good (with GND plane) | Excellent | Variable |
| **DIY / academic lab** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Power handling** | Limited | Good | Excellent | Limited |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Fabrication of a Single-Sided PCB (KTU Lab Standard Procedure)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical lab procedure** that students must know for both the practical exam and the viva. Total time: **2 – 3 hours**.

**Step 1 — Design the Circuit and Layout**
- Draw the schematic in any CAD tool (KiCad, EAGLE, or even by hand on graph paper).
- Convert to a PCB layout with proper pad sizes (e.g., 2.0 mm for resistors, 2.5 mm for electrolytic capacitors, 0.9 mm drill).

**Step 2 — Print the Artwork on Glossy Paper / Transparency**
- Use a **laser printer** (toner is the etch resist) at **600 dpi minimum**.
- **Mirror image** the layout (so the print transfers the right way onto the copper).
- Use **photo paper** or **glossy magazine paper** for clean transfer.

**Step 3 — Prepare the Copper-Clad Board**
- Cut the FR-4 / FR-2 board to size (e.g., 80 mm × 60 mm).
- Scrub the copper side with **Scotch-Brite / abrasive pad** until shiny.
- Clean with isopropyl alcohol or acetone to remove oils.

**Step 4 — Toner Transfer (Iron-On Method)**
- Place the printed paper **face-down** on the copper.
- Iron at **cotton setting (180 – 200 °C)** for **3 – 5 minutes**, applying firm pressure.
- Move the iron in circular motions to ensure uniform heat.
- Allow to cool, then **peel the paper** under running water — the toner remains on the copper.

**Step 5 — Inspect and Touch Up**
- Check for any broken or missing traces with a **loupe / magnifying glass**.
- Touch up gaps with a **permanent marker (CD/DVD marker pen)** filled with etch resist.

**Step 6 — Etching**
- Prepare **ferric chloride (FeCl₃) solution** in a **plastic tray** (never metal).
- Submerge the board, copper-side up.
- **Agitate gently** and maintain temperature at **35 – 45 °C**.
- Etch time: **5 – 15 minutes** (until all unwanted copper is gone).
- **Rinse thoroughly** with running water.

**Step 7 — Strip the Toner**
- Wipe the board with **acetone** or **isopropyl alcohol + soft cloth** to remove the toner.
- The copper traces are now exposed and shiny.

**Step 8 — Drilling**
- Mark hole centers using the printed layout as a guide (or use the drill coordinates).
- Use a **PCB micro drill bit (0.8 – 1.0 mm)** at **10,000 – 15,000 RPM**.
- Drill slowly with light pressure; backing the board with wood prevents blowout.

**Step 9 — Solder Mask (Optional)**
- Apply **liquid solder mask** using a brush or screen, or use a **UV-curable film**.
- Cure per manufacturer instructions.

**Step 10 — Silkscreen (Optional)**
- Print component designators (R1, C1, D1) and the **student's name and roll number** on transparent sheet.
- Iron-on or hand-paint with white paint.

**Step 11 — Component Mounting and Soldering**
- Insert component leads through the holes from the **non-copper side**.
- Bend leads slightly to hold them in place.
- Solder on the **copper side** using a **25 – 40 W soldering iron** and **lead-free solder wire (SAC305)**.
- Trim leads with side cutters.

**Step 12 — Testing**
- Visual inspection under magnification.
- **Continuity test** with a multimeter (beep mode) to verify all connections.
- **Power-on test** with the actual circuit (e.g., 230 V AC input → 5 V DC output for a regulated supply).
