# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**

**Q: Define a Printed Circuit Board (PCB). List any four advantages of using PCBs over conventional point-to-point wiring.**

**Model Answer:**

A Printed Circuit Board (PCB) is a flat board made of an insulating substrate (typically FR-4) with a thin layer of copper foil laminated on one or both sides. The copper is etched to form conductive traces that interconnect electronic components mounted on the board.

**Advantages of PCBs over point-to-point wiring:**

1. **Compactness:** All interconnections are printed as thin copper traces, drastically reducing the physical size of the circuit. A circuit that occupies several square centimeters with wired joints fits onto a PCB a fraction of that size.

2. **Reliability & Repeatability:** Soldered connections on PCBs are mechanically robust and identical in every production unit. Point-to-point wiring suffers from loose joints, cold joints, and human error.

3. **Mass Production Economy:** Once the artwork is finalized, thousands of identical boards can be produced with no incremental labor cost per unit, making PCBs ideal for commercial production.

4. **Electrical Performance:** Traces have controlled impedance, and ground planes (in multi-layer boards) reduce EMI/EMC, crosstalk, and noise pickup — features impossible with hand-wired circuits.

5. **Easy Fault Diagnosis & Repair:** Standardized layouts, silkscreen labels, and test points make troubleshooting straightforward.

6. **Mechanical Strength:** The rigid substrate supports component weight and resists vibration, essential for automotive and aerospace use.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, write the **definition (1 mark) + 4 advantages with one-line explanations (2 marks)**. A neat diagram of a single-sided PCB elevates the answer by half a mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**

**Q: Differentiate between single-sided and double-sided PCBs. State two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Sided PCB | Double-Sided PCB |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Copper layers | One side only | Both sides |
| Inter-layer connection | Not possible | Plated through-holes (vias) |
| Routing density | Low; jumpers often needed | High; vias resolve crossings |
| Cost | Low | Higher (plating step) |
| Components on both sides | No | Yes |
| Fabrication complexity | Simple | Complex |

**Applications of single-sided PCBs:**
1. **LED chasers and decorative light circuits** — simple sequential logic with few interconnections.
2. **Linear power supplies (5V/12V regulators)** — few components, all on one side.
3. Toy circuits, remote controls, calculators, basic radio receivers.

**Applications of double-sided PCBs:**
1. **Microcontroller-based boards** (e.g., Arduino UNO) — dense routing with SMD + through-hole mix.
2. **Audio amplifiers** — one side dedicated to the ground plane for noise reduction.
3. Switched-mode power supplies (SMPS), mobile phone motherboards, computer peripherals.

> [!TIP]
> Use a **comparison table** in your KTU answer — it conveys more information in less space and is favored by examiners.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS, INTERNAL CHOICE)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU ESE pattern, Part B has an **internal choice** — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern, Module Experiment 2]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the different types of PCBs based on the number of conductive layers. Describe the construction and key features of a single-sided PCB with a neat cross-sectional diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the step-by-step procedure for fabricating a single-sided PCB using the **toner-transfer (iron-on) method** for a simple 5V regulated DC power supply circuit. Mention the materials and chemicals required. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):

PCBs are classified based on the number of conductive copper layers into the following types:

**1. Single-Sided PCB:**
- Has copper laminated on **one side only** of the substrate.
- Components are mounted on the opposite (top) side; their leads pass through drilled holes to be soldered on the bottom (copper) side.
- All routing must occur on the bottom side, so jumpers (zero-ohm resistors or insulated wires) are often needed when traces must cross.
- **Cheapest** and **simplest** to fabricate; ideal for low-density, low-frequency circuits.
- **Applications:** LED indicators, linear power supplies, calculators, toys.

**2. Double-Sided PCB:**
- Copper laminated on **both sides** of the substrate.
- The two sides are interconnected by **plated through-holes (PTHs)** or **vias**.
- Allows higher component density and routing flexibility.
- One side can be used as a continuous **ground plane** for improved EMI performance.
- **Applications:** Audio amplifiers, microcontroller boards, motor controllers.

**3. Multi-Layer PCB:**
- Consists of **4, 6, 8, or more** alternating layers of copper and dielectric, laminated together under heat and pressure.
- Contains **buried vias** (connect inner layers) and **blind vias** (connect outer to inner).
- Provides dedicated power and ground planes, controlled impedance, and excellent signal integrity.
- **Applications:** Smartphones, computers, high-speed digital systems, satellite communication.

**4. Flexible PCB (Flex PCB):**
- Built on **polyimide** substrate; can be bent, folded, or twisted.
- Used where space constraints or dynamic movement prohibit rigid boards.
- **Applications:** Camera modules, wearables, medical implants, aerospace wiring harnesses.

**Cross-Section of a Single-Sided PCB (top to bottom):**
- Top silkscreen layer (white legend, component designators)
- Solder mask (green lacquer, optional but recommended)
- Substrate (FR-4, typically 1.6 mm thick)
- Copper foil (1 oz / 35 µm, etched to form traces and pads)
- Bottom silkscreen (rarely used)

**Key features:** Low cost, simple fabrication, no plating, limited routing density, components only on top side, no inter-layer connections.

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Materials and Chemicals Required:**
- FR-4 copper-clad laminate (1 oz copper, 1.6 mm thickness)
- Laser printer + glossy photo paper
- Electric iron (cotton setting, 180 – 200 °C)
- Ferric chloride (FeCl₃) etchant
- Plastic tray (non-metallic)
- PCB micro drill bits (0.8 mm, 1.0 mm)
- Soldering iron (25 – 40 W), lead-free solder wire (SAC305)
- Acetone / isopropyl alcohol for cleaning
- Components: bridge rectifier (4 × 1N4007), 1000 µF electrolytic capacitor, 7805 regulator IC, 0.1 µF ceramic capacitor, 330 Ω resistor, LED, screw terminals.

**Fabrication Steps (with flow diagram):**

1. **Schematic Capture:** Draw the schematic of the 5V regulated supply in KiCad or by hand.

2. **PCB Layout Design:** Place component footprints on a 60 mm × 40 mm board. Set trace width to 1.0 mm (for ~2 A current), pad diameters to 2.0 mm for through-hole, drill sizes 0.9 mm.

3. **Artwork Printing:** Print the layout (in **mirror image**) on glossy paper using a laser printer at 600 dpi. The black toner acts as the etch resist.

4. **Board Preparation:** Cut the copper-clad board to size. Scrub the copper side with an abrasive pad until shiny; clean with acetone.

5. **Toner Transfer:** Place the printed paper face-down on the copper. Iron firmly for 3 – 5 minutes at 180 – 200 °C. Let cool, then peel the paper under running water. The toner pattern remains on the copper.

6. **Inspection & Touch-Up:** Examine under magnification. Touch up any missing traces with a CD/DVD marker pen (permanent marker with etch-resistant ink).

7. **Etching:** Submerge the board in FeCl₃ solution (40 °C) in a plastic tray. Agitate gently for 5 – 15 minutes until all unwanted copper is dissolved. Rinse thoroughly with water.

8. **Toner Stripping:** Wipe the board with acetone to remove the toner, exposing shiny copper traces.

9. **Drilling:** Mark hole centers using the layout. Drill with a 0.8 / 0.9 mm bit at 10,000 – 15,000 RPM. Use a wooden backer plate to prevent blowout.

10. **Component Mounting & Soldering:** Insert components from the top (non-copper) side, bend leads outward, solder on the bottom (copper) side. Trim excess leads.

11. **Testing:** Verify continuity with a multimeter. Apply 230 V AC input and measure 5 V DC at the output using a regulated DC power supply bench setup.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mirror the artwork** — the print will be reversed, and traces won't match the board. This is the **#1 fabrication failure** in KTU labs.
> 2. **Insufficient ironing time** — toner doesn't fully transfer, leading to broken traces.
> 3. **Using an inkjet printer** — ink is water-based and dissolves in FeCl₃; **only laser toner is chemically resistant**.
> 4. **Not agitating the etchant** — leads to uneven etching and undercutting.
> 5. **Skipping the safety note on FeCl₃** — examiner expects: "Use gloves, goggles, and a plastic tray; never pour etchant into a metal container."
> 6. **Not mentioning mirror image, etch rate, or solder type** — these are common sub-part (b) follow-ups in viva.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern, Module Experiment 2]**

**Q: (a)** List the different PCB processing methods. Explain the **subtractive method** in detail with a neat flow diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-sided PCB is to carry a current of **3 A** continuously with a maximum temperature rise of **25 °C** above ambient. Using the IPC-2152 empirical equation for external traces, calculate the **minimum trace width** required if the copper weight is **1 oz/ft²**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):

**PCB Processing Methods (Three Industrial Families):**

1. **Subtractive Method** — Start with fully copper-clad board; remove (etch away) unwanted copper. *Most common.*
2. **Additive Method** — Start with bare substrate; add copper only where needed by electroless deposition. *Copper-saving but slow.*
3. **Semi-Additive Method (mSAP)** — Combine both: thin electroless seed, pattern plate, flash etch. *Used for fine-pitch IC substrates.*

**Subtractive Method — Detailed Explanation (Toner-Transfer Variant for Lab):**

The subtractive method is the most widely used PCB fabrication process in both industry and academic labs. The principle is **chemical removal of unwanted copper** to leave behind the desired conductive pattern.

**Process Flow (10 Steps):**

1. **Substrate Preparation:** Cut the copper-clad laminate (FR-2 or FR-4) to the required size. The standard thickness is 1.6 mm with 1 oz copper (35 µm).

2. **Surface Cleaning:** Scrub the copper surface with fine abrasive (Scotch-Brite) to remove oxide and grease. Wipe with acetone or isopropyl alcohol. A clean surface ensures strong toner adhesion.

3. **Artwork Generation:** Design the PCB layout using CAD software (KiCad, EAGLE) or hand-draw on graph paper. Print onto glossy paper using a **laser printer at 600 dpi** in **mirror image**. The black laser toner is the etch resist.

4. **Toner Transfer:** Place the print face-down on the copper. Iron at 180 – 200 °C for 3 – 5 minutes with firm, even pressure. The heat melts the toner and fuses it to the copper. Peel the paper under running water.

5. **Inspection & Touch-Up:** Examine the transferred pattern under a magnifier. Touch up any missing lines with a CD/DVD marker (which contains solvent-resistant ink).

6. **Etching:** Submerge the board (copper side up) in a ferric chloride (FeCl₃) bath maintained at 35 – 45 °C. Agitate gently with a plastic rod or by rocking the tray. Etch time: typically 5 – 15 minutes. The FeCl₃ oxidizes and dissolves the exposed copper:

$$
\text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2
$$

7. **Rinse:** Remove the board and rinse under running water to halt etching.

8. **Resist Stripping:** Wipe with acetone-soaked cotton to remove the toner, revealing the shiny copper traces.

9. **Drilling:** Drill component holes (0.8 – 1.0 mm) at marked positions. Use a high-speed (10,000 – 15,000 RPM) micro drill with light pressure.

10. **Finishing:** Apply solder mask (optional), silkscreen, and cut to final shape. Mount and solder components. Test for continuity and function.

**Merits:** Simple, low cost, suitable for prototypes and academic labs.
**Demerits:** Wastes copper, undercutting (over-etching of trace edges), not suitable for ultra-fine pitch.

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Given:**
- Current $I = 3$ A
- Allowable temperature rise $\Delta T = 25$ °C
- Copper weight = 1 oz → thickness $T = 1.35$ mil
- External trace: $k = 0.048$, $b = 0.44$, $c = 0.725$

**Step 1 — Governing Equation (IPC-2152 external trace):**

$$
I = 0.048 \cdot (\Delta T)^{0.44} \cdot (A)^{0.725}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for cross-sectional area A:**

$$
A = \left[ \frac{I}{0.048 \cdot (\Delta T)^{0.44}} \right]^{1/0.725}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
(25)^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln(25)} = e^{0.44 \cdot 3.2189} = e^{1.4163} = 4.1217
$$

$$
0.048 \times 4.1217 = 0.1978
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio I / denominator:**

$$
\frac{3}{0.1978} = 15.167
$$

**Step 5 — Raise to the power 1 / 0.725 = 1.3793:**

$$
A = (15.167)^{1.3793}
$$

Computing via natural logarithms:

$$
\ln(15.167) = 2.7189
$$

$$
\ln(A) = 1.3793 \times 2.7189 = 3.7502
$$

$$
A = e^{3.7502} = 42.55 \, \text{mil}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Compute trace width:**

$$
W = \frac{A}{T} = \frac{42.55 \, \text{mil}^2}{1.35 \, \text{mil}} = 31.52 \, \text{mil}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to millimeters:**

$$
W = 31.52 \times 0.0254 = 0.80 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Result and Comment:**

A minimum trace width of **~0.80 mm (31.5 mil)** is required to carry 3 A with a 25 °C temperature rise on a 1 oz external copper layer. In practice, the designer should select the **next standard size up, i.e., 1.0 mm (40 mil)**, to provide a safety margin against manufacturing tolerance variation, copper thickness variation (±10%), and localized hot spots near component pads.

If the application requires the trace to fit in a tight space, the alternatives are:
- Use **2 oz copper** ($T = 2.70$ mil), which halves the required width to **~0.4 mm**.
- Add a **solder coating** (HASL or ENIG), which increases the effective cross-section.
- Use a **polygon fill (copper pour)** to spread the current over a wider area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Calculation Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong constants** — using $k = 0.024$ (internal) instead of $0.048$ (external) for a single-sided board loses full marks. Always identify the trace location first.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — using $A$ in mm² instead of mil². The empirical constants $k, b, c$ are **valid only for mil²** unless explicitly converted.
> 3. **Forgetting the exponent inversion** — dividing by 0.725 instead of taking the 1/0.725 power.
> 4. **Not rounding up** — never round down a calculated trace width; round up to the nearest 0.1 mm.
> 5. **No engineering comment** — the "comment on the result" sub-part is worth **1 – 2 marks**; skipping it loses marks even if the math is perfect.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Final 60-second revision list for KTU viva and last-minute exam prep.

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **PCB:** Insulating substrate with conductive copper traces replacing wired connections.
- **Single-Sided:** Copper on **one** side only; components on top, soldered on bottom.
- **Double-Sided:** Copper on **both** sides; connected by **plated through-holes (vias)**.
- **Multi-Layer:** 4+ layers with buried/blind vias; for high-speed and dense designs.

### ✅ Three Processing Methods
- **Subtractive** (etch away unwanted Cu) — *most common in labs*.
- **Additive** (deposit Cu where needed) — copper-saving.
- **Semi-Additive (mSAP)** — for ultra-fine pitch.

### ✅ Toner-Transfer Lab Procedure (Memorize the 10 Steps)
1. Design → 2. Print (mirror) → 3. Cut board → 4. Clean Cu → 5. Iron-on (180–200 °C) → 6. Inspect/touch-up → 7. Etch in FeCl₃ (40 °C, 5–15 min) → 8. Strip toner → 9. Drill (0.8–0.9 mm) → 10. Solder components.

### ✅ Key Formulas
- **Trace area:** $A = W \times T$ (mil²)
- **Current capacity:** $I = 0.048 \cdot (\Delta T)^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ (external)
- **Etch time:** $t = T_{\text{Cu}} / R_{\text{etch}}$
- **Drill size (PTH):** $D_{\text{drill}} = D_{\text{finished}} + 2 t_{\text{plating}}$
- **Annular ring:** $W_{\text{annular}} = (D_{\text{pad}} - D_{\text{drill}}) / 2$
- **Copper thickness:** 1 oz = 35 µm = 1.35 mil

### ✅ Critical Numbers to Memorize
- Standard board thickness: **1.6 mm**
- Standard Cu weight: **1 oz/ft² = 35 µm**
- FeCl₃ etch rate at 40 °C: **1 – 2 mil/min**
- Min trace width (DIY): **0.2 mm**
- Min annular ring: **0.2 mm (8 mil)**
- Iron temperature for toner transfer: **180 – 200 °C**
- Drill RPM: **10,000 – 15,000**
- Soldering iron: **25 – 40 W**

### ✅ Common Viva Questions
1. *Why is the print mirrored before toner transfer?* — So the trace pattern is correct (not reversed) on the copper.
2. *Why must an inkjet printer NOT be used?* — Ink is water-based and dissolves in the etchant.
3. *Why is FeCl₃ preferred in academic labs?* — Cheap, readily available, and works at room temperature (with warming).
4. *Why plastic tray for etching?* — FeCl₃ reacts with metals (iron, steel, copper containers).
5. *What is the role of solder mask?* — Prevents solder bridges between adjacent traces and protects copper from oxidation.
6. *Why is the substrate heated during lamination?* — To cure the epoxy resin and bond copper firmly to the dielectric.

### ✅ Quick Comparison — Single vs Double
| | Single | Double |
|-|--------|--------|
| Layers | 1 | 2 |
| Vias | ✗ | ✓ |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Routing | Limited | Flexible |
| Plating | Not needed | Required |

### ✅ Safety Notes (Examiner Loves This)
- Always wear **gloves and safety goggles** while handling FeCl₃ — it stains skin and clothes permanently.
- Etch in a **well-ventilated area**; Fumes are mildly corrosive.
- **Never** pour used FeCl₃ down the drain; neutralize with baking soda and dispose as chemical waste.
- **Wash hands** thoroughly after the lab.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-connection methods and soldering practice (Breadboard, Wrapping, Crimping, Soldering - types, selection of materials and safety precautions)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Inter-connection methods and soldering practice (Breadboard, Wrapping, Crimping, Soldering - types, selection of materials and safety precautions)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Inter-Connection Methods

In any electronic or electrical system, individual components such as **resistors**, **capacitors**, **transistors**, **integrated circuits (ICs)**, and **switches** must be electrically joined to form a working circuit. The method used to establish these electrical and mechanical joints is termed an **Inter-Connection Method**. The choice of method directly influences circuit reliability, signal integrity, current-carrying capacity, and ease of prototyping/maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Inter-connection methods* refer to the standardized mechanical and electrical techniques used to join electronic components, wires, and terminals in a circuit. They range from temporary, solderless methods (breadboard) to permanent, high-reliability methods (soldering, crimping, wire wrapping).

### 1.2 The Four Primary Inter-Connection Methods

| Sl. No. | Method | Nature of Joint | Typical Application |
|---------|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Breadboard** | Temporary, Solderless | Lab prototyping, educational kits |
| 2 | **Wire Wrapping** | Semi-permanent | Aerospace, military, telecom backplanes |
| 3 | **Crimping** | Permanent (mechanical) | Power cables, automotive wiring, connectors |
| 4 | **Soldering** | Permanent (metallurgical) | PCB assembly, electronics manufacturing |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of building a house. A **breadboard** is like arranging furniture with removable adhesive strips — easy to rearrange, but not for heavy load. **Wire wrapping** is like tying ropes tightly around a post — strong, repeatable, but needs skill. **Crimping** is like squeezing a metal clamp onto a hose — once done, it's permanent and rugged. **Soldering** is like welding two metal pipes with molten metal — a strong, permanent, electrically excellent bond that becomes part of the structure itself.

### 1.4 Why Different Methods Exist

Different engineering applications demand different joint characteristics:

- **Low-frequency DC signals** → any method works.
- **High-frequency RF signals** → require controlled-impedance methods (soldering/crimping with proper shielding).
- **Harsh environments (vibration, temperature)** → crimping and wire wrapping excel.
- **Rapid prototyping / education** → breadboard is the universal choice.
- **Mass production** → automated soldering (wave, reflow) dominates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Selection Parameter:**
> The two most important metrics in selecting an interconnection are:
> 1. **Contact Resistance** (must be low, ideally < **10 mΩ**).
> 2. **Mechanical Strength** (must withstand vibration, thermal cycling).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Inter-Connection Method

#### 2.1.1 Breadboard (Solderless Breadboard)

A breadboard is a rectangular plastic board with a grid of holes spaced at **0.1 inch (2.54 mm)** pitch — matching standard DIP (Dual In-line Package) IC pin spacing.

**Internal Construction:**
- Each hole contains a **phosphor-bronze or nickel-silver spring clip** that grips component leads.
- Internal metal strips connect groups of 5 holes horizontally (terminal strips).
- Two vertical columns on each side form **power rails** (typically marked **+** and **−**).

**Working Principle:**
When a component lead is inserted, the spring clip exerts a normal force, creating a gas-tight contact. The contact is *temporary* and *reusable* thousands of times.

**Advantages:**
- Zero soldering required → safe for students.
- Instant modification of circuits.
- Reusable, eco-friendly.

**Limitations:**
- Poor performance at high frequencies (parasitic capacitance ~**2–5 pF** per node).
- Loose contact over time due to spring fatigue.
- Not suitable for currents above **1 A** continuously.

#### 2.1.2 Wire Wrapping

Wire wrapping is a solderless technique where a solid wire (typically **AWG 30**, silver-plated copper) is tightly wound around a square or rectangular post.

**Tool Used:** Manual or electric **wire-wrap gun** (bit + sleeve).

**Wrapping Specifications:**
- **7 turns** of insulated wire around the post (for AWG 30 on standard 0.025-inch square post).
- The corner of the post *plows* into the wire, creating a gas-tight, cold-welded connection.
- Contact resistance: typically **< 5 mΩ**.

**Types of Wire Wrap:**
1. **Regular Wrap** — 7 turns insulated + bare termination.
2. **Modified Wrap** — wire continues to next post (daisy-chaining).
3. **Coaxial Wrap** — for shielded signal lines.

#### 2.1.3 Crimping

Crimping is the process of deforming a metal terminal (crimp connector) around a stripped wire to form a gas-tight, low-resistance joint.

**Mechanism:**
- A **crimp tool** applies controlled pressure (calibrated in PSI).
- The terminal barrel is compressed, causing the metal to flow around the conductor strands.
- The result is a *cold-weld* — no heat, no solder.

**Critical Parameters:**
- **Crimp Height** — must match tool/terminal specification.
- **Pull-out Force** — must meet UL/CSA standards (e.g., **> 50 N** for typical signal crimps).
- **Insulation Grip** — the terminal must also grip the wire's insulation to provide strain relief.

#### 2.1.4 Soldering

Soldering is the process of joining two or more metal items by melting and flowing a **filler metal (solder)** into the joint. The base metals are *not* melted — only the solder.

**Soldering Temperature Range:** **180 °C to 450 °C** (well below the melting point of copper at **1085 °C**).

**Types of Soldering:**

| Type | Temperature | Application |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Soft Soldering** | < **450 °C** | General electronics, PCB assembly |
| **Hard (Brazing) Soldering** | > **450 °C** | Plumbing, high-mechanical joints |
| **Induction Soldering** | Localized heating via EM induction | Mass production |
| **Wave Soldering** | Molten solder wave | Through-hole PCB assembly |
| **Reflow Soldering** | Controlled oven heating | SMD / Surface Mount Devices |
| **Spot / Resistance Soldering** | Localized heat via electrodes | Repair work |

### 2.2 KTU Formula & Standards Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Reference Table for KTU Examinations**

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Unit | Significance |
|-----------|------------------------|------|--------------|
| Breadboard hole pitch | **2.54** | mm | Matches DIP IC pins |
| Typical contact resistance (breadboard) | $\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 50}$ | m$\Omega$ | Higher than soldered joints |
| Wire wrap turns (AWG 30) | **7** | turns | Insulated portion |
| Wire wrap post size | **0.025 × 0.025** | inch (square) | Standard |
| Wire wrap wire gauge | **AWG 30** | — | Silver-plated copper |
| Solder melting point (60/40 Sn-Pb) | **188** | °C | Eutectic alloy |
| Lead-free solder (SAC305) melting | **217 – 220** | °C | RoHS compliant |
| Soldering iron tip temperature (typical) | **300 – 400** | °C | Adjustable stations |
| Crimp pull-out force (signal wire) | **> 50** | N | UL 486A standard |
| Flux type for electronics | **Rosin (RMA / R)** | — | Non-corrosive |
| Safe lead-free work temperature | **< 400** | °C | Prevents pad lift |

### 2.3 Selection of Materials

#### 2.3.1 Solder Materials
- **Tin-Lead (Sn-Pb) 60/40** — traditional, easy to use, melting at **188 °C**. (Restricted under RoHS in many regions.)
- **Lead-Free Solders (e.g., SAC305 — Sn 96.5 %, Ag 3.0 %, Cu 0.5 %)** — melting point **217 – 220 °C**, RoHS-compliant.
- **Flux-core solder** — built-in flux for easier wetting.

#### 2.3.2 Flux
- **Rosin (R, RMA, RA)** — most common in electronics. Non-corrosive residue.
- **Water-soluble / organic** — stronger cleaning action, must be washed off.
- **No-clean flux** — minimal residue, no post-cleaning required.

#### 2.3.3 Soldering Iron Tips
- **Conical** — precision work.
- **Chisel** — general purpose, high heat transfer.
- **Knife / Bevel** — drag soldering SMDs.
- **Hakko / Weller** type tips standardized at **6 mm shank**.

#### 2.3.4 Crimp Terminals
- **Ring terminals**, **spade (fork) terminals**, **butt connectors**, **JST connectors**, **Molex KK series**, **D-sub crimps** — selected based on current rating and connector type.

#### 2.3.5 Wire-Wrap Wire
- **Kynar-insulated, silver-plated, solid copper**, **AWG 30**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (e.g., NASA, ISRO satellites):** Wire wrapping is still used in satellite harnesses because each joint can be *individually* removed and rewrapped without damaging the post.
- **Automotive (CAN bus wiring):** Crimped Deutsch and AMP connectors dominate due to vibration tolerance.
- **Consumer electronics (smartphones, laptops):** Reflow soldering of BGA and QFN packages is universal.
- **University labs:** Breadboards remain the foundation of every introductory electronics lab worldwide.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Standard Soldering Procedure (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> *This is the canonical "how to solder" procedure examiners expect in KTU lab viva and theory questions.*

**Step 1: Preparation**
Clean the soldering iron tip using a **brass wool / wet sponge** to remove oxidized solder. The tip should appear bright and shiny.

**Step 2: Tinning the Tip**
Apply a small amount of fresh solder to the tip. The molten solder should flow uniformly and coat the tip surface — this is called **tinning**. Tinning improves thermal conductivity and prevents oxidation.

**Step 3: Component Insertion**
Insert the component lead through the PCB hole (or hold it on a pad for SMD). Bend the leads slightly outward (~**45°**) on the solder side to hold the component in place (for through-hole).

**Step 4: Heat Application**
Touch the soldering iron tip to *both* the component lead *and* the PCB pad simultaneously. Hold for approximately **2 – 3 seconds**. The goal is to heat the *workpieces*, not the solder.

**Step 5: Solder Application**
Feed the solder onto the *opposite* side of the joint (the side away from the iron tip). The solder should melt on contact with the heated metal, not the iron itself. Apply approximately **3 – 5 mm** of solder wire for a typical through-hole joint.

**Step 6: Cool Without Motion**
Remove the solder wire first, then the iron. **Do not move the joint** while it solidifies. Movement during cooling causes a **cold joint** (dull, grainy, high resistance).

**Step 7: Inspection**
A good joint is **shiny, concave, and exhibits a volcano-like fillet** between the lead and the pad. It should pass the *pull test* — moderate force should not break the joint.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Joint Quality Inspection

| Quality Indicator | Good Joint ✅ | Defective Joint ❌ |
|-------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Appearance** | Shiny, smooth, concave fillet | Dull, grainy, or blistered |
| **Wetting Angle** | $\theta < \mathbf{30°}$ (good wetting) | $\theta > \mathbf{90°}$ (de-wetting) |
| **Wicking** | Solder fills the barrel/plated hole | Solder sits on top, doesn't fill |
| **Mechanical** | Passes pull test (> **10 N** for THT) | Lifts off pad under slight force |
| **Electrical** | $R_{contact} < \mathbf{10 \text{ m}\Omega}$ | $R_{contact} > \mathbf{100 \text{ m}\Omega}$ |
| **Common Defect** | — | **Cold Joint**, **Solder Bridge**, **Lifted Pad**, **Insufficient Solder**, **Excess Solder (spike)** |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Crimping Procedure

**Step 1: Strip the Wire**
Strip the insulation to expose **5 – 7 mm** of bare conductor. Avoid nicking the strands.

**Step 2: Select the Right Terminal & Die**
Match the terminal size to the wire gauge. On a ratcheting crimper, select the die marked for the appropriate AWG range.

**Step 3: Insert the Terminal**
Place the terminal into the crimper's nest, with the conductor barrel over the anvil.

**Step 4: Position the Wire**
Insert the stripped wire into the terminal barrel until the insulation butts against the insulation grip.

**Step 5: Crimp**
Squeeze the handles firmly until the ratchet releases. Do not stop mid-crimp — partial crimps are weak.

**Step 6: Inspect**
- **Visual:** No exposed copper strands; insulation grip holds the jacket.
- **Pull test:** Apply firm tension — the wire should not slide out.
- **Cross-section (lab):** A proper crimp shows the wire strands deformed into a near-solid mass.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Wire Wrapping Procedure

**Step 1: Select Tools**
Use an electric wire-wrap gun with the correct bit (regular or modified) and a sleeve matched to the wire gauge.

**Step 2: Strip the Wire**
Wire-wrap wire is pre-stripped. For non-pre-stripped wire, strip exactly **~25 mm**.

**Step 3: Position the Wire**
Insert the bare end into the small hole at the end of the bit.

**Step 4: Place the Post**
Seat the square post into the larger hole in the bit's center.

**Step 5: Activate the Gun**
The gun rotates the bit, wrapping **7 turns** of insulated wire around the post. The corner of the square post deforms the wire, creating a gas-tight joint.

**Step 6: Remove & Inspect**
Lift the gun. The wrap should be tight, with no gaps, and the wire should not be able to rotate around the post.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Breadboard Usage

**Step 1: Power Distribution**
Connect the positive and negative terminals of the power supply to the **red (+) and blue (−) power rails** on the breadboard.

**Step 2: Component Placement**
Insert components and jumper wires into the terminal strips. Each terminal strip of **5 holes** is internally connected.

**Step 3: IC Placement**
Place ICs across the **central channel** (notch). Each pin must connect to an independent node.

**Step 4: Verification**
Use a multimeter in continuity mode to verify connections between intended nodes.

**Step 5: Power-On Test**
Apply power and measure voltages/currents before connecting sensitive components.

### 3.6 Derivation of Heat Transfer in Soldering (Thermal Model)

The time required to reach solder flow temperature can be approximated using the lumped-capacitance heat equation:

$$
Q = m c \Delta T
$$

where $Q$ is the heat energy supplied (Joules), $m$ is the mass of the joint (kg), $c$ is the specific heat capacity (J/kg·K), and $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise (K).

For a typical through-hole joint on a **35 µm copper pad** with mass approximately $m = 0.05 \text{ g} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}$, and copper $c_{Cu} = 385 \text{ J/kg·K}$, heating from room temperature ($25 \text{ °C}$) to solder flow temperature ($200 \text{ °C}$):

$$
\Delta T = 200 - 25 = 175 \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 385 \times 175
$$

$$
Q = 3.37 \text{ J}
$$

Assuming the soldering iron delivers approximately $P = 25 \text{ W}$ of thermal power to the joint, the theoretical heating time is:

$$
t = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{3.37}{25} \approx 0.135 \text{ s}
$$

In practice, the iron must also heat the **component lead, pad, and PCB substrate**, and there are conduction losses. Empirical observation shows **2 – 4 seconds** is the practical heating window — which is the figure examiners expect.

### 3.7 Mechanical Strength Calculation (Wire Wrap Pull Force)

The pull-out force $F$ for a wire-wrapped joint can be estimated from the contact pressure $p$ between the wire and post:

$$
F = p \cdot A_{contact}
$$

For **AWG 30 wire** (diameter $d = 0.255 \text{ mm}$) wrapped on a square post of side $a = 0.635 \text{ mm}$, with **7 turns**, the contact area per turn is approximately:

$$
A_{contact,1turn} = a \cdot d = 0.635 \times 0.255 = 0.162 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{contact,total} = 7 \times 0.162 = 1.134 \text{ mm}^2
$$

With contact pressure $p \approx 200 \text{ MPa}$ (due to post corner deformation):

$$
F = 200 \times 10^6 \times 1.134 \times 10^{-6} \approx 227 \text{ N}
$$

This far exceeds the typical **50 N pull-out** requirement, confirming wire wrap's mechanical robustness.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture: Inter-Connection Method Selection Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart helps a designer or student choose the right interconnection method based on application requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Need to Build a Circuit]:::start
    B{Is it a prototype or final product?}:::decision
    C[Use BREADBOARD]:::breadboard
    D{Final product?}:::decision
    E{Will it face vibration and heat stress?}:::decision
    F[Use CRIMPING for power cables]:::crimp
    G[Use SOLDERING for PCBs]:::solder
    H{Need high reliability military/aerospace grade?}:::decision
    I[Use WIRE WRAPPING]:::wrap
    J[Use SOLDERING as default]:::solder
    K[END: Joint Selected]:::end

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> C --> K
    B -- No --> D
    D -- Yes --> E
    D -- No --> J
    E -- Yes --> F --> K
    E -- No --> G --> K
    F --> H
    H -- Yes --> I --> K
    H -- No --> K

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef breadboard fill:#F4A460,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef crimp fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef solder fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wrap fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Soldering Station Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains 230V / 50Hz]:::input
    B[Step-Down Transformer]:::block
    C[Rectifier and Filter]:::block
    D[Voltage Regulator with Temperature Sensor]:::control
    E[Soldering Iron Tip]:::output
    F[Thermocouple Feedback Loop]:::feedback

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    E -. Heat flow measured .-> F
    F -. Adjusts power .-> D

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef block fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional View: Wire-Wrap Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Square Post cross-section 0.025 inch]:::post
    B[Insulated Kynar Wire 7 turns]:::wire
    C[Gas-tight cold-weld contact at post corner]:::contact
    D[Insulation jacket intact]:::insul

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D

    classDef post fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef wire fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef contact fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef insul fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: Crimping Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strip wire 5 to 7 mm]:::step
    B[Select correct die on crimper]:::step
    C[Insert terminal into crimper nest]:::step
    D[Place stripped wire into terminal barrel]:::step
    E[Squeeze crimper until ratchet releases]:::step
    F[Inspect pull test and visual]:::step
    G[Crimp complete]:::end

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G

    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Solder Joint Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Lead Copper]:::part
    B[Through-hole Plated Pad]:::part
    C[PCB Substrate FR4]:::part
    D[Solder Fillet concave volcano shape]:::fillet
    E[Insulation gap]:::gap

    A --> D
    B --> D
    B --> C
    A --> E

    classDef part fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef fillet fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef gap fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.6 Safety Precautions Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Safety in Inter-connection Workshop]:::title
    B[Eye Protection Safety Goggles]:::safety
    C[Ventilation Fume Extractor]:::safety
    D[Heat-resistant Mat on Workbench]:::safety
    E[Lead-free Solder when possible]:::safety
    F[Tip Cleaner brass wool not wet sponge preferred]:::safety
    G[Soldering Iron Stand]:::safety
    H[First Aid Kit accessible]:::safety
    I[No eating drinking in lab]:::safety
    J[Power Off when not in use]:::safety
    K[Inspect Tools for Damage]:::safety

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    A --> G
    A --> H
    A --> I
    A --> J
    A --> K

    classDef title fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px
    classDef safety fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three types of soldering methods used in electronics manufacturing and state one specific application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per type):**

1. **Wave Soldering** — Used for mass soldering of through-hole components on PCBs. A wave of molten solder is pumped across the bottom of the board.
2. **Reflow Soldering** — Used for surface-mount device (SMD) assembly. Solder paste is pre-applied and melted in a controlled oven.
3. **Induction Soldering** — Used where localized, rapid heating is needed. High-frequency electromagnetic fields heat only the joint area.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*State the composition and melting point of the standard lead-free solder SAC305. Why is it preferred over tin-lead solder in modern manufacturing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Composition:** Tin (Sn) **96.5 %**, Silver (Ag) **3.0 %**, Copper (Cu) **0.5 %**.
- **Melting Point:** Approximately **217 – 220 °C**.
- **Preference (1 mark):** SAC305 is preferred because **lead (Pb) is toxic and restricted under the RoHS (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) directive**. SAC305 provides comparable mechanical strength and is environmentally compliant for consumer and industrial electronics sold in regulated markets.

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Module 2 Pattern]:**

**(a)** *Explain the following interconnection methods with neat diagrams:*
*(i) Breadboard (ii) Wire Wrapping*

**(b)** *Discuss the materials, tools, and step-by-step procedure for crimping a spade terminal on a stranded copper wire. Mention the safety precautions to be observed.*

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) (i) Breadboard (3.5 marks)**

A breadboard is a **solderless prototyping platform** with a matrix of holes on a 0.1-inch (2.54 mm) grid. Internally, **phosphor-bronze spring clips** connect groups of **5 holes horizontally** (terminal strips), while the **two vertical edge columns** form power rails marked + and −.

- **Construction:** ABS plastic body, nickel-silver spring contacts, spring force ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 N** per contact.
- **Operation:** Component leads or jumper wires are inserted; the spring clip exerts a normal force creating a gas-tight electrical contact.
- **Advantages:** Solderless, reusable, ideal for beginners.
- **Limitations:** Parasitic capacitance, not suitable for high-current or high-frequency work.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Breadboard Top View]:::board
    A1[Terminal Strip 5 holes connected horizontally]:::strip
    A2[Central Channel for ICs]:::channel
    A3[Power Rail + positive]:::railP
    A4[Power Rail - negative]:::railN

    A --> A1
    A --> A2
    A --> A3
    A --> A4

    classDef board fill:#F5DEB3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef strip fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef channel fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef railP fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    classDef railN fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
```

**(a) (ii) Wire Wrapping (3.5 marks)**

Wire wrapping is a **solderless, high-reliability** technique where a solid AWG 30 silver-plated copper wire (Kynar-insulated) is tightly wound around a **square or rectangular post** of side **0.025 inch (0.635 mm)**.

- **Tool:** Electric wire-wrap gun (bit + sleeve).
- **Specification:** **7 turns** of insulated wire per post.
- **Mechanism:** The **corner of the square post** cuts slightly into the wire, forming a **gas-tight, cold-welded joint** with contact resistance < **5 mΩ**.
- **Applications:** Aerospace, defense, telecom exchanges, computer backplanes.

**(b) Crimping Procedure (7 marks)**

**Materials Required (1.5 marks):**
- Stranded copper wire (e.g., 18 AWG).
- Spade (fork) terminal with insulation grip.
- Ratcheting crimper with appropriate die.
- Wire stripper.

**Tools (1 mark):**
- Ratcheting crimping tool.
- Multimeter (for continuity check).
- Inspection gauge (optional).

**Step-by-Step Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Strip the wire** to expose **5 – 7 mm** of bare copper. Avoid nicking strands.
2. **Select the correct die** on the crimper matching the wire gauge (printed on the die nest).
3. **Position the terminal** in the lower jaw (anvil) such that the conductor barrel is centered.
4. **Insert the wire** into the barrel until the insulation butts against the insulation grip section.
5. **Close the crimper** until the ratchet releases — this ensures full, calibrated pressure.
6. **Remove and inspect:** the crimp should be uniform, with the wire held firmly. A gentle pull should not dislodge the wire.
7. **Test continuity** with a multimeter.

**Safety Precautions (1.5 marks):**

- Wear **safety goggles** — terminals can fly off if the wrong die is used.
- **Power off** the circuit before crimping into an active system.
- Use **insulated crimpers** when working near live circuits.
- Do **not** crimp live wires.
- Discard deformed or over-crimped terminals — reuse leads to poor joints.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — Alternative]:**

**(a)** *Explain the different types of soldering with their temperature ranges and applications.*
**(b)** *Describe the step-by-step procedure of through-hole soldering. What are the common soldering defects and how are they avoided?*

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Types of Soldering (7 marks)**

| Type | Temperature | Application |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Soft Soldering** | < **450 °C** (typically 200 – 350 °C) | General electronics, PCB joints |
| **Hard Soldering / Brazing** | > **450 °C** (450 – 950 °C) | Plumbing, refrigeration, high-strength joints |
| **Wave Soldering** | Molten solder at **~250 °C** | Mass production of through-hole PCBs |
| **Reflow Soldering** | Pre-heat + peak at **~240 °C** (for SAC305) | SMD / BGA assembly on PCBs |
| **Induction Soldering** | Localized heating via EM induction | Selective joints, automated lines |
| **Resistance / Spot Soldering** | Localized Joule heating | Repair, battery tab welding |

> **Note for Examiner:** Students should be awarded full marks if they cover at least **three** types with **temperature** and **application**. Hard soldering = **brazing**, often confused by students.

**(b) Through-Hole Soldering Procedure (4 marks)**

1. **Clean and tin** the soldering iron tip.
2. **Insert** the component lead through the PCB hole; bend leads ~**45°** to hold the part.
3. **Heat** the lead and pad simultaneously with the iron tip.
4. **Apply solder** to the *opposite* side of the joint until it flows and forms a concave fillet.
5. **Remove solder first, then iron.** Hold the joint still until solid.
6. **Trim** the excess lead with a side cutter.
7. **Inspect** — fillet should be shiny and volcano-shaped.

**Common Defects and Remedies (3 marks):**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Cold Joint** | Joint moved during cooling; insufficient heat | Reheat, hold still, ensure proper temperature |
| **Solder Bridge** | Excess solder between adjacent pads/pins | Use solder wick or sucker to remove excess |
| **Lifted Pad** | Excessive heat or force on pad | Use lower temperature, support the board; repair with jumper wire |
| **Insufficient Solder** | Too little solder applied | Add solder and reheat |
| **Dewetting** | Contaminated pad (oxidation, oil) | Clean pad with isopropyl alcohol before soldering |
| **Icicle / Spike** | Soldering iron too cold or angle wrong | Adjust temperature; reflow with proper iron geometry |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing brazing with soldering** — Brazing is a *type* of soldering only if temperature > **450 °C** is explicitly mentioned. Many students write "hard soldering" without noting the temperature threshold. Always state the temperature.
> 2. **Calling solder a glue or adhesive** — Solder forms a **metallurgical bond (intermetallic compound)** with the base metal, *not* a glue bond. Examiners dock marks for this.
> 3. **Omitting flux** — Flux is *mandatory* in any soldering answer. Without flux, oxide layers prevent wetting.
> 4. **Forgetting safety precautions** — At least **2 – 3 safety points** (eye protection, ventilation, lead-free recommendation) are compulsory for full marks in any "explain soldering" question.
> 5. **Mixing up wire-wrap post size and wire gauge** — The standard is **0.025 inch square post** with **AWG 30 wire** and **7 turns**. Reversed numbers are a frequent error.
> 6. **Breadboard rated current** — A common examiner question: "What is the maximum current a breadboard can handle?" Answer: approximately **1 A per node** (and even less continuously). Students often guess 5 A or 10 A.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Breadboard:** Solderless, 2.54 mm pitch, 5-hole terminal strips, phosphor-bronze contacts, ~**1 A** current limit, parasitic capacitance limits high-frequency use.
- ✅ **Wire Wrap:** **AWG 30**, **7 turns**, **0.025 inch square post**, gas-tight cold weld, used in **aerospace/defense**, contact resistance < **5 mΩ**.
- ✅ **Crimping:** Permanent mechanical bond, **ratcheting crimper** essential, **pull-out force > 50 N** for signal wires, no heat required.
- ✅ **Soldering:** Soft < **450 °C**, Hard/Brazing > **450 °C**; lead-free SAC305 melts at **217 – 220 °C**; flux is mandatory.
- ✅ **Soldering Iron Tip Temperature:** **300 – 400 °C** for typical electronics; **tinning** the tip is non-negotiable.
- ✅ **Common Defects:** Cold joint, solder bridge, lifted pad, dewetting, icicle, insufficient/excess solder.
- ✅ **Materials:** Tin-lead (60/40, melts **188 °C**), lead-free SAC305, **rosin flux (RMA)**, **Kynar-insulated AWG 30** for wire wrap.
- ✅ **Tools:** Soldering iron with temperature control, brass wool tip cleaner, side cutter, tweezers, flux pen, solder sucker / wick, safety goggles, fume extractor.
- ✅ **Safety:** Eye protection, fume extraction, lead-free solder preferred, never touch the tip, always place iron in a stand, wash hands after handling leaded solder, first aid kit accessible.
- ✅ **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Prototype → **Breadboard**; High-reliability aerospace → **Wire Wrap**; Power cables / connectors → **Crimp**; PCB assembly → **Solder**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembling of electronic circuit/system on general purpose PCB, test and demonstration of functioning (Fixed voltage power supply, Square wave generation using IC 555 timer)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS  ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembling of electronic circuit/system on general purpose PCB, test and demonstration of functioning (Fixed voltage power supply, Square wave generation using IC 555 timer)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**General Purpose PCB (Printed Circuit Board)** is a fibreglass-epoxy substrate laminated with a thin layer of copper, on which electronic components are mounted and soldered to form a working electronic circuit. The board contains pre-drilled holes on a standard **2.54 mm (0.1 inch) grid pitch** and is used for prototyping, laboratory experiments, and low-volume circuit development.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (GXESL106), the workshop experiment involves:
1. **Assembling** two distinct electronic sub-systems on a single general-purpose PCB.
2. **Testing** the assembled circuit for continuity, polarity, and functional parameters.
3. **Demonstrating** the working of the assembled circuit using standard test and measurement instruments.

The two reference circuits mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:
- **Circuit A:** Fixed Voltage DC Power Supply using linear regulator IC 78xx series.
- **Circuit B:** Square Wave Generator using IC 555 Timer in Astable Multivibrator mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to physically identify components, understand the schematic-to-PCB mapping, perform soldering on a general-purpose (perforated) board, and demonstrate the output on a **CRO (Cathode Ray Oscilloscope)** or DSO for the 555 timer circuit, and a **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** for the power supply.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy for PCB Assembly:** Think of a PCB as the *skeleton* of a building and the electronic components as the *organs*. Just as a human body cannot function without placing the heart, lungs, and brain in the correct anatomical positions connected by veins and nerves, an electronic circuit cannot function unless components (resistors, capacitors, ICs) are placed in their correct *node positions* and connected by *conductive solder tracks* (the veins of the circuit).

- **Fixed Voltage Power Supply Analogy:** Imagine a water tap that fluctuates wildly between high and low pressure. A *fixed voltage regulator* is the equivalent of a *pressure stabilizer* — no matter what pressure comes in (within limits), the outlet delivers a constant, smooth, fixed pressure. So whether your AC mains is 200 V or 240 V, the output is always a stable **+5 V DC** (for 7805) or **+12 V DC** (for 7812).

- **555 Timer Square Wave Analogy:** A 555 timer in astable mode is like a *ping-pong ball machine*. It continuously throws a ball high (HIGH state), waits a moment, then throws it low (LOW state), and repeats forever. The "wait time" is controlled by **resistors and a capacitor** — like adjusting the speed of a conveyor belt in a factory.

### 1.3 Standard PCB Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Hole pitch on general purpose PCB | **2.54 mm (0.1")** | mm / inches |
| Standard copper thickness | **35 µm (1 oz/ft²)** | micrometres |
| Substrate material | **FR-2 (phenolic) or FR-4 (fibreglass epoxy)** | — |
| Soldering iron temperature (Lead-free) | **350 – 400 °C** | degrees Celsius |
| Soldering iron temperature (Lead-based Sn-Pb) | **280 – 320 °C** | degrees Celsius |
| Solder wire composition (preferred lead-free) | **Sn 99.3% / Cu 0.7%** | — |
| Standard IC 555 supply voltage (Vcc) | **+4.5 V to +15 V** | volts DC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Course Outcomes (CO):** This experiment directly maps to **CO2**: *"Identify and test the basic electronic components and circuits, and assemble simple electronic circuits on PCB."* It is assessed under the **Lab Performance** and **Viva-Voce** components in the Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subsystem A: Fixed Voltage Power Supply — Operational Breakdown

A linear DC power supply has four well-defined blocks. The understanding of each stage is essential for KTU lab viva questions.

**Stage 1 — Step-Down Transformer**
- Reduces the standard 230 V AC mains to a low AC voltage (typically 9 V, 12 V, or 15 V RMS) suitable for the rectifier.
- Operates on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Turns ratio determines output.
- Provides **isolation** between the mains and the low-voltage DC side (critical safety feature).

**Stage 2 — Bridge Rectifier (Full-Wave)**
- A bridge of **four diodes** (1N4007) arranged in a diamond configuration.
- Converts the bipolar AC input into a **pulsating unipolar DC** output.
- Conducts two diodes per half-cycle, producing output at double the input frequency (100 Hz for 50 Hz mains).
- Peak inverse voltage (PIV) of each diode must be ≥ **2 × Vmax(secondary)**.

**Stage 3 — Smoothing / Filter Capacitor**
- A large electrolytic capacitor connected across the rectifier output.
- Charges to the peak of the rectified waveform and discharges slowly through the load, reducing ripple.
- Acts as a **reservoir** of charge.
- The time constant $\tau = R_L \cdot C$ must be much greater than the ripple period **T = 1/(2f)** for low ripple.

**Stage 4 — Voltage Regulator (IC 78xx)**
- A three-terminal linear regulator (e.g., **7805 for +5 V**, **7812 for +12 V**).
- Maintains a constant output voltage regardless of variations in input voltage or load current (within specifications).
- Pin configuration (looking at the flat face with leads pointing down):
  - **Pin 1 →** INPUT (unregulated DC)
  - **Pin 2 →** GROUND
  - **Pin 3 →** OUTPUT (regulated fixed DC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the input voltage must be ≥ output + 3 V:** A linear regulator is essentially a *variable resistance* (a pass transistor) that drops the excess voltage as heat. The minimum voltage drop required across the regulator for proper regulation is called the **dropout voltage**, typically **2 V to 3 V** for the 78xx series. So for 7805, Vin must be at least **7.5 V to 8 V DC** to get a clean 5 V output.

### 2.2 Subsystem B: Square Wave Generator using IC 555 Timer

The **IC 555** is one of the most widely used analog timing ICs in the world. In this experiment, it is wired in the **Astable Multivibrator** mode — meaning it has **no stable state**. The output continuously toggles between HIGH and LOW, producing a **square wave**.

**Key Internal Blocks of IC 555:**
- A **voltage divider** made of three equal **5 kΩ** resistors (hence the name "555").
- Two **comparators** (Threshold and Trigger).
- An **SR Flip-Flop** (Set-Reset Latch).
- A **discharge transistor** (open-collector).
- An **output driver** stage.

**Operating Sequence (Astable Mode):**
1. Initially, the capacitor C is uncharged. Trigger comparator output is HIGH, so flip-flop is SET, output is HIGH, and the discharge transistor is OFF.
2. Capacitor C charges through **R1 and R2** toward Vcc. Voltage across C rises exponentially.
3. When voltage across C reaches **2/3 Vcc**, the threshold comparator fires, RESETting the flip-flop. Output goes LOW, and the discharge transistor turns ON.
4. Capacitor C now discharges through **R2 only** (via the discharge transistor) toward ground.
5. When voltage across C falls to **1/3 Vcc**, the trigger comparator fires again, SETting the flip-flop. Output goes HIGH, discharge transistor turns OFF, and the cycle repeats.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **core derivations** expected in KTU viva, lab record, and ESE questions. Memorize the table below thoroughly.

**Subsystem A — Power Supply Formulas**

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| RMS secondary voltage | $V_{sec(RMS)} = V_{primary} \times \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Transformer turns ratio equation |
| Peak secondary voltage | $V_{peak} = V_{sec(RMS)} \times \sqrt{2}$ | Peak of AC waveform |
| DC output after rectifier | $V_{DC} = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{peak}}{\pi}$ | Average DC of full-wave rectified sine |
| Ripple voltage (peak-to-peak) | $V_{ripple(pp)} = \dfrac{I_{load}}{f_r \cdot C}$ | $f_r = 2f_{line}$ for full-wave |
| Ripple factor | $\gamma = \dfrac{V_{ripple(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}$ for full-wave | For ideal full-wave rectifier |
| Regulator dropout condition | $V_{in(min)} \geq V_{out} + V_{dropout}$ | $V_{dropout} \approx 2$ to $3$ V |

**Subsystem B — IC 555 Astable Formulas**

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Time HIGH (charging) | $T_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C$ | Capacitor charges from 1/3 Vcc to 2/3 Vcc |
| Time LOW (discharging) | $T_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C$ | Capacitor discharges from 2/3 Vcc to 1/3 Vcc |
| Total time period | $T = T_H + T_L = 0.693 \times (R_1 + 2R_2) \times C$ | Sum of charge + discharge |
| Frequency of oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C}$ | **Most important KTU formula** |
| Duty Cycle (D) | $D = \dfrac{T_H}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$ | Always > 50% in standard astable |
| Maximum output frequency | $f_{max} \approx 500$ kHz (typical) | Limited by internal switching delays |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear regulators like 7805 are used in nearly every commercial electronic device — mobile chargers (after SMPS), microcontroller power rails, sensor circuits, and digital logic systems. The 555 timer is used in toys, timers, pulse generators, PWM controllers, LED flashers, and burglar alarms — it is the **Swiss Army knife of analog ICs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 555 Timer Frequency Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU exam on this topic. The derivation begins from the standard RC charging equation.

**Starting Point: Voltage across a charging capacitor in an RC series circuit.**

Let the capacitor be charging from initial voltage $V_0$ towards final voltage $V_{cc}$ through a series resistance $R_{eq}$. The instantaneous voltage across the capacitor at time $t$ is given by the universal RC charging formula:

$$V_C(t) = V_{cc} - (V_{cc} - V_0) \cdot e^{-t / (R_{eq} \cdot C)}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the charging path and resistance.**

During the charging phase, current flows from $V_{cc}$ through **R1**, then through **R2**, then into the capacitor C. The discharge transistor is OFF (open circuit). Therefore, the equivalent series charging resistance is:

$$R_{eq,charge} = R_1 + R_2$$

The capacitor charges from the lower threshold voltage $V_{low} = \dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ to the upper threshold voltage $V_{high} = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary conditions.**

Substituting $V_C(0) = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$ and $V_C(T_H) = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3}$ into the charging equation:

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3} = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}\right) \cdot e^{-T_H / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the term inside the bracket.**

$$V_{cc} - \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3} = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3}$$

Substituting back:

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3} = V_{cc} - \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the exponential term.**

Move $V_{cc}$ to the left side:

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3} - V_{cc} = -\dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

$$-\dfrac{V_{cc}}{3} = -\dfrac{2 \cdot V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel common factors on both sides.**

Dividing both sides by $-\dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$:

$$1 = 2 \cdot e^{-T_H / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]}$$

$$e^{-T_H / [(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C]} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-\dfrac{T_H}{(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $T_H$.**

$$T_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C$$

This is the **Time HIGH** of the 555 astable output.

**Step 8 — Repeat for the discharging phase.**

During discharge, the discharge transistor is ON, effectively shorting Pin 7 to ground. The capacitor now discharges from $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ to $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ through **R2 only** (R1 is bypassed because the discharge transistor provides a low-impedance path to ground for the junction between R1 and R2).

Following an identical procedure with $R_{eq,discharge} = R_2$:

$$T_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C$$

**Step 9 — Compute total period and frequency.**

$$T = T_H + T_L = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C + 0.693 \times R_2 \times C$$

$$T = 0.693 \times (R_1 + 2R_2) \times C$$

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the 555 astable frequency formula.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:** Design a 555 astable multivibrator to generate a square wave of frequency **f = 1 kHz** with a duty cycle of approximately **75%**. Assume C = 0.1 µF.

**Step 1 — Use the duty cycle equation to find the ratio of R2 to R1.**

$$D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

$$0.75 = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2}$$

$$0.75 \times (R_1 + 2R_2) = R_1 + R_2$$

$$0.75 R_1 + 1.5 R_2 = R_1 + R_2$$

$$0.5 R_2 = 0.25 R_1$$

$$R_1 = 2 R_2$$

**Step 2 — Use the frequency equation to find absolute values.**

$$f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

$$R_1 + 2R_2 = \dfrac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_1 + 2R_2 = 14400 \, \Omega = 14.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R_1 = 2R_2$ and solve.**

$$2R_2 + 2R_2 = 14400$$

$$4R_2 = 14400$$

$$R_2 = 3600 \, \Omega \approx 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_1 = 2 \times 3600 = 7200 \, \Omega \approx 7.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Verify by computing back.**

$$T_H = 0.693 \times (7200 + 3600) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.747 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_L = 0.693 \times 3600 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.249 \text{ ms}$$

$$T = 0.747 + 0.249 = 0.996 \text{ ms} \approx 1 \text{ ms} \rightarrow f = 1 \text{ kHz} \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Power Supply Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A 230 V / 12 V-1A step-down transformer feeds a bridge rectifier followed by a 2200 µF filter capacitor and a 7805 regulator. Determine: (a) Peak secondary voltage, (b) DC output after rectification, (c) Ripple voltage (rms) at full load.

**Step 1 — Peak secondary voltage.**

$$V_{peak} = V_{sec(RMS)} \times \sqrt{2} = 12 \times 1.414 = 16.97 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — DC output after full-wave rectification.**

$$V_{DC} = \dfrac{2 \times V_{peak}}{\pi} = \dfrac{2 \times 16.97}{3.1416} = 10.81 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Ripple voltage (peak-to-peak) at full load (1 A).**

Ripple frequency for full-wave rectifier with 50 Hz mains: $f_r = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Hz}$

$$V_{ripple(pp)} = \dfrac{I_{load}}{f_r \times C} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 2200 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.55 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Ripple voltage (rms).**

$$V_{ripple(rms)} = \dfrac{V_{ripple(pp)}}{2\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{4.55}{3.464} = 1.31 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the regulator dropout condition.**

Minimum input to 7805 for clean 5 V output: $V_{in(min)} = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ V}$

The minimum instantaneous input to the regulator = $V_{DC} - V_{ripple(pp)}/2 = 10.81 - 2.27 = 8.54 \text{ V}$

Since $8.54 \text{ V} > 8 \text{ V}$ → the regulator will work correctly at full load. **Design is valid.**

### 3.4 Component Identification Quick Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Viva Favourite:** Know how to physically identify each component by its appearance and markings.

| Component | Visual Cue | Function in Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor | Cylindrical, colour-coded bands | Limits current, sets time constant |
| Electrolytic Capacitor | Cylindrical, has polarity stripe | Stores charge, smooths DC |
| Ceramic Capacitor | Small disc, orange/yellow | Bypass / decoupling |
| Diode 1N4007 | Black cylinder, silver band at cathode | Rectification |
| IC 7805 / 7812 | Black rectangular with 3 leads, metal tab | Voltage regulation |
| IC 555 Timer | 8-pin DIP, "NE555" marking | Oscillator / timer |
| LED | Domed, longer lead is anode | Visual indicator of output |
| Transformer | Heavy, two windings on iron core | AC voltage step-down |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Experiment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        nodeA[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> nodeB[Step Down Transformer 230V to 12V]
    end

    subgraph POWER["CIRCUIT A: FIXED VOLTAGE POWER SUPPLY"]
        nodeB --> nodeC[Bridge Rectifier 4 x 1N4007]
        nodeC --> nodeD[Filter Capacitor 2200 uF 25V]
        nodeD --> nodeE[Voltage Regulator IC 7805]
        nodeE --> nodeF[Regulated DC Output 5V]
        nodeF --> nodeG[LED Indicator with 330 ohm Resistor]
    end

    subgraph OSCILLATOR["CIRCUIT B: 555 TIMER ASTABLE"]
        nodeF -. Power Supply for 555 .-> nodeH[IC 555 Timer Astable Mode]
        nodeH --> nodeI[Resistor R1 1 kilo ohm]
        nodeH --> nodeJ[Resistor R2 1 kilo ohm]
        nodeH --> nodeK[Capacitor C 0.1 uF]
        nodeH --> nodeL[Square Wave Output Pin 3]
        nodeL --> nodeM[Output LED Indicator]
    end

    subgraph TEST["TEST AND MEASUREMENT"]
        nodeF --> nodeN[Digital Multimeter DMM]
        nodeL --> nodeO[CRO or DSO]
    end

    style INPUT fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style POWER fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeB -->|AC 12V RMS| nodeC[Bridge Rectifier D1 D2 D3 D4]
    nodeC -->|Pulsating DC ~16.97V peak| nodeD[Capacitor C1 2200uF 25V]
    nodeD -->|Filtered DC ~10.8V average| nodeE[Pin 1 of 7805]
    nodeE -->|Internal Regulation| nodeF[Pin 3 of 7805]
    nodeF -->|Regulated 5V DC| nodeG[Output Terminal Plus]
    nodeF -->|Current Limiting| nodeH[330 ohm Resistor]
    nodeH --> nodeI[LED Anode]

    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeK
```

```mermaid
    nodeA -->|Pin 1 Ground Reference| nodeB

    nodeC[Resistor R1 1 kilo ohm] -->|Pin 7 to Pin 8 path| nodeB
```

### 4.4 PCB Assembly Procedure — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Step 1: Study Schematic and Identify All Components] --> nodeB[Step 2: Test Each Component with Multimeter]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Place Components on General Purpose PCB]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Bend Leads and Insert into Holes]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Solder Each Joint Using 25W Iron]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: Cut Excess Leads with Cutter]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 7: Visual Inspection for Cold Joints]

    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 10: Demonstrate Working to Faculty]
```
