
### 4.5 Waveform Diagram — Expected Output on CRO

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHARGING["CHARGING PHASE - OUTPUT HIGH"]
        nodeA[Capacitor Voltage 1/3 Vcc] -->|Rises Exponentially through R1 and R2| nodeB[Capacitor Voltage 2/3 Vcc]
    end
    subgraph DISCHARGING["DISCHARGING PHASE - OUTPUT LOW"]
        nodeB -->|Falls Exponentially through R2 only| nodeA
    end
    nodeB -. Threshold Comparator trips .-> nodeC[Output Pin 3 goes LOW]
    nodeA -. Trigger Comparator trips .-> nodeD[Output Pin 3 goes HIGH]
    nodeC -. Discharge Transistor ON .-> nodeE[Capacitor discharges via R2]
    nodeD -. Discharge Transistor OFF .-> nodeF[Capacitor charges via R1 and R2]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] List any three important characteristics of a general-purpose PCB used for assembling prototype electronic circuits.**

**Model Answer:**
A general-purpose PCB has the following characteristics:
1. **Standard hole pitch** of **2.54 mm (0.1")** allowing easy insertion of through-hole components like resistors, capacitors, and DIP ICs.
2. **Pre-drilled holes** on a uniform grid with **copper pads (lands)** around each hole for soldering component leads.
3. Made of **FR-2 phenolic** or **FR-4 fibreglass epoxy** substrate laminated with a **35 µm copper layer**, providing mechanical strength and electrical conductivity.
4. Often has separate **power rails** and **ground planes** along the edges for easy distribution of supply voltages.
5. Suitable only for **low-frequency, low-power, prototype-stage** circuits — not for high-speed or RF designs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Mention the function of the control voltage pin (Pin 5) of IC 555 timer in astable mode. What is its standard purpose?**

**Model Answer:**
Pin 5 of the IC 555 is the **Control Voltage (CV) pin**. It is internally connected to the inverting input of the upper threshold comparator at a node that sits at **2/3 Vcc** (set by the internal 5 kΩ resistor divider).

**Functions:**
1. It allows external modulation of the threshold level, which in turn **changes the frequency** of the astable output.
2. By applying a varying DC voltage at Pin 5, the 555 can be converted into a **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** or used for **Frequency Modulation (FM)**.
3. In normal fixed-frequency applications, a **decoupling capacitor of 0.01 µF** is connected from Pin 5 to ground to **filter out noise** that could otherwise cause jitter or unstable oscillation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a fixed voltage DC power supply using IC 7805. List the functions of each block and calculate the value of the filter capacitor required to limit the peak-to-peak ripple voltage to **1 V** when delivering a load current of **500 mA** from a **full-wave rectifier** operating at **50 Hz** mains frequency.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram Working:**

The fixed voltage power supply consists of four cascaded blocks:

1. **Step-Down Transformer:** Reduces 230 V AC mains to 12 V AC (RMS) using electromagnetic induction and electromagnetic coupling. Provides **galvanic isolation** for safety.
2. **Bridge Rectifier:** Four diodes 1N4007 arranged in a Wheatstone-bridge configuration perform full-wave rectification. During the positive half-cycle, D1 and D2 conduct; during the negative half-cycle, D3 and D4 conduct. The output is **unipolar pulsating DC** at 100 Hz (double the line frequency).
3. **Filter Capacitor:** A large electrolytic capacitor (e.g., 2200 µF, 25 V) connected in parallel with the load. It charges to the peak voltage during each rectified pulse and discharges through the load during the gaps, smoothing the DC output. Reduces the AC ripple content.
4. **Voltage Regulator (IC 7805):** A three-terminal linear regulator. Pin 1 receives the unregulated DC (≥ 7.5 V), Pin 2 is ground, and Pin 3 provides a **constant +5 V DC** output. It uses an internal bandgap reference and a feedback loop to maintain output voltage constant despite variations in input voltage or load current.

**Capacitor Calculation:**

For a full-wave rectifier, ripple frequency $f_r = 2 \times 50 = 100$ Hz.

The peak-to-peak ripple voltage for a capacitor-filtered full-wave rectifier is given by:

$$V_{ripple(pp)} = \dfrac{I_{load}}{f_r \times C}$$

Rearranging for C:

$$C = \dfrac{I_{load}}{f_r \times V_{ripple(pp)}}$$

Substituting values $I_{load} = 500$ mA $= 0.5$ A, $f_r = 100$ Hz, $V_{ripple(pp)} = 1$ V:

$$C = \dfrac{0.5}{100 \times 1} = 0.005 \text{ F} = 5000 \text{ µF}$$

A standard value of **4700 µF / 25 V** is the closest commercial choice and may be used. **Design complete.**

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** For an astable multivibrator using IC 555 timer, derive the expression for the **frequency of oscillation** in terms of resistors R1, R2, and capacitor C. Also calculate the frequency and duty cycle when **R1 = 4.7 kΩ**, **R2 = 10 kΩ**, and **C = 0.1 µF**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1 of these notes):**

During the charging phase, the capacitor charges from $\dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{2 V_{cc}}{3}$ through $R_1 + R_2$. Applying the universal RC charging equation and isolating the exponential term gives:

$$T_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C$$

During the discharging phase, the capacitor discharges from $\dfrac{2 V_{cc}}{3}$ to $\dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$ through $R_2$ only. A similar derivation gives:

$$T_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C$$

The total time period is:

$$T = T_H + T_L = 0.693 \times (R_1 + 2R_2) \times C$$

Therefore, the frequency of oscillation is:

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $R_1 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega = 4700 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10000 \, \Omega$, $C = 0.1 \, \muF = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ F:

**Step 1 — Compute total period.**

$$T = 0.693 \times (4700 + 2 \times 10000) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T = 0.693 \times 24700 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$T = 0.693 \times 2.47 \times 10^{-3} = 1.711 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.711 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute frequency.**

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{1.711 \times 10^{-3}} = 584.5 \text{ Hz} \approx 585 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute duty cycle.**

$$D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4700 + 10000}{4700 + 20000} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \dfrac{14700}{24700} \times 100\% = 59.5\%$$

**Result:** $f \approx 585$ Hz, Duty Cycle $D \approx 59.5\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of IC 555 timer connected in **astable multivibrator mode**. Draw the waveforms at (i) the capacitor, and (ii) the output pin.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Description:**
The IC 555 is wired in astable mode with the following connections:
- **Pin 1:** Ground
- **Pin 2 (Trigger) and Pin 6 (Threshold):** Tied together and connected to the junction of R2 and C
- **Pin 3:** Output (square wave)
- **Pin 4 (Reset):** Connected to Vcc (active low reset disabled)
- **Pin 5 (Control Voltage):** Decoupled to ground via 0.01 µF capacitor
- **Pin 7 (Discharge):** Connected to the junction of R1 and R2
- **Pin 8 (Vcc):** Connected to +5 V (supplied by the 7805 power supply built on the same PCB)

**Operating Cycle:**

When power is applied, capacitor C begins charging through R1 and R2 from 0 V. The output is initially HIGH.

**Phase 1 — Capacitor Charging (Output HIGH):**
- The discharge transistor (Q1) is OFF.
- C charges through R1 and R2 toward Vcc.
- When voltage across C reaches $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$, the **threshold comparator** fires and resets the internal flip-flop.
- Output goes LOW, and the discharge transistor turns ON.

**Phase 2 — Capacitor Discharging (Output LOW):**
- C discharges through R2 only (and into the discharge transistor) toward 0 V.
- When voltage across C drops to $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$, the **trigger comparator** fires and sets the flip-flop.
- Output goes HIGH, discharge transistor turns OFF, and the cycle repeats indefinitely.

**Waveforms:**
- The **capacitor voltage** is a **sawtooth-like exponential waveform** oscillating between $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ and $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$.
- The **output at Pin 3** is a **square wave** that is HIGH during the charging phase and LOW during the discharging phase.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to assemble a fixed voltage power supply on a **general purpose PCB**, including the tools used and precautions to be taken.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Required Tools and Components:**
- General purpose PCB (FR-2 / FR-4, 2.54 mm pitch)
- Step-down transformer (230 V / 12 V, 1 A)
- Four diodes 1N4007
- Electrolytic capacitor 2200 µF / 25 V
- IC 7805 voltage regulator
- LED, 330 Ω resistor
- Soldering iron (25 W – 40 W), solder wire (Sn-Pb or lead-free)
- Wire cutters, needle-nose pliers
- Digital multimeter (for testing)
- CRO / DSO (for waveform observation in 555 section)

**Assembly Procedure:**

1. **Study the schematic** carefully. Identify each component visually using the markings.
2. **Test components before soldering:** Use a multimeter to check resistor values, diode polarity, and capacitor health.
3. **Plan component placement:** Place taller components first (transformer, big capacitors), then smaller ones.
4. **Insert components into PCB holes** in the correct orientation. Pay special attention to:
   - **Polarity of electrolytic capacitors** (longer lead = positive, stripe = negative).
   - **Polarity of diodes** (silver band = cathode).
   - **Pin 1 of IC 7805 and IC 555** (marked by a dot or notch).
5. **Bend the leads** slightly outward on the solder side to hold the component in place.
6. **Solder each joint:** Heat the pad and lead simultaneously, then feed solder wire into the joint (not onto the iron tip). A good joint should be **shiny, concave, and volcano-shaped**.
7. **Trim the excess leads** with a cutter, leaving 1–2 mm above the solder joint.
8. **Perform a visual inspection** for cold joints, solder bridges, or missing connections.
9. **Continuity test:** Use the multimeter in continuity mode to verify that connected nodes are properly joined and no short circuits exist between power and ground.
10. **Power ON and test:** Connect to 230 V AC mains. Measure the DC output with a multimeter (should read **+5 V** for 7805) and observe the LED glow.

**Precautions:**
- Never touch the **mains side** of the transformer — it is **lethal**.
- Use an **isolated soldering iron** or a **low-voltage (12 V) soldering station** if available.
- Avoid breathing solder fumes — work in a **well-ventilated area**.
- Do not overheat components — the ICs can be damaged by excessive heat.
- Use a **heat sink clip** on the IC 7805 if the load current exceeds 500 mA.
- Always **disconnect mains power** before making any wiring changes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the 555 timing formulas:** Students frequently write $f = \dfrac{1}{(R_1 + R_2) \cdot C}$ or $f = \dfrac{0.693}{RC}$. The correct factor is **1.44**, not **0.693**, in the final frequency formula. The **0.693** appears in $T_H$ and $T_L$ individually, not in the final $f$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the dropout voltage for 78xx regulators:** Students often assume a 7805 will work with any input voltage. Examiners expect you to mention that **Vin must be ≥ Vout + (2 to 3) V** for proper regulation.
> 
> 3. **Not mentioning the role of the control voltage pin (Pin 5) and reset pin (Pin 4):** Pin 4 is an **active-low reset** — leaving it floating can cause erratic behaviour or no output. Examiners specifically test this in viva.
> 
> 4. **Missing the derivation step in frequency formula questions:** Writing just the final formula $f = 1.44 / [(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C]$ without showing the RC charging derivation will earn only 2 out of 7 marks. Always show the step where you set the boundary conditions $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ and $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ and solve the exponential.
> 
> 5. **Reversing capacitor polarity in power supply circuit:** This is the single most common cause of **component explosion** during lab testing. A reversed electrolytic capacitor can burst violently, and the student is marked **zero** for the lab session.
> 
> 6. **Not specifying practical component values:** KTU examiners expect practical resistor values like **1 kΩ, 2.2 kΩ, 4.7 kΩ, 10 kΩ** (E12 series) — not unrealistic values like 3725 Ω.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this section 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ A **general purpose PCB** has a **2.54 mm (0.1") hole pitch** and is used for prototype assembly only.
- ✅ A **fixed voltage power supply** has four blocks: **Transformer → Bridge Rectifier → Filter Capacitor → Voltage Regulator**.
- ✅ The **IC 78xx** regulator family is a **linear, three-terminal, fixed-output** device. The "xx" denotes the output voltage (05 for 5 V, 12 for 12 V).
- ✅ The minimum input to a 78xx regulator must satisfy **Vin ≥ Vout + 3 V (dropout voltage)**.
- ✅ The **IC 555 timer** in astable mode generates a continuous **square wave** with no external triggering.
- ✅ The frequency formula is the **golden equation** for this topic: 
$$f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C}$$
- ✅ The **duty cycle** of a standard 555 astable is **always greater than 50%** (it can never go below 50% in the basic configuration). To get below 50%, a **diode** must be added in parallel with R2.
- ✅ The capacitor voltage in astable mode swings between **Vcc/3** and **2Vcc/3** — not between 0 and Vcc.
- ✅ **Pin 4 (Reset)** of the 555 is **active low** — must be tied to Vcc for normal operation. Floating this pin disables the output.
- ✅ **Pin 5 (Control Voltage)** should be decoupled to ground with a **0.01 µF capacitor** to prevent noise injection.
- ✅ The **discharge transistor** (internal to 555) shorts Pin 7 to ground during the LOW phase, forcing the capacitor to discharge through R2 only.
- ✅ The **bridge rectifier** uses **four diodes** and produces a **full-wave rectified** output with ripple frequency = **2 × mains frequency** (100 Hz for 50 Hz).
- ✅ The **ripple voltage** for a capacitor-filtered rectifier is given by **Vripple(pp) = I_load / (f_r × C)** — a higher capacitance means lower ripple.
- ✅ The **PEAK inverse voltage (PIV)** of each diode in a bridge rectifier must be **≥ 2 × Vpeak(secondary)**.
- ✅ For the **lab demonstration**, the **power supply output** is measured using a **DMM** (expect a reading of **+5.00 V ± 0.25 V** for 7805), and the **555 output** is observed on a **CRO** (expect a square wave of frequency calculated from the chosen R1, R2, C values).
- ✅ **Soldering safety:** Always use a **third hand / PCB holder**, keep the **soldering iron in a stand** when not in use, and **never touch the mains side** of the transformer during testing.
- ✅ The **IC 555** is the most popular timer IC in history — originally designed by **Hans Camenzind** in 1971. It has been in continuous production for over **50 years** and is still sold in billions of units annually.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembling of electronic circuits using SMT (Surface Mount Technology) stations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS  ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembling of electronic circuits using SMT (Surface Mount Technology) stations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Surface Mount Technology (SMT)** is a modern method of constructing electronic circuits in which the components, known as **Surface Mount Devices (SMDs)**, are mounted directly onto the surface of a Printed Circuit Board (PCB), as opposed to the older **Through-Hole Technology (THT)** where components are inserted into drilled holes and soldered on the opposite side. The entire process is performed using a coordinated chain of automated **SMT stations** (also called a *Surface Mount Assembly Line* or *SMT Pick-and-Place Line*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** SMT is a technology that automates the placement of SMD components onto a PCB using solder paste and a reflow soldering process, enabling high component density, miniaturization, and high-speed mass production of electronic assemblies.

An **SMT Station** typically refers to a single bench-top or semi-automated workstation used in educational/laboratory settings, comprising:
- A **solder paste stencil printer** (manual or semi-auto)
- A **pick-and-place machine** (manual vacuum pen / semi-automatic)
- A **reflow soldering oven / hot plate / hot air gun**
- **Inspection tools** (magnifier, microscope, continuity tester)
- **Soldering accessories** (tweezers, flux, wick, cleaning solvent)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy: "Stickers vs. Sewing Buttons"**
> - Imagine a **school shirt**: Old way (**THT**) — buttons are *sewn* (pierced through holes in the fabric and locked at the back). New way (**SMT**) — *iron-on patches/stickers* are placed on the surface and bonded by heat.
> - The shirt is your **PCB**, the buttons/patches are **electronic components**, the glue is **solder paste**, and the hot iron is the **reflow oven**. SMT is faster, neater, allows tinier "patches" on both sides, and works beautifully for mass production — exactly how modern smartphones are built.

## 1.3 Why SMT? – Key Engineering Advantages

| Parameter | Through-Hole Technology (THT) | Surface Mount Technology (SMT) |
|---|---|---|
| Component Lead | Axial / Radial leads | Leadless or short leads |
| Mounting Side | One side only | Both sides possible |
| Component Density | Low (≈ 2–3 components / cm²) | High (≈ 10–30 components / cm²) |
| Hole Drilling | Required on PCB | **Not required** (saves drilling cost) |
| High-Frequency Performance | Poor (long leads = parasitic L) | **Excellent** (short leads = low ESL) |
| Production Speed | Slow, manual | **Automated, very fast** |
| Mechanical Strength | High (good for connectors) | Lower (good for ICs, resistors) |
| Repair / Prototyping | Easy with hand soldering | Requires special hot-air tools |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot Keyword:** In exams, always justify SMT by mentioning the three pillars: **miniaturization, automation, and high-frequency performance**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Bold Highlighted)

- **IPC-A-610 Standard:** Industry-accepted acceptability standard for electronic assemblies (used globally in SMT).
- **Standard SMD Resistor Sizes:** 0201, 0402, 0603, 0805, 1206 (imperial code = length × width in inches × 10⁻²).
- **Typical Reflow Peak Temperature (lead-free SAC305):** **245 °C ± 5 °C** above the melting point of solder.
- **Melting Point of Sn-Pb (63/37) solder:** **183 °C**.
- **Melting Point of SAC305 (Sn96.5/Ag3.0/Cu0.5) lead-free:** **217 °C**.
- **Solder Paste Metal Load (typical):** **88 – 90 %** metal, balance flux.
- **Solder Powder Particle Size:** Type 3 (25 – 45 µm), Type 4 (20 – 38 µm) for fine-pitch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Operational Pillars of an SMT Line

The entire SMT process, although executed by different stations, follows a strict logical sequence. We can break it into six pillars:

1. **Solder Paste Preparation** – Flux + tin alloy powder mixed into a paste of correct viscosity.
2. **PCB Preparation & Cleaning** – PCB is cleaned, dried, and clamped on the station. Pad surfaces are inspected for oxidation.
3. **Solder Paste Printing** – A **stainless-steel stencil** with apertures is aligned over the PCB; paste is forced through apertures using a **squeegee**.
4. **Component Pick-and-Place** – SMDs are picked (vacuum nozzle) and placed accurately on the wet paste deposits.
5. **Reflow Soldering** – The PCB travels through a **temperature profile** in a reflow oven (or hot plate) with zones: *Preheat → Soak → Reflow → Cooling*.
6. **Inspection & Quality Control (AOI/Visual)** – Optical inspection, X-ray for BGAs, and electrical continuity test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Each Pillar:**
> - Paste must be **rheologically correct** to hold the component before reflow (*tackiness*).
> - Stencil aperture **geometry** (Area Ratio) decides whether paste releases cleanly.
> - Reflow profile must reach **above liquidus** for a fixed time to form proper intermetallic joints (e.g., Cu₆Sn₅, Cu₃Sn).

## 2.2 The Reflow Soldering Temperature Profile (Critical Theory)

A standard **lead-free reflow profile** has four zones, each with a specific thermal job:

| Zone | Purpose | Typical Temp Range | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preheat (Ramp-Up)** | Slowly raise PCB temperature to avoid thermal shock | 25 °C → 150 °C | 60 – 90 s |
| **Soak / Pre-flow** | Activate flux, evaporate solvents, equalize temp | 150 °C → 200 °C | 60 – 120 s |
| **Reflow (Talcum Peak)** | Melting of solder, wetting, joint formation | 217 °C → 245 °C (peak) | 30 – 60 s above 217 °C |
| **Cooling** | Solidify joints, control grain structure | 245 °C → < 100 °C | 60 – 90 s |

**Ramp-Up Rate (typical):** **1 – 3 °C / second** (faster rates cause tombstoning / solder splatter).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations and design rules are directly testable in KTU university exams. Memorize the table verbatim.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Area Ratio (AR)} = \dfrac{\text{Aperture Area}}{\text{Aperture Wall Area}} = \dfrac{L \times W}{2\,(L+W)\,T}$ | Predicts solder paste release from stencil; **AR ≥ 0.66** is the industry acceptance rule (IPC-7525) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_{\text{peak}} \geq T_{\text{liquidus}} + 20\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Reflow peak must exceed liquidus by at least 20 °C for proper wetting | °C |
| 3 | $\text{Tombstoning Force Balance} \quad F_{\text{wet}} = \gamma \cdot L \cdot \cos\theta$ | Uneven wetting force on small chips (e.g., 0201) lifts one end, forming a "tombstone" defect | Newtons |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{\text{PCB}}(t) = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | Approximate transient heat rise in PCB during preheat (k depends on mass/area) | °C |
| 5 | $\text{Stencil Thickness: } T_s = 0.05\,\text{mm to } 0.20\,\text{mm}$ | Standard range; finer pitch → thinner stencil | mm |
| 6 | $\text{Solder Paste Viscosity (Brookfield):} \;\eta \approx 800 \text{ – } 1100$ kcps | Spindle 5 rpm, malcom/spindle viscometer | kcps |
| 7 | $\text{Cooling Rate} \le 4\,^{\circ}\text{C/s}$ | Above this, joints become brittle due to coarse grain | °C/s |
| 8 | $\text{Pitch Limit for Manual SMT} \approx 0.5\,\text{mm}$ | Finer pitches require AOI + AAR (auto-alignment) | mm |
| 9 | $\text{Solder Joint Strength} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\text{PCB CTE mismatch}}}$ | Minimizing Coefficient-of-Thermal-Expansion mismatch reduces thermal fatigue cycles | cycles to failure |
| 10 | $\text{Resistor Code Conversion: } \text{0805} \equiv 0.08'' \times 0.05''$ | Imperial to metric: multiply by 25.4 → **2.0 mm × 1.25 mm** | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never write `|x|` inside a markdown table** — KTU board evaluations use raw markdown; vertical bars break column syntax. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Electronics:** Every smartphone motherboard (Apple A-series chips) is built via SMT. A single iPhone motherboard contains **> 1000 SMDs** assembled in seconds.
- **Automotive (ECU, ADAS):** SMT's reliability at high vibration/temperature is critical — through-hole would be too bulky.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers, Hearing Aids):** SMT enables *miniaturization* that THT simply cannot achieve.
- **Aerospace & IoT:** SMT is the *de facto* standard for high-frequency RF circuits (5G, Wi-Fi, satellite).
- **Educational Labs (KTU Workshop):** Manual SMT stations help students understand **process engineering**, **yield optimization**, and **defect analysis** (AAR > 60% in KTU lab record marks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: The Area Ratio (AR) of a Stencil Aperture

> [!NOTE]
> The Area Ratio is the **single most important stencil design rule** for KTU exams and is derived from basic geometry.

**Problem Setup:** A rectangular stencil aperture has length $L$, width $W$, and stencil thickness $T$. We must show that for clean paste release, $\text{AR} \ge 0.66$.

### Step 1 — Aperture (Top) Area
The top surface of the paste deposit equals the area of the opening in the stencil:

$$
A_{\text{aperture}} = L \times W
$$

### Step 2 — Aperture Wall Area (Paste-to-Wall Adhesion)
The paste must also slide along the **inner walls** of the aperture. The total wall area is the perimeter multiplied by stencil thickness:

$$
P = 2\,(L + W)
$$

$$
A_{\text{wall}} = P \times T = 2\,(L+W)\,T
$$

### Step 3 — Definition of Area Ratio
By definition (IPC-7525), the Area Ratio is the **adhesion area to the PCB (top)** divided by the **adhesion area to the wall (during release)**:

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{wall}}} = \frac{L \times W}{2\,(L+W)\,T}
$$

### Step 4 — Applying the 0.66 Rule
For the paste to release cleanly from a vertical-walled stencil, gravity (which pulls paste out) must overcome paste-to-wall adhesion. Empirically, this happens when:

$$
\text{AR} \ge 0.66
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
Given: $L = 2.0\,\text{mm}$, $W = 1.0\,\text{mm}$, $T = 0.15\,\text{mm}$ (typical 0805 footprint).

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{2.0 \times 1.0}{2\,(2.0 + 1.0)\times 0.15}
$$

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{2.0}{2 \times 3.0 \times 0.15}
$$

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{2.0}{0.9}
$$

$$
\text{AR} \approx 2.22
$$

Since $2.22 \ge 0.66$, the stencil design is **acceptable** for the 0805 pad.

> [!TIP]
> **Reverse Calculation:** If the same aperture had a thickness $T = 0.30\,\text{mm}$ (a thicker stencil), the AR would drop to ≈ 1.11, still acceptable. If the chip were 0201 (0.5 mm × 0.25 mm), the AR would be $\dfrac{0.5 \times 0.25}{2\,(0.5+0.25) \times 0.15} = \dfrac{0.125}{0.225} \approx 0.556 < 0.66$, indicating **poor paste release** — hence a thinner stencil (~ 0.10 mm) is required for 0201 parts.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common SMT Defects, Root Cause, and Cure

This **defect → root cause → cure** matrix is a **guaranteed 5–7 mark question** in KTU viva and theory.

| # | Defect Name | Appearance | Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tombstoning** | One end of a chip resistor lifted | Uneven paste volume / unequal pad heating | Use balanced aperture; symmetrical reflow profile |
| 2 | **Solder Bridging** | Solder connects two adjacent pads | Excess paste / misaligned stencil | Reduce stencil thickness; clean apertures |
| 3 | **Insufficient Solder** | Thin, dry-looking joint | Aperture too small / clogged stencil | Increase aperture size; clean stencil |
| 4 | **Cold Joint (Dull/Grainy)** | Frosty, porous joint | Peak temp not reached; flux not activated | Increase reflow time / temp within limits |
| 5 | **Solder Balling** | Tiny balls of solder around joint | Solder splatter; poor paste quality | Use fresh paste; control preheat ramp |
| 6 | **Head-in-Pillow (HiP)** | BGA ball not wetted fully | Warped BGA / uneven thermal mass | Use nitrogen reflow; profile tuning |
| 7 | **Skewed Component** | Component rotated by ≥ 5° | Inaccurate placement / vibration | Recalibrate pick-and-place; secure PCB |
| 8 | **Voiding** | Air bubbles inside joint | Outgassing of flux / moisture | Bake components before assembly (MSL handling) |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Style)

1. **Step 1 — Inspection of Bare PCB:** Check copper pads for oxidation, scratches, and alignment marks (fiducials).
2. **Step 2 — Stencil Alignment:** Place the stencil on the stencil printer; align apertures with PCB pads using fiducials.
3. **Step 3 — Solder Paste Application:** Apply a bead of solder paste at one end of the stencil. Use a **45° squeegee** to roll the paste across the stencil at a uniform speed (~ 20–25 mm/s).
4. **Step 4 — Stencil Lift (Snap-off):** Lift the stencil vertically; the paste should release cleanly onto the pads (validated by AR rule).
5. **Step 5 — Component Placement:** Use a **vacuum pen** (manual station) or **pick-and-place head** to pick each SMD and place it on the corresponding pad.
6. **Step 6 — Visual Pre-Reflow Inspection:** Check polarity, orientation (diodes, ICs), and offset (< 25% of pad width).
7. **Step 7 — Reflow Soldering:** Place the PCB on the reflow oven / hot plate. Run the **4-zone profile** (Preheat → Soak → Reflow → Cooling).
8. **Step 8 — Post-Reflow Inspection (AOI/Visual):** Use a microscope to check joints. Use a multimeter to test continuity.
9. **Step 9 — Cleaning:** Clean flux residue with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and a soft brush.
10. **Step 10 — Record Yield:** Count acceptable joints vs. total joints. KTU mandates a **yield of ≥ 90%** for full marks in the lab record.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    B --> C[stepC["STEP 3 SOLDER PASTE PRINTING SQUEEGEE"]:::stage]
    C --> D[stepD["STEP 4 PASTE INSPECTION SPI OPTICAL"]:::stage]

    H --> I[stepI["STEP 9 REFLOW PEAK 245 DEG C"]:::stage]
    I --> J[stepJ["STEP 10 COOLING ZONE"]:::stage]

    K --> L{stepL["YIELD ABOVE 90 PERCENT"]}
    L -- YES --> M[stepM["STEP 12 PASS TO FUNCTIONAL TEST"]:::stage]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Surface Mount Technology (SMT). List any four advantages of SMT over Through-Hole Technology (THT).
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
SMT is a method of assembling electronic circuits where the components (called SMDs) are directly mounted on the surface of the PCB, instead of being inserted into drilled holes.

**Four Advantages (½ mark each):**
1. Higher component density (miniaturization).
2. Faster and automated assembly → lower production cost.
3. Better high-frequency performance due to shorter leads.
4. Components can be placed on **both sides** of the PCB.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's Note: Students often write only 2 advantages → loss of 1 mark. Always list exactly 4 in a bulleted manner for full marks.

---

### **Q2.** What is a *fiducial mark* in an SMT stencil printer? Why is it important?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A fiducial mark is a small **circular or cross-shaped copper pad** placed on the PCB (outside the component area) that serves as a **reference point** for the vision system of the pick-and-place machine and the stencil alignment camera.

**Importance (any two):**
- Enables accurate **stencil-to-PCB alignment** (within ± 0.025 mm).
- Allows automated correction of **X-Y offset and rotation**.
- Improves **first-pass yield** and reduces solder defects.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU ESE Module Choice Rule**, you are given **Question A** OR **Question B**. Only attempt one of the two.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Process + Numerical + Defect Focus)*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the sequential steps of assembling an electronic circuit on an SMT station. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A stencil aperture of length $L = 1.6\,\text{mm}$ and width $W = 0.8\,\text{mm}$ is etched into a stainless-steel stencil of thickness $T = 0.12\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the **Area Ratio** and comment on its acceptability for paste release. If the same pad must accommodate a smaller 0603 SMD, suggest the appropriate stencil thickness. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Sequential SMT Process (7 Marks)** — *(List each step, 1 mark each, with a small block-diagram description)*

1. **PCB Preparation:** Cleaning, baking to remove moisture, fiducial verification.
2. **Solder Paste Printing:** Stencil placed, paste rolled with squeegee at 45° angle.
3. **Paste Inspection (SPI):** Optical / 3-D check of paste height & area.
4. **Pick-and-Place:** Vacuum nozzle picks SMDs from reels and places them on paste deposits.
5. **Pre-Reflow Visual Check:** Verify polarity, rotation, and placement offset.
6. **Reflow Soldering:** 4-zone temperature profile in reflow oven.
7. **Post-Reflow Inspection (AOI/X-Ray) and Rework:** Identify defects; rework with hot-air gun.

*Add a small Mermaid-style block (1 mark):*

```
PCB → STENCIL PRINT → PICK-PLACE → REFLOW OVEN → AOI → FINISHED ASSEMBLY
```

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Use the Area Ratio formula derived in Section 3.1.

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{L \times W}{2\,(L+W)\,T}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values.

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{1.6 \times 0.8}{2 \times (1.6 + 0.8) \times 0.12}
$$

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{1.28}{2 \times 2.4 \times 0.12}
$$

$$
\text{AR} = \frac{1.28}{0.576}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$
\text{AR} \approx 2.22
$$

**Step 4:** Comment on acceptability.
Since $2.22 \gg 0.66$, the design easily passes the **IPC-7525 paste-release criterion**. Excellent.

**Step 5:** Suggest a thinner stencil for 0603.
For a 0603 footprint (1.6 mm × 0.8 mm), the safe stencil thickness range is:

$$
T_{\text{recommended}} = 0.10 \,\text{mm to } 0.12\,\text{mm}
$$

(Thinner stencil ensures better paste release for fine-pitch components and avoids solder bridges.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Profile + Defect + Standards Focus)*

**(a)** Draw and explain the standard **reflow soldering temperature profile** for lead-free (SAC305) solder. Clearly mark all four zones. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** List any **six common SMT defects** with their probable causes and corrective actions. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Reflow Profile (7 Marks)**

*(2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explanation)*

| Zone | Temp Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Preheat** | 25 °C → 150 °C | Slow ramp (1–3 °C/s); activates flux & avoids thermal shock. |
| **2. Soak** | 150 °C → 200 °C | Equalizes PCB temperature; evaporates solvents in flux. |
| **3. Reflow (Peak)** | 217 °C → 245 °C (peak) | Solder melts; wetting of pads; intermetallic bond forms. |
| **4. Cooling** | 245 °C → 100 °C | Solidifies joint; controls grain structure (≤ 4 °C/s). |

**Key Data:** Time above liquidus (TAL) = 30 – 60 s. Peak must exceed $T_{\text{liquidus}} + 20\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, so $\ge 237\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for SAC305.

**(b) Six SMT Defects (7 Marks)** — *(1 mark for naming + 1 mark for each correct cause/cure pair, pick best 3 to fill 7 marks)*

| # | Defect | Cause | Cure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tombstoning** | Uneven paste volume | Balanced aperture; symmetric profile |
| 2 | **Solder Bridging** | Excess solder / misaligned stencil | Clean stencil; reduce thickness |
| 3 | **Insufficient Solder** | Clogged aperture | Increase aperture; clean stencil |
| 4 | **Cold Joint** | Peak temp not reached | Increase reflow temp (within limits) |
| 5 | **Solder Balling** | Paste contamination; moisture | Use fresh paste; preheat correctly |
| 6 | **Skewed Component** | Vibrations during placement | Calibrate placement machine; clamp PCB |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse **tombstoning** with **billboarding**. (Tombstoning = one end lifted; Billboard = component stands vertically.)
> - Do **NOT** write "reflow" as a one-step process. Always mention the **4-zone profile**.
> - In the numerical, if you forget to **comment on the AR ≥ 0.66 rule**, you lose 1 mark — comments are mandatory.
> - Spelling: "SAC305" and "SAC 305" are both accepted; **"SAC30"** or **"SAC350"** → 0 mark for that part.
> - For defects, examiners reward **triplets** (defect + cause + cure), not just names.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Bullet Checklist — Revise Before Exam:**

- ✅ SMT = mounting SMDs directly on PCB surface using solder paste + reflow.
- ✅ Six sequential stations: **Print → Place → Reflow → Inspect → Test → Rework**.
- ✅ Solder paste = **88–90 % metal powder + flux**; particle size **Type 3 (25–45 µm)**.
- ✅ Stencil **Area Ratio (AR)** must be $\ge 0.66$ (IPC-7525 rule).
- ✅ Lead-free peak temperature = **245 °C ± 5 °C** (SAC305 alloy).
- ✅ Standard SMD sizes: **0201, 0402, 0603, 0805, 1206** (imperial code).
- ✅ Reflow profile has 4 zones: **Preheat → Soak → Reflow → Cooling**.
- ✅ Time Above Liquidus (TAL) for SAC305 = **30 – 60 s**.
- ✅ Cooling rate $\le 4\,^{\circ}\text{C/s}$ to avoid brittle joints.
- ✅ Common defects: **Tombstoning, Bridging, Cold Joint, Solder Balling, Skew, Voiding**.
- ✅ Industry standards to mention in answers: **IPC-A-610, IPC-7525, IPC-J-STD-020**.
- ✅ KTU lab yield target: **≥ 90 %** acceptable joints for full record marks.
- ✅ Fiducial marks = **optical alignment reference** on PCB.
- ✅ SMT enables both **top and bottom** side component mounting.
- ✅ Through-hole vs SMT: SMT = high density, automated, high frequency; THT = strong mechanical bonds.
- ✅ Solder paste storage: refrigerated (2–10 °C); bring to room temperature before opening.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme (GXESL106)**

topics.title: Introduction to EDA tools (such as KiCad or XCircuit)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL106 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS  ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to EDA tools (such as KiCad or XCircuit)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** refers to the category of software tools used for designing, simulating, verifying, and manufacturing electronic systems — ranging from simple analog circuits to complex integrated circuits (ICs) and printed circuit boards (PCBs). Under the KTU 2024 syllabus for **GXESL106**, EDA tools form the digital backbone of the modern electronics workshop, replacing traditional hand-drawn schematics and manual breadboard prototyping with computer-aided precision.

In the context of **GXESL106 – Basic Electrical and Electronics Engineering Workshop**, students are introduced to two prominent, **open-source** EDA tools:

| Tool | Origin & License | Primary Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **KiCad** | Developed by Jean-Pierre Charras, GNU GPL v2 | End-to-end schematic capture + PCB layout + 3D viewer |
| **XCircuit** | Developed by Tim Edwards, GNU GPL | Schematic capture with netlist generation (academic focus) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Engineering Workbench"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):**
> Think of an EDA tool as a **"Digital Engineering Workbench"** — much like how an architect uses *AutoCAD* to design a house before construction, an electronics engineer uses **KiCad or XCircuit** to *virtually design* a circuit before physically soldering components on a board.
>
> - **Schematic Capture** = the *blueprint* of the circuit (showing components and connections).
> - **Simulation** = the *wind-tunnel test* (checking if the design works before building it).
> - **PCB Layout** = the *construction plan* (where each brick/component will physically sit on a real board).
> - **Gerber File Generation** = the *final instruction manual* sent to the PCB fabrication machine.

## 1.3 Why EDA Tools are Critical in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (GXESL106, Module 2):**
> The KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum mandates that every engineering graduate, irrespective of their specialization, must be capable of **schematic design, basic simulation, and interpreting PCB layouts**. EDA tools bridge the gap between **theoretical circuit analysis** and **practical hardware realization**.

The three foundational pillars that an EDA tool must support (KTU 2024 CO mapping):

1. **Schematic Capture** → Drawing the circuit using standard symbols.
2. **Circuit Simulation (SPICE-based)** → Verifying electrical behavior (e.g., voltage, current, waveforms).
3. **PCB Design & Fabrication Output** → Converting the schematic into a manufacturable board layout (Gerber/Drill files).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of an EDA Tool

Every EDA tool — whether commercial (like *Altium Designer*, *Cadence OrCAD*) or open-source (like *KiCad*, *XCircuit*) — follows a standardized **three-tier functional architecture**:

### Tier 1 — Front-End (Schematic Domain)
- **Function:** Symbolic representation of the circuit.
- **Components used:** Resistors, capacitors, inductors, ICs, transistors.
- **Output:** A *netlist* (a text file describing every component and the connections between them).
- **Analogy:** This is the **language translator** — it converts human-readable circuit diagrams into a machine-readable electrical description.

### Tier 2 — Simulation Engine (Behavioral Domain)
- **Function:** Numerically solves the circuit equations (Kirchhoff's Laws, nodal analysis) over time.
- **Engine:** Typically uses **SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis)** algorithms.
- **Output:** Waveforms, Bode plots, operating point values.

### Tier 3 — Back-End (Physical Layout Domain)
- **Function:** Translates the logical netlist into a physical geometry.
- **Outputs:** *Gerber files* (`.gbr`), *Drill files* (`.drl`), *Pick-and-Place files* (`.pos`).
- **Analogy:** This is the **construction blueprint** — instructing the CNC machine on where to cut, drill, and etch the copper.

## 2.2 Detailed Comparison: KiCad vs. XCircuit

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** KiCad is the **primary recommended tool** for KTU lab evaluations. XCircuit is mentioned as an academic alternative for understanding netlist generation. Students may be asked to *compare* the two in viva voce.

| Feature | **KiCad (v8.x and above)** | **XCircuit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **License** | Open-source (GPL) | Open-source (GPL) |
| **Schematic Capture** | Yes (Eeschema module) | Yes (Primary function) |
| **SPICE Simulation** | Integrated via `ngspice` | Not native (requires external SPICE) |
| **PCB Layout** | Yes (Pcbnew module — full feature) | No (Limited/Legacy) |
| **3D Visualization** | Yes (3D viewer with STEP export) | No |
| **Component Library** | Extensive (Global + Project-specific) | Basic (User-defined focus) |
| **Gerber Output** | Yes (Industry-standard RS-274X) | No |
| **Learning Curve** | Moderate (GUI-rich) | Steep (Scripting-oriented) |
| **Industrial Adoption** | High (used in startups, academia) | Low (mostly academic/research) |

## 2.3 The KTU Workflow — From Idea to Physical Board

The canonical workflow taught in the KTU 2024 workshop is the **"Schematic → Simulation → Layout"** pipeline:

1. **Project Initialization** — Create a new project directory (`.kicad_pro` file in KiCad).
2. **Symbol Selection** — Pick components from the library (e.g., `R`, `C`, `LM741`).
3. **Schematic Drafting** — Place symbols and wire them using the **Wire tool (`W`)**.
4. **Electrical Rule Check (ERC)** — Detects unconnected pins, power conflicts, etc.
5. **Netlist Annotation** — Assigns unique reference designators (R1, R2, U1).
6. **SPICE Simulation** — Solve the circuit's differential equations to predict behavior.
7. **Footprint Assignment** — Map each schematic symbol to a physical PCB package (e.g., 0805, SOIC-8).
8. **PCB Routing** — Place components on the board and draw copper traces.
9. **Design Rule Check (DRC)** — Verifies trace widths, clearances, and annular rings.
10. **Gerber Export** — Generate fabrication files for the PCB manufacturer.

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the *core conceptual equations* that underpin the EDA simulation engine. While EDA tools are primarily software, their internal mathematics is based on **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA)** and **SPICE algorithms**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Ohm's Law (Resistor Element):} \quad & V = I \cdot R \\
\textbf{2. Capacitor I-V Relation:} \quad & i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dv(t)}{dt} \\
\textbf{3. Inductor V-I Relation:} \quad & v(t) = L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt} \\
\textbf{4. Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):} \quad & \sum_{k=1}^{n} i_k(t) = 0 \\
\textbf{5. Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):} \quad & \sum_{k=1}^{n} v_k(t) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

| Concept / Parameter | Symbol / Unit | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operating Point (DC Analysis) | `.op` | Calculates all node voltages in **steady state** |
| Transient Analysis | `.tran` | Plots voltage/current vs. time (e.g., RC charging) |
| AC Sweep (Frequency Response) | `.ac` | Generates Bode plot (magnitude/phase vs. frequency) |
| Reference Designator | R1, C3, U1 | Unique ID for every component on the schematic |
| Net | `NetA`, `VCC` | A single electrical connection point (wire) |
| Netlist | `.net` file | Text-based machine-readable circuit description |
| Footprint | `0805`, `SOIC-8` | Physical land pattern on the PCB |
| Trace Width | $w$ (in **mils** or **mm**) | Width of the copper conductor on the PCB |
| Clearance | $c$ (in **mils** or **mm**) | Minimum distance between two copper features |
| Gerber File Extension | `.gbr` / `.gtl`, `.gbl` | Top copper layer, bottom copper layer, etc. |
| Drill File Extension | `.drl` (Excellon format) | Defines hole positions and sizes for vias/pads |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

EDA tools are the **silent enablers** of every modern electronic device. From the smartphone in a student's pocket to the avionics in a Boeing 747:

- **Semiconductor Industry:** Chips with **billions of transistors** (e.g., Apple M-series, AMD Ryzen) are *impossible* to design without EDA tools from Synopsys, Cadence, or Mentor Graphics.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Rapid prototyping of sensor nodes using KiCad + Arduino ecosystems.
- **Automotive Electronics:** Engine Control Units (ECUs), ADAS modules, and infotainment systems rely on multi-layer PCBs designed in Altium/Cadence.
- **Academic Research:** KiCad's open-source nature democratizes hardware research in universities worldwide — including IITs, NITs, and KTU-affiliated colleges.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — The SPICE Simulation Backbone

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this section matters for KTU:** Even though GXESL106 is a workshop course, the KTU 2024 scheme under NEP 2020 encourages **conceptual understanding of tool internals**. Examiners may ask: *"How does the EDA tool mathematically verify your circuit?"* — This section answers that.

When a student presses the **"Run Simulation"** button in KiCad (with `ngspice`), the software executes the following mathematical pipeline:

### Step 1 — Schematic to Netlist Conversion
The graphical schematic is first converted into a **SPICE netlist**, a plain-text file. For a simple **RC low-pass filter**, the netlist looks like:

```spice
* RC Low-Pass Filter
V1  IN 0  SIN(0 5 1k)
R1  IN OUT  1k
C1  OUT 0   100n
.tran 1us 5ms
.end
```

### Step 2 — Formulation of the MNA Matrix (Modified Nodal Analysis)
SPICE uses **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA)** to formulate a system of linear/non-linear equations. For the RC circuit above, applying KCL at node `OUT`:

$$
i_{C1}(t) + i_{R1\_to\_OUT}(t) = 0
$$

Substituting the element equations:

$$
C_1 \cdot \frac{dv_{OUT}(t)}{dt} + \frac{v_{OUT}(t) - v_{IN}(t)}{R_1} = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Discretization using Numerical Integration
SPICE does not solve differential equations analytically. It uses **numerical integration methods**. The most common are:

| Method | Formula (Capacitor Current) | Accuracy | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Backward Euler** | $i_{C,n+1} = \frac{C}{\Delta t} \cdot (v_{n+1} - v_n)$ | **1st order** | Robust, simple |
| **Trapezoidal Rule** | $i_{C,n+1} = \frac{2C}{\Delta t} \cdot (v_{n+1} - v_n) - i_{C,n}$ | **2nd order** | Default in SPICE |

Using the **Backward Euler** method, the KCL equation becomes an **algebraic equation** at each time step $n+1$:

$$
\frac{C_1}{\Delta t} \cdot (v_{OUT,n+1} - v_{OUT,n}) + \frac{v_{OUT,n+1} - v_{IN,n+1}}{R_1} = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Solving the System (Newton-Raphson Iteration)
For non-linear components (diodes, transistors), SPICE uses the **Newton-Raphson method** to iteratively converge to the solution at each time step. The update rule is:

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}
$$

where $f(x)$ is the residual of the MNA equation and $f'(x)$ is its Jacobian. Convergence is achieved when:

$$
\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \vert < \epsilon
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the **tolerance** (default: $10^{-12}$ in SPICE).

### Step 5 — Waveform Output Generation
Once all time steps are solved, SPICE writes a **`.raw` file** containing the numerical samples. The KiCad waveform viewer plots these values on a graph.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — KiCad File Ecosystem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Tip:** Examiners love asking — *"What files does KiCad generate and what is the purpose of each?"* — Master this table.

| File Extension | Module That Creates It | Purpose | Mandatory for Fabrication? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `.kicad_pro` | Project Manager | Master project file (settings, libraries) | No (project metadata) |
| `.kicad_sch` | Eeschema (Schematic) | The schematic drawing itself | No (source file) |
| `.kicad_pcb` | Pcbnew (PCB Layout) | The physical board layout | No (source file) |
| `.net` | Eeschema → Netlist | Machine-readable circuit description | No (intermediate) |
| `.gbr` / `.gtl` / `.gbl` | Pcbnew → Plot | Gerber files (copper layers, silkscreen) | **YES** |
| `.drl` | Pcbnew → Drill | Excellon drill file (hole positions) | **YES** |
| `.pos` | Pcbnew → Position | Pick-and-place file for assembly machines | Optional |
| `.step` / `.wrl` | 3D Viewer | 3D mechanical model of the board | No (visualization) |
| `.csv` / `.lib` | Symbol Editor | Custom component library definitions | No (library) |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Designing a Simple LED Circuit in KiCad (KTU Lab Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU lab exercise** for Module Experiment 2. Students are expected to demonstrate this flow during the continuous evaluation.

**Target Circuit:** A 5V DC source driving an LED (V_f = 2V, I_f = 20mA) through a current-limiting resistor.

**Step 1 — Calculate the resistor value (manual pre-check):**
$$
R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.020} = 150 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Open KiCad → New Project → "LED_Circuit".**
**Step 3 — In Eeschema, place components:**
- Battery symbol (`Battery_Cell`) → label as `V1` with value `5V`.
- Resistor symbol (`R`) → label as `R1` with value `150`.
- LED symbol (`LED`) → from the `Device` library.
- Ground symbol (`GND`) → power reference.

**Step 4 — Wire them using the `W` (Wire) tool.**
**Step 5 — Run Electrical Rule Check (ERC):** `Inspect → Electrical Rules Checker`. Should report `0 errors`.
**Step 6 — Assign Footprints:**
- Battery → `BatteryHolder_Keystone_1042`
- Resistor → `0805` (SMD) or `R_Axial_DIN0207` (through-hole)
- LED → `LED_0805` or `LED_D5.0mm`

**Step 7 — Switch to Pcbnew → Update PCB from Schematic.**
**Step 8 — Route the board** using the `Route` tool (`F9` shortcut). Use a trace width of **0.25 mm** (10 mils) for signal lines and **0.5 mm** for power.
**Step 9 — Run Design Rule Check (DRC):** Ensure no clearance violations.
**Step 10 — Generate Gerbers:** `File → Plot → Generate Drill Files`. The output is a ZIP archive sent to the PCB manufacturer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled after the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern for the workshop course GXESL106. Marks are distributed as per the official KTU pattern: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define EDA. List any two open-source EDA tools used in circuit design.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** refers to the category of computer software tools used to design, simulate, verify, and fabricate electronic circuits and systems. It replaces manual hand-drafting and breadboard-based prototyping with a digital, computer-aided workflow.
>
> Two open-source EDA tools are:
> 1. **KiCad** — A complete end-to-end EDA suite with schematic capture, SPICE simulation, and PCB layout.
> 2. **XCircuit** — An open-source schematic capture tool primarily used in academic settings for netlist generation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is a netlist? Why is it important in the EDA design flow?**

**Model Answer:**
> A **netlist** is a text-based, machine-readable file that describes every component in a circuit and the electrical connections (*nets*) between their pins. It is generated by the schematic editor and serves as the **bridge** between the schematic domain and the simulation/PCB layout domain.
>
> **Importance:**
> - It is the **input** to the SPICE simulation engine.
> - It is the **input** to the PCB layout tool (Pcbnew) for footprint mapping.
> - It enables **design portability** across different EDA tools.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture** of an EDA tool. Differentiate between **Schematic Capture**, **Simulation**, and **PCB Layout** domains. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **KiCad** and **XCircuit** as EDA tools based on at least **six parameters** (e.g., license, features, simulation support, PCB layout, industry use, learning curve). **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Three-Tier Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step explanation expected by KTU examiner:**

| Tier | Name | Function | Tool Example (KiCad) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tier 1** | Schematic Domain | Symbolic circuit representation; outputs netlist. | Eeschema |
| **Tier 2** | Simulation Domain | SPICE-based numerical solving; outputs waveforms. | ngspice + KiCad Simulator |
| **Tier 3** | Physical Domain | PCB layout, routing, Gerber generation. | Pcbnew + 3D Viewer |

**Explanation:**
- **Tier 1 (Schematic Capture):** The user places component symbols (resistor, capacitor, IC) on a virtual canvas and wires them using the wire tool. The output is a `.kicad_sch` file. The schematic editor then generates a **netlist** that lists every component (with reference designator and value) and every connection.
- **Tier 2 (Simulation):** The netlist is fed to the SPICE engine, which formulates **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA)** equations and solves them using numerical integration (Backward Euler / Trapezoidal rule) and Newton-Raphson iteration. The output is a `.raw` file containing time-domain or frequency-domain data.
- **Tier 3 (PCB Layout):** The netlist is also used to import components into the PCB editor. The user assigns **footprints** (e.g., 0805, SOIC-8), places components on the board, and routes copper traces. The final output is a set of **Gerber files** (`.gtl`, `.gbl`, etc.) and a **Drill file** (`.drl`) sent to the PCB manufacturer.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (KiCad vs. XCircuit)

| Parameter | **KiCad** | **XCircuit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **License** | GNU GPL (Open-source) | GNU GPL (Open-source) |
| **Schematic Capture** | Advanced GUI-based | Basic, script-friendly |
| **SPICE Simulation** | Native integration with `ngspice` | Not natively supported |
| **PCB Layout** | Full-featured (multi-layer, BGA support) | Limited / No native support |
| **3D Visualization** | Built-in 3D viewer with STEP export | Not available |
| **Gerber Output** | Yes (RS-274X standard) | No |
| **Component Library** | Extensive, with online repositories (SnapEDA, Digi-Key) | User-defined, minimal defaults |
| **Industry Adoption** | High (startups, academia, hobbyists) | Low (academic/research only) |
| **Learning Curve** | Moderate (rich GUI, but well-documented) | Steep (command-line heavy) |
| **KTU Recommendation** | **Primary tool** for lab evaluations | Supplementary / conceptual study |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **Schematic Capture** with **PCB Layout** — they are *two distinct domains*. Schematic = logical/symbolic. PCB = physical/geometric.
> - **Do NOT** claim that XCircuit supports PCB design — it is primarily a schematic capture tool.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention **Gerber files** when discussing the output of the PCB layout stage — this is a frequent omission in answer sheets.
> - **Always** use proper terminology: *netlist*, *footprint*, *reference designator*, *DRC*, *ERC*.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **SPICE-based simulation pipeline** of an EDA tool. Describe how a simple RC circuit is converted from a schematic to a waveform plot, mentioning the role of **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA)**, **Backward Euler discretization**, and **Newton-Raphson iteration**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List the **ten sequential steps** of the standard KiCad design flow as taught in the KTU 2024 workshop. Explain any **three steps** in detail with diagrams/screenshots-equivalent descriptions. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — SPICE Simulation Pipeline

The **SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis)** simulation pipeline consists of five stages:

**Stage 1 — Netlist Generation:**
The schematic editor exports a plain-text netlist. For an RC low-pass filter with $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 100\,\text{nF}$, the netlist is:

```spice
V1  IN 0  SIN(0 5 1k)
R1  IN OUT 1k
C1  OUT 0  100n
.tran 1us 5ms
.end
```

**Stage 2 — MNA Formulation:**
Applying KCL at node `OUT`:

$$
C_1 \cdot \frac{dv_{OUT}(t)}{dt} + \frac{v_{OUT}(t) - v_{IN}(t)}{R_1} = 0
$$

This is rearranged into the standard MNA matrix form:

$$
\mathbf{G} \cdot \mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{C} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{x}(t)}{dt} = \mathbf{b}(t)
$$

**Stage 3 — Discretization (Backward Euler):**

$$
i_{C,n+1} = \frac{C_1}{\Delta t} \cdot (v_{OUT,n+1} - v_{OUT,n})
$$

Substituting into the MNA equation:

$$
\left(\frac{C_1}{\Delta t} + \frac{1}{R_1}\right) v_{OUT,n+1} = \frac{C_1}{\Delta t} \cdot v_{OUT,n} + \frac{v_{IN,n+1}}{R_1}
$$

**Stage 4 — Newton-Raphson Solution (for non-linear elements):**

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}
$$

Convergence criterion: $\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \vert < 10^{-12}$.

**Stage 5 — Waveform Output:**
The solver writes time-domain samples to a `.raw` file, which the KiCad waveform viewer plots as a graph showing the RC charging curve (exponential rise from 0V to 5V with time constant $\tau = R \cdot C = 100\,\mu\text{s}$).

#### Part (b) — Ten Sequential Steps of KiCad Design Flow

1. **Project Initialization** — Create a new project directory and `.kicad_pro` file.
2. **Schematic Symbol Placement** — Open Eeschema, place components from the library.
3. **Wiring** — Connect components using the wire tool (`W` shortcut).
4. **Annotation** — Assign unique reference designators (R1, R2, C1, U1).
5. **Electrical Rules Check (ERC)** — Verify no unconnected pins or power conflicts.
6. **Footprint Assignment** — Map each symbol to a physical PCB package.
7. **PCB Layout Import** — Update Pcbnew from the schematic netlist.
8. **Component Placement & Routing** — Arrange components and draw copper traces.
9. **Design Rule Check (DRC)** — Verify trace widths, clearances, and via sizes.
10. **Gerber Export** — Generate fabrication files and a ZIP archive for the manufacturer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes in Part (b):**
> - Students often **skip Step 5 (ERC)** and **Step 9 (DRC)**, treating them as optional. They are **mandatory quality gates** — omitting them is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> - Confusing the **time constant formula** $\tau = RC$ with the **cutoff frequency** $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — the examiner will deduct marks if the units are inconsistent.
> - Failing to mention the **default SPICE tolerance** ($10^{-12}$) — this is a high-yield KTU fact.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **EDA = Electronic Design Automation** — software for designing, simulating, and fabricating electronic circuits.
- **KiCad** is the **primary open-source EDA tool** recommended by KTU 2024; **XCircuit** is a supplementary academic tool.
- The **three-tier architecture** of any EDA tool is: **Schematic → Simulation → PCB Layout**.
- The output of the schematic editor is a **netlist** (`.net`) — this is the **bridge** between logical and physical domains.
- **SPICE** (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis) is the standard simulation engine used internally.
- **MNA (Modified Nodal Analysis)** is the matrix-based method used by SPICE to formulate circuit equations.
- **Backward Euler** and **Trapezoidal Rule** are the two numerical integration methods used to discretize capacitor/inductor equations.
- **Newton-Raphson iteration** solves non-linear equations (e.g., for diodes, transistors) with convergence tolerance of $10^{-12}$.
- The **time constant** of an RC circuit is $\tau = R \cdot C$ (in **seconds**).
- The **cutoff frequency** of an RC low-pass filter is $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (in **Hz**).
- **ERC (Electrical Rules Check)** validates the schematic; **DRC (Design Rule Check)** validates the PCB layout.
- **Gerber files** (`.gtl`, `.gbl`) and **Drill files** (`.drl`) are the **mandatory fabrication outputs** sent to the PCB manufacturer.
- **Reference designators** (R1, C3, U1) uniquely identify every component on the schematic.
- **Footprints** (0805, SOIC-8, DIP-14) define the physical land pattern on the PCB.
- KiCad modules to remember: **Eeschema** (schematic), **Pcbnew** (layout), **ngspice** (simulation), **3D Viewer** (visualization).
- **PCB trace width** is typically **0.25 mm** for signals and **0.5 mm** for power lines.
- **Clearance** is the minimum spacing between two copper features — typical value is **0.2 mm** for hobbyist boards.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Introduction to EDA Tools (KiCad / XCircuit)**
